One thing I did find genuinely interesting is the training-only property of LCA — the fact that inference cost is unchanged is a real practical advantage and I think this deserves more emphasis.

Overall, I think this is a borderline paper. The idea is sensible, the execution is okay, but the experimental scope is too limited and the novelty argument needs to be made more carefully. I'd encourage the authors to either strengthen the theoretical grounding of the loss design or significantly expand the empirical evaluation before this is ready for publication.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically motivated problem — building a single neural model capable of solving multiple VRP variants, including zero-shot transfer to unseen problem combinations. The core idea of decomposing VRP variants into "attributes" and using this as a multi-task training signal is reasonable and I think has real merit. That said, I have several concerns that I think need to be addressed before this work is ready for publication.

The attribute decomposition idea (Section 3, around page 4-5) is the heart of the paper, but I found the justification for *why* these specific attributes were chosen somewhat hand-wavy. How sensitive are results to this particular decomposition? Could different choices of attributes lead to substantially different performance? The paper doesn't really engage with this question, which feels like a significant gap. In my experience, these design choices often matter enormously in practice.

The experimental results in Table 2 are the main empirical contribution, and they look decent on the surface. But I'm skeptical of the comparison baselines. Comparing against single-task models that were only trained on individual VRPs seems a bit too easy — what about other unified/generalist neural solvers? POMO and its variants have been extended in various ways; I'd want to see comparisons against more recent work on generalist combinatorial optimization, not just narrow single-task specialists. The zero-shot numbers (4.6% and 7.0% gap) sound good but I genuinely can't tell if this is impressive without a stronger baseline.

The eleven VRP variants tested — I found myself wondering whether these are truly "diverse" or whether they cluster around similar problem structures. CVRP, OVRP, VRPB, etc. are all fairly closely related. Zero-shot to a completely different problem class would be more convincing. I might be wrong about how different these actually are structurally, but the paper doesn't do enough to convince me.

Writing is generally clear but Figure 3 is honestly a bit hard to parse on first read — took me a while to understand what the color coding was indicating. Also, the ablation study (Table 3 I think?) is appreciated but feels somewhat thin. I'd want to see more systematic ablations on the attribute embedding design.

One thing that genuinely bothered me: the computational cost comparison is almost entirely absent. A unified model trained on 11 tasks presumably takes significantly longer to train than individual models. For practitioners, this matters a lot.

Overall — the idea is solid and the zero-shot generalization angle is genuinely novel in this space. But the paper in its current form feels like it needs stronger baselines, better ablations, and more honest engagement with the design choices. I'm leaning toward rejection but would reconsider with major revisions. Not a bad paper, just not quite there yet.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — what happens to federated learning when client data distributions shift over time and clients come and go? The motivation is sound, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to formalize something that practitioners almost certainly encounter constantly but that the theory community has largely swept under the rug.

That said, I have significant concerns about both the technical novelty and the experimental validation.

On the theory side: the convergence analysis in Section 4 feels like a fairly incremental extension of existing FedAvg analyses (e.g., the work by Li et al. 2020, Karimireddy et al. 2020). The main result showing faster convergence than FedAvg in time-evolving scenarios is contingent on the "approximation quality" of local objective functions, which is essentially a free parameter. I found myself reading Theorem 2 twice and wondering — under what realistic conditions is this approximation quality actually guaranteed to be good? The bound feels vacuous if you can't bound the approximation error independently. The authors sort of acknowledge this but then move on. That's not satisfying.

The CFL framework itself — the idea of "extracting information from past local datasets" — is conceptually similar to what the continual learning community has been doing for years with regularization-based methods (EWC, SI, etc.) and replay buffers. I think the paper would be stronger if it engaged more seriously with this literature rather than treating FL and continual learning as almost entirely separate bodies of work. The connection is mentioned briefly but deserves much more careful treatment. Are there fundamental differences, or is this essentially EWC applied to the federated setting? If the latter, that's fine, but be upfront about it.

Experimentally, I'm not fully convinced. The baselines chosen are reasonable but I notice the paper doesn't compare against some recent work on personalized FL (e.g., pFedMe, Ditto) which also handles heterogeneity and might be competitive in the time-evolving setting. Also the datasets used are fairly standard benchmarks — I'd want to see at least one experiment on a dataset that actually reflects time-evolving dynamics in a realistic way, not just artificially partitioned CIFAR. The "intermittent clients" experiments in Table 3 are the most interesting to me but they feel underdeveloped.

Minor things: Figure 2 is hard to read — the axis labels are tiny and the color scheme doesn't print well in grayscale. Page 7 has a notation inconsistency between equation 5 and the surrounding text that confused me briefly.

I think this is potentially publishable work but in its current form it reads more like a workshop paper that got extended. The core ideas need sharper positioning relative to both the FL optimization literature AND the continual learning literature. The convergence result needs either a stronger grounding or more honest discussion of its limitations. I'm leaning toward rejection but would genuinely reconsider with a strong revision.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically important problem — how do we build better domain-specific benchmarks for VLMs without drowning in annotation costs? The task augmentation concept is the central contribution, and honestly I think it's a reasonable idea, though I'm not sure it's as novel as the authors seem to believe. The basic idea of deriving multiple evaluation tasks from existing annotations plus metadata is something practitioners have been doing informally for years; what this paper does is formalize and systematize it, which has value but probably deserves a more measured framing.

The scale of the effort is genuinely impressive. 162,946 human baseline answers across 37,171 tasks is a lot of data, and I appreciate that they collected six rater responses per item to quantify ambiguity — this is actually one of the stronger methodological choices in the paper and somewhat underexplored in related benchmarking work. Figure 3 (if I'm reading the draft correctly) showing the distribution of human agreement scores is informative, though I'd want to see more analysis of what happens to model rankings when you filter by ambiguity level. Does the ranking change substantially? That seems crucial.

My bigger concern is about construct validity. The metadata sources — human annotators, rules, and existing models — vary enormously in reliability, and I found the discussion of how errors propagate from these sources into the benchmark tasks somewhat hand-wavy. Specifically, using depth estimation models to generate ground truth labels for "which object is closer" feels circular when you're then benchmarking VLMs that may use similar underlying representations. I might be wrong about the specific models used, but this needs a much more careful treatment.

The benchmarking results across 21 models are interesting but I found myself wanting more insight rather than just more numbers. The finding that frontier closed models outperform open ones is... not surprising. After reading this twice I still wasn't sure what the actionable takeaway is for someone building a domain-specific VLM. The autonomous driving vs. kitchen domain performance gaps are suggestive but underdiscussed.

The COCO and KITTI choices are defensible but also a bit dated at this point. I understand the appeal — well-known, lots of existing annotations — but it does raise questions about whether the findings generalize to more recent data distributions. Also the abstract gets cut off, which I assume is a formatting issue but it's a bit sloppy for submission.

Overall this is solid applied work with a genuine contribution to benchmark methodology, but the paper needs to sharpen its novelty claims, address the circularity concern with model-derived labels more rigorously, and do more with the ambiguity data it's collected. I lean toward weak accept but it needs revision. The community would benefit from this benchmark being public and well-documented, and I'd want assurance that will happen.

This is a solid engineering paper that demonstrates impressive results across a wide range of vision-language benchmarks. The core contribution — jointly scaling vision and language components in a unified multimodal model — is well-motivated and the empirical results are hard to argue with. State-of-the-art on VQAv2, TextVQA, and several captioning benchmarks is genuinely impressive.

That said, I have some concerns about the novelty framing. The architecture itself is fairly straightforward — a ViT encoder feeding into an encoder-decoder language model — and I think the paper sometimes oversells this as more architecturally novel than it is. The real contribution is the scale and the careful mixture of training data, which is valuable but feels underemphasized relative to the architectural description. After reading sections 3 and 4 twice, I'm still not entirely clear what the "new" design decisions are versus what was inherited wholesale from PaLM and existing ViT work.

The ViT-e scaling experiments are genuinely interesting and I appreciate that they actually ran these ablations (Table 4 I think? the vision scaling results). In my experience, papers often claim scaling benefits without actually showing the curves. Here they do show it, though I wish the analysis went deeper — why does vision scaling help more on some tasks than others? The OCR-heavy tasks seem to benefit disproportionately and there's a handwavy explanation but no real mechanistic insight.

The multilingual angle is highlighted in the title but honestly feels like an afterthought in the actual technical content. The WebLI dataset construction is described briefly but I have real questions about quality control, especially for lower-resource languages. How does performance degrade across the 100+ languages? There's aggregate multilingual performance reported but I couldn't find per-language breakdowns for anything beyond a handful of languages. This feels like a gap.

I'm also mildly concerned about the evaluation setup. For several benchmarks the model is fine-tuned rather than evaluated zero-shot or few-shot, which makes direct comparison with other methods tricky. The paper is generally upfront about this but the benchmark numbers in the abstract could mislead a casual reader.

Minor things: Figure 2 is quite hard to read at print size. The related work section is competent but doesn't engage seriously with concurrent work like Flamingo (the comparison in Table 5 is there but the discussion is thin).

Overall I think this paper deserves acceptance — the scale of the effort, the multilingual training data, and the empirical results all represent real contributions to the community. But I'd push the authors to be more honest about what's genuinely new architecturally versus what's "we scaled it up," and to provide more thorough multilingual analysis. The paper as written sometimes reads like a technical report dressed up as a research paper, which I find slightly frustrating even when the underlying work is strong.

Lean accept.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely question — can we build more continually-learning systems by incorporating multiple brain-inspired mechanisms together rather than in isolation? The premise is appealing and I think the motivation is generally well-articulated in the introduction.

That said, I have significant concerns about the paper's contributions and execution.

The work feels more like an ablation study or engineering report than a scientific contribution with clear hypotheses. The authors combine sparse representations, Hebbian learning, synaptic consolidation, and replay, then test combinations of these — but I found myself asking "so what?" after reading the results. What did we *learn* about why these mechanisms interact the way they do? The paper is descriptive rather than explanatory. Table 2 and Table 3 show performance numbers across configurations, but the analysis of *why* certain combinations work is shallow. I kept waiting for a deeper mechanistic insight that never arrived.

The biological plausibility claims also need more scrutiny. Dale's principle and dendritic compartments are incorporated (Section 3.1-3.2), which I appreciate, but the connection between the computational abstractions used here and actual neuroscience remains quite loose. Calling something "dendritic-like" because it has a two-compartment structure is... a stretch. I'm not saying it's wrong, but the authors should be more careful with their language throughout. The word "plausible" appears frequently without always being rigorously justified.

On the experiments — the benchmarks used (Split MNIST, Permuted MNIST) are honestly getting a bit stale in the continual learning literature. After reading this twice I wasn't convinced the chosen benchmarks adequately stress-test the claimed advantages. How does this framework scale? What happens on something like Split CIFAR or a more realistic sequential task? The absence of harder benchmarks is a real weakness for a paper appearing in 2024.

I also found the comparison to baseline methods in Section 5 somewhat unfair. The proposed framework combines multiple mechanisms simultaneously, while many baselines use single mechanisms. Of course it's going to win. This isn't a controlled comparison — it's comparing a kitchen sink to individual ingredients. The ablation in Table 3 helps somewhat but doesn't fully address this.

I might be wrong about this, but I think the paper's framing would be stronger if it committed more clearly to one of two stories: either "here's a new architecture" (in which case, compare more seriously to SOTA) or "here's a scientific study of mechanism interactions" (in which case, develop much clearer hypotheses upfront and test them rigorously).

The writing is generally clear, though Section 3.3 on Hebbian learning gets dense and could use a clearer intuitive explanation before the equations.

In my experience, this type of survey-meets-ablation paper often struggles to find its identity. I think there's a publishable idea somewhere here, but the current submission needs substantial revision — both in terms of experimental scope and in terms of sharpening its scientific claims. I lean toward rejection in current form.

This paper tackles large-batch training, a topic I've spent considerable time thinking about, and the core observation motivating the work — that data augmentation's benefits diminish at very large batch sizes — is reasonable and worth investigating. The proposed ConAdv method is conceptually straightforward: decouple the two sequential gradient computations in adversarial training by using stale parameters from the previous step, allowing them to run concurrently. I get it. It's a clean engineering insight.

That said, I have several concerns that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

First, the experimental comparison feels thin. The 96K batch size result for ResNet-50 (75.3% / 76.2%) is presented somewhat triumphantly as a first, but the comparisons to prior large-batch methods in Table 2 are not entirely convincing to me. I'd really want to see a more careful ablation separating the effect of the staleness approximation from the adversarial perturbation itself. The authors kind of gesture at this but don't nail it down. How much accuracy loss comes from using stale parameters specifically? This is the crux of the method and it deserves more scrutiny.

Second, the theoretical justification in Section 3 feels incomplete. The argument that stale gradients are a reasonable approximation rests on some smoothness assumptions that are, in my experience with this literature, often not satisfied in practice, especially at large batch sizes where the loss landscape can be quite weird. I might be wrong, but I don't think the bound in Equation 6 is tight enough to be convincing — the staleness error term looks like it could dominate in realistic settings. The authors should either tighten this or be more honest about the limitations.

I also found the writing in Section 2 a bit muddled. After reading it twice I was still unclear about exactly how the concurrent scheduling works in practice — the description of the pipeline in Figure 2 helps, but the text around lines 180-210 could be substantially clearer.

One thing that genuinely impressed me: the result on EfficientNet is interesting and somewhat underplayed. EfficientNet is notoriously sensitive to training recipes and getting it to scale to large batches is non-trivial. I wish the authors had spent more time on this rather than the extended ResNet-50 analysis.

The related work section misses some relevant connections to sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) literature, which addresses similar flat-minima motivations and has had a lot of traction recently. The relationship to SAM deserves explicit discussion — ConAdv feels like it's in the same family of ideas and ignoring that lineage is a problem.

Overall, I think this is a decent paper with a useful practical contribution, but the theoretical analysis needs work and the empirical evaluation needs to be more thorough. I'm leaning toward weak reject currently, but I'd genuinely reconsider if the authors can address the staleness analysis and the SAM comparison in the rebuttal.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely question: can we understand AlphaGo Zero formally and extend its principles beyond two-player games? The motivation is clear and I appreciate the ambition here. That said, I have some significant concerns about the execution.

The theoretical contribution in Section 3 — establishing a bound on policy performance differences via KL divergence — is the most interesting part to me. The bound relating expected return differences to KL divergence (around Equation 6-7) is novel as far as I know, and provides a cleaner theoretical justification for why the cross-entropy training in AGZ makes sense. I found myself rereading this section twice and I think it holds up, though the proof in the appendix feels rushed and I'd like to see the constants made more explicit. Are these bounds tight? The paper doesn't really discuss this.

The robust MDP framing is where I'm more skeptical. The idea of casting the agent vs. nature interaction as a zero-sum game is not new — Nilim and El Ghaoui (2005), Iyengar (2005), and quite a bit of subsequent work have explored robust MDPs extensively. I think the authors are aware of this but I felt the paper undersells how much prior work exists here and overstates the novelty of this particular framing. The connection to the existing robust MDP literature needed to be much more carefully positioned. What specifically does this paper add to what we already know from that literature?

The networking scheduling experiment (Section 5) is interesting and the results look good — matching Earliest Deadline First after decades of research is a compelling story. But I'm honestly not sure how much of this is due to the theoretical contributions versus just "MCTS works well empirically." The ablation studies are thin. What happens without the robust MDP framing? What's the baseline just using standard MCTS/self-play without the nature adversary? Table 2 doesn't answer this satisfactorily.

I also found the self-play = Nash equilibrium argument (Section 2) somewhat hand-wavy. The claim that AGZ's self-play converges to Nash is stated fairly confidently but the formal conditions under which this holds seem understated. In my experience with two-player zero-sum games, convergence to Nash via self-play is highly dependent on implementation details that AGZ uses (like the specific MCTS formulation, temperature parameters, etc.) and I don't think the paper adequately addresses this.

Minor things: Figure 3 is hard to read, the font is tiny. Some notation in Section 3 is introduced without adequate definition.

Overall I think there's a real paper here — the KL divergence bound is genuinely useful and the empirical results are solid — but the theoretical novelty relative to existing robust MDP literature needs to be clarified substantially, and the experimental evaluation needs more rigorous ablations. I'm leaning toward rejection in current form but would be open to reconsidering with major revisions.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically important problem — why does beam search produce such degenerate text even from strong language models, and what should we do instead? The core observation about maximization-based decoding being fundamentally mismatched with open-ended generation is compelling, and I think the "nucleus sampling" proposal is intuitive enough that I can see it getting traction in the community.

That said, I have some real concerns here.

The theoretical justification for why the probability distribution develops an "unreliable tail" (Section 3, roughly) feels underdeveloped. The authors gesture at distributional mismatch and the "surprising" tokens argument, but I wanted something more rigorous. Why exactly does training with MLE cause this tail behavior? I've read related work on exposure bias (Bengio et al., Ranzato et al.) and I don't feel like this paper adequately situates itself there or explains whether nucleus sampling actually addresses the root cause versus just patching a symptom. After reading this section twice I'm still not fully satisfied.

The human evaluation (Section 5 I believe) is the empirical centerpiece, and while I appreciate that the authors ran one at all, the setup concerns me. The evaluators are judging "quality" and "coherence" — notoriously subjective axes — and the inter-annotator agreement numbers reported aren't great. I'm also not sure the story generation prompts are representative enough to make broad claims about "open-ended text generation" writ large. That's a big claim.

Figure 3 is actually quite nice and probably the most convincing piece of evidence in the paper. The repetition curves for beam search are damning and the contrast with nucleus sampling is clear. I wish the authors leaned into this more quantitatively rather than relying so heavily on the human eval.

The comparison to top-k sampling feels a bit strawmanned — the authors set k to fixed values and then show it underperforms, but in my experience practitioners tune k pretty carefully. I'd want to see nucleus sampling compared against well-tuned top-k more rigorously before declaring victory.

I'm also mildly bothered that the method introduces a hyperparameter p that needs to be set, and the sensitivity analysis is... thin. The authors seem to suggest p is robust across [0.9, 0.95] but that's not a very wide range to test and the claim deserves more scrutiny.

Minor thing: the writing is generally clear but there are a few places in Section 2 where the notation gets sloppy and I had to re-read to follow.

Overall — I think this paper has a genuinely useful contribution. Nucleus sampling is simple, makes intuitive sense, and the empirical results are mostly convincing. My hesitation is that the theoretical framing oversells what's been proven versus observed. I'd lean toward acceptance with revisions, but the authors need to either sharpen the theoretical claims or walk them back to match what the experiments actually demonstrate.

This paper proposes SePO, a token-level selective preference optimization method that uses DPO as a reward estimator to identify "key tokens" for training. The core idea is reasonably novel and the paper is generally well-written, though I have several concerns.

The theoretical contribution in Section 3 — proving DPO can function as a token-level reward estimator — is the paper's main claim to novelty, and I found myself going back to Proposition 1 and 2 multiple times. I'm not entirely sure the theoretical guarantees are as strong as presented. The connection to the Bradley-Terry model feels a bit hand-wavy, and in my experience these kinds of token-level decompositions of sequence-level objectives carry implicit independence assumptions that aren't fully acknowledged. The authors should be more upfront about when these assumptions break down.

The empirical results in Table 2 are impressive on the surface — outperforming baselines with only 30% of tokens is a compelling headline. But I'm bothered by the baseline selection. TDPO and some of the other comparisons use different base models or training setups, making direct comparison difficult. I'd want to see cleaner ablations controlling for compute budget, since the whole pitch is efficiency.

The weak-to-strong generalization experiments (Section 5.3) are interesting and I think actually undersell themselves. The finding that a 7B oracle can meaningfully supervise a 70B policy model is genuinely surprising. That said, Figure 4 is pretty hard to read at conference print size, and the error bars are missing — are these results stable across seeds?

I'm also skeptical about the 30% threshold being presented almost as a universal finding. The ablation in Figure 3 shows performance vs. token selection ratio, but this is only on one benchmark if I'm reading correctly. Does this hold across domains? Coding tasks vs. open-ended generation likely have very different "key token" distributions, and this feels underexplored.

The out-of-distribution generalization result is interesting but the experimental setup in Section 5.4 needs clarification. What exactly counts as OOD here? The description on page 8 is vague enough that I couldn't reproduce the setup.

Minor things: the related work section is fine but doesn't engage seriously with RLHF-based token-level methods like those from Ziegler et al. or more recent process reward model literature. The writing occasionally gets sloppy — "supervise the target policy model with a contrastive objective function" appears in both abstract and intro without ever really being defined until page 5.

Overall: the paper has a good core idea and solid results, but the theoretical claims are stronger than the proofs warrant, and the empirical evaluation has enough gaps that I'm not fully convinced. I lean toward weak accept — the contribution is real, but the paper would benefit significantly from tightening the theory and adding the missing ablations. If the authors can address the compute-controlled comparisons and clarify the OOD setup, this would be a clearer accept.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — reducing the computational cost of simulating high-dimensional fluid dynamics while preserving time-reversibility and capturing nonlinear behavior. The core idea of optimizing over the Grassmannian manifold using stochastic Riemannian optimization is sound, and I appreciate that the authors think carefully about the geometric structure of the problem rather than just throwing a neural network at it.

That said, I have significant concerns that prevent me from recommending acceptance in the current form.

The claims in the abstract are bold — "45%-97% reduction in discrepancy" — but after reading the experimental section twice I'm genuinely uncertain how robust these numbers are. The benchmarks described feel somewhat cherry-picked, and I'd want to see comparisons against stronger baselines. In particular, the paper compares against what I'd consider fairly standard linear subspace methods, but doesn't seriously engage with more recent data-driven reduced order modeling approaches (e.g., operator inference, or the line of work from Peherstorfer and colleagues). This omission is conspicuous.

The theoretical contributions around differentiability of reduced trajectories (Section 4, around equation 12 if I recall correctly) are interesting but the conditions stated feel quite restrictive. The requirement on timestep sizes is stated somewhat casually — I'd like to see a more careful analysis of what happens near the boundary of these conditions in practice. Do the authors have any empirical evidence that these conditions are actually satisfied in their experiments? This felt hand-wavy to me.

The tensor precomputation trick is honestly the most novel part of this work in my view, and I think it's undersold. The efficiency argument is compelling. But I'm not entirely sure the complexity analysis in Table 2 is apples-to-apples with the baselines — the memory overhead of storing precomputed tensors for large systems seems like it could be substantial, and I didn't see this discussed adequately.

A minor but genuine annoyance: Figure 3 is nearly illegible at normal zoom levels. The color coding is very hard to distinguish. Please fix this.

I'm also a bit skeptical of the framing around "time reversibility." In my experience with fluid simulation, strict time reversibility is theoretically appealing but practically the simulations people actually care about involve dissipation. The paper acknowledges this briefly but I think the motivation deserves more scrutiny — who exactly is the target user who needs reversibility AND wants model reduction?

The writing is generally clear but there are several places (particularly in Section 3) where the notation gets quite dense and I had to re-read multiple times. Not a dealbreaker but it needs cleanup.

Overall: interesting ideas, some genuine technical contributions, but the experimental validation needs strengthening and several theoretical gaps need addressing. I lean toward rejection but would reconsider with substantial revisions.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — reducing the computational cost of Vision Transformers by pruning/fusing inattentive tokens during inference. The core idea of using class token attention as a proxy for token importance, then either discarding or fusing inattentive tokens into a single "fused" representation, is intuitive and the results on DeiT are reasonably compelling.

That said, I have some concerns I want to work through.

The main technical contribution (Section 3) is fairly straightforward — identify top-k tokens by CLS attention score, keep those, fuse the rest via attention-weighted mean pooling, concatenate back. It works, but I think the paper somewhat oversells the novelty here. ToMe (Token Merging, Bolya et al.) and prior work like DynamicViT do very similar things, and while the authors discuss related work, I didn't feel the differentiation was crisp enough. The "fusing" vs "dropping" distinction is real but feels incremental.

On the experiments: Table 1 is the centerpiece and the numbers look good, but I'm a bit skeptical about the throughput measurements. What hardware? What batch size? These details seem to be buried or missing — I looked twice and couldn't find a consistent specification. Throughput numbers can vary dramatically with batch size and memory layout, and I've seen papers cherry-pick these. Please be explicit.

The second use case — using EViT to process higher-resolution inputs at the same FLOPs budget — is actually the more interesting contribution to me, and I think the paper undersells it relative to the efficiency story. The 1% accuracy improvement on DeiT-S (Table 2, if I'm reading it right) from higher-res input at matched cost is genuinely useful. I'd like to see this explored more deeply.

Figure 3 showing the attention visualizations is nice but a bit cherry-picked looking. Are these representative? I'd want to see failure cases — what happens when the class token attention is misleading or noisy, e.g., on images with distributed or ambiguous subjects? This feels important for understanding where the method breaks down.

One thing that genuinely bothers me: the method is trained end-to-end with the token reorganization in place, which means the model learns to "work with" fused tokens. But at inference with different keep-ratios than training, does performance degrade gracefully? The ablation in Table 4 touches on this but I'm not fully satisfied. I might be wrong but it seems like there could be a mismatch between training and deployment configurations.

Minor gripes: the writing in the intro is repetitive and could be tightened. Equation 2 is fine but the notation is a bit overloaded.

Overall, this is solid work with real practical value. The execution is competent. I lean toward acceptance but the authors need to address the throughput measurement transparency and better position against concurrent token merging methods. Not a groundbreaking paper, but a useful one.

This paper tackles an interesting and underexplored problem — continual learning for multivariate time series forecasting with explicit dependency modeling. The problem setup is well-motivated and I appreciate that the authors consider the realistic scenario where regime identities are unavailable at test time. That said, I have significant concerns about several aspects of the work.

The proposed SKI-CL framework combines three components: a graph-learning-based forecaster, a structural knowledge regularization term, and a representation-matching memory replay scheme. Each piece individually is not particularly novel — graph-based MTS forecasting (e.g., GWaveNet, MTGNN, etc.) and replay-based continual learning are both well-established. I think the contribution here is really in the combination and the specific problem framing, but the paper doesn't always make this distinction clearly. After reading Section 3 twice, I found the interaction between the structural knowledge regularization (Eq. 5-6 area) and the replay scheme somewhat underexplained. How do these two components interact during the update step? Is the structural regularization applied on replayed samples too?

The empirical evaluation is reasonably thorough, and I do appreciate the inclusion of synthetic benchmarks where ground-truth dependency structures are known. Table 2 shows consistent improvements. However, I'm skeptical about some of the baselines. The continual learning baselines (EWC, PackNet, etc.) are not really designed for time series forecasting — it feels like the comparison is a bit stacked in SKI-CL's favor. I would have liked to see stronger domain-specific baselines, or at least an ablation that isolates how much gain comes specifically from the continual learning machinery versus just having a better base forecasting model.

The ablation in Table 4 is helpful but incomplete. Removing the structural regularization ("w/o SR") hurts performance, sure, but I'd want to understand under what conditions the structural knowledge is actually informative. What happens when the structural prior is noisy or wrong? This seems like a critical failure mode and it's not addressed.

My experience with this kind of work is that the "regime" assumption is doing a lot of heavy lifting. In practice, how are regime boundaries detected? The paper seems to assume they're given during training — which is a strong assumption that deserves more scrutiny in the paper's framing.

Minor things: Figure 2 is quite dense and hard to parse. Some notation in Section 3 (around the memory buffer description) is inconsistently defined. The related work section is fine but rushes through the continual learning literature.

Overall, I think this is a reasonable paper with a genuine contribution to a relevant problem, but the experimental comparisons need strengthening and the structural knowledge robustness question feels like a gap that could undermine the whole approach. I'm leaning toward weak reject but could be convinced otherwise if the authors address the baseline fairness concern and the sensitivity to prior quality.

This paper tackles an important and timely problem — the English-centric bias of LLMs — and the general direction of using cross-lingual instruction tuning to "extrapolate" capabilities to other languages is reasonable. I've seen a lot of work in this space lately and this sits somewhere in the middle in terms of novelty.

My biggest concern is the baseline comparisons. The paper compares x-LLaMA primarily against Alpaca, which is a relatively weak baseline at this point. By the time this paper would appear in any venue, there are numerous stronger multilingual baselines (BLOOM, mT5-based instruction tuned models, Bactrian-X, etc.) that aren't seriously engaged with. The 27.83% improvement over Alpaca sounds impressive but I genuinely don't know what to make of it without harder comparisons. This feels like cherry-picking the weakest possible baseline to inflate the numbers.

The scaling laws analysis is interesting but I found myself confused after reading Section 4 twice. The formulation in equations 3-4 (or thereabouts) — I'm not entirely sure the authors have established a "scaling law" in any rigorous sense. They've fit a curve to a small number of data points and called it a scaling law. Real scaling law work (Hoffmann et al., Kaplan et al.) involves orders of magnitude variation and careful controls. This feels more like a data ablation with a trendline fitted to it. I think calling it a "scaling law" is an overstatement that borders on misleading.

The multilingual results in Table 3 are honestly a bit underwhelming. m-LLaMA achieving "comparable" performance to individual x-LLaMAs is fine, but the authors don't sufficiently discuss the tradeoffs — model size, training compute, the fact that you're essentially training N separate models for the cross-lingual case. The comparison isn't really apples-to-apples.

I did find the representation space analysis (Figure 5 or 6, the t-SNE plots) genuinely interesting — it's a nice sanity check that something real is happening in the middle layers. That said, t-SNE visualizations are notoriously easy to manipulate and I'd want to see more quantitative measures of alignment.

The paper is written clearly enough, though there are awkward phrasings throughout that suggest it wasn't proofread carefully. Minor thing, but it matters for readability.

In my experience, the "translation data + general task data" recipe for multilingual fine-tuning is fairly well-established at this point. The contribution feels incremental. The paper would be significantly stronger with: (1) better baselines, (2) more honest framing of the "scaling laws" claims, and (3) analysis of failure cases or languages where the approach doesn't work well. I'd lean toward rejection in current form but could be convinced otherwise if the authors addressed the baseline comparison issue seriously.

This paper tackles an interesting question about how CLIP processes text in images, specifically whether typographic attacks work through genuine semantic understanding or through texture/orthographic cues. The research question is timely given the proliferation of multimodal models and the practical security implications of typographic attacks.

That said, I have significant concerns about the paper that I think need to be addressed.

The core claim — that semantic understanding in CLIP primarily emerges from texture learning that aids orthographic recognition rather than true semantic comprehension — is interesting, but I found the argument somewhat circular. The paper uses Intrinsic Dimension as the primary analytical tool, but I'm not fully convinced that ID changes across layers actually tell us what the authors claim they tell us. After reading the methodology section twice, I still couldn't convince myself that a decrease in ID necessarily indicates "texture-dominated" representation vs. semantic representation. The connection between ID and the texture/semantic distinction needs much stronger theoretical grounding. I might be wrong about this, but it feels like the authors are interpreting ID changes in a way that conveniently supports their narrative.

The ToT dataset is probably the most concrete contribution here. I appreciate the effort to disentangle orthographic form from semantics — that's genuinely clever. But Table 2 (if I'm reading it correctly) shows relatively modest differences between conditions that the authors interpret as quite dramatic. The effect sizes matter here and I'd want to see statistical significance reported more carefully throughout.

The "compete in early layers" finding is interesting but not entirely surprising. This is consistent with what we know about feature hierarchy in vision transformers more broadly — I'm thinking of work like Raghu et al. on representation geometry in ViTs. The authors don't really engage with this literature deeply enough, which makes their contributions feel less precisely situated.

I'm also concerned about generalizability. The analysis focuses heavily on CLIP and the specific typographic attack setup. The abstract says "visual models" but really this is about one model family. The conclusions are stated with more confidence than the evidence warrants.

The writing is generally clear but there are places where the argument gets muddy — particularly around page 6-7 where the transition between the ID analysis and the semantic accuracy experiments felt abrupt to me.

To be blunt: I think there's a real paper here struggling to get out, but right now the theoretical framework feels underbaked and the empirical claims are overstated. The dataset contribution alone might not be sufficient for acceptance at this venue. I'd want to see either a stronger theoretical account of why ID maps onto the texture/semantic distinction, or significantly more experimental validation across different model architectures. Borderline reject, leaning toward rejection, but I'd genuinely read a revised version.

This paper proposes DPD-LoRA, which combines prompt learning with LoRA-style adaptation through dynamic prompt-driven low-rank updates. I've read through this twice and have significant concerns that I think the authors need to address before this could be accepted.

The core idea — using task-aware prompt tokens to dynamically modulate low-rank weight updates — is reasonable and not entirely novel (there's clear overlap with prior work like VPT, PromptKD, and various conditional LoRA variants). The paper doesn't do a great job distinguishing itself from, say, PromptKD or the conditional adapter literature. The related work section feels thin and somewhat cherry-picked.

My biggest concern is the empirical evaluation. The authors claim "significant" improvements across 11 benchmarks, but looking at Table 2 and Table 3, many of the gains are within 0.5-1% accuracy. Whether that's statistically significant is never addressed — no error bars, no significance tests, nothing. In my experience, this is a major red flag. The baselines also seem selectively chosen; I notice CoCoOp and ProDA are included but more recent strong baselines like PromptSRC or MaPLe variants are conspicuously absent. Why?

The decomposition of LoRA into multiple sub-matrices (Section 3.3 I think, around page 5) is presented as a contribution, but I'm not entirely sure this is doing what the authors claim. They say it doesn't add parameters, which is true, but the expressivity argument is hand-wavy. Is there any theoretical justification here beyond intuition? The claim that this "breaks down standard LoRA into multiple low-rank sub-matrices without adding parameters" needs more rigorous treatment.

The adaptive loss function is introduced somewhat abruptly and the self-regulated mechanism described around equation 8 is honestly confusing to parse. I found myself re-reading that section multiple times without feeling confident I understood the stabilization mechanism. This needs clearer exposition.

The soft-gated selection mechanism is interesting and probably the most novel piece here — I'd actually like to see this explored more deeply. But it gets relatively brief treatment compared to other components that feel more incremental.

Some smaller things: the abstract mentions "outliers" being a problem for low-rank approximation but this is never really substantiated or addressed empirically. Also there are grammatical issues throughout ("task-aware prompts in the DPD-LoRA dynamically influences" — subject-verb agreement) that suggest the writing needs another pass.

I'm leaning toward rejection primarily because the novelty is incremental, the empirical evaluation lacks rigor, and several key components lack sufficient justification. The ablation in Table 4 helps somewhat, but it doesn't address my concerns about the baseline comparisons. I'd encourage the authors to do a more thorough comparison with recent state-of-the-art, add statistical significance testing, and tighten up the theoretical motivation considerably.

This paper tackles an important theoretical question about the loss surface of deep CNNs, and I appreciate the attempt to extend existing theoretical results (which have largely focused on fully connected networks) to architectures that people actually use in practice. That said, I have significant concerns about the gap between what's claimed and what's actually proven.

The main result — that wide CNNs produce linearly independent features when the layer width exceeds the number of training samples — feels somewhat unsatisfying to me. The condition that the network be "wider than the training set" is a very strong assumption that, while technically satisfied by VGG on small benchmarks, doesn't really reflect how these networks are used in practice. When I train VGG on ImageNet with millions of samples, this condition obviously fails. The authors mention VGG specifically as a motivating example (which is fine) but I worry readers will overgeneralize the takeaway.

The technical machinery in Section 3 is dense and I found myself going back and forth between the lemmas and the main theorems several times. I'm not entirely sure the proof of Theorem 2 is complete as stated — specifically the step where the authors invoke the linear independence condition to guarantee that every critical point is a global minimum. I might be wrong here, but the argument seems to gloss over the case where the max-pooling layer creates non-differentiable points in the loss. The authors acknowledge non-differentiability exists but I don't think they handle it rigorously enough. This is actually a hard problem and in my experience papers that wave their hands at it tend to have subtle gaps.

The "almost every" qualifier in the abstract is doing a lot of heavy lifting. The measure-zero argument is standard in this literature (I'm thinking of the Kawaguchi 2016 and Choromanska et al. lineage of results) but needs to be stated much more carefully. What measure? On what space? This needs to be explicit.

I do think the expressivity results for shared-weight CNNs are genuinely novel — I haven't seen this handled cleanly elsewhere and that contribution has real value. The discussion connecting depth to representational power and width to landscape smoothness is intuitive and well-articulated in the introduction.

But honestly the empirical section is weak. Two small experiments don't really validate the theoretical claims in any meaningful way. I'd want to see something more systematic.

Minor things: Figure 2 is hard to read, the notation in equation (4) is introduced before it's defined, and there are several grammatical issues throughout suggesting this was written in a hurry.

Overall I think the core ideas are interesting enough to warrant further development, but the paper in its current form overstates its results and has technical gaps that need addressing before I'd be comfortable recommending acceptance. I lean toward reject with encouragement to resubmit.

This paper tackles a practically important problem — deciding when to retrain a deployed ML model — and I appreciate that the authors try to formalize it carefully. The framing in the introduction is reasonable, and the three challenges they identify (limited data, unknown shift nature, cost specification) do capture real pain points I've seen discussed in the MLOps literature. That said, I have significant concerns about the paper as it stands.

My biggest issue is with the novelty claim. The authors position this as a "comprehensive solution" that existing work fails to provide, but I found the actual technical contribution somewhat thin. The core idea — using uncertainty estimates to forecast model performance and compare against a cost threshold — is sensible but not exactly groundbreaking. After reading Section 3 twice, I'm still not entirely clear what makes this substantially different from, say, adaptive threshold methods in the drift detection literature, or even simple performance monitoring with confidence intervals. The connection to the cost trade-off is the most interesting part, but the treatment feels undercooked.

The experimental setup raises questions. Seven datasets for classification is fine, but I'd want to know: are these datasets actually representative of the kinds of distribution shifts practitioners face? The abstract mentions "bounded metric" — I assume this is something like accuracy or F1 — but the choice of bounding and why it matters for the forecasting step isn't well-motivated in the main text. Also, I couldn't find a clear description of the baselines until fairly late in the paper, and even then the comparison felt a bit stacked. Are the baselines tuned with similar care as the proposed method?

The robustness experiments (varying cost trade-off values and mis-specified costs) are genuinely valuable and I think this is the strongest part of the paper. More papers should do this. But the results in Table 2 show that performance degrades non-trivially when the cost is mis-specified by even moderate amounts — the authors acknowledge this but I think they undersell how problematic it is for practical deployment, where cost specification is often wildly off.

I'm also a bit bothered by the framing around offline RL. Dismissing offline RL methods in the abstract because of "unusual structure" of the data is a strong claim that deserves more rigorous justification. What specifically fails? A brief empirical demonstration would strengthen this.

Minor things: Figure 3 is hard to read at print size, the related work section is missing some obvious citations on concept drift (Gama et al. and the Losing et al. survey come to mind immediately), and the notation in Section 4 gets unwieldy.

Overall I think this is a borderline paper. The problem is real and the approach is reasonable, but the contribution needs sharper articulation and the experiments need more rigorous comparison. I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, but a strong revision could change my mind.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically important problem: how to differentiate through blackbox combinatorial solvers so they can be embedded in end-to-end neural network pipelines. The core idea — perturbing the cost vector and using finite differences on the solver's output to approximate a gradient — is clean and I appreciate that the authors are honest about what they're doing rather than claiming some elegant closed-form solution.

That said, I have some real concerns here. The theoretical contribution feels somewhat thin to me. Proposition 1 and the surrounding analysis in Section 3 essentially formalize something that is, at its core, a finite difference approximation with a particular interpolation scheme. After reading this twice I wasn't fully convinced this rises to the level of a novel theoretical contribution versus a careful engineering observation. I might be wrong — there is some nuance in how they handle the piecewise-linear structure of the combinatorial solver's output — but the authors should work harder to distinguish their theoretical results from what already exists in the perturbation/smoothing literature (e.g., work by Hazan et al. on perturbation-based methods).

The experiments are where this paper is most convincing. The TSP and shortest path results are genuinely interesting, and Figure 3 in particular does a good job showing that the approach learns meaningful cost functions from raw input. I do wish there was more comparison to alternatives though. The SPO+ paper (Elmachtoub & Grigas) is relevant and the comparison to it feels a bit brief. Also, the warcraft shortest path experiment is nice but I'd want to see error bars — are these results stable across seeds?

One thing that bothers me: the choice of lambda (the perturbation parameter) seems quite important and the sensitivity analysis in the appendix is not thorough enough. In my experience with these kinds of methods, hyperparameter sensitivity can completely change the practical conclusions, and I don't think the paper adequately addresses this.

The blackbox framing is both the strength and weakness of the paper. It's practically useful — you can plug in Gurobi, great. But it also means the method can't exploit any structure of the specific combinatorial problem, which feels like a missed opportunity. The authors acknowledge this but I think the tradeoff deserves more honest discussion.

Writing is generally clear, though Section 2 is dense and could use another pass. The related work section is adequate but misses some relevant citations on differentiable algorithms more broadly.

Overall I think this is a borderline paper. The practical contribution is real and the experiments are decent. The theoretical framing is a bit oversold. I'm leaning weak accept but would want the authors to address the comparison to SPO+, provide more stability analysis, and tone down some of the theoretical claims in the introduction. The idea is good enough to be in the conference but needs more honest scoping.

This paper tackles an interesting and underexplored problem — how do you compare the *information content* of probabilistic representation spaces, like those produced by VAEs or InfoGANs? The framing through information theory and communication channels is appealing, and I appreciate the connection back to classical clustering similarity measures as a grounding point.

That said, I found the paper quite hard to follow in places. The transition from Section 3 to Section 4, where the authors introduce the fingerprinting approach, felt rushed to me. After reading it twice I'm still not entirely clear on the assumptions being made about the "handful of bits" regime — is this a fundamental limitation of the method or just a practical approximation? The authors gesture at this but don't fully address it. I think the paper would benefit significantly from a clearer discussion of when the fingerprinting estimator breaks down.

The experiments are where things get interesting but also where I have the most concerns. The VAE ensemble experiments are suggestive, but the datasets used (I'm looking at the results around Table 2 and Figure 4) feel somewhat cherry-picked. The "striking difference in homogeneity depending on the dataset" claim — which is one of the headline results — rests on comparisons across only a few datasets. In my experience, this kind of variance in training dynamics can be highly sensitive to hyperparameters, and I didn't see a convincing ablation here.

The boosting application in the final section is clever and I genuinely liked it conceptually. But the empirical gains shown feel modest and I'm not sure the method is actually doing what the authors claim versus just benefiting from ensemble averaging in a more standard sense. More rigorous baselines are needed.

I'm also somewhat skeptical about the disentanglement claims. The connection to "channel centrality" as a signature of unsupervised disentanglement is interesting but the theoretical justification is thin. The authors cite some prior work on model centrality but the leap from that literature to the probabilistic channel setting isn't fully justified. I might be wrong here, but this feels like it needs more theoretical grounding.

On the writing: some of the notation in Section 2 is dense and a few equations (particularly around eq. 4-5) could use more intuitive explanation before being formally stated. Not a dealbreaker but it slows reading considerably.

Overall, I think this paper has a genuinely interesting core idea and the channel-theoretic framing is novel enough to be worth publishing somewhere. But in its current form the empirical validation feels incomplete and some of the stronger claims outrun the evidence. I'm on the fence — leaning weak reject, but I'd be willing to reconsider if the authors can address the experimental concerns and clarify the fingerprinting assumptions more carefully.

This paper tackles cross-domain offline RL, specifically the setting where you have limited target domain data and want to leverage a source domain dataset with potentially different dynamics. The core idea is to use optimal transport to align transitions across domains and then selectively filter source domain data before combining with target data for offline RL training. I've seen a fair amount of work in this space (DARA, CCFDM, etc.) and this is a reasonable contribution, though I have some significant concerns.

The theoretical motivation is the strongest part of the paper. The performance bound derived in Section 3 is a nice way to motivate the OT-based filtering approach, and I appreciate that the authors tried to ground the method theoretically rather than just proposing a heuristic. That said, I found the bound somewhat loose and the connection between Theorem 1 and the actual algorithm choices feels hand-wavy at times. Specifically, the jump from the bound to "therefore we use Wasserstein distance as our filtering criterion" on page 5 deserves more careful justification. I might be wrong here, but I don't think the bound directly implies that particular design choice over alternatives.

The experimental results are mixed in a way that concerns me. OTDF does well on several MuJoCo locomotion tasks but the margins vary wildly — sometimes it's clearly better, sometimes it's comparable, and Table 2 shows a few cases where it underperforms. The authors explain some of these away but I didn't find the explanations fully convincing. Also, I really want to see ablations on the dataset regularization term more carefully. Figure 3 touches on this but it's not thorough enough.

My biggest concern is the "without training any neural networks" claim in the abstract. While technically true for the filtering step, the downstream offline RL algorithm obviously trains neural networks, and I think this framing is a bit misleading. It's presented as a major advantage but in practice the computational bottleneck of OT on large datasets could be substantial — I didn't see a runtime comparison anywhere, which feels like an important omission.

The limited target data regime is the most interesting and practically relevant part of the story. The experiments with varying amounts of target data (Section 4.3 I believe) are good and show the method holds up better than baselines at low data regimes. This is the paper's clearest contribution and I wish the authors had emphasized this more throughout.

Writing is generally clear but there are some awkward passages, particularly in the related work section. The notation in Section 3 gets a bit cluttered.

Overall: interesting work with a solid theoretical framing, but the experimental validation needs strengthening and some claims feel overstated. I lean toward borderline rejection currently, but a revision addressing the runtime analysis and the ablations more thoroughly could change my mind. The core idea has merit.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — how to select high-quality subsets of instruction tuning data rather than training on everything indiscriminately. The core idea of using noise injection as a proxy for data quality is actually somewhat novel, and I appreciate that the authors are thinking beyond simple heuristics like response length or perplexity scores.

That said, I have fairly significant concerns about the paper as presented.

The noise injection methodology (Section 3, around page 4-5) is described at a level of abstraction that makes it genuinely hard to reproduce. What kind of noise? Gaussian perturbations to embeddings? Token-level corruptions? The authors gesture at this but I had to read it twice and still felt uncertain about the exact procedure. This is the central technical contribution — it needs to be airtight.

The experimental setup also raises questions. The comparison against "full dataset" training is fine, but the baselines feel cherry-picked. LIMA, Alpagasus, and IFD are mentioned but the comparison in Table 2 seems to favor the proposed method in ways that could easily be explained by hyperparameter tuning differences rather than genuine methodological superiority. I'm not saying the authors did anything wrong, but the ablations don't fully convince me. Also — 12% of the dataset outperforming the full set is a strong claim that deserves much more careful analysis. Is this just overfitting on the full set? Is the selected 12% simply avoiding low-quality or duplicated samples that hurt training? These aren't the same thing as your noise injection being the key ingredient.

The diversity component (inter-class and intra-class) feels bolted on rather than organically integrated with the quality scoring. I think the authors know this too, because the motivation for combining these two objectives in the way they do (Section 3.3 or thereabouts) is hand-wavy. In my experience, the tension between quality and diversity in data selection is genuinely hard and deserves more than a paragraph.

Figure 3 is helpful for intuition but the t-SNE visualizations are notoriously hard to interpret quantitatively — I wouldn't lean on them too heavily as evidence.

Minor things: the writing is serviceable but there are grammatical issues throughout that a native speaker should clean up before final submission. The related work section is decent but misses some recent work on data pruning for LLMs (e.g., work from the data-centric AI community).

I might be wrong about the noise injection being underspecified — maybe I missed something in the appendix — but on balance I think this paper needs substantial revision. The core idea has merit and I'd encourage the authors to resubmit with clearer methodology, stronger baselines, and a more honest discussion of what's actually doing the work here. Currently borderline reject leaning toward reject, but I could be persuaded otherwise.

This paper presents a differentiable tactile simulation system that combines FEM-based soft body modeling with a neural rendering module for optical response prediction. The overall system is ambitious and I can see genuine engineering effort here. That said, I have significant concerns about the paper as currently written.

Let me start with what I think is the most fundamental issue: the evaluation is weak. The paper demonstrates several applications — system identification, grasping, cable manipulation — but in each case the quantitative comparisons are either absent or cherry-picked. Table 2 shows some sim-to-real transfer results but after reading this twice I'm still not convinced the baselines are fair. What are the competing methods actually doing differently? The paper gestures at "prior tactile simulators" throughout but the actual head-to-head comparisons feel thin. I found myself wanting to see, for instance, how much the differentiable gradient actually helps vs. just running a zeroth-order optimizer on a non-differentiable baseline.

The FEM formulation itself (Section 3.1, around equations 4-7) is fairly standard — I don't think the authors are claiming novelty here exactly, but the paper's framing sometimes implies more novelty than is warranted. The penalty-based contact model is also quite conventional. I might be wrong but it feels like the actual technical contribution is more in the *integration* of these components into a unified differentiable pipeline plus the neural optical module, and the paper should be clearer about this framing rather than presenting each component as if it were independently novel.

The optical inference module (Section 3.4) is interesting but underexplored. The pixel-based neural module feels somewhat bolted on — it's not differentiable through in the downstream experiments if I'm reading correctly? This needs clarification. If gradients don't flow through it, that significantly limits the claimed differentiable nature of the full system.

Figure 5 is confusing. The color coding for stress visualization is hard to parse and the caption doesn't help enough. Minor thing but it reflects a general presentation issue throughout.

Runtime performance is mentioned briefly but I want actual numbers. FEM simulation is notoriously slow — what's the actual throughput for policy learning? How does this compare to, say, MuJoCo-based tactile approximations in wall-clock time?

I'm also somewhat skeptical of the real robot experiments. The success rates in Table 3 look reasonable but n=20 trials is not enough to draw strong conclusions, especially without confidence intervals.

In my experience reviewing simulation papers, the bar should be: does this enable something that wasn't possible before, demonstrated convincingly? Right now the paper shows it *can* do various things but doesn't fully demonstrate it does them *better* than alternatives. The sim-to-real story is the most compelling part and I'd encourage the authors to double down on that.

Leaning toward reject but I'd reconsider with stronger experiments and clearer positioning of contributions.

This paper tackles individual privacy accounting under Gaussian DP, extending the framework of Feldman and Zrnic (2021) to what the authors call "optimal" accountants. The core idea — that individual participants may accumulate much less privacy loss than worst-case bounds suggest — is genuinely useful and the GDP framing is a natural fit given that GDP gives tight guarantees for the Gaussian mechanism specifically.

Let me be honest about where I stand: I think the technical contributions are real but the paper oversells some of them. The supermartingale construction for the hockey-stick divergence (Section 4) is the most interesting piece, and I found it reasonably convincing after going through it twice. However, the connection to Feldman and Zrnic via the Blackwell theorem in Section 5 feels somewhat indirect — you're essentially saying "use their results, but plug in GDP-based quantities." I'm not entirely sure this constitutes a major technical advance over just applying their framework, and the paper doesn't do enough to distinguish what's genuinely new here versus what's bookkeeping.

The FFT speedup via the Plancherel theorem is nice but feels like it belongs in an appendix or a short note rather than being presented as a main contribution. It's a fairly standard trick once you've decided to work with privacy loss distributions in the frequency domain.

On the experiments/examples: Figure 2 and the numerical results in Section 6 are helpful for building intuition, but I'd want to see a more realistic workload. The examples feel somewhat constructed to show favorable individual losses. In practice, how often do real participants actually end up with substantially tighter bounds? The paper would benefit from some empirical grounding with, say, DP-SGD on an actual dataset where participation patterns vary.

A few specific issues: Equation (7) — I'd appreciate more discussion of when the supermartingale condition is tight versus loose. The paper states it but doesn't give much intuition for practitioners. Also, around page 8, the transition between the GDP composition theorem and the individual accounting setup feels abrupt. I found this confusing and had to reread the preceding section.

The related work treatment of PRV/PLD accountants (Koskela et al., Gopi et al.) is adequate but I think the paper should be clearer about exactly where GDP individual accounting is strictly better than, say, doing individual accounting with PRV accountants. The abstract claims "optimal bounds" somewhat aggressively.

Writing quality is generally fine, though there are spots where the formalism gets heavy and a motivating example earlier would help. The paper is clearly written by people who know this area well.

My overall take: solid, incremental contribution to a niche but important problem. The GDP supermartingale result is worth publishing. I'd push back on the framing of some secondary contributions as major results. Leaning toward acceptance but the authors should significantly revise the presentation and be more honest about the limitations of the Blackwell-based approach.

This paper tackles an interesting question — does monosemanticity, beyond its interpretability benefits, actually help with robustness? I appreciate the ambition here, and the hypothesis connecting interpretability to robustness is genuinely worth exploring. That said, I have significant concerns about the execution that I think need to be addressed before this work is ready for publication.

My biggest issue is with the experimental setup. The paper evaluates across noise robustness, few-shot learning, and OOD generalization, which sounds comprehensive, but I found myself repeatedly asking: is the monosemanticity actually *causing* the gains, or is it a confound? For instance, the methods used to encourage monosemanticity (e.g., sparse autoencoders, dictionary learning variants) often introduce regularization that could independently explain robustness improvements. The ablations in Section 4 don't fully disentangle this. I went back and read this section twice and I'm still not convinced.

The theoretical analysis (Section 5, Theorem 1) is a nice gesture but honestly feels underbaked. The assumptions required to derive the margin-separation result are quite strong, and I think the theorem as stated applies to a fairly narrow setting. I might be wrong about the generality here, but the proof in the appendix doesn't fully address the gap between the idealized setup and the real experiments.

On the empirical side — the gains on some benchmarks are compelling (Table 2, the label noise results are actually pretty striking), but on others they're marginal. The few-shot results in Table 3 show inconsistent improvements across shots, and I don't see a satisfying explanation for why monosemanticity helps at 1-shot but not consistently at 5-shot. This inconsistency is kind of swept under the rug.

I also think the paper needs to be more careful about what "monosemanticity" even means in this context. The definition used operationally (low activation overlap across concepts) doesn't quite match the mechanistic interpretability literature's usage (Elhage et al., Anthropic's work). This conflation bothered me throughout.

One genuinely strong aspect: the connection to OOD generalization is the most novel and interesting contribution, and I wish the paper leaned into this more rather than trying to cover everything at once.

Minor things: Figure 3 is hard to read at print size, and the related work section is missing several relevant citations on disentangled representations (Bengio et al., early VAE disentanglement work) which actually predate the monosemanticity framing and are clearly relevant.

Overall, I think this is a paper with a good idea that needs sharper focus and more rigorous causal attribution. The current version feels like it's trying to do too much. I'm leaning toward rejection but would genuinely reconsider with a major revision that tightens the causal story and either strengthens or narrows the theoretical claims.

This paper proposes using the inverse of an attention mask as a noise reference signal for denoising rPPG (remote photoplethysmography) measurements. The core idea is intuitive — if attention highlights skin regions containing the physiological signal, then the "distractor" regions should capture mostly noise/motion artifacts, which can then be subtracted out. I've seen related ideas in the signal processing literature (e.g., adaptive noise cancellation goes back decades), and the novelty here is really in how this is instantiated within a learned attention framework for video-based vitals.

Let me start with what I think works. The idea is clean and the paper is generally well-written. The SNR improvements reported in Table 2 are substantial (up to 5.8 dB), and the generalization to NIR without retraining (Section 4.4) is genuinely impressive and probably the most compelling result in the paper. Figure 3 does a nice job visualizing what the attention masks are actually learning.

That said, I have several concerns. The biggest one is about the independence assumption underlying the whole approach. The method assumes that the noise statistics in the inverse attention regions are representative of the noise corrupting the signal regions. But is this actually true? For example, if a person is talking or chewing, the mouth region will be in the "signal" mask (it's skin), but the motion there is very different from, say, the background. The paper doesn't really address this formally or empirically. I'd want to see some ablation or analysis on failure cases where this assumption breaks down.

Second, the comparison to baselines in Table 1 feels a bit selective. I'm not entirely sure all the compared methods are using identical train/test splits on PURE and UBFC. This has been a recurring problem in the rPPG literature — I've seen papers where small differences in data partitioning make huge differences in reported numbers. The authors should be more explicit about this.

The architecture description in Section 3.2 is also somewhat vague. After reading it twice I'm still not 100% clear on exactly how the inverse mask signal is combined with the forward pass estimate. Is this a simple subtraction in the temporal domain? A learned combination? Equation 4 helps but I found the surrounding prose confusing.

Minor things: the related work on adaptive noise cancellation (Widrow et al.) should probably be cited — this feels like an oversight. Also Figure 5 is hard to read in grayscale print.

Overall, I think this is a reasonable contribution with a nice core idea and strong experimental results. My main hesitation is that the theoretical justification feels thin and some experimental details need clarification. I'd lean toward accepting a revised version, but the authors really need to address the noise independence assumption more rigorously. It's not a fatal flaw but it's the load-bearing wall of the whole approach.

This paper tackles continual category discovery (CCD), which is indeed an interesting and underexplored problem sitting at the intersection of continual learning and novel category discovery. I appreciate that the authors are trying to unify these two challenges, and the overall motivation is sound.

That said, I have some significant concerns after reading through this twice.

The core technical contribution — the Gaussian Mixture Prompt Module (GMP) — feels somewhat incremental to me. Using GMMs for prompt generation is a reasonable idea, but the paper doesn't sufficiently justify *why* a Gaussian mixture is the right inductive bias here compared to, say, a simpler prototype-based approach or even just learned prompt vectors. The ablation in Table 3 helps a bit, but I found myself wanting a more principled argument. Why should category distributions in the feature space be well-approximated by Gaussians, especially for fine-grained datasets? This assumption feels underexamined.

The category estimation component is interesting and I think actually undersold. In my experience, the "how many categories?" question is one of the most practically important issues in open-world learning, and I'd have liked to see this get more thorough experimental treatment. The evaluation in Table 4 feels thin — what happens when the model is wrong by a large margin? How does estimation error propagate through subsequent time steps?

On the experiments: the benchmark comparison is appreciated, and I can see the authors put real effort into extending evaluation metrics (the metric extension to CCD in Section 4.1 is reasonable). But I'm a bit skeptical of the baselines. Some of the compared methods weren't designed for this exact problem setup, and adapting them may not be entirely fair. The paper should be more careful here. Also, performance gaps on some datasets (e.g., CUB in Table 2) are less convincing than on others — this deserves discussion rather than glossing over.

The writing is generally clear but there are places — particularly around equations 3-5 — where the notation gets dense and I found myself confused about what's being conditioned on what. A cleaner exposition would help.

One thing that genuinely bothers me: the paper claims the approach avoids catastrophic forgetting, but the analysis of *why* the GMP pool prevents forgetting is mostly empirical. I think there's an opportunity to provide at least an intuitive theoretical argument, or at minimum a more thorough analysis of the forgetting dynamics across time steps rather than just final numbers.

I'm somewhat on the fence about this paper. The problem is real and important, the approach is reasonable, and results are mostly positive. But the technical novelty feels modest, and some claims outpace the evidence. I'd lean toward weak reject with encouragement to revise — particularly to strengthen the theoretical motivation for the GMM choice and to expand the category estimation analysis. Not a bad paper, just not quite there yet.

This paper proposes the "manifold memorization hypothesis" (MMH) as a geometric framework for understanding memorization in generative models, building on the manifold hypothesis to categorize memorization based on dimensionality relationships between the true data manifold and the learned model manifold.
