I found the core idea genuinely interesting and I think there's a real contribution here, but I have significant concerns about the execution that I need to air.

The conceptual framing is the strongest part of the paper. The distinction between "overfitting-driven" and "distribution-driven" memorization is useful and I think the community will find this vocabulary helpful. After reading this twice, I do believe this framework provides some genuine clarifying power over prior work — the authors do a reasonable job in Section 3 of reinterpreting existing empirical observations through the MMH lens.

That said, I'm skeptical of several things. The dimensionality estimation approach underlying the empirical validation is doing a LOT of heavy lifting here, and I'm not sure the authors are sufficiently careful about its limitations. Intrinsic dimensionality estimation in high-dimensional spaces is notoriously unreliable — there's a long history of this problem in the manifold learning literature and the paper treats these estimates somewhat uncritically. The experiments on synthetic data (Section 4, Figure 3) are fine but almost too easy — of course the framework works when you construct data that perfectly satisfies the manifold hypothesis. The real test is the image experiments, and honestly I found those results somewhat underwhelming given the claims. The Stable Diffusion experiments feel a bit bolted on, like the authors wanted to put "Stable Diffusion" in the abstract for impact.

The memorization detection and prevention tools developed in Section 5 are potentially the most practically valuable contribution, but they're underdeveloped. How do they compare to existing detection methods? Table 2 shows some numbers but I'm not entirely sure what baseline I should be comparing against. This needs more careful experimental design.

I might be wrong about this, but I think the theoretical formalization (Definition 2, equations 3-5) is doing less work than the authors believe. The definitions feel somewhat circular in places — you're defining memorization in terms of dimensionality, then validating by checking dimensionality. The framework would be more convincing with a result showing it predicts something non-obvious that is then empirically verified.

Minor things: Figure 4 is quite hard to read in grayscale. The related work section is fine but misses some relevant work on differential privacy and memorization (e.g., the Feldman long-tail memorization paper deserves more engagement given its direct relevance).

My overall take: interesting idea, real conceptual contribution, but the empirical validation needs strengthening and the practical tools need more rigorous comparison to baselines. I'm borderline on this one. I'd lean toward weak reject but I could be convinced otherwise with major revisions.

This paper tackles an interesting angle on data efficiency — specifically the role of targets (not just samples) in training efficiency. The core observation that soft targets on 10% of data can match or outperform one-hot targets on the full dataset is genuinely interesting and I think underappreciated in the community. That said, I have significant concerns about the paper as currently written.

The framing feels scattered. After reading this twice, I'm still not entirely sure what the central thesis is beyond "targets matter too." The paper attempts to do several things simultaneously: introduce a taxonomy of sample-to-target mappings, propose a unified loss framework, and provide empirical insights — but none of these feel fully developed. The unified loss framework (I think around Section 4?) reads more like a notational exercise than a genuine theoretical contribution. I might be wrong, but I don't see what the framework enables that wasn't possible before.

The six "key insights" are presented somewhat breathlessly, and several feel like restatements of known results from knowledge distillation and label smoothing literature. The connection to Hinton et al.'s distillation work and Müller et al.'s label smoothing analysis feels underexplored. These are foundational papers that directly address soft targets — the authors cite them but don't seriously engage with how their findings differ or extend those results.

On the experimental side: what architectures and datasets are used? I found the experimental setup descriptions vague in places. The claim about 10% subset with soft targets outperforming full dataset training is the headline result, but I want to see this validated more rigorously across settings. Is this specific to certain architectures, dataset sizes, or label noise levels? The paper doesn't give me enough confidence this generalizes.

I also found the "sustainability" framing in the abstract a bit of a stretch. Invoking scaling laws and sustainability challenges is trendy but the paper doesn't actually engage with compute costs in any rigorous way — there's no FLOPs analysis or wall-clock comparison that I could find.

The writing needs work. Several sentences in the introduction are genuinely hard to parse on first read. The categorization of "sample-to-target mapping strategies" uses terminology that isn't standard and I kept having to mentally re-map it to concepts I know.

Look, there's a real paper buried here. The empirical observation is compelling and the framing around targets being neglected relative to samples is a valid point. But right now it reads like a workshop paper that's been stretched. The taxonomy needs sharpening, the theoretical contribution needs justification, and the experiments need to be more thorough and clearly described. I'd encourage the authors to focus the story considerably and pick one strong thread to pull on.

I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, but I think this work has potential if substantially revised.

This paper proposes GANF, a method combining normalizing flows with Bayesian network structure learning for anomaly detection in multivariate time series. The core idea is to factorize the joint density using a DAG over the constituent series and learn this structure jointly with the flow parameters. I've read through the paper carefully and have mixed feelings about it.

The idea itself is reasonable and I think the motivation is sound — leveraging causal/dependency structure to improve density estimation is intuitive, and normalizing flows are indeed well-suited for this type of anomaly detection. The connection between Bayesian network factorization and the flow model (Equations 3-5) is the clearest part of the paper and I appreciated how the authors formalize this.

That said, I have several concerns. The experimental comparison feels thin to me. Table 1 shows results on SWaT and WADI, but the baselines are relatively weak — notably absent are more recent methods like MTAD-GAT, TranAD, or even simpler but strong baselines like OCSVM with hand-crafted features. I'm not convinced the gains shown are as significant as claimed without these comparisons. The F1 scores reported also depend heavily on threshold selection, and I found the discussion of how thresholds are set (Section 5.2 I think?) vague. This is a known issue in the anomaly detection literature and deserves more careful treatment.

The DAG learning component concerns me technically. The authors use a NOTEARS-style continuous relaxation for DAG learning, but the interaction between this optimization and the flow training seems underexplored. How sensitive is the final performance to the learned graph structure? Is the improvement coming from the graph or from the flow architecture itself? An ablation where the graph is replaced by a random DAG or a complete graph would be very informative and is conspicuously absent. I might be wrong but I suspect much of the benefit comes from the flow component alone.

Figure 3 is interesting but I found it hard to interpret — what exactly are we supposed to take away from the visualization of the learned DAG on the water treatment dataset? Do the learned edges correspond to known physical relationships? The authors gesture at this but don't really commit to an analysis.

After reading this twice, I'm also not sure the "distribution drift" experiment in Section 5.4 adds much. It feels tacked on and the connection to anomaly detection is loose.

The writing is generally clear but there are spots where it gets sloppy. The related work section covers normalizing flows and graph neural networks but the treatment of prior work specifically on multivariate time series anomaly detection is shallow — there's a lot of relevant work being glossed over.

Overall, I think there's a publishable idea here but the paper needs stronger baselines, an ablation on the graph structure, and more honest engagement with the threshold selection problem. As it stands it's borderline for me, leaning toward weak reject, but I could be persuaded otherwise.

This paper tackles N:M structured sparsity training, specifically arguing that existing recipes break down at high sparsity regimes (beyond 50%) due to gradient noise issues. The proposed solution — two training recipes called MdGf and SdGf — uses decay mechanisms to control gradient flow during sparse training. The core intuition is reasonable and I found myself nodding along to the problem motivation in Section 3.

That said, I have several concerns that give me pause.

The central claim — that "elevated levels of induced noise in gradient magnitudes" is THE significant factor causing quality degradation at high sparsity — feels undersubstantiated to me. The analysis in Section 3 shows correlation but I'm not convinced the paper rules out alternative explanations. What about weight magnitude distribution collapse? Dead neuron phenomena? I've seen papers (e.g., work from the Hoefler group on SR-STE and related methods) point to multiple interacting failure modes, and picking one and running with it feels a bit hasty here. I might be wrong but the paper reads like the conclusion preceded the analysis rather than following from it.

The experimental evaluation is my bigger concern. Results on "attention-based models" for vision and language tasks show improvements of up to ~2% accuracy, which sounds nice, but after reading Table 2 and Table 3 twice I'm struggling to understand the baseline selection. Why are some comparisons missing for certain configurations? The footnotes don't adequately explain this. Also, the "up to" framing in the abstract is doing a lot of heavy lifting — the median improvements look considerably more modest.

I also found the description of SdGf somewhat confusing. The transition between Equation 4 and the actual implementation described in Section 4.2 left me uncertain about exactly what's being decayed and on what schedule. A cleaner algorithm box would help enormously here.

On the positive side — I genuinely think the problem is important and underexplored. High-sparsity training is practically relevant as hardware like Ampere/Hopper becomes more widespread, and the observation that existing recipes weren't designed with this regime in mind is a legitimate contribution. Some of the ablation experiments (Figure 4 I think?) are informative.

But honestly, the writing needs work. There are grammatical issues throughout and some sentences in the introduction are just confusing on first read. The related work section is thin on recent gradient-flow literature.

My overall feeling is that this is a paper with a reasonable idea that hasn't been developed rigorously enough yet. The empirical gains are real but modest, the theoretical justification is shaky, and the experimental protocol has enough gaps that I can't fully trust the numbers. I'd encourage the authors to substantially strengthen the analysis section and be more careful about their baseline comparisons. Not ready for acceptance in this form.

This paper proposes a plug-and-play method for pixel-based RL that combines positive-unlabeled learning with contrastive objectives to address what the authors call a "paradox" in visual representation learning for RL. I've spent a fair amount of time with this paper and I have mixed feelings about it.

Let me start with what genuinely interested me: the core motivation around the tension between exploring visual semantics in early vs. late training phases is real and underappreciated. I've seen similar observations scattered across papers like CURL, DrQ, and SPR, but I don't think anyone has framed it quite this way. The PU learning angle is somewhat novel here.

That said, I have several significant concerns. The writing is honestly quite poor throughout — phrases like "profoundly elaborate" and "sufficiently elucidate the proposed method's innate superiority" in the abstract set a bad tone. This reads like it was written with a thesaurus open rather than with clarity in mind. After reading the introduction twice I still wasn't entirely sure what the "paradox" concretely is — the formal definition doesn't appear until much later and the intuitive explanation in section 1 is muddled.

The theoretical section (Section 4, around the mutual information lower bound argument) — I'm not entirely convinced this is tight or meaningful. The bound in Equation 7 seems to follow fairly standard MINE-type arguments, and calling it "tighter" relative to baselines requires a much more careful comparison than what's presented. The assumptions made to derive this are buried and I worry they're doing a lot of work.

On the experimental side: the DMControl benchmark results in Table 1 are somewhat compelling, especially on harder tasks like walker-run and hopper-hop at 500k steps. But I'm bothered by the absence of Dreamer-v3 and TD-MPC2 as baselines — these are the current state of the art and not including them makes the comparison feel dated. The ablation in Table 3 is helpful but doesn't fully isolate the PU component from the contrastive component. I want to see those separated more cleanly.

Also — and this might be a small thing but it bugged me — Figure 3 is referenced before Figure 2 in the text flow, which is a basic organizational error that suggests the paper was assembled hastily.

The "over-redundancy" diagnosis is interesting but I'm not fully persuaded the proposed method actually fixes it versus just performing better for other reasons. The redundancy measurement in Section 3.2 feels somewhat post-hoc and cherry-picked.

In my experience, papers that propose "plug-and-play" modules for RL often struggle to show consistent gains across the board, and this paper is no exception — there are several tasks where improvements are marginal or within noise.

I lean toward rejection in current form. The core idea has merit and I'd encourage resubmission with significantly cleaner writing, stronger baselines, and a more rigorous theoretical treatment.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely problem — the idea that safety judgments for MLLMs shouldn't be context-free, but should depend on the visual situation the user is in. I appreciate the motivation, and the benchmark construction seems like a reasonable first attempt. That said, I have some fairly significant concerns that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

The core concept is sound. A query like "how do I use this tool?" means something very different depending on whether the accompanying image shows a kitchen or a crime scene. The authors deserve credit for formalizing this intuition. But honestly, after reading this twice, I'm not convinced the formalization is as tight as it needs to be. The definition of "situational safety" in Section 3 feels somewhat circular — safe contexts vs. unsafe contexts are defined in ways that seem to presuppose the very judgment the model is supposed to make. I found this confusing and think the authors need to be more precise here.

My bigger concern is with the benchmark itself. 1,960 query-image pairs sounds reasonable, but the 50/50 split between safe and unsafe contexts feels artificially balanced in a way that doesn't reflect real deployment conditions. More importantly, I want to know more about inter-annotator agreement. Table 1 gives some statistics but I didn't see IAA scores — what's the Kappa? This matters a lot because "situational safety" is inherently subjective, and I worry the dataset encodes a particular (possibly narrow) cultural perspective on what constitutes unsafe situations without sufficient scrutiny.

The multi-agent pipeline results in Section 5 show "consistent improvement" but the gains in Table 3 are... modest? I'd hesitate to call improvements of 2-4 points on some metrics a strong validation of the approach. Also, the failure analysis feels thin. Figure 4 shows some qualitative examples which are cherry-picked in the usual way — I'd really like to see a more systematic breakdown of where and why models fail.

I think the paper also undersells its own contribution by not engaging seriously enough with prior work on contextual safety, jailbreaking under visual perturbations (e.g., the visual adversarial examples literature), and situation-aware AI more broadly. The related work section reads like it was written quickly.

One more thing — the claim in the abstract that this is "the first evaluation and analysis" of multimodal situational safety is a strong claim. I'm not entirely sure it's wrong, but the authors need to be more careful about distinguishing their work from things like MM-SafetyBench and related efforts, which do touch on context-dependent safety to some degree.

Overall: interesting problem, execution needs work. I'm leaning toward rejection but would genuinely reconsider with a stronger benchmark validation, clearer theoretical framing, and more rigorous empirical analysis. Not a bad paper, just not ready yet.

This paper proposes AASeg, a real-time semantic segmentation network combining spatial attention, channel attention, and multi-scale context modules. I'll be direct: after reading this twice, I have significant concerns about the contribution and presentation.

The claimed 74.4% mIoU at 202.7 FPS on Cityscapes sounds reasonable at first glance, but Table (wherever it appears in the paper) needs more careful contextualization. BiSeNet, STDC, DDRNet and several other real-time methods have been pushing well past 75-77% mIoU in recent years while maintaining competitive speed. The authors seem to be comparing against a somewhat cherry-picked or outdated set of baselines. I'm not convinced this is truly state-of-the-art anymore, and the paper doesn't really grapple with that honestly.

The individual components — spatial attention, channel attention, multi-scale context — are frankly not novel in isolation. CBAM, DANet, PSPNet, and DeepLab variants have explored all of these ideas extensively. What I needed to see, and largely didn't, is a compelling argument for WHY this specific combination and architecture design produces the observed efficiency-accuracy tradeoff. The ablation study exists, which I appreciate, but it reads more like a checkbox exercise than a genuine investigation. What's the computational cost of each module individually? Where are the FLOPs breakdowns?

I found the description of the MSC module genuinely confusing on first read. The text around that section isn't clear about how the multi-scale features are aggregated before concatenation. Are these parallel branches? Sequential? The figure helps somewhat but I had to go back and forth several times. This should be tightened considerably.

ADE20K results are included which is good — too many real-time segmentation papers only report Cityscapes. However, the CamVid numbers feel like padding. I'm not sure CamVid tells us much at this point.

My bigger concern is conceptual. The paper doesn't have a clear thesis beyond "we combined these modules and it works." In my experience, the papers that get accepted at top venues articulate a specific problem or failure mode in existing approaches, then show how their design directly addresses it. This paper skips that step almost entirely. Why does spatial+channel attention specifically help real-time segmentation? What's the bottleneck being addressed?

Writing quality is adequate but there are several grammatical issues throughout, and some sentences in the introduction are oddly vague ("feature maps are concatenated individually" — what does individually mean here exactly?).

I might be wrong about the novelty ceiling here — maybe the specific architectural choices are more clever than they appear — but the paper as written doesn't convince me. This feels like a competent engineering exercise that would fit better in a workshop or a more applied venue than a top ML conference. I'd recommend rejection in current form.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely question — whether pre-trained language models actually learn to abstract in a meaningful sense — and I appreciate the effort put into constructing a controlled experimental framework. The "memorize-then-abstract" finding in particular is intriguing and resonates with some observations I've seen in the mechanistic interpretability literature, though the connection to that body of work is surprisingly absent here.

That said, I have significant concerns about the paper as it stands.

My biggest issue is with the operationalization of "abstraction." The authors define it through transferability, which is reasonable as a starting point, but I found myself unconvinced that what they're measuring is genuinely abstraction rather than, say, systematic generalization or pattern interpolation. After reading the experimental setup twice, I'm still not sure the probing tasks cleanly separate these. The distinction matters enormously for the paper's central claim. The authors cite some philosophy and cognitive science literature gesturally but never really grapple with the hard definitional problem. This feels like a paper that wants to make a strong conceptual claim while doing the conceptual work on the cheap.

The finding about abstract concepts clustering in middle-layer attention heads (finding #2) is the most technically interesting result, but Figure 4 is honestly pretty hard to read, and I think the analysis here could be much sharper. What exactly are these heads doing? The authors identify where abstraction seems to live but don't offer much mechanistic insight into how. I'd want to see ablation studies here — what happens when you knock out those heads?

The robustness experiments in finding #3 are reasonable, though the terminology "concept mutations" feels a bit idiosyncratic and took me a while to parse. I'm also not entirely sure the asymmetry between source-side and target-side mutations is as surprising as the authors present it — it seems somewhat expected given how these models are trained.

On the positive side, the experimental design is generally careful, the controlled conditions are well-thought-out, and the scale experiments in finding #4 add real value. The paper is also clearly written for the most part.

But here's my blunt assessment: the framing is overreaching relative to the actual evidence. Showing that PLMs can transfer learned patterns to new instances is interesting but doesn't unambiguously establish "abstraction" as cognitive scientists or philosophers would recognize it. The paper would be stronger if it made more modest claims or did more work to defend the stronger ones.

I'm also bothered by the focus on T5 and GPT2 specifically — these feel a bit dated at this point, and I wonder whether the findings generalize to more recent architectures.

Overall I lean toward rejection in current form, but I think there's a publishable paper here with significant revision. The authors need to either tighten the conceptual claims or do more to earn them empirically.

This paper tackles an interesting question about hippocampal representations during continual learning of spatial navigation tasks, applying dPCA to CA1 recordings from rodents switching between allocentric and egocentric strategies. I appreciate the ambition here — bridging neuroscience and machine learning in this way is genuinely valuable, and the dataset of 612 neurons is reasonable. But I have significant concerns about the execution.

The dPCA analysis (Section 3 or so) is the core contribution, and I found the presentation confusing on first and second read. The authors claim components encode "decisions, navigational strategies and reward location" but the evidence for this decomposition felt underdeveloped to me. Specifically, how much variance is explained by each component? I couldn't find clear numbers, and without that, the interpretation of what's being "encoded" is largely hand-wavy. The figures (particularly Figure 3 I think) show nice-looking trajectories but I'm not convinced the labeling of components is rigorously justified beyond visual inspection.

The comparison to reinforcement learning algorithms is where the paper really loses me. The authors compare animal behavior to a "standard deep RL model" but the choice of this model seems almost arbitrary. Why this particular architecture? Why not compare to successor representation models, which have a much more direct biological motivation for hippocampus? There's substantial literature — Stachenfeld et al. 2017 being the obvious citation — that connects hippocampal place cell representations to predictive coding and SR-based RL, and I'm genuinely surprised this doesn't appear more prominently. The framing feels like it's ignoring decades of relevant computational neuroscience.

The task-switching analysis is potentially the most interesting part, and the finding that the deep RL model fails to mimic animals during strategy switching is intriguing. But it's presented almost as an afterthought. What exactly does "fails to mimic" mean quantitatively? The behavioral comparison feels imprecise, and I want more — is this a speed-of-adaptation difference? Error pattern difference? This deserves its own dedicated analysis, not a brief paragraph.

I'm also not entirely sure the paper knows what its primary audience is. It reads like it's simultaneously trying to be a neuroscience paper and an ML paper, and it doesn't fully satisfy either. The neuroscience framing is sometimes superficial, and the ML comparisons aren't rigorous enough for an ML venue.

Minor things: the abstract says "results gives" which is a grammatical error that suggests the writing could use another careful pass. Some figure captions are too terse to be interpretable without hunting through the text.

I think there's a real paper buried here, and the core experimental data seems solid. But in its current form the analysis is underpowered and the framing needs sharpening considerably. I'd lean toward rejection with encouragement to resubmit after substantially strengthening the RL comparison section and tightening the dPCA interpretation.

This paper proposes RFMamba, applying State Space Models (specifically Mamba) to RF-based human perception tasks. The core idea — that SSMs might be better suited than Transformers for long RF signal sequences due to linear complexity — is reasonable and worth exploring. I appreciate that the authors tackle three downstream tasks simultaneously.

That said, I have significant concerns that I think need to be addressed.

The motivation for the dual-branch design feels undercooked to me. The paper claims RF signals have "distinct frequency responses in different scenarios" and that this necessitates their frequency-aware branch, but the justification for *why* an SSM needs to be modified specifically for this property — as opposed to just using existing frequency-domain processing — is never really made rigorous. I read Section 3 twice and still wasn't fully convinced this couldn't be handled by a simpler preprocessing step. The ablation in Table 3 (I think?) does show gains from the frequency branch, but the margins are modest enough that I'm not sure they justify the added architectural complexity.

The "incomplete capture caused by specular reflection" problem is mentioned in the abstract and introduction as a key motivation, but then I found it somewhat dropped in the technical sections. How exactly does the proposed architecture address this? I couldn't trace a direct line from this stated problem to any specific design choice.

On experiments: the comparisons look reasonable on the surface, but I'm concerned about the baselines. Several of the Transformer-based methods being compared against — are they fine-tuned to the same degree as RFMamba? In my experience, SSM papers often show favorable comparisons partly because the baseline implementations are not optimally tuned. The authors should be more explicit about implementation details for each baseline. Also, the datasets used — I'm not entirely sure some of them are standard enough for the community to independently verify these results.

The writing needs work. There are grammatical issues throughout ("two branchs" in the abstract is a small but telling sign), and some sentences in Section 4 are genuinely hard to parse. This isn't a dealbreaker but for a top venue it matters.

I think the claim of being "the first attempt to introduce SSM into RF-based human-centric perception" is probably accurate, and novelty-wise that's something. But novelty of application alone isn't sufficient — the technical contribution needs to be more cleanly articulated. Right now the paper reads a bit like "we took Mamba and added a frequency branch" without deeply engaging with *why* this is the right architectural prior for RF data specifically.

My honest assessment: this is an interesting direction and the empirical results seem promising, but the paper in its current form isn't ready. The motivation needs sharpening, the connection between stated challenges and proposed solutions needs to be explicit, and the experimental comparisons need more rigor. I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit rather than accept in current form.

This paper presents S4, a structured state space model for sequence modeling that addresses the computational bottleneck of prior SSM-based approaches (specifically LSSL). The core technical contribution is a clever parameterization that decomposes the state matrix A as a normal plus low-rank matrix, enabling stable diagonalization and reduction to Cauchy kernel computations. The results are genuinely impressive — solving Path-X at length 16k when everything else fails is not a small thing, and the 91% on sequential CIFAR without augmentation is striking.

I'll be honest: I found the mathematical development in Section 3 quite dense, and after reading it twice I'm still not 100% sure I followed every step of the NPLR diagonalization argument. The leap from equation (8) to the Cauchy kernel formulation felt a bit rushed. I think a cleaner proof sketch or a worked small example in the appendix would help readers who aren't already steeped in this particular line of work. That said, I believe the technical claims are correct — the empirical results are hard to fake, and the logic checks out at a high level.

The Long Range Arena results are the headline finding and they're convincing. Table 3 is the money table. What I'm less sure about is how much of the gain comes from the HiPPO initialization of A specifically versus the general SSM architecture. The ablations in Section 5 touch on this but I didn't feel fully satisfied. I might be wrong but it seems like this deserves more careful treatment — if you just randomly initialize A with the NPLR structure, how much do you lose?

The language modeling results (Table 2, WikiText-103) show S4 closing the gap to Transformers but not beating them. This is fine and the paper is honest about it, though I suspect reviewers will push on this. In my experience, models that don't beat Transformers on language tasks get scrutinized harder even when they win everywhere else. The generation speed argument (60x faster) is compelling but I'd want to see wall-clock numbers in a more controlled setting, not just asymptotic arguments.

Minor things: Figure 1 is a nice overview but the caption is too terse. Page 6, the discussion of numerical stability issues with naive diagonalization is important but somewhat buried. The related work on Transformers with linear attention (Katharopoulos et al., Performer, etc.) feels thin — there's a whole family of efficient sequence models that deserves more comparison.

Overall I think this is a strong paper with a genuine technical contribution and impressive empirical results. The Path-X result alone would make me want to accept this. The writing needs some polish and the ablations could be stronger, but these are fixable. I lean toward acceptance, probably borderline strong accept depending on how the authors handle the rebuttal questions about ablations and the Cauchy kernel derivation clarity.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — the suboptimality of autoregressive decoding — and proposes a sliding "reflection window" mechanism that triggers local refinement when uncertainty exceeds some threshold. I've seen a wave of papers on this topic recently (e.g., work on speculative decoding, iterative refinement, self-correction), and I think the motivation here is sound, but I have significant concerns about both the theoretical contributions and the experimental validation.

Starting with the theory: the authors characterize the deviation between the autoregressive solution and the "globally optimal" joint probability maximizer. After reading Section 3 twice, I'm still not entirely clear on how tight these bounds are in practice. The analysis in Proposition 2 relies on assumptions that feel quite strong, and I found myself wondering whether these bounds are vacuous for typical vocabulary sizes and sequence lengths. The connection between the theoretical analysis and the actual algorithm design (the window size, the pausing criterion) feels loose to me — it reads more like post-hoc justification than genuine guidance for the method. I might be wrong, but I don't see how one would set hyperparameters based on the theory.

The method itself is intuitive. The idea of pausing generation when token-level entropy exceeds a threshold and then running a refinement pass over a local window is simple and I actually like it. But the efficiency story is undersold and probably overstated. Table 2 shows inference overhead but I don't think the authors adequately account for the KV-cache invalidation costs when tokens in the window get modified — this can be substantial and in my experience is often the killer for these kinds of approaches.

Experimentally, I found the evaluation pretty weak. The benchmarks chosen (mostly standard QA and reasoning tasks) don't obviously stress-test the specific failure mode the paper claims to fix. I'd want to see more analysis on *when* the method actually triggers and what kinds of revisions it makes. The ablations in Table 3 are appreciated but don't fully answer this. Also, the baselines feel cherry-picked — where's comparison to beam search or even simple top-k with reranking? Those are obvious points of comparison for any paper claiming to improve over greedy autoregressive decoding.

I also want to flag that "optimality" is used somewhat loosely throughout. Finding the globally optimal joint probability sequence is NP-hard in general. The paper kind of acknowledges this but then proceeds to use "optimal" freely in a way that I found misleading.

Overall, this is a decent idea with a paper that isn't yet ready. The writing is generally clear, the problem is real, but the gap between the theory and the method, and between the claims and the empirical evidence, is too large. I'd encourage the authors to either strengthen the theoretical grounding significantly or drop the theory framing and lean into an empirical contribution — trying to do both halfway doesn't serve either goal well.

Leaning toward rejection in current form.

This paper tackles team formation negotiation using deep RL, which is a reasonable problem to work on. I've seen a fair amount of work in this space over the years, and the general direction here — learning negotiation strategies without baking in protocol-specific assumptions — is genuinely interesting. That said, I have some significant concerns that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

The core technical contribution feels a bit thin to me. After reading the method section twice, I'm still not entirely clear what's novel here beyond applying fairly standard deep RL machinery (seems like PPO-based?) to a negotiation setting. The claim that the approach is "protocol-agnostic" is appealing, but I'm not sure it's fully substantiated. How does the observation/action space encoding actually handle arbitrary protocols? The details in Section 3 are vague enough that I couldn't convince myself this generalizes the way the authors claim.

The experimental evaluation is where I have the most issues. Beating hand-crafted bots is a pretty low bar — I'd want to see comparisons against established baselines from the negotiation/mechanism design literature, even simple ones like Rubinstein bargaining agents. The comparison to cooperative game theory solutions (Shapley value, I assume?) is more interesting, but the fact that outcomes are "consistent with" fair solutions is doing a lot of work here. Consistent to what degree? The error bars in Table 2 are wide enough to drive a truck through.

The spatial extension stuff in Section 5 is honestly the most interesting part of the paper, and I think it's underdeveloped. The finding that physical location influences negotiation outcomes is intuitive but the analysis feels preliminary. I wanted to see much more here.

I'm also a bit concerned about scalability claims. The experiments seem to top out at fairly small team sizes — if I'm reading Figure 4 correctly, we're talking 4-5 agents max? Real team formation problems are often much larger, and I'd want at least some discussion of how this scales or where it breaks.

Minor things: the related work misses some relevant work on automated negotiation (the ANAC competition literature in particular), and the writing in the intro is a bit breathless in places — phrases like "does not scale" applied to prior work need more precise qualification.

Look, the paper isn't bad, and I think there's probably a publishable idea somewhere in here. But right now it feels like a workshop paper that's been stretched. The claims in the abstract outrun what the experiments actually demonstrate. I'd recommend a major revision focused on tightening the technical contribution, strengthening baselines, and either expanding or cutting the spatial experiments (commit to them or don't). I'm genuinely on the fence about this one.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely question — can we theoretically justify the empirical scaling behavior observed in 1-bit LLMs like BitNet? The motivation is clear and the problem is genuinely important. That said, I have significant concerns about the depth and novelty of the theoretical contributions.

The main results (kernel convergence as width grows, bounded generalization difference) feel like relatively straightforward adaptations of existing NTK-style analyses to the binary weight setting. I've read through the proofs twice now and I'm not convinced the authors have done more than carefully substitute {-1,+1} constraints into standard infinite-width arguments from Jacot et al. and subsequent work. The paper frames this as a "theoretical breakthrough" in the abstract, which I think is overselling it considerably. The convergence results in Section 3 in particular look very similar to what you'd get from standard overparameterized network theory — I'd want a much clearer explanation of what specifically makes the binary constraint hard to handle and where the technical novelty actually lives.

The generalization difference analysis is more interesting to me, but the bound derived feels loose. I can't tell from what's presented whether this bound is tight enough to actually be meaningful in practice — the constants involved matter a lot here and they're sort of swept under the rug.

The connection to Kaplan et al. scaling laws in the final section is, honestly, the weakest part. It reads like the authors are retrofitting the power-law framing onto their theoretical results without a clean derivation. What exactly is being claimed here? That the theoretical results imply a power law? The argument feels hand-wavy and I found this section confusing after reading it carefully. If you're going to claim a formal justification of scaling laws, you need to be much more precise about what the dependent and independent variables are and why a power law specifically emerges from your framework rather than some other functional form.

I also think the paper undersells its own assumptions. The infinite-width / NTK regime is a significant idealization and I'd want to see a more honest discussion of how far these results are from the practical regime where 1-bit LLMs actually operate. Billions of parameters is large but it's not infinite, and the training dynamics of real BitNet models involve things (e.g., adaptive optimizers, learning rate schedules, layer norm interactions) that are completely absent from this analysis.

Minor things: Figure 2 is hard to read, some notation in Section 3 is introduced without definition (what is the κ in equation 4?), and the related work section misses several relevant papers on binary neural network theory from the last two years.

I'm not recommending rejection outright — the topic deserves theoretical attention and there's something here — but in my view this needs substantial revision to either sharpen the technical contributions or honestly reframe what is actually being proved versus conjectured.

This paper tackles AMR-to-text generation by combining sequence-based and graph-based encoders, which is a reasonable motivation. The core idea — that PLMs struggle with graph structure while GNNs struggle with fluent generation — is intuitive, and I've seen similar dual-encoder arguments made in related structured prediction work. My concern is whether the paper actually delivers something novel enough to merit publication at a top venue.

Let me start with what genuinely bothered me. The abstract claims the model "outperforms the most powerful general-purpose PLM GPT-4." This is a bold claim, and after reading the experimental section carefully, I think this comparison is deeply misleading. GPT-4 is being used zero-shot (or few-shot?) for a task it was never specifically trained on, while the proposed model is fully fine-tuned on AMR-specific data. That's not a fair comparison — it's almost a strawman. I'd actually remove this claim entirely or substantially caveat it, because right now it reads like marketing rather than science.

The architecture itself (Section 3, I think around pages 4-5) is reasonable but not particularly surprising. Integrating a GNN encoder alongside a pretrained seq2seq backbone has been done in several prior works — I'm thinking of papers like Ribeiro et al. and others in the graph-to-text space. The authors cite some of these but I'm not fully convinced the proposed fusion mechanism is sufficiently differentiated. The "dual decoder" aspect is interesting but the ablations in Table 3 don't fully isolate its contribution in a way I found satisfying.

The experimental results on LDC2017T10 and LDC2020T02 look solid, and the BLEU/BLEURT numbers are competitive. That said, I found myself wishing for more analysis. The case study in Section 5 shows one cherry-picked example — one! In my experience this tells us almost nothing. Where does the model fail? What kinds of AMR graphs trip it up? The human evaluation is appreciated but the inter-annotator agreement reporting feels thin.

I also found the writing to be a bit rough in places. The phrase "advantages and disadvantages are exactly the opposite" in the abstract is clunky. Several paragraphs in the related work section feel padded.

I might be wrong about this, but I suspect the gains over strong baselines are partly attributable to the specific pretrained backbone chosen rather than the architectural innovation per se. The ablation doesn't fully rule this out.

Overall: the idea is sound, results are decent, but the novelty is incremental and the GPT-4 comparison is irresponsible as currently framed. I'd lean toward rejection at a top venue but could see this fitting a workshop or a second-tier venue after revision. The authors should either strengthen the novelty argument considerably or be more honest about the contribution's scope.

This paper proposes LLaPA, a multimodal LLM framework for protein-protein interaction prediction that combines protein sequence encoders with PPI network information. The general direction is interesting and I appreciate the attempt to bring LLM-style flexible architectures to this problem. That said, I have significant concerns about the paper as submitted.

Let me start with what I think works. The idea of using natural language instructions to handle arbitrary numbers of proteins in a complex is genuinely clever, and the semi-supervised vs. fully-supervised framing of the PPI network utilization problem is a real issue that the authors identify correctly. The handling of "unseen" proteins through the network-based prior is also a legitimate contribution worth exploring.

However, after reading this twice, I'm left frustrated by a number of things. The experimental evaluation feels thin. Table 2 shows competitive numbers on multi-label PPI type prediction, but the baselines are not convincingly up to date. I'm not seeing comparisons with more recent graph transformer approaches or the latest protein language model fine-tuning work (e.g., ESM2-based methods specifically tuned for PPI). The authors claim SOTA but I'm not sure this claim holds under scrutiny.

The binding affinity prediction results (Section 4.3 I believe) are honestly underwhelming. The improvements over baselines are marginal and the discussion doesn't adequately address why a much heavier architecture is justified here. In my experience, simpler regression heads on top of ESM embeddings can get you surprisingly far, and I'd want to see that ablated more carefully.

I also found the architecture description in Section 3 somewhat hard to follow on first pass. The protein-to-token projection mechanism—is this just a linear layer? The paper is vague about this and Figure 2 doesn't fully resolve the ambiguity. Equation 3 in particular needs more explanation.

The "unseen protein" experiment is the most interesting part of the paper to me, but it gets buried. I think this should be front and center. The results in Table 4 show meaningful improvement over baselines for cold-start proteins, and that story is more compelling than the standard benchmark performance.

One thing that genuinely bothers me: the paper repeatedly claims that existing methods "cannot handle complexes containing an arbitrary number of proteins" but the practical scenarios where you need more than 2-3 proteins are not well motivated. This feels like a straw man argument. Most PPI datasets are pairwise. Show me a real use case.

Minor things: the writing has some awkward phrasing throughout, particularly in the introduction. "macromolecular drugs" is an unusual framing. The related work section is decent but skips some relevant multimodal protein work from the past year.

Overall I lean toward rejection at this stage. The ideas have merit but the execution and evaluation need substantial work. I'd encourage the authors to focus the narrative on the cold-start/unseen protein contribution and build the experiments around that more carefully.

This paper tackles the interesting problem of learning calibrated distributions over segmentation predictions when multiple ground truth annotations are available. The core idea — a two-stage approach where a first stage models categorical likelihoods and a second adversarial stage refines samples — is reasonable, and I appreciate that the authors evaluated on LIDC and Cityscapes variants.

That said, I have several concerns that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

The calibration story feels undercooked to me. The authors claim "calibrated" in the title and abstract quite prominently, but after reading the evaluation section twice I'm still not convinced the paper delivers rigorously on this promise. The metrics used (GED, Hungarian-matched IoU) are standard for this line of work but don't directly measure calibration in the sense that, say, reliability diagrams or ECE would. I might be wrong that this is essential, but given that calibration is the central selling point, I expected something more direct. The word "calibrated" in the title sets an expectation the experiments only partially meet.

The comparison to the Probabilistic U-Net (Kohl et al.) and PHiSeg is appreciated, but I'm not fully convinced the gains in Table 1 and Table 2 are as decisive as presented. The variance across runs isn't always reported, and some differences look within noise range to me. I'd want to see statistical significance tests, or at least multiple seeds reported more carefully.

The toy regression experiment in Section 4.1 is cute but honestly feels like padding. I don't think it adds much — we know adversarial approaches can model multimodal 1D distributions, this has been shown many times. I'd rather see that space used for ablations on the two-stage design, which I found myself wanting more of. Specifically: how much does each stage contribute? What happens if you just do the adversarial refinement end-to-end?

The writing is generally clear, though the description of the training procedure around equations 4-6 gets a bit dense and I had to re-read it several times. A cleaner algorithm box would help enormously here.

One thing I genuinely liked is the "black-box" framing — the idea that this can wrap around any existing segmentation backbone is practically appealing. But the paper doesn't fully stress-test this claim. It's demonstrated with one backbone choice essentially. More heterogeneous experiments would strengthen this.

Minor: Figure 3 is hard to read at print size. Also, the related work section is fairly thin on discussion of flow-based approaches like Normalizing Flows for segmentation uncertainty, which seem relevant.

Overall, I think this is a borderline paper. The idea is solid and the results are competitive, but the calibration framing oversells what's actually demonstrated, and the experimental section has gaps. I lean toward weak reject but would reconsider if the authors can tighten the calibration evaluation substantially and provide cleaner ablations.

This paper proposes using persistent homology to define a complexity measure ("neural persistence") for neural networks, applied to weighted graphs derived from network layers. The core idea is interesting and I appreciate the attempt to bring TDA tools to bear on understanding neural network structure rather than just behavior on inputs. That said, I have significant concerns about the work as presented.

Let me start with what I think is the most fundamental issue: the justification for why persistent homology on the specific filtration described (Section 3) is the *right* tool here is never convincingly made. The authors choose to look at H0 (connected components) essentially, and while the computation is tractable, I found myself asking repeatedly — why not other topological features? Why is this particular graph construction (stratified, weighted by parameter magnitudes) privileged? The paper would be much stronger with an ablation or at least a serious theoretical argument. I might be missing something, but after reading Section 3 twice I still felt the construction felt somewhat ad hoc.

The experiments in Section 4 showing that neural persistence tracks dropout and batch normalization effects — I found these mildly convincing but honestly not that surprising. If you're adding regularization, of course the weight structure changes. Showing that a topological summary of weights changes when you regularize weights is a bit... circular? The more interesting claim is the stopping criterion in Section 4.3, and I'll admit the results there are more compelling. Table 2 shows comparable accuracy with shorter training, which is nice. But the comparison baselines are thin. Early stopping on validation loss is one baseline — what about other complexity-based stopping criteria? What about just stopping at a fixed epoch?

Page 6, Figure 3 — I found this figure genuinely hard to read. The color scheme doesn't work well in grayscale and the caption doesn't explain enough.

I'm also skeptical about the scalability story. The authors mention computational complexity but the experiments are on relatively small networks (MNIST-scale mostly). In my experience with TDA applied to ML, the computational overhead tends to become prohibitive at scale, and I don't see convincing evidence this approach would work on modern architectures.

The related work section is adequate but I think the authors underengage with prior work on network complexity measures — things like parameter counting, effective dimensionality measures, and especially the PAC-Bayes literature on margin-based complexity. The topological framing is novel but shouldn't ignore this context.

Overall: the idea is genuinely interesting and I think there's a real paper here. But right now it reads more like a proof-of-concept than a fully developed contribution. The theoretical grounding needs work, the experiments need stronger baselines, and the claims should be more carefully scoped. I'm leaning toward rejection but would be willing to reconsider with major revisions.

This paper tackles the problem of training binary neural networks and presents a combination of techniques that together achieve impressive accuracy gains on ImageNet with ResNet-18. The core contributions are: a strong baseline built by combining existing tricks, an attention-matching loss between binary and real-valued convolutions, and a data-driven rescaling mechanism using real-valued activations. The results are genuinely strong — a 5%+ improvement over prior SOTA on ImageNet is not nothing.

That said, I have some concerns about the novelty framing. After reading this twice, I kept asking myself: how much of the gain comes from the baseline engineering versus the proposed technical contributions? The ablation in Table 3 is helpful here, but I think the paper undersells how much of the heavy lifting is being done by the baseline improvements (better optimization, etc.) versus the novel real-to-binary matching idea. I'd want to see a cleaner decomposition. The attention distillation idea (Section 3.2) is also not entirely new — Zagoruyko & Komodakis already proposed attention transfer for knowledge distillation, and while the authors acknowledge this, I think the connection deserves more discussion. Is this really a fundamentally different use case or just the same idea applied in a slightly different context?

The rescaling mechanism (Section 3.3) is actually the more interesting contribution to me. Using the real-valued activations that are available pre-binarization to rescale post-binarization outputs is clever and practically motivated. I'm not entirely sure the theoretical justification in Section 3.3 is airtight — equation (4) feels a bit hand-wavy in how it derives the optimal scaling factor — but the empirical results support it, so I'm willing to let that slide somewhat.

On the experimental side: I appreciate the CIFAR-100 and ImageNet results, but I'm bothered by the exclusive use of ResNet-18. In my experience with binary network papers, architectural choice can dramatically affect relative comparisons. Does this approach generalize to other architectures? The paper would be substantially stronger with even one additional architecture. Also, Figure 2 is actually quite useful for building intuition about what the attention maps look like, but the resolution in my PDF made it hard to read — minor point.

The writing is generally clear, though Section 2 (related work) feels rushed and could be better organized. Some claims in the introduction are a bit aggressive given the narrow experimental scope.

Bottom line: I think this is a solid paper with genuinely useful empirical contributions. The combination of tricks is well-executed and the results are convincing. But the novelty of individual components is somewhat limited, and the evaluation scope is narrower than I'd like for a top venue. I'm leaning weakly positive — borderline accept. Addressing the architecture generalization question and clarifying the ablation story would push me firmly to accept.

This paper proposes using Minimum Description Length as an objective function for evolving RNN architectures via genetic algorithms, with the goal of improving both generalization and interpretability. The core idea is interesting and I appreciate the authors taking a principled information-theoretic approach rather than just throwing more parameters at the problem.

That said, I have significant concerns about the empirical evaluation and the scope of claims being made.

The addition task result — four cells, 100% accuracy on arbitrary-length numbers — is genuinely impressive and I don't want to dismiss it. But I kept asking myself while reading: is addition really the right benchmark here? It's a task with extremely regular structure, almost perfectly suited to this kind of compressed representation search. The authors present it as a demonstration of increasing complexity, but in my experience these hand-selected toy tasks can be misleading about what a method actually buys you in practice. What happens on tasks where the underlying regularity is less obvious? The paper gestures at "tasks of increasing complexity" but the complexity range feels narrow.

The comparison to backpropagation-based alternatives (Section 4, and Table 2) is where I found myself most frustrated. The baselines feel undertuned. Standard LSTMs and GRUs with even modest regularization can generalize surprisingly well on these kinds of formal language tasks, and I'm not convinced the authors have given them a fair shake. What hyperparameter search was done? What sequence lengths were used during training? These details matter enormously and I had to hunt through the paper to find partial answers.

The MDL formulation itself (equations 3-5) — I think I understand what's being computed, but the description around page 6 is genuinely confusing on first read. I had to go back twice. The relationship between the encoding length and the network's actual parameter space could be made much cleaner.

On the interpretability claim: I find "provably appropriate for sequences of arbitrary length" a strong statement. The proof sketch in Section 5 seems reasonable for the specific network topology they discover, but I'm not sure this generalizes beyond these particular case studies in any meaningful way. The word "provably" is doing a lot of heavy lifting here.

The genetic algorithm itself — I'm a bit skeptical of the computational cost being adequately acknowledged. The paper mentions evolution over populations of networks but doesn't give a clear picture of wall-clock time versus the backprop baselines. For practical adoption this matters.

I think there's a real contribution buried here, particularly around the theoretical connection between MDL and out-of-distribution generalization in sequence models. But the paper in its current form oversells the results and undersells the limitations. I'd want to see stronger baselines, more diverse tasks, and a clearer empirical section before recommending acceptance. Leaning toward rejection, but I could be persuaded if the authors can address the baseline fairness issue convincingly.

This paper proposes using Signal Temporal Logic (STL) robustness scores as an additional loss signal during training of end-to-end driving models, validated on NAVSIM/OpenScene. The core idea is interesting enough — leveraging formal methods to shape learning — but I have significant concerns about both the technical depth and the empirical evaluation that prevent me from recommending acceptance.

Let me start with what I think is the most fundamental issue: the paper conflates "formal verification" with "using STL robustness as a differentiable loss." These are very different things. Formal verification implies some guarantee about system behavior. What's actually happening here is that STL robustness is being used as a soft penalty during training, which is a reasonable technique but calling it "formal verification" in the title and throughout is, frankly, misleading. After reading this twice I still couldn't find where any actual verification (in the formal sense) is being performed. The RSS constraints being "encoded" in STL also feels superficial — I'd want to see a much more rigorous treatment of how RSS conditions translate to STL formulae and whether completeness/soundness properties are preserved.

The results in Table 1 and Table 2 show improvements, but the margins are modest and I'm not convinced the ablations are thorough enough to isolate what's actually doing the work. Is it the STL formulation specifically, or just the fact that you're adding any safety-aware penalty? There's no comparison against simpler baselines like adding an RSS-based penalty directly without the STL layer of indirection. This feels like a significant missing experiment.

I also found the RSS-to-STL encoding section (Section 3.2 if I recall correctly) underspecified. The specific temporal operators chosen and the parameter settings seem to be chosen without strong justification. In my experience with temporal logic specifications, these choices matter enormously for the resulting robustness landscape and gradient behavior.

On the positive side — I do think the general research direction has merit, and the paper is tackling a real problem. The open-loop evaluation on NAVSIM is appropriate given the scope. And the writing is generally clear, though some passages in the introduction feel repetitive.

Minor things: Figure 3 is hard to read at print size. The related work section doesn't engage seriously with prior work on differentiable STL (e.g., Leung et al., Roque et al.) which seems directly relevant and should at minimum be discussed.

I'm not entirely sure the NAVSIM benchmark is sufficient to make strong safety claims — it's an open-loop simulator and safety violations in open-loop evaluation are a proxy at best. The authors acknowledge this but I think the limitation deserves more prominent treatment.

Overall, I think this needs substantial revision: clearer framing (drop or qualify the "formal verification" framing), stronger baselines, and more rigorous specification of the STL encoding. Not ready in its current form.

This paper proposes DMNet, a dual-branch vocoder that combines a complex spectrogram reconstruction network (CondNet) with a GAN branch, along with multi-band processing and a harmonic discriminator. I've read through this twice and have some fairly significant concerns.

The core idea — combining frequency-domain and time-domain processing in a vocoder — isn't new. Vocoders like SoundStream, EnCodec, and especially Vocos already explore spectral-domain generation. The authors don't do a convincing job distinguishing their contribution from this line of work. The related work section feels thin and somewhat dated in its citations. I'd really like to see a direct comparison against Vocos in particular, since that paper is quite relevant and essentially asks the same question about whether you even need time-domain generation at all.

On the harmonic discriminator specifically — I think this is actually the most interesting part of the paper, but it's also the least well-explained. The "learnable harmonic filters" described around equation 4 are not sufficiently motivated. How exactly are the filter frequencies initialized? Are they learned end-to-end? I found Figure 2 pretty hard to parse. After staring at it for a while I still wasn't sure how the harmonic filter bank interacts with the multi-scale structure. This needs significant clarification.

The experimental results in Table 1 show improvements in MOS scores, but the confidence intervals are quite narrow and the differences between DMNet and HiFi-GAN v2 in some objective metrics are honestly within what I'd consider noise. The PESQ and UTMOS numbers look reasonable, but I'm skeptical of MOS evaluations with what appears to be a relatively small listener pool — how many raters? This isn't stated clearly enough in Section 4.1.

The multi-band processing scheme also raises questions. The authors claim computational efficiency as a motivation but I don't see a clear FLOPs or real-time factor comparison. For a paper partly motivated by efficiency, this omission is glaring. HiFi-GAN runs in real-time on CPU in many implementations — does DMNet?

One thing I genuinely appreciate is the ablation study (Table 2), which does support the utility of each component, particularly the harmonic discriminator. The drop in quality when removing it is notable. I wish the rest of the paper were as carefully structured.

The writing needs work. There are grammatical issues throughout ("DMNet validates the effectiveness" in the abstract is awkward), and some sentences in Section 3 are genuinely hard to parse on first reading.

My overall feeling is that this paper has a reasonable idea but the execution and presentation aren't quite there yet. The harmonic discriminator is interesting enough to warrant further development, but the claims about efficiency need to be backed up with actual numbers, and the novelty argument needs to be made more carefully given recent work. I lean toward rejection in current form but would encourage a thorough revision.

This paper proposes an interesting extension of the Matryoshka representation learning idea to multimodal models, specifically targeting the visual token efficiency problem in LMMs like LLaVA. The core idea — learning nested visual token representations that can be truncated at inference time — is clean and I appreciate that it addresses a real practical problem. Anyone who has tried to run LLaVA on videos or high-res images knows the token explosion issue is genuinely painful.

That said, I have significant concerns about the paper's contributions and evaluation.

The technical novelty is, frankly, modest. The key idea is essentially applying the Matryoshka training objective (train with multiple granularities simultaneously) to visual tokens after clustering/merging. The connection to the original Matryoshka representation learning paper (Kusupati et al.) feels underexplored — the authors should be much more explicit about what's fundamentally different here versus a straightforward adaptation. After reading the method section twice, I'm still not entirely convinced this required a full paper rather than a workshop note, though I might be wrong about that.

The finding in contribution (2) — that COCO-style benchmarks need only ~9 tokens — is actually the most interesting empirical result in the paper to me, and I think it's undersold. This says something important about benchmark saturation/simplicity that the community should care about. However, I worry this result is somewhat obvious to practitioners and has been implicitly noted in prior token pruning work (e.g., the LLaVA-PruMerge line of work).

The gap between oracle upper bound and fixed-scale representations (contribution 3) is intriguing but the analysis feels incomplete. What's actually preventing us from closing this gap? The paper identifies the problem but offers limited insight into solutions. This feels like the paper is advertising future work as a current contribution.

On the experiments: Table 2 and 3 show reasonable results but the comparisons aren't always apples-to-apples. I'd want to see more careful ablations — specifically, how much of the gain comes from the multi-scale training itself versus just having fewer tokens as a form of regularization? The efficiency numbers are presented somewhat favorably; actual wall-clock speedups on real hardware would be more convincing than FLOPs estimates.

Minor issues: Figure 1 is helpful but the "nesting" visualization could be clearer. Some notation in Section 3 is a bit loose. The abstract uses an em-dash mid-sentence in a slightly awkward way.

Overall, I'm leaning toward weak reject. The problem is real, the approach is reasonable, but the novelty bar isn't quite cleared for a top venue in my opinion. The empirical findings are interesting but not thoroughly analyzed. I'd encourage the authors to either sharpen the technical contribution or lean much harder into the empirical analysis angle — right now it's trying to be both and not fully succeeding at either.

This paper tackles an interesting and somewhat underexplored problem in VFL — what happens when passive parties unexpectedly drop out during deployment. I'll admit the motivation section (around pages 2-3) is reasonably compelling, and the framing of two simultaneous vulnerabilities (performance degradation + IP leakage) is clever. However, after reading this twice, I have significant concerns about both the technical contribution and the experimental validation.

My biggest issue is with the proposed PlugVFL framework itself. The core idea — essentially training auxiliary modules to substitute for missing party embeddings — feels incremental relative to existing work on missing modality learning and knowledge distillation. I'm not entirely sure the authors are sufficiently aware of the missing modality literature (e.g., work by Ma et al. on multimodal learning under modality dropout), because the technical novelty claimed here would look considerably thinner if compared directly to those methods. The paper would benefit from a much more thorough related work discussion.

The IP protection component is where things get murkier. The authors claim to "disguise" label information from passive parties' feature extractors, but the evaluation of this protection is weak. Table 3 shows results against a few inference attacks, but I found myself asking: why these specific attacks? The threat model feels hand-picked to make the defense look good. A stronger passive adversary or more recent label inference attacks might break the proposed protection — and the paper doesn't really grapple with this possibility. This is a serious gap.

On experiments: the datasets used are fairly standard (I see MNIST-based splits, NUS-WIDE, etc.) which is fine, but the baselines are underpowered. Where is the comparison against recent VFL robustness work? The ablation in Section 5.3 is appreciated but doesn't really isolate the most important design choices clearly. Figure 4 is honestly hard to read — the line styles are too similar.

I also think the theoretical analysis, if you can call it that, is thin. The "analysis" in Section 4.2 is more of an informal argument than a proper bound or guarantee. I'd either strengthen it or drop the pretense of theoretical justification entirely.

One thing I genuinely liked: the problem setup in Section 3 is cleanly written and the distinction between training-phase and deployment-phase quitting is a useful clarification that I don't think has been made this explicitly before. That's real value.

But overall, in my experience, papers at this venue need either stronger theoretical grounding or significantly more comprehensive empirical validation — and this paper currently has neither. The writing is competent but the contribution feels like it was sized for a workshop rather than the main track. I'd encourage the authors to substantially expand the threat model evaluation and engage more seriously with related work before resubmission. Leaning toward rejection.

This paper proposes Q-map, a goal-conditioned RL agent that uses an autoencoder-style network to predict minimum step counts to every spatial coordinate simultaneously, and applies this as an exploration mechanism. The core idea is appealing — sharing parameters across goals to exploit spatial structure is sensible, and the connection to Horde with parameter sharing is a nice framing.

That said, I have significant concerns about the empirical evaluation that undermine my confidence in the contributions.

The results on Montezuma's Revenge are the centerpiece of the paper, but the comparison is quite thin. The authors compare against standard DQN and a couple baselines, but don't compare against contemporaneous exploration methods like count-based approaches (Bellemare et al. 2016), curiosity-driven exploration (Pathak et al. 2017), or Go-Explore — methods that any reader will immediately wonder about. I get that some of these are concurrent work, but the paper should at least situate itself more carefully. The numbers in Table 2 are hard to interpret without error bars or variance information. Were these averaged over multiple seeds? I couldn't find this information clearly stated anywhere.

The grid world experiments (Section 4, Figure 3) are nice and I found the visualization of the Q-map outputs genuinely compelling — this is where the paper is at its strongest, showing the learned distance estimates look reasonable. But it's a toy domain and the generalization claims feel overstated given what's actually demonstrated.

I'm also confused by the exploration mechanism described in Section 3.3. The goal selection procedure — picking goals at a "desired distance" — seems like it introduces a non-trivial hyperparameter (the distance threshold), and I didn't see a proper ablation studying sensitivity to this. This feels like it could easily be doing most of the work, and the architectural contribution might be secondary.

The writing is generally clear but gets sloppy in places. The abstract calls Montezuma's Revenge "notoriously difficult" which is... fine, everyone says this, but the framing throughout feels a bit like the difficulty of the domain is doing rhetorical work that the results don't fully support.

One thing I genuinely liked: the 3D Q-learning update (Equation 4 or thereabouts) where you update all goal estimates simultaneously is clean and the efficiency argument is well-made. This is probably the most technically solid contribution.

I'm on the fence here. The core idea is good and there's something real in this paper. But in my experience, papers making exploration claims on hard Atari games need much tighter empirical comparisons than what's presented. I'd want to see: (1) comparison against stronger baselines, (2) variance over seeds, (3) ablation on the distance hyperparameter. As is, I lean toward rejection but would reconsider with a major revision addressing the evaluation gaps.

This paper tackles an important theoretical gap — the convergence analysis of Variance Exploding diffusion models, which have lagged behind VP models in terms of theoretical understanding. The core contribution is introducing a modified forward process with a small drift term that enables exponential convergence rates, and a "unified tangent-based analysis framework" for handling both reverse SDE and probability flow ODE settings. I appreciate the ambition here.

That said, I have significant concerns about the paper.

The motivation for adding the drift term feels somewhat ad hoc to me. Yes, it achieves the exp(-T) rate, but I worry this modification changes the fundamental character of VE models in a way that makes the theoretical results less relevant to what practitioners actually use. The authors gesture at this on pages 6-7 but I don't think they adequately address whether their modified process is actually close to DDPM++ or EDM in any meaningful sense. How sensitive are the final bounds to the specific form of the drift? I found this whole section somewhat hand-wavy.

The manifold hypothesis treatment — I've read a lot of papers invoking this lately and I'm increasingly skeptical of how it's being used. The assumption structure in Section 4 (particularly the conditions around Assumption 3) seems quite strong. I might be wrong but I think the intrinsic dimension bounds are doing a lot of work here and deserve more scrutiny. What happens when the manifold has high intrinsic dimension? The polynomial sample complexity claim could look quite bad in practice.

The unified framework in Section 5 is probably the most technically interesting contribution. But the presentation is dense to the point of being difficult to follow. After reading the proof sketch twice, I'm still not entirely clear on how the "tangent-based" analysis actually handles the ODE case differently from previous approaches. Figure 2 helps a little but not enough. The authors need to do significantly better at explaining the key technical insight here — what is the geometric intuition?

On the comparison with prior work (Table 1): I appreciate the effort to contextualize the results, but some of the comparisons feel unfair. The baseline works operate under different assumptions, and presenting this as a clean improvement requires more careful qualification.

Minor things: there are several places where the English is awkward (e.g., page 3 "introduces hardness in balancing"), and Equation 7 has a notation conflict with the definition introduced two pages earlier.

My bottom line: the problem is important and there's real technical content here. But the paper needs substantial revision — clearer motivation for the modified forward process, more honest discussion of assumption strength, and a much better exposition of the core technical ideas. I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, but I could be persuaded if the authors address these points in the rebuttal. The theoretical ML community needs good VE convergence results, so I hope the authors keep pushing on this.

This paper proposes CLDA (continuous latent Dirichlet allocation), a hierarchical Bayesian approach layered on top of pretrained language models to provide "concept-level" interpretations beyond raw attention weights. I've read this twice now and have pretty mixed feelings.

The core motivation is reasonable — attention weights as interpretations have been criticized extensively (Jain & Wallace 2019, Wiegreffe & Pinter 2019 are the obvious citations here, and I was glad to see them referenced), so finding richer, more human-readable explanations is a legitimate goal. And the dual-mode framing (interpreter vs. regulator) is a nice organizational choice that I appreciated.

That said, I have serious concerns about the technical contribution and the evaluation. The "continuous" extension of LDA feels underspecified. The derivation in Section 3 moves quickly through the variational inference steps, and I genuinely couldn't tell from equations (4)-(7) whether the reparameterization trick being used here is novel or essentially lifted from Kingma & Welling with minor modifications.
