The authors claim "high classification accuracy" but the numbers they're reporting are not competitive with modern methods — they're competitive with architectures from 5-7 years ago. Replacing only the deepest layers of CNNs and calling that a win seems like it's cherry-picking the easiest comparison. What happens if you apply this to a full network rather than grafting it onto existing convolutional feature extractors?

The theoretical sections (Section 4, particularly Theorem 2 and its proof sketch) feel undercooked. The proof that conditional dependencies are preserved in the discriminative graph rests on assumptions that are stated somewhat informally, and I'm not entirely sure the result is as strong as the authors imply. I'd want to see this tightened considerably, or at least a clearer statement of what breaks if the assumptions don't hold.

The computational efficiency claims — "runs on a standard desktop CPU" — need quantification. How long does structure learning take as the number of variables grows? The conditional independence tests scale poorly in general and I didn't see any complexity analysis beyond vague reassurances.

Figure 3 is confusing. I stared at it for a while and couldn't immediately parse the difference between the generative and discriminative graph constructions. Better visual design would help significantly.

I might be wrong about this, but I suspect the performance gains over the ablated baselines come primarily from the feature reuse enabled by skip connections rather than anything specific to the dependency-analysis-driven structure. The paper doesn't isolate this.

Overall: interesting idea that isn't ready yet. The theory needs sharpening, the experiments need a serious overhaul, and the authors need to be more honest about the limitations. I'd encourage a major revision and resubmission rather than acceptance in current form.

This paper proposes EVF-SAM, which adapts SAM for referring expression segmentation by using a vision-language model (specifically BEIT-3) to generate prompts. The core finding — that early fusion VLMs work better than late-fusion alternatives like CLIP for this task — is the main empirical contribution here.

I'll be honest: the paper is reasonably well-executed but I'm not fully convinced it rises to the level of a top ML venue contribution. The architecture is fairly straightforward — you take a pre-trained VLM, use it to produce point/box prompts, feed those to SAM. The "surprising" observations flagged in the abstract (multimodal prompts help, early fusion helps) are... not that surprising to me? After reading the related work in section 2, it felt like these conclusions were somewhat predictable given what the community already knows about cross-modal attention in early fusion models.

That said, the experimental results in Table 1 are genuinely strong. The numbers on RefCOCO/+/g are competitive or better than much larger models, and the efficiency argument (82% parameter reduction vs. prior SAM-based methods) is compelling and practically meaningful. I appreciate that the authors didn't just chase SOTA numbers but also made an honest case for efficiency.

My bigger concern is the ablation study in Table 3. It's helpful but feels incomplete. Specifically, I'd want to understand better why BEIT-3 outperforms CLIP beyond "early fusion" — is it the architecture, the pretraining data, the scale? The paper attributes everything to early fusion but doesn't fully disentangle these factors. BEIT-3 is also a much larger and more recently trained model, so the comparison isn't exactly apples-to-apples. I might be wrong, but I think a fairer comparison would control for model scale more carefully.

Figure 3 is useful for intuition but I found the qualitative examples somewhat cherry-picked — I'd love to see some failure cases. Where does EVF-SAM break down? The discussion of limitations is thin.

The writing is generally clear, though section 3.2 gets a bit dense and I had to read it twice to follow the exact prompt generation procedure. A cleaner diagram of the full pipeline would help (Figure 2 is okay but could show the prompt generation more explicitly).

One minor thing: the claim in the abstract that SAM "lacks further exploration of text prompts" undersells some prior work — LISA and others have done meaningful work here, and while you cite them, the framing feels a bit dismissive.

Overall, this is solid incremental work with a clear practical contribution. In my experience, papers like this — clean implementation, good numbers, reasonable insights — tend to be useful to the community even if they're not groundbreaking. I lean toward acceptance but the authors really need to address the confounded comparison between BEIT-3 and CLIP before I'd feel confident recommending it.

This paper investigates the Pareto efficiency of weight quantization in CNNs, arguing that lower precision can dominate higher precision in accuracy vs. model size tradeoffs when channel counts are allowed to vary. The core claim — that lower bit-widths Pareto dominate higher ones under iso-model-size constraints — is interesting and I think worth exploring, but I have significant concerns about the execution and novelty of the work as presented.

Let me start with what I found genuinely interesting. The framing around the channel-to-filter ratio as a predictor for optimal precision is novel enough and provides a somewhat principled way to think about mixed-precision selection. The empirical results on ResNet50 and MobileNetV2 are decent, though 0.9% and 2.2% top-1 improvements aren't exactly jaw-dropping given the complexity of the proposed analysis.

My main concern is the theoretical "insights" section. After reading it twice I'm still not fully convinced the theoretical grounding is rigorous enough to support the claims being made. The analysis feels more like post-hoc rationalization of empirical observations than genuine theoretical contribution. The connection between the channel ratio and precision optimality is plausible but the derivation has several hand-wavy steps — particularly around the approximations made near equation (what I believe is around eq. 5-6 in the main text). I might be wrong here, but it reads like the theory was written to match the experiments rather than predict them.

The experimental setup also troubles me. The paper focuses almost exclusively on standard convolutions, and the extension to depthwise separable convolutions (critical for MobileNets!) feels tacked on and underanalyzed. MobileNetV2 uses depthwise convolutions extensively, and the paper's own theoretical framework apparently doesn't cleanly apply there — yet the authors present MobileNetV2 results without adequately acknowledging this gap. This inconsistency is honestly a bit frustrating.

I'd also push back on the framing relative to existing mixed-precision literature. Works like HAQ, HAWQ, and EdMIPS already address mixed-precision allocation in more general settings. The paper's comparison to these methods is limited, and I'm not convinced the proposed approach meaningfully advances beyond them in practical applicability, even if the specific Pareto framing is distinct.

On presentation: Figure 3 is genuinely useful and I appreciated it, but several figures (particularly Figure 5) are hard to read at print size. The writing is generally clear but there are some awkward passages in Section 4 that could use another pass.

Bottom line: there's a real idea here and the empirical work is solid enough, but the theoretical contribution is overstated and the scope is too narrow. The authors need to either strengthen the theory substantially or be more honest about this being primarily an empirical study with a useful heuristic. As it stands, I lean toward rejection but would consider a revised version that addresses these issues.

This paper proposes a relatively straightforward data pruning approach for CLIP pre-training based on word frequency distributions. The core idea — prune image-text pairs that contain high-frequency words to achieve a more balanced word frequency distribution — is intuitive and I can see the motivation connecting back to word embedding literature (word2vec, GloVe, etc.). That connection is actually one of the more interesting aspects of this submission.

That said, I have significant concerns.

The empirical evaluation feels thin to me. The gains reported in Table 2 and Table 3 are modest, and I'm not convinced the experimental setup is rigorous enough to support the claims being made. Specifically, training on a pruned subset and then fine-tuning on the full dataset for one additional epoch feels like it introduces a confound — how do we know the gains aren't just coming from the additional fine-tuning epoch rather than the pruning itself? I'd want to see ablations that control for total compute and total data passes more carefully. This is actually a pretty serious methodological issue that I think needs to be addressed directly.

The comparison to MetaCLIP is framed as an advantage ("we don't need metadata") but I found this framing a bit misleading. MetaCLIP and WFPP are solving somewhat different problems and the comparison deserves more nuance. After reading this twice I'm still not sure the authors fully understand what MetaCLIP is doing vs. what they're doing.

I'm also puzzled by the theoretical justification. The argument that a more balanced word frequency distribution improves training because it worked for word embeddings — I'm not entirely sure this transfers cleanly to the multi-modal setting. The visual grounding component adds complexity that the paper doesn't really grapple with. What happens to the image side of the pairs? Are there systematic biases introduced by pruning on text alone?

The visualization in Figure 3 (I think?) showing the before/after frequency distributions is nice and I appreciated that the authors included this analysis. It's probably the most convincing part of the paper. But the paper would benefit enormously from a deeper investigation of *what* gets pruned — what concepts, what visual categories, what domains — rather than just showing the aggregate frequency curves.

Writing quality is decent but there are several places where the prose is awkward and the paper reads like it was assembled somewhat hastily. The related work section is notably thin.

In my experience, a solid data curation paper needs either (1) strong theoretical grounding, (2) very convincing large-scale empirics, or (3) both. This paper currently has neither in sufficient measure. The idea has some merit and I don't want to dismiss it entirely, but in its current form I don't think it clears the bar for acceptance. Major revision at minimum.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — using human-provided natural language instructions to improve hierarchical RL in a multi-task crafting environment. The core idea is sensible: decompose complex tasks via language, train a high-level instruction generator alongside a low-level policy conditioned on those instructions. I've seen related work in this space (Andreas et al.'s modular networks, the ALFRED benchmark work, Jiang et al. on language grounding in RL), and this paper sits in a reasonable place relative to that literature, though I'm not entirely convinced it advances things as much as the authors claim.

Let me start with what I found genuinely interesting. The dataset itself seems like a real contribution — step-by-step human demonstrations paired with natural language in a crafting grid world. The zero-shot generalization results are the most compelling part of the paper, and Figure 4 (if I'm reading it right) does show meaningful transfer to unseen tasks. The interpretability angle is nice too, though I wish the authors had done more with it beyond showing a few qualitative examples.

My main concerns are around the baselines and experimental rigor. The comparisons feel a bit thin. I kept asking myself: what happens with a flat policy trained end-to-end with enough data? The paper doesn't convincingly rule out simpler alternatives. The ablations help somewhat but I'd want to see more. Also, the environment is relatively simple — this is a grid world after all — and I'm genuinely uncertain whether the conclusions would hold in more complex settings. The authors claim generalization but the task space they test on feels hand-crafted to favor their approach.

The writing is generally clear but there are places where the model description gets muddy. Around Section 3.2, I found the description of how the high-level generator interfaces with the low-level policy confusing on first read. I had to go back twice to understand the training procedure. A diagram would help enormously here.

I'm also a bit bothered by the framing around "human instructions." In practice, collecting these demonstrations is costly, and the paper doesn't really grapple with how many you need or how sensitive the approach is to instruction quality. There's a brief few-shot experiment but it feels underdeveloped.

The related work section is decent but misses some relevant citations on hierarchical RL with language (particularly some 2019-2020 work on instruction following that feels directly relevant).

Overall: the paper has a reasonable core idea and some promising results, but the experimental validation feels incomplete and the claims outpace the evidence a bit. I think this is closer to a workshop paper right now than a full conference contribution. The dataset might be the most standalone valuable thing here, and I'd encourage the authors to position it more explicitly as such.

Borderline reject, leaning toward rejection unless the rebuttal can address the baseline concerns seriously.

This paper introduces MME-RealWorld, a large-scale benchmark for evaluating multimodal LLMs on high-resolution real-world scenarios. The scale is impressive — 13,366 images, 29,429 QA pairs, 43 subtasks — and I appreciate that the authors invested in genuine human annotation rather than relying on GPT-4 or similar models to generate labels, which has become an increasingly problematic trend in benchmark papers.

That said, I have some real concerns here.

The core claim is that existing benchmarks are too easy and suffer from data quality issues due to model-based annotation. Fair enough, and the motivation is reasonable. But I found the paper surprisingly thin on *why* the specific 5 scenarios (OCR, autonomous driving, remote sensing, monitoring, medical) were chosen over others. This feels somewhat arbitrary. Why these five? Is there a principled framework here, or did the authors just pick domains where high-resolution images are naturally available? I'd want to see this justified more carefully.

The human performance analysis is actually one of the more interesting parts of the paper, and I think it's undersold. The fact that humans themselves struggle on certain subtasks (the numbers in Table 3 are striking) is genuinely informative and rare for a benchmark paper. I found myself wanting much more discussion of *where* and *why* humans fail — this could be a paper on its own honestly.

On the evaluation results: 29 models is a lot, and I respect the effort. But the analysis in Section 4 feels somewhat surface-level. We get accuracy numbers and some high-level observations about resolution being a bottleneck, but I'm not convinced by the resolution analysis specifically. The authors show performance degrades with higher resolution inputs, but I'm not entirely sure this is controlled properly — are the questions themselves also harder for high-res images, or is it purely a resolution effect? This conflation bothers me.

One thing that genuinely annoyed me: the benchmark claims to be "challenging for humans" in the title, but the human evaluation methodology is described very briefly. How many humans? What was their background? How were disagreements handled? After reading this twice I still couldn't find clear answers. For a paper making this a central selling point, that's a significant omission.

The annotation quality control process is described but I'd want more detail on inter-annotator agreement. 25 annotators and 7 experts sounds impressive but without IAA statistics it's hard to evaluate what that actually means for data quality.

Overall I think this is a useful contribution to the community — we do need harder, higher-quality benchmarks — but the paper reads more like a data report than a research paper with strong scientific insights. The analysis depth doesn't match the scale of the data collection effort. I'm leaning weak accept, but the authors really need to strengthen the analysis sections and be more rigorous about the human evaluation methodology.

This paper proposes BSFP, a quantization scheme that decomposes weight vectors into superpositions of subword vectors with floating-point scaling factors. The core idea is interesting and the hardware story is reasonably well-developed, but I have significant concerns about the evaluation and positioning that need to be addressed before I could recommend acceptance.

Let me start with what I think works. The decomposition idea itself is clever — representing weights as a sum of scaled subword components does give you more expressive power than simple block floating point, and I can see why this would help with skewed distributions. The hardware engine description in Section 4 is more concrete than most papers of this type, and I appreciate that the authors actually did area/energy analysis rather than just claiming efficiency in the abstract.

That said, the comparison landscape here is... thin. The paper essentially benchmarks everything against MSFP (Microsoft's block floating point scheme), and while beating MSFP is fine, it's not exactly a comprehensive evaluation. By 2023-2024 standards, I would expect comparisons against at minimum GPTQ, AWQ, or similar learned quantization approaches, even if the target application domain is different. The 20.56% top-1 accuracy improvement over MSFP sounds dramatic until you realize MSFP at low bit-widths is genuinely quite bad — this isn't a hard bar to clear. I found myself on page 7 wondering whether any of these results actually compare favorably against standard INT8 or INT4 quantization baselines that practitioners actually use.

The ImageNet experiments in Table 2 only cover ResNet variants. After reading this twice I still couldn't find results on transformers or any language model, which feels like a major gap given that low-precision inference is predominantly motivated by LLM deployment these days. I might be wrong about the target use case here, but the abstract doesn't restrict scope to CNNs.

The grid search quantization flow described in Section 3.2 — I'm not entirely sure the computational overhead of this search is adequately characterized. How expensive is this at deployment time? The paper glosses over this.

On the hardware side: the 2x throughput and 5.3x energy efficiency claims are compelling if true, but these are under "same silicon area budget" which is doing a lot of work. The baseline being compared to needs to be much more carefully specified. What process node? What clock frequency assumptions?

The writing is generally clear though occasionally awkward, and Figure 3 is actually quite helpful for understanding the data format. But Figure 5 is too small to read comfortably.

Bottom line: the core contribution is real but incremental, the evaluation is insufficient for the current landscape, and the claims in the abstract oversell what's actually demonstrated. I lean toward rejection in current form, but I think a substantially expanded experimental section comparing against modern quantization methods on transformer architectures could make this publishable.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely problem — using LLMs for detecting sexism in political discourse — but I have significant concerns about both the scientific contributions and the execution that I think need to be addressed before this could be published at a top venue.

Let me start with what I found genuinely interesting. The focus on political discourse as a specific subdomain for sexism detection is a reasonable framing, and the observation that trigger events (elections, scandals, etc.) correlate with spikes in sexist content is intuitive but worth documenting empirically. I also appreciated that the authors didn't just report rosy results — the finding that more instructive prompts don't necessarily improve performance is actually a useful negative result that the community should know about.

That said, I have serious problems with the paper as written.

The proposed "confidence algorithm" — which seems to be the second main contribution — is described quite vaguely in the methodology section. After reading it twice I still wasn't sure exactly what makes it novel. It appears to be some variant of sampling-based uncertainty estimation or aggregating across prompt variations, but the formalization (around equation 2-3 if I recall correctly) doesn't cleanly distinguish this from existing work on LLM calibration or ensemble-based confidence scoring. The authors need to be much more explicit about what's new here versus what's borrowed from prior literature. Platt scaling, temperature scaling, and verbalized confidence approaches all exist — where does this sit relative to those?

The experimental setup also raises questions. Which specific LLMs were tested, at what sizes, with what versions? I found this information scattered and inconsistently reported. The dataset description feels thin — how was ground truth established? Inter-annotator agreement? This matters enormously for a sensitive topic like sexism where annotation is notoriously noisy.

I'm also a bit troubled by the title, honestly. "We are confident in trusting Language Models... but are they good?" reads like clickbait and the rhetorical setup isn't really paid off analytically. The conclusion that there's "no clear advantage" of LLMs is stated somewhat flatly without digging into *why* — is it the domain mismatch? Ambiguity in the labels? Political context confusing the models with partisan signals? These are the interesting questions and they're largely left unexamined.

The writing needs work throughout. There are grammatical issues, some sentences are genuinely hard to parse, and the related work section reads like a list rather than a motivated narrative.

I might be wrong about the confidence algorithm being unoriginal — maybe I'm missing something in the presentation — but that's partly the authors' fault for not being clearer. The core ideas here aren't without merit, but the paper needs substantially more rigor, cleaner experimental reporting, and a sharper argument for what we actually learn. Not ready in current form.

This paper tackles an important and timely problem — automatic construction of hallucination datasets for LLMs — and I appreciate the motivation. The manual annotation bottleneck is real, and I've seen too many papers in this space that either rely on small hand-labeled sets or just repurpose existing NLI benchmarks in questionable ways. So the direction is right.

That said, I have significant concerns about this work in its current form.

The core technical contribution (AutoHall itself) is, frankly, underwhelming when you dig into the details. The method essentially prompts LLMs with claims derived from fact-checking datasets and then uses consistency checks to identify hallucinations. I might be missing something, but this feels incremental — similar ideas appear in work on self-consistency (Wang et al., 2023) and in several hallucination detection papers from late 2023. The authors need to more clearly articulate what is genuinely novel here versus what is a straightforward application of existing techniques. The related work section doesn't do enough work to position this contribution properly.

On the experiments: Table 2 shows some promising numbers, but I found the baseline selection confusing. Why these specific baselines? After reading this twice, I still couldn't determine whether the comparisons are truly apples-to-apples. The zero-resource setting is appealing in principle, but the black-box self-contradiction approach seems to require multiple queries per instance — the computational cost analysis is basically absent, and in my experience this matters enormously for practical deployment.

The claim about "model-specific" datasets is interesting but I think undersold AND underexamined. The paper gestures at variations across models (the last sentence of the abstract) but the analysis in Section 4.3 feels rushed. I wanted to see a much deeper investigation here — this is actually the most interesting empirical finding and it gets maybe two paragraphs.

Some smaller issues: the writing needs work throughout. There are awkward constructions on page 3 and page 6 that made me stop and re-read. Figure 1 is genuinely helpful for understanding the pipeline, but Figure 3 is cluttered and hard to parse. The "zero-resource" framing also bothers me slightly — you still need the fact-checking datasets as seed data, so the resource requirements aren't zero, they're just different. Be more precise about this.

I'm not entirely sure the hallucination detection method and the dataset construction method belong in the same paper — they feel like two separate contributions somewhat awkwardly stitched together, with neither getting the full treatment it deserves.

Overall I lean toward rejection, but I want to be clear: the problem is important and there are seeds of interesting ideas here. The authors should substantially expand the analysis of model-specific hallucination patterns, tighten the technical novelty claims, and consider whether the scope needs to be narrowed. With major revisions this could be a solid contribution.

This paper tackles one of the most important open problems in deep learning — why do overparameterized networks generalize? — and proposes what the authors call the "Coherent Gradients" hypothesis. The core idea is intuitive: gradients from similar training examples point in similar directions and therefore reinforce each other, biasing optimization toward solutions that generalize. I've thought about this problem a lot, and I found the framing genuinely interesting on first read.

That said, I have significant concerns about the depth of the theoretical and empirical support provided.

The heuristic arguments in Section 3 are, well, heuristic. I don't think that's necessarily fatal, but the paper sometimes presents these arguments with more confidence than is warranted. The transition from "gradients from similar examples are similar" to "therefore gradient descent finds generalizing solutions" skips over a lot. In particular, the argument seems to implicitly assume some structure in the data distribution that isn't made precise. What counts as "similar" examples? The paper gestures at this but never formalizes it, which makes the hypothesis somewhat unfalsifiable as stated. I might be wrong, but I think without at least a toy theoretical model, the core claim remains more of an intuition than a hypothesis.

The perturbative experiments are more interesting to me, actually. The random label experiments (following Zhang et al. 2017, which the authors cite) do provide some evidence, and I appreciated Figure 3 showing gradient coherence scores on real vs. random data. But after reading this twice, I'm still not convinced the metric being used is doing what the authors claim. The coherence score as defined in equation (4) — I'm paraphrasing — seems like it could be high for reasons unrelated to generalization. Has this been ablated carefully?

The prescriptive part of the paper — the proposed modification to gradient descent — is actually what I found most compelling and also most underexplored. The results in Table 2 show meaningful reductions in overfitting on CIFAR, but the comparison baselines feel thin. How does this compare to dropout, or weight decay, or modern variants of SAM? These are obvious comparisons that are missing.

My overall feeling is that this paper is onto something real. The coherent gradients framing is a useful lens and I've already found myself thinking about it in relation to other work on loss landscape geometry and implicit bias. But as currently written, it sits awkwardly between a position paper and an empirical paper without fully succeeding as either. The theoretical story needs more rigor, and the experiments need more careful design and comparison.

I lean toward rejection in the current form, but I'd genuinely encourage the authors to revise and resubmit. This could be a strong paper. Clean up the theoretical claims, be more explicit about what would falsify the hypothesis, and run the missing baselines.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely problem — detecting LDM-generated images — and the core idea is genuinely clever: instead of training on real vs. full diffusion-generated images, train on real vs. autoencoder-reconstructed images to isolate decoder artifacts. I appreciate the simplicity and the motivation is sound. That said, I have some significant concerns about the execution and the claims being made.

The central argument (that dataset misalignment causes detectors to latch onto spurious correlations) is well-motivated, and the autoencoder reconstruction idea neatly sidesteps both the computational cost of running full denoising chains AND the semantic/resolution mismatches. I found the texture/frequency analysis in Figure 3 somewhat convincing, though I wish the authors had done more rigorous quantitative analysis here rather than relying on visual inspection.

My biggest concern is the experimental scope. The comparisons feel thin. After reading this twice, I'm still not confident the gains hold up broadly — Table 2 shows improvements in some settings but the margins are modest in several cases, and I'm not sure the statistical significance is ever addressed. Real reviewers at top venues expect this. Also, how does this method perform against detectors specifically trained to be robust (e.g., methods that use augmentation or domain randomization during training)? The comparison to CNNDetect feels a bit like picking a soft target at this point.

The claim about "spurious correlations" is interesting but I think undersupported. The authors show their method is less susceptible, but the mechanism isn't fully characterized. Is it the alignment, the removal of semantic content variation, the fact you're training on reconstruction artifacts specifically? I'm not entirely sure the paper disentangles these factors cleanly. An ablation isolating each would strengthen this considerably.

The section on non-natural images (textures/patterns, if I'm reading correctly) is intriguing but honestly feels like it's padding out the paper. The results are described as "promising" which is basically academic for "not conclusive." I'd either cut this or develop it into something more substantial.

Minor things: the writing is generally clear but section 4 gets a bit dense. Equation 1 is fine but almost trivially simple — I wonder if it needs to be an equation at all. The related work is adequate though I'd have liked to see engagement with some of the more recent generalization-focused detection work from 2023-2024.

In my experience, the best papers in this space not only identify a problem but provide deep insight into WHY it exists. This paper does the former reasonably well but the latter less so. The idea is good enough that I think it merits publication somewhere, but in its current form I lean toward weak reject — borderline. The authors need to significantly strengthen the experimental validation and the mechanistic analysis before this is ready. Not a bad paper. Just not quite there yet.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — estimating controllability of networked dynamical systems when you only have access to coarse (community-level) structural information rather than the full adjacency matrix. The motivation is reasonable and the problem setup is clearly inspired by real applications like brain networks and power systems.

That said, I have significant concerns about the contribution and its framing.

The core technical approach leans heavily on the stochastic block model, which is fine, but the assumptions required to make the bounds work seem quite strong in practice. Specifically, the concentration results depend on the SBM in ways that I think limit applicability to the real-world systems the authors keep invoking. Brain networks, for instance, are notoriously not well-described by homogeneous SBMs — there's substantial heterogeneity in degree and community structure. The authors acknowledge this briefly but I don't think they adequately address it. I found myself unconvinced after reading Section 4 twice that these bounds would be informative in any realistic regime.

The average controllability metric is a reasonable choice, but it's also a somewhat blunt instrument. It's well known (Pasqualetti et al. and others) that average controllability can give misleading pictures of actual steering capability, especially for high-dimensional target states. The paper doesn't really engage with this limitation.

On the positive side, I appreciate that the authors are trying to flip the model-order-reduction paradigm — going from coarse to fine rather than fine to coarse. That's genuinely a different angle. The connection to community detection literature is also nice in principle. And Figure 2 does help clarify the conceptual setup.

My main technical gripe is with Theorem 2 (page 11 I think — hard to tell with the formatting). The bound involves quantities that themselves depend on fine-scale structure, which creates a bit of a circular problem. If I knew those quantities, why would I be in the "coarsely characterized" regime in the first place? I might be wrong about this and maybe I'm missing something, but the authors need to address this much more directly.

The experiments feel thin. Two synthetic examples on SBM-generated graphs doesn't convince me the approach generalizes. Where are the real-world experiments? The authors mention brain networks in the abstract but never actually test on neural data.

Writing is generally clear though occasionally dense. The related work section is adequate but misses some recent work on approximate controllability under model uncertainty.

Overall I think this is a potentially interesting direction that needs more work. The theory needs tightening (particularly around the circularity issue I mentioned), the experiments need to be substantially expanded with real data, and the gap between the theoretical assumptions and stated applications needs honest discussion. I lean toward rejection in current form, but I'd be willing to reconsider with major revisions addressing these points.

This paper presents xCodeEval, a large-scale multilingual benchmark for code-related tasks with execution-based evaluation. The scale is genuinely impressive — 25M examples, 11 languages, 7 tasks — and I think the community does need something like this. The execution-based evaluation via ExecEval is the right approach; we've known for years that BLEU-based code evaluation is basically meaningless, so I appreciate that the authors are pushing in this direction.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The data splitting methodology (Section 4, around page 6-7) involving geometric mean and graph-theoretic principles sounds clever, but honestly after reading it twice I'm still not entirely sure I understand the motivation clearly enough to evaluate whether it actually solves the distribution balancing problem it claims to address. The description feels rushed. Figure 3 helps a bit but not enough. I'd want to see ablations showing what happens with naive splitting vs. their proposed approach — without that, I can't tell if this complexity is justified.

The benchmark construction relies heavily on competitive programming problems from existing online judges. This is fine and practical, but it does raise a real question about contamination that the paper doesn't address seriously enough. GPT-4 and other models have almost certainly been trained on CodeForces data. The reported numbers in Table 3 and 4 might therefore be inflated for OpenAI models specifically, which undermines some of the comparative analysis. I'm surprised this isn't discussed more carefully.

On the translation tasks specifically — I find it a bit odd that translation is evaluated only on whether the translated code passes the same test cases. That's execution correctness, which is good, but it conflates translation quality with general problem-solving ability. A model that re-solves the problem from scratch in the target language would score identically to one that actually translates. I might be wrong but I think this is a genuine measurement validity issue.

The ExecEval engine itself seems like a real contribution, and I'd argue it might actually be the more durable artifact here. But the paper somewhat undersells the engineering challenges involved in sandboxed multi-language execution. More details on reliability/reproducibility would strengthen this.

The experiments feel somewhat thin given the scale of the benchmark. Testing primarily on GPT-3.5, Codex, and a couple open-source models gives a snapshot, but the paper would benefit from more analysis of *why* models fail — is it language-specific, task-specific, problem complexity? Table 5 starts to do this but doesn't go deep enough.

Overall: I think this is useful work and the benchmark will likely be used by the community. But the paper itself has presentation issues, the contamination question is unaddressed, and some methodological choices need better justification. I lean toward acceptance but with moderate confidence — it needs revision.

This paper introduces the concept of "local elasticity" in neural networks — the idea that an SGD update at one point doesn't much affect predictions at dissimilar points. The phenomenon itself is interesting and I think there's something real being observed here. But I have significant concerns about the paper in its current form.

Let me start with what I found compelling. The simulation results do seem to consistently show this behavior across different architectures, and the connection to the neural tangent kernel is a natural one. Figure 3 in particular gives a reasonably intuitive picture of what's happening geometrically. I also appreciate that the authors test on both synthetic and real data.

That said, I have some serious issues. The formalization of local elasticity feels underspecified to me. The definition relies on "dissimilar in a certain sense" — but this vagueness doesn't really get resolved rigorously. What exactly counts as dissimilar? The paper gestures at this but I kept reading waiting for a crisp characterization that never fully arrived. After reading this twice I'm still not sure if local elasticity is being *defined* or just *observed*, and that ambiguity is frustrating in a paper that clearly wants to make a theoretical contribution.

The NTK interpretation in Section 4 feels somewhat tacked on. The connection is plausible but I'm not convinced it's doing the explanatory work the authors claim. The NTK regime applies to infinitely wide networks at initialization — how much does this really tell us about the finite, trained networks in the experiments? This gap between the theoretical framing and the empirical setup is never adequately addressed, and in my experience this is where these kinds of papers can fall apart under scrutiny.

The clustering application (Section 5) is interesting but honestly feels like a separate paper. The MNIST and CIFAR-10 results are decent but not dramatically better than existing methods. I think the authors are using this to *validate* local elasticity, but the logic feels a bit circular — they define a similarity measure based on a phenomenon they want to validate, then use clustering performance as evidence the phenomenon is real. I might be wrong about this but it bothered me.

On writing: some parts are genuinely clear, others are quite dense. Page 6-7 in particular lost me. The notation becomes heavy and I think some of those derivations could be moved to an appendix.

The broader discussion at the end about implications for generalization and memorization is speculative but I didn't mind it — at least the authors are upfront that it's speculative.

Bottom line: there's a genuinely interesting observation here and I think it's worth publishing somewhere, but the current version needs a much tighter theoretical treatment of what local elasticity actually *is*, and more honest discussion of the NTK gap. Not ready in current form.

This paper tackles an interesting and underexplored angle on spurious correlations — specifically, what happens when these correlations appear in only a tiny fraction of training examples. The privacy angle connecting rare spurious correlations to potential membership inference or data poisoning concerns is genuinely novel framing, and I appreciated that the authors tried to connect empirical observations to some theoretical grounding.

That said, I have significant concerns about the paper as currently written.

The experimental setup feels underpowered throughout. The authors demonstrate that networks pick up rare spurious patterns, which... honestly doesn't surprise me much? Neural networks are known to memorize individual training examples (see Feldman's long-tail memorization work, Carlini et al.'s memorization results), so the finding that a handful of poisoned/spurious examples get learned feels like it's rediscovering memorization under a different name. I think the paper needs to work harder to distinguish "rare spurious correlation learning" from plain memorization. This distinction is never cleanly made, and after reading the theory section twice I'm still not sure the authors have a crisp answer.

The theoretical analysis in Section 4 (I think?) relies on assumptions that feel quite strong and not well-motivated. I'd want to see more discussion of whether these assumptions hold in practice, or at least some ablation showing when the theory breaks down. As written, the theory feels somewhat disconnected from the empirical findings.

On the mitigation side — L2 regularization and Gaussian noise are both reasonable suggestions but these are extremely standard tools. The recommendation to add Gaussian noise in particular, I'm not entirely sure this is doing what the authors think it's doing. Is it actually suppressing the spurious feature, or just adding noise that incidentally hurts memorization of all rare patterns equally? This needs a more careful ablation. The paper would be considerably stronger if it could show that these mitigations specifically target the spurious correlation mechanism rather than just acting as generic regularizers.

Figure 3 (if I'm reading the right one) is quite hard to parse — the color scheme and axis labels need work. I had to read the caption three times.

The related work section is fine but notably omits some relevant work on shortcut learning (Geirhos et al.) and dataset artifacts that I would have expected to see cited.

I want to be clear: the core question this paper asks is genuinely important, especially the privacy implications. But the execution doesn't quite get there yet. The contributions feel incremental relative to the memorization literature, the theory needs tightening, and the mitigations are underwhelming. I'm leaning toward rejection but I think with significant revision this could be a solid paper at a workshop or potentially a main conference venue if the authors sharpen the distinction from memorization and strengthen the mitigation analysis considerably.

This paper proposes CapsGNN, a graph neural network that incorporates capsule networks (originally proposed by Sabour et al. for image classification) to generate graph-level embeddings. The core idea is to treat node features as capsules and use dynamic routing to aggregate them into graph-level representations. I think the motivation is reasonable — scalar aggregation methods do lose information — but I have significant concerns about both the technical contribution and the experimental evaluation.

Let me start with what I found genuinely interesting. The idea of using routing-by-agreement to aggregate node capsules into graph capsules is conceptually appealing, and the attention module for handling variable-size graphs addresses a real practical problem. Figure 2 gives a decent overview of the architecture.

That said, I have real issues here. The technical novelty feels thin to me. Essentially, the paper takes CapsNet (Sabour et al., 2017) and replaces the convolutional feature extraction with a GNN. After reading this twice, I'm not convinced this requires a full paper rather than a short note. The routing algorithm in Section 3.2 appears to be essentially identical to the original dynamic routing — what exactly is being adapted for the graph setting? The authors owe the reader a much clearer explanation of what changes were necessary and why they were non-trivial.

The experimental section (Section 4) is where I have the most complaints. The datasets used are mostly the standard graph kernel benchmarks (COLLAB, IMDB-B, etc.), which is fine, but the comparison baselines are somewhat dated. More importantly, the variance across runs is not consistently reported, and on several datasets the improvements over baselines are within what I'd expect from random seed variation. Table 2 shows CapsGNN winning on some benchmarks but the margins are modest and the statistical significance is never addressed. This is a serious omission.

I'm also not entirely sure the ablation study in Section 4.3 is convincing. They remove the attention module and routing separately, but the analysis feels superficial — I wanted to understand *why* certain components help on certain graph types. The discussion gestures at this but doesn't deliver.

The writing is functional but occasionally awkward, particularly in the abstract ("by virtue of the new instrument" is a strange phrase). Section 3 could be tightened considerably.

In my experience, papers combining two existing architectures need to work harder to justify the combination both theoretically and empirically. This paper doesn't quite get there. The idea has potential and I don't think it's wrong, but it reads like a first draft of a stronger paper. I'd want to see a more rigorous comparison with recent graph pooling methods (DiffPool, for instance) and a clearer theoretical or empirical story for when and why capsules help over standard aggregation. As it stands, I lean toward rejection but would reconsider with a substantial revision.

This paper tackles adversarial robustness through the lens of the manifold assumption, arguing that confident regions of well-trained models should be well-separated, and then using this intuition to build an embedding-based defense on top of Madry et al.'s PGD-trained model. I've spent a good chunk of time with this and have mixed feelings.

Let me start with what I think is genuinely interesting here. The "goodness property" formalization is a reasonable theoretical framing, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to connect the geometry of decision boundaries to confidence. The thought experiment motivating separated confident regions is intuitive. And the empirical observation about Madry et al.'s model — that it leaves low-confidence "uncertain" regions that are exploitable — is a real and underappreciated point that I haven't seen stated quite this clearly elsewhere.

That said, I have serious concerns about the defense itself. The core proposal is to embed uncertain points back to the nearest confident region. But this is essentially a form of input preprocessing/purification, and we know from a large body of work (Athalye et al. 2018, Carlini & Wagner) that such defenses are notoriously vulnerable to adaptive attacks where the adversary accounts for the preprocessing step. I don't see anywhere in the paper where the authors seriously evaluate against an adaptive adversary who is aware of the embedding procedure. The experiments seem to evaluate the combined system against attacks designed for the base model alone. This is a fundamental flaw, not a minor weakness. After reading this twice I kept looking for the adaptive attack experiments and they're simply not there.

The theoretical results feel somewhat loose to me. The formalizations of the goodness property (Section 3, around equations 3-5 if I recall correctly) involve several parameters that are hard to interpret or verify in practice. I'm not entirely sure the formal version adds much beyond the informal intuition. It's not clear these conditions are checkable for real networks.

On the empirical side — Table 2 shows "almost perfect success rate" defending against attacks the base model fails on, but I'm skeptical this holds against white-box attacks on the full pipeline. The experimental setup on MNIST and CIFAR-10 is fine but feels dated at this point, and the threat model needs to be stated more precisely upfront.

I also found the writing in places a bit meandering, particularly in Section 4. The connection between the theoretical goodness property and the actual embedding algorithm could be much tighter.

To be blunt: the high-level idea is interesting enough to publish somewhere, but the missing adaptive attack analysis is a dealbreaker for a top venue. This is not a minor oversight — it's the central question any knowledgeable reader will ask immediately. I'd encourage the authors to run the embedding defense against attacks that differentiate through (or at least approximate) the full defense pipeline before resubmitting.

This paper proposes LODAC, an algorithm combining latent space representation learning with distributional RL for offline settings, targeting risk-sensitive objectives (specifically CVaR). The core theoretical contribution is showing equivalence between minimizing a risk measure in latent vs. natural space under certain assumptions. I appreciate the ambition here — combining offline RL, distributional RL, and representation learning into a coherent framework is genuinely non-trivial.

That said, I have significant concerns after reading this twice.

On the theory: the equivalence result (Section 3, around the main proposition) feels undercooked. The assumptions required are stated but I found myself wanting much more discussion of when these assumptions actually hold in practice. In my experience with distributional RL papers, these kinds of "equivalent under assumptions" results can be technically correct but practically vacuous if the assumptions are rarely satisfied. The authors should be more upfront about this gap. Specifically, what does it mean for the encoder to be "sufficiently expressive" in the stochastic reward case? I'm not entirely sure the proof handles the stochastic reward setting as cleanly as claimed.

The empirical evaluation is where I have the most complaints. The environments used are... not very convincing. I'd want to see evaluation on more standard benchmarks — D4RL comes to mind — even if the authors need to add synthetic stochasticity. The current set of environments feels hand-picked to favor the method. Table 2 shows improvements but the variance across seeds is concerning, and I'm not confident the differences are statistically significant. How many seeds were used? I couldn't find this clearly stated.

The comparison to baselines also needs work. Why isn't OPAL or similar latent-space offline RL methods included as baselines? The paper compares against methods that don't operate in latent space at all, which makes the comparison somewhat unfair in the method's favor. You're conflating two improvements (latent space + distributional) and attributing all gains to your method.

I also found Figure 3 confusing — the caption doesn't explain what the shaded regions represent.

The writing needs polish. There are grammatical errors throughout ("in the top of," "the past few years, algorithms have been introduced"), and the abstract itself has issues that should have been caught before submission.

Look, I think there's a real paper here. The combination of offline + distributional + latent is timely and the theoretical angle is interesting. But right now it reads like a first draft that needed another few months. The theory needs stronger justification of assumptions, the experiments need more baselines and statistical rigor, and the writing needs a serious pass. I'm leaning reject but would genuinely reconsider with a stronger rebuttal addressing the baseline comparison and statistical significance questions.

This paper proposes AdaLoRA, an extension of LoRA that adaptively allocates parameter budgets across weight matrices using an SVD-based parameterization. The core idea is intuitive — not all weight matrices deserve equal parameter allocation, and importance-based pruning of singular values is a reasonable way to operationalize this. I've read a fair amount of the PEFT literature and this sits comfortably in a lineage of work on structured pruning and adaptive rank selection.

That said, I have some real concerns.

The importance score formulation (Equation 4, if I'm reading correctly) feels somewhat ad hoc. The authors use a combination of gradient-weight products to approximate sensitivity, which is borrowed pretty directly from prior pruning literature. I'm not entirely sure the theoretical justification for applying this specifically to singular values is as clean as the paper implies. The authors gesture at this in Section 3.2 but don't fully close the argument. I found this section confusing even after reading it twice.

The experimental results are generally convincing. The GLUE benchmark numbers in Table 1 look solid, and I appreciate that they include low-budget regimes where the method shines brightest — that's an honest way to present results. The QA experiments are also a nice addition. However, I'm bothered by the comparison setup. The LoRA baseline uses uniform rank allocation, so of course adaptive allocation wins. The more interesting question is: how does this compare to simply doing a cheap sensitivity analysis first and then running standard LoRA with non-uniform ranks? That ablation is missing and I think it's important.

The SVD parameterization trick to avoid exact SVD computations is clever and I give the authors credit for that engineering insight. But the orthogonality regularization introduced to keep the decomposition well-behaved (Section 3.3) adds a hyperparameter that the paper somewhat glosses over. In my experience, these kinds of auxiliary losses can be finicky and the paper doesn't really address sensitivity to that regularization weight λ.

Minor things: Figure 2 is actually pretty helpful for building intuition, good choice. The writing is generally clear though there are a few spots in Section 4 where the experimental setup descriptions are rushed. Also, I'd push back slightly on some of the framing in the introduction — claiming this "bridges the gap" is a bit grandiose given that adaptive rank methods have been explored before (DiffPruning, etc.).

Overall I think this is a solid, publishable contribution. The idea works, the experiments are reasonably thorough, and the SVD framing is genuinely novel in this context. But the missing ablation and the somewhat hand-wavy theoretical motivation keep me from being fully enthusiastic. I lean toward acceptance but the authors need to address the comparison fairness question seriously in the revision.

This paper tackles an interesting and somewhat underexplored problem: how to build point cloud networks that are equivariant with respect to *local* symmetries when the partition defining those local regions is itself unknown and must be predicted. The core idea — that equivariance w.r.t. a finer partition implies equivariance w.r.t. a coarser true partition — is clean and I think genuinely useful as a theoretical anchor.

That said, I have real concerns about the paper as submitted.

The theoretical framework feels solid at a high level, but I found the presentation of the bounds (Section 4, around equations 6-9 if I'm reading the numbering right) quite dense. After reading this section twice I'm still not entirely sure how tight these bounds are in practice. The authors acknowledge the bounds depend on "uncertainty quantification of partition prediction" and "probability of failing to suggest a proper subpartition," but neither of these quantities seems easy to compute reliably in real applications. How sensitive are the downstream performance guarantees to errors in estimating these quantities? This feels like the most important practical question and it's somewhat glossed over.

The experimental results are... fine. Table 2 shows improvements on the articulated object benchmarks, but the margins over baselines are modest in several cases, and I'm not convinced the ablations fully isolate what's driving the gains. Is it the equivariance framework itself, or just the compositional partition prediction architecture? The paper would be stronger if this were disentangled more carefully. Also, I'd want to see more discussion of failure cases — the method presumably degrades when the partition prediction is very wrong, and I'd like to understand how gracefully.

I'm also a bit skeptical about the claim of being a "general framework." In practice it seems fairly tailored to specific choices of partition predictor and the two data types evaluated. This isn't necessarily a flaw but the generality claims in the abstract feel slightly overclaimed relative to what's demonstrated.

On the positive side: the motivation is clear, the problem is real, and the writing is generally good. The idea of starting with a fine partition and coarsening is elegant and I think the community will find it useful. Figure 3 is helpful for building intuition.

Minor things: some notation in Section 3 is introduced before it's fully defined, which is mildly annoying. And the related work section could engage more seriously with recent work on frame averaging and stochastic equivariance — these feel like relevant points of comparison that are somewhat underaddressed.

Overall I'm leaning weak reject, though I could be convinced otherwise. The core contribution is real but the paper needs stronger empirical validation and clearer exposition of when and how the theoretical bounds translate into practical guarantees. I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit.

This paper tackles instance-dependent noise (IDN) in noisy label learning, which is indeed an important and underexplored direction compared to the more commonly studied symmetric/asymmetric noise settings. The core idea — distinguishing "hard clean" samples from noisy ones via anchor hallucination — is intuitive and the motivation is clear enough.

That said, I have some real concerns here.

The anchor hallucination mechanism (Section 3.2 if I'm reading the structure right) is the paper's main technical contribution, but I found the description surprisingly vague on first and second read. Specifically, how exactly are anchors "hallucinated" from the easy subset? The word "hallucinate" suggests something generative, but the actual procedure seems more like interpolation or feature mixing — which isn't particularly novel. I kept waiting for a crisp formalization and it never quite arrived. Equations 3-5 need more explanation of what's actually happening geometrically or statistically.

The experimental results look strong on CIFAR-N and the synthetic IDN benchmarks (Tables 1-2), and I appreciate that the authors tested on real-world noise. But I'm skeptical about some of the comparisons. Several strong baselines from 2022-2023 seem to be missing or underperforming in ways that don't match what I've seen in other papers. DivideMix numbers in particular look lower than what's reported in recent follow-up work I've read. This could be a hyperparameter issue or different data splits, but the authors need to be more transparent about experimental setup.

The ablation in Table 3 is helpful but incomplete. I'd really want to see an ablation that isolates the hallucination step specifically — what happens if you just use the easy/hard split without the anchor-based selection? Without this, it's hard to know how much of the gain comes from the partition strategy versus the anchor mechanism itself.

My other concern is the semi-supervised training component, which feels somewhat bolted on. The paper frames it as a contribution but honestly this is standard practice in NLL by now (DivideMix, etc.) and I don't think this part adds much novelty. The authors should be clearer about what's genuinely new versus what's inherited from prior work.

Figure 2 is decent but I'd push back on the t-SNE visualizations — in my experience these can be cherry-picked to look convincing and they don't substitute for quantitative analysis of the subset quality.

I might be wrong about the novelty assessment here, and the problem setting is genuinely relevant. But right now the paper reads like the technical contribution is thinner than the performance gains suggest, and I'm not fully convinced the gains are reliable across fair comparisons. The writing is clear enough though, and the motivation section is well done.

Overall: borderline. I'd want the authors to clarify the hallucination mechanism, fix the baseline comparisons, and strengthen the ablations before I'd feel comfortable recommending acceptance.

This paper tackles an interesting theoretical question about depth requirements for in-context learning with diverse tasks, and I think the core contribution — establishing log(κ) lower bounds on the number of layers needed and connecting this to looped transformers — is genuinely novel and worth publishing in some form. That said, I have some significant concerns.

Let me start with what I found compelling. The lower bound results feel right to me intuitively, and the connection to condition numbers is clean. The observation that multilayer transformers can match these bounds but at the cost of robustness is the kind of insight that makes you stop and think. The Wasserstein distance characterization of fragility (O(e^{-L})) is striking — actually alarming if you think about what it implies for deep networks in practice.

However, I'm troubled by several things. First, the setup restricts to linear attention and linear regression throughout. I understand why — it makes things tractable — but the authors need to be much more upfront about whether any of this tells us something about real transformers doing real ICL, or whether it's a pleasant theoretical sandbox. After reading this twice, I still felt the gap between theory and practice was hand-waved. The related work discussion on page 3-4 doesn't adequately address competing explanations for why depth helps.

The robustness result in particular needs more unpacking. The claim that distributional shifts of O(e^{-L}) break multilayer transformers sounds dramatic, but this bound gets vanishingly small as L grows — so deeper networks become MORE fragile? I found this confusing on first read and I'm not entirely sure I followed the argument correctly even after going through it carefully. The proof in the appendix (around Theorem 4) could use a cleaner proof sketch in the main text.

The looped transformer results are interesting but I want to push back on the framing. The authors present looping as a near-perfect solution but the empirical validation is thin. Figure 3 shows the monotonic loss behavior but the experimental setup feels somewhat constructed to confirm the theory rather than stress-test it. What happens with actual language model tasks?

Minor issues: there's a duplicate "(1)" in the abstract that should be caught before submission. Some of the notation in Section 3 is introduced casually and I had to flip back multiple times.

In my experience, papers that prove lower bounds AND matching upper bounds AND robustness results in one go often end up doing none of them with full rigor. I'd encourage the authors to perhaps trim scope slightly and strengthen the core arguments. As it stands I'm leaning toward weak accept — the ideas are good enough — but the presentation needs work and the empirical section needs to either be strengthened substantially or cut in favor of more careful theoretical exposition.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — extending video-text retrieval beyond single-event queries to handle multi-event scenarios while simultaneously performing temporal grounding. The motivation is reasonable and I don't dispute that the problem is worth solving.

That said, I have significant concerns about this submission.

The writing quality is honestly quite poor throughout. The abstract cuts off mid-sentence ("The innovation of th...") which is a basic submission error that makes me wonder how carefully this was proofread. More broadly, the description of the method in Sections 3 and 4 is dense to the point of being nearly impenetrable. After reading the method section twice I still had trouble understanding exactly what the "Relational Event-Centric Video-Text Retrieval" module is doing differently from, say, a standard hierarchical contrastive approach. The figures (particularly Figure 3) are doing a lot of heavy lifting but aren't explained carefully enough in the caption or surrounding text.

The technical contributions feel somewhat incremental to me. The inter/intra video disentanglement idea is fine but not particularly surprising — this kind of hierarchical relationship modeling has been explored in works like QD-DETR, UniVTG, and several others in the moment retrieval space. I'm not convinced the paper adequately distinguishes itself. The "divergence learning of event locations" described for grounding — I might be wrong but this seems to be essentially a standard contrastive temporal loss with some relabeling? The paper needs to be much more explicit about what is genuinely novel here versus what is being adapted from prior work.

On the experiments: results on ActivityNet-Captions and Charades-STA look competitive, but I'm skeptical of the evaluation setup. Charades-STA is primarily a single-event grounding benchmark — using it to validate a *multi-event* method feels like a mismatch. The authors should explain this choice more carefully or ideally evaluate on datasets that more naturally support multi-event queries. The ablation study in Table 4 is helpful but the gain from individual components is sometimes quite small (e.g., 0.3-0.5 R@1 points), which makes me uncertain whether these are real improvements or noise.

I also think the paper undersells the baseline comparison. Where is the comparison against more recent strong baselines from 2023? In my experience, papers in this space often cherry-pick the comparison set.

The core idea has merit and the problem setup is genuinely underexplored. But the execution — writing, clarity, experimental design, and articulation of novelty — needs substantial work before this is ready. I lean toward rejection in the current state, though I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit. Fixing the truncated abstract alone would be a start.

**Score: 4 (Reject)**

This paper proposes having a large cloud-side model generate LoRA parameters for a smaller edge-side model based on task descriptions, then merging those parameters for efficient inference. The core idea is interesting and I can see the practical motivation — edge deployment is a real constraint and there's something appealing about leveraging a larger model's knowledge to specialize a smaller one on the fly.

That said, I have pretty significant concerns after reading this carefully.

The comparison to "conventional LoRA fine-tuning" feels like a strawman to me. Standard LoRA fine-tuning requires training data and gradient updates — LoRA-Gen is doing something more like few-shot or zero-shot adaptation from task descriptions. These are solving different problems! The speedup and accuracy comparisons in Table 2 and Table 3 need much more careful framing. What exactly is being compared? Is the LoRA fine-tuning baseline trained on the same number of examples the description is derived from? This is never made fully clear, and I found this confusing after reading the experimental section twice.

The 2.1x speedup claim in the abstract — this is almost entirely coming from reduced context length, right? That's a somewhat trivial benefit of removing the in-context examples from the prompt, not really a property of the LoRA generation mechanism itself. Any method that compresses demonstrations into model weights would get this speedup. I think the paper would be stronger if it was honest about this rather than presenting it as a core contribution.

I'm also not entirely sure the "online" aspect is well-motivated. The latency of calling a large cloud-side model to generate LoRA weights seems like it could easily dominate any inference savings, especially for edge scenarios. There's no latency analysis for the generation step itself, which feels like a serious omission for a paper targeting edge deployment.

The commonsense reasoning experiments are fine but honestly not that exciting. These benchmarks (HellaSwag, PIQA, etc.) are pretty saturated and the gains are modest. The agent task compression results are more compelling to me — 10.1x context compression is actually impressive if it holds up, but the evaluation feels thin. How robust is this across different task types?

The related work section doesn't engage seriously with hypernetwork literature, which this work is clearly related to. Ha et al.'s hypernetworks and more recent work on generating adapter weights should be discussed more thoroughly.

Minor things: Figure 2 is helpful but the caption is too terse. Some notation in Section 3 (around equation 3-4) is sloppy and I had to re-read it multiple times.

Overall: the idea has merit and I think there's a publishable paper here, but the current version oversells results through misleading comparisons and undersells the actual interesting contributions (context compression, knowledge distillation angle). I'd want to see a major revision with clearer baselines and more honest framing before accepting this.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — the tension between OOD detection and OOD generalization — which I agree is underexplored and practically important. The framing of a "Detection-Generalization Paradox" is catchy, and I can see why the authors found it worth investigating. That said, after reading this twice, I have significant concerns about both the technical contributions and the presentation.

My biggest issue is with the novelty of DR-SAM itself. SAM and its variants have been extensively studied (Foret et al., 2021 and many followups), and the proposed modification feels incremental. The paper doesn't do enough work to convince me that the specific design choices in DR-SAM are principled rather than empirically tuned. Equation 3 in particular — the perturbation term that supposedly balances detection vs. generalization — is introduced somewhat informally, and I found myself wanting a cleaner theoretical motivation. Why does this specific formulation break the paradox? The hand-wavy argument in section 3.2 isn't convincing.

The empirical analysis in Section 2 is actually the most interesting part of the paper, and I think the authors undersell it. The observations about how representations behave differently under covariate vs. semantic shift are genuinely useful. But the figures (particularly Figure 2 and Figure 3) are hard to read — the color scheme is poor and the caption text is too small. I had to zoom in significantly, which is annoying.

On the experiments: the benchmarks chosen are reasonable but I'm not entirely sure the comparison baselines are up to date. Some of the OOD detection baselines look like they're from 2021-2022 vintage, and there are more recent methods they should be comparing against. The improvements reported in Table 2 are modest in several settings — sometimes within what I'd expect from variance — and I don't see error bars or statistical significance anywhere. This is a real problem. In my experience, OOD benchmarks can be quite noisy and a 0.5% improvement means very little without proper uncertainty quantification.

Also, the writing needs work. "generalizationability" in the abstract is a typo/portmanteau that should have been caught. More broadly, some paragraphs in the related work section read like they were written hastily — the transitions are abrupt and a few citations feel dropped in without real engagement.

I want to be fair: the problem is well-motivated, and the diagnostic analysis is worthwhile. But the core contribution (DR-SAM) needs stronger justification, the experiments need error bars and fresher baselines, and the writing needs a serious pass. I'm not convinced this is ready in its current form. I'd encourage the authors to either strengthen the theoretical grounding of DR-SAM substantially, or lean harder into the empirical analysis as the primary contribution and reframe accordingly.

Leaning toward rejection currently, but I'd genuinely reconsider with major revisions.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — the question of how to automatically grow neural network architectures during training to avoid expressivity bottlenecks. The core idea, extracting architectural change signals from backpropagation, is conceptually appealing and the paper attempts to provide a mathematically grounded framework for this.

That said, I have significant concerns about the scope and novelty of the contribution as presented.

Let me start with what I think works. The theoretical framing around functional gradients and expressivity bottlenecks is reasonably clear, and I appreciated the attempt to connect the neuron-adding criterion to something principled rather than heuristic. Section 3 in particular has some nice ideas, though I found the notation in equations (4)-(7) a bit dense and had to read it twice to follow the argument. I'm also genuinely uncertain whether the projection-based argument fully holds in the nonlinear regime — the paper seems to implicitly rely on local linearization at several points without fully acknowledging this.

My main issue is the experimental evaluation. MNIST in 2024 is just not convincing. Full stop. The authors describe this explicitly as a "proof of concept" which I understand, but for a venue like this, we need to see whether the method scales. Does it work on CIFAR-10? ImageNet? Any regression benchmark? Any modern architecture beyond vanilla MLPs? The comparison in Table 2 is against fairly weak baselines, and I don't see comparisons against other network growing methods like Net2Net, progressive training approaches, or more recent work on dynamic architectures. This literature is not small — Evci et al., Bellec et al., and several others come to mind — and the related work section feels thin on engagement with these.

The claimed benefit of "removing the need for hyperparameter optimization" is also a bit overstated. The method itself introduces new hyperparameters (thresholds for bottleneck detection, how many neurons to add, etc.), and I didn't see a sensitivity analysis for these. In my experience, these kinds of threshold-based growing methods can be quite brittle.

I'm also not entirely sure the training time comparison is fair. Adding neurons mid-training introduces overhead that may not be fully accounted for in the wall-clock comparisons shown in Figure 4.

I want to be fair here — there's a genuine idea in this paper and the theory section has real effort behind it. But as currently presented, this reads more like a workshop paper or technical report than a full conference submission. The gap between the theoretical ambition of the framework and the narrow experimental validation is too large. I'd encourage the authors to substantially broaden the experiments before resubmission. The core contribution might be solid but right now I can't tell because it's only tested on one easy dataset.

Leaning toward rejection, but would reconsider with significantly expanded experiments.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically important problem — automating formal proof generation for Rust code when training data is scarce. The self-evolution loop idea (synthesize data, verify with symbolic checker, fine-tune, repeat) is clean and I appreciate that the authors are leveraging the verifier as an oracle rather than treating this as a pure generation problem. That's the right instinct.

That said, I have some significant concerns I'd like the authors to address.

The performance numbers are striking — 52.52% vs GPT-4o's 14.39% is a big gap, and I'll be honest, my first reaction was skepticism. After reading Section 4 twice I'm still not entirely clear on whether the benchmark used for evaluation has any overlap with the synthesized training data. The authors say the benchmark is "crafted by human experts" but I didn't find a sufficiently detailed description of how they ensured no data contamination. This needs to be addressed much more carefully. In my experience, this is exactly where self-training approaches can quietly overfit.

The self-debugging component (Section 3.3 if I'm reading the structure right) is interesting but I think underevaluated. The ablation in Table 3 shows it helps, but by how much exactly in different difficulty tiers? I'd want to see a breakdown. Also, the framing of "re-purposing incorrect proofs" is a bit oversold — this is essentially training on error-correction pairs, which others have done in code generation contexts (Self-Repair, etc.). The connection to prior work here feels underdeveloped.

I'm also mildly bothered by the comparison baseline. GPT-4o with zero-shot or few-shot prompting versus a fine-tuned model specifically trained on this task distribution... I mean, is this really a fair fight? I think it probably is directionally useful as a comparison, but the paper would be stronger if it included a fine-tuned GPT-4o or at least a stronger prompted baseline with chain-of-thought.

The Verus-specific aspects are interesting but also limiting. I'm not entirely sure how well these findings generalize beyond Verus to other verification frameworks like Dafny or F*, and the paper doesn't really engage with this question. Is something fundamental here or is this a Verus-shaped solution?

Minor things: Figure 2 is helpful but a bit cluttered. The writing in the introduction is occasionally redundant — the phrase "definitive assurance" appears multiple times. Page 7 has a run-on paragraph that should be broken up.

Overall I think this is solid work with a real contribution — the self-evolution cycle is well-motivated and the results are impressive if they hold up under scrutiny.
