My main concerns are around evaluation rigor and baseline fairness. I'd lean toward acceptance conditional on the authors providing clearer contamination analysis and stronger baselines discussion.

This paper tackles sparse-view 3D reconstruction by reframing it as a video generation problem, conditioning a pretrained video diffusion model on a global point cloud representation. The core idea is clever and I appreciate the motivation — leveraging video diffusion priors to hallucinate geometrically consistent novel views is a reasonable approach to the fundamental ill-posedness of sparse-view reconstruction.

That said, I have several concerns that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

The technical contribution feels a bit incremental to me. Conditioning diffusion models on point clouds for 3D-consistent generation has appeared in works like SyncDreamer, Zero123++, and various NeRF-diffusion hybrids. I spent some time after reading this trying to pin down exactly what's novel here versus prior art, and the paper doesn't do a great job drawing that distinction sharply. The "contextual space encoding" of the point cloud (Section 3.2 if I recall correctly) needs more justification — why this specific encoding and not alternatives?

The confidence-aware Gaussian Splatting scheme is interesting and I think actually the more technically novel piece here, but it feels somewhat buried. I'd actually like to see this get more attention in the paper, maybe even its own ablation that goes deeper.

On experiments: Table 1 shows strong numbers, but I'm skeptical of some of the baselines. Several of the compared methods weren't really designed for the exact same sparse-view setting, which inflates the apparent gains. The generalizability claims in the abstract feel a bit oversold based on what I see in the actual results. In my experience, when a method claims to work on "any scene," that claim requires significantly more diverse evaluation than what's presented here.

Figure 4 is genuinely impressive visually, I'll admit. But qualitative comparisons are easy to cherry-pick and the supplementary doesn't fully address this concern for me.

I'm also confused about the computational cost. How long does inference take? For a method that involves running a video diffusion model plus Gaussian Splatting optimization, I'd expect this to be quite slow. There's essentially no discussion of runtime, which is a significant omission for a practical reconstruction method.

One more thing — the writing could use another pass. Some parts of Section 4 are quite unclear, and I found myself rereading the description of the 3D structure condition encoding twice without fully understanding the dimensionality of the representations being used.

Overall: interesting direction, decent results, but the paper needs clearer positioning relative to prior work, better computational analysis, and frankly a more honest evaluation setup. I'm leaning toward weak reject right now, though I could be convinced otherwise if the authors can sharpen the novelty claims and address the experimental concerns. Not a bad paper — just not quite there yet.

This paper proposes a transformer-based architecture (Periodic Set Transformer) for crystal property prediction using the Pointwise Distance Distribution (PDD) as the structural representation. The core idea — adapting PDD, a provably continuous isometry invariant, as input to a set transformer — is interesting and theoretically motivated. I appreciate that the authors take the invariance/equivariance question seriously, which a lot of papers in this space frankly don't.

That said, I have a number of concerns that prevent me from recommending acceptance in the current form.

The empirical results are... underwhelming. Looking at Table 2, the proposed method doesn't consistently outperform baselines like CGCNN or MEGNet on the Materials Project benchmarks, and the margins where it does win are small. I've been reviewing papers in geometric deep learning for materials for several years now, and the community has moved on significantly — comparisons against DimeNet++, PaiNN, or more recent architectures like Equiformer are conspicuously absent. The authors justify this by claiming their method uses a fundamentally different representation, but that doesn't excuse the omission of competitive baselines. Readers need to know where this sits in the current landscape.

I found the description of the modified self-attention mechanism in Section 3.2 genuinely confusing. After reading it twice, I'm still not entirely clear how the spatial encoding integrates compositional information at the attention level vs. as a simple concatenation scheme. Equation 4 needs more explanation — specifically, what happens when two atoms have the same species but different local environments? I might be missing something, but the current writing doesn't make this clear.

The ablation in Table 3 is helpful and I think it's actually the most interesting part of the paper. It clearly shows the compositional encoding matters significantly. More analysis here would strengthen the paper considerably — for instance, does the model fail systematically on certain crystal symmetry groups?

One thing I genuinely liked: the theoretical grounding via PDD completeness properties is well-explained and the connection to the learning framework is tighter than I usually see in applied papers. The authors know this literature (Edelsbrunner, Widdowson et al.) and engage with it seriously.

Minor issues: Figure 3 is hard to read in grayscale, the Related Work section is thin for 2024 standards and misses several relevant works on SE(3)-equivariant networks, and there are grammatical issues throughout Section 4 that suggest rushed writing.

Overall, the theoretical motivation is solid but the experimental validation doesn't yet convince me this approach is competitive or that it offers practical advantages beyond the invariance guarantees. The paper needs stronger baselines, clearer architectural description, and frankly more datasets beyond Materials Project. I lean toward rejection but would genuinely reconsider with a strong rebuttal addressing the baseline comparison issue specifically.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — how to learn from singly-labeled noisy crowdsourced data. The motivation is clear and I appreciate that the authors frame it as a budget allocation question, which gives the work a nice unifying lens. That said, I have significant concerns about both the technical contributions and the empirical evaluation.

On the theory side, the generalization bound in Section 4 feels somewhat disconnected from the algorithm. After reading it twice, I'm not entirely sure the bound is tight enough to be practically informative — it seems to hide constants that could swamp the main effect in realistic settings. The threshold condition for "label many once vs. few multiply" is intuitive, but I'd want to see this threshold characterized more concretely. What does it actually evaluate to for typical AMT workers? The theory feels like it's gesturing at an answer rather than providing one.

The algorithm itself — alternating between estimating worker quality and updating the model — is reasonable but not obviously novel. This feels closely related to the EM-style approaches in Dawid-Skene and subsequent work (Raykar et al. 2010, Zhang et al. 2014), and the claimed advantage of working with single labels per example needs stronger justification. I might be wrong, but I think there are identifiability issues lurking here that aren't fully addressed. If you only have one label per example, how are you separating worker noise from genuine label ambiguity? The paper doesn't convince me this is solved.

The experiments are where I got most frustrated. The ImageNet experiments use *simulated* noisy workers, which is fine for controlled analysis but limits how much we can conclude. The MS-COCO results are more compelling, but Table 2 (I think it's Table 2?) shows pretty modest improvements over baselines in several conditions. The confidence intervals overlap in a few cases and I'd want significance tests. Also, the comparison set feels cherry-picked — there are several recent learning-with-noisy-labels methods (notably stuff from the 2021-2023 period on sample selection and meta-learning approaches) that aren't discussed or compared against.

Writing quality is generally okay but the abstract says "less multiply" which is awkward phrasing and suggests the paper could use another editing pass. Section 3's notation becomes unwieldy around equation 6-8.

In my experience reviewing crowdsourcing papers, the hardest part is always convincing the community that your method works better than just collecting more labels when budget allows, or using off-the-shelf robust loss functions. I don't think this paper fully clears that bar yet. There's a kernel of something interesting here, and the budget allocation framing is genuinely useful, but the current version needs stronger baselines, tighter theory, and more careful positioning relative to existing literature. I lean toward rejection but would be open to reconsidering with major revisions.

This paper tackles the modality imbalance problem in multimodal learning, which is a real and well-documented issue. The core idea — alternating between unimodal adaptation steps rather than jointly optimizing all modalities simultaneously — is reasonably intuitive, and I can see the appeal. But I have significant concerns about both the novelty framing and some technical claims.

The proposed MLA framework essentially combines three components: alternating unimodal updates, a shared prediction head, and a gradient modification mechanism to prevent catastrophic forgetting. After reading this twice, I found myself thinking: isn't this basically a variant of continual learning applied to multimodal settings? The gradient modification mechanism described around Section 3.2 bears a very strong resemblance to gradient projection / orthogonal gradient descent methods from the continual learning literature (e.g., Farajtabar et al., GEM, OGD). The paper doesn't engage seriously with this connection, which I think is a significant oversight. I'd want the authors to clearly differentiate their approach.

The experiments span five datasets which is good, and the missing modality results in Table 3 are actually the most convincing part of the paper to me. The gains there are meaningful. However, Table 2 shows some surprisingly thin margins on certain benchmarks — a few of those differences look within noise range, and I don't see statistical significance reported anywhere. That's a problem. In my experience, papers in this space tend to cherry-pick favorable comparisons, so I'd want error bars or significance tests before trusting these numbers.

I'm also confused by the test-time fusion mechanism. The uncertainty-based model fusion is described somewhat vaguely — how exactly is uncertainty estimated? If it's just prediction entropy, that's quite crude and has well-known failure modes when models are confidently wrong. The ablation in Table 4 does show this component matters, but the explanation is insufficient. Figure 3 helps a little but doesn't resolve my questions.

One thing that genuinely bothers me: the "alternating" process raises obvious questions about sensitivity to the alternation schedule and ordering. There's almost no analysis of this. What happens if you always start with the weaker modality? Does the ordering matter? I might be wrong but I suspect it does, and the absence of this analysis feels like a gap the authors should have caught.

The writing is generally clear, though Section 3.1 gets a bit dense with notation and could be streamlined.

Overall, I think there's a real paper here, but in its current form it reads more like an engineering contribution with modest novelty rather than a principled advance. The connection to continual learning needs to be addressed head-on, the experimental rigor needs improvement, and the uncertainty estimation deserves more careful treatment. I'm leaning toward rejection but would genuinely reconsider with substantial revisions.

This paper tackles single-image 3D reconstruction using a combination of 2D and 3D diffusion priors, which is certainly a relevant and active problem. After reading this twice, I have significant concerns about the work that I think need to be addressed before this could be accepted at a top venue.

My biggest issue is the vagueness throughout the paper. The "case-aware adaptive detection mechanism" is described as CLIP-based, but I genuinely struggled to understand what exactly is being detected or adapted. What are the "cases"? The abstract mentions "case-agnostic rigid strategy" as a weakness of prior work, but this framing is never made crisp. By page 4 I still didn't have a clear picture of what the detection mechanism outputs and how it feeds into the rest of the pipeline. This is a core contribution and it needs much more precise treatment.

The two-stage design (3D priors first, then 2D texture refinement) is intuitive and I don't think it's wrong, but it's also not particularly novel on its own. The paper needs to be clearer about what is technically new here versus what is standard practice in NeRF-based optimization pipelines. The "progressive" handoff between 3D and 2D guidance — how is this actually implemented? Is there a scheduling function? Equation 3 seems to gesture at this but I found it underspecified.

On the experiments: the quantitative results in Table 1 look favorable, but I'm skeptical. Which evaluation set is being used? The choice of test objects can dramatically affect PSNR/LPIPS numbers in this domain, and I've seen papers cherry-pick favorable subsets before. The comparison baselines — are they run with their official code and recommended hyperparameters? This needs explicit confirmation. Figure 5 shows nice qualitative results but these are clearly cherry-picked examples. I want to see failure cases. Where does the method break down?

I'm also not entirely convinced by the claim of "strong generalization ability." The examples shown are mostly clean, object-centric images with simple backgrounds. What happens with in-the-wild images? Cluttered backgrounds? Partial occlusions? These are the hard cases that actually matter.

The writing needs work. Phrases like "delicately sculpting the details" and "aligns more closely with the evolving trends" are marketing language, not scientific description. I found this mildly irritating throughout — just say what the method does.

To be fair, the core idea of separating geometric and texture optimization phases with adaptive prior weighting is reasonable, and if the technical details are as solid as the results suggest, there could be something here. But right now the paper reads more like a workshop paper than a full conference submission. The method section needs to be substantially tightened and made more precise, and the evaluation needs more rigorous analysis.

I lean toward rejection in the current form, but I'd genuinely encourage the authors to revise and resubmit with clearer technical exposition.

This paper tackles an important and timely question: are membership inference attacks on diffusion models actually as effective as the literature suggests? The short answer the authors arrive at is "no," and I think they make a reasonable case for this, though with some significant caveats I'll get to.

The core contribution — CopyMark — is essentially a benchmark that tries to fix what the authors identify as three main problems in existing MIA evaluation: (1) testing only on fine-tuned rather than pre-trained models, (2) biased dataset construction where members and non-members come from visually distinct distributions, and (3) overly convenient evaluation setups. These are legitimate criticisms. I've seen papers in this space and the distributional mismatch issue in particular has been an open secret — if your member images are LAION samples and your non-members are something visually different, any half-decent feature extractor will separate them without needing anything about the model at all.

That said, I found the paper's framing a bit overclaiming. The abstract says "we are the first to discover the performance overestimation of MIAs on diffusion models" but I'm not entirely sure this is accurate — there have been critical analyses of MIA evaluation methodology in the classification setting (Carlini et al.'s work comes to mind immediately) and at least some of this skepticism has appeared in diffusion-specific discussions. The novelty feels more incremental than revolutionary to me.

On the experimental side, Table 2 and Table 3 show the dramatic performance drop under CopyMark conditions, and this is genuinely striking. Near-random AUC for most methods is a strong result. However — and this bothered me after reading through the experimental setup twice — I wasn't fully convinced the benchmark construction is itself unimpeachable. How exactly are the non-member images sourced and matched to members? The distributional matching procedure described around Section 3.2 seems reasonable in principle but I worry the paper is trading one form of bias for another. If you over-correct the matching, you might be making the task artificially harder than it would be in realistic legal/audit scenarios.

The practical implications section is fine but somewhat hand-wavy. Saying MIA "is not reliable" is a strong claim and I think the paper would benefit from more nuance — under *what specific conditions* does it fail? Are there any signals that survive? The current framing feels a bit like throwing up hands.

Minor things: Figure 3 is hard to read at conference print size. Some notation in Section 4 is introduced without adequate explanation. The related work is decent but misses a few relevant papers on dataset inference as an alternative to MIA.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with an important message. The execution has some rough edges and the novelty claims are slightly inflated, but the core empirical finding is valuable for the community. I'd lean toward acceptance with revisions.

This paper presents CAAP, an LLM-based agent for GUI automation that operates purely from screenshots (converted to text) without requiring HTML access or application-specific APIs. The core contribution is the prompting strategy that encourages multi-perspective context examination during action planning.

I'll be honest — I went into this review somewhat skeptical, and came out with mixed feelings. The results on MiniWoB++ (94.5%) and WebShop (62.3) are genuinely impressive, especially given the constraint of using only visual input. That said, I think the paper oversells some of its novelty claims.

The biggest issue I keep coming back to is the evaluation. MiniWoB++ is, at this point, a fairly saturated benchmark, and the comparison set is oddly curated. Table 2 specifically — the authors cherry-pick comparisons against "screen-image only" methods, but this framing conveniently sidesteps comparisons with stronger HTML-access baselines that achieve higher numbers. I understand the point is to show you can compete without HTML, but the framing feels a bit slippery. Also, I'd want to know how sensitive these results are to the specific GPT-4V version used. These models get updated constantly and reproducibility is a real concern here.

The CAAP prompting strategy itself (described around Section 3.2) is the claimed technical contribution, but I found myself wanting more ablation. There IS an ablation in Table 4, which is good, but it doesn't fully disentangle which aspects of the "multiple perspectives" examination are actually doing the work. Is it the task decomposition? The state reflection? I genuinely couldn't tell from the experiments provided.

I'm also not entirely sure the screenshot-to-text conversion pipeline is treated with enough rigor. The OCR and element detection steps seem to do a lot of heavy lifting here, and errors in that stage could cascade significantly. The paper mentions this briefly but doesn't characterize failure modes systematically. After reading the failure analysis section twice, I still felt like this was underexplored.

On the positive side — the writing is generally clear, the motivation is solid, and the application to multi-app desktop/mobile scenarios is genuinely interesting. The WebShop results especially feel meaningful given how much harder that task is in practice.

My main recommendation: the paper needs stronger ablations on the CAAP components and a more honest positioning against non-image-only baselines (even if just to contextualize the gap). The reproducibility concern around specific LLM versions also needs addressing — maybe a section on this? In my experience, papers that rely heavily on closed API models without discussing version sensitivity tend to age very poorly.

Borderline accept for me. The empirical results are real and the approach is practically motivated, but the technical contribution feels thinner than advertised. I'd want to see the camera-ready address the ablation gaps before I'd feel fully comfortable.

This paper tackles an important and somewhat underappreciated problem — the gap between how continual learning is typically benchmarked and what "in the wild" online learning actually looks like. The core observation that most OCL benchmarks explicitly signal task boundaries is valid and worth addressing. I've seen this criticism floated in workshops for years and it's good to see someone take a proper swing at it.

That said, I have some real concerns about the execution.

The benchmark itself (CALM) is built around language modeling across languages and domains, which is a reasonable choice, but I found myself unconvinced that this is clearly *harder* or more realistic than existing benchmarks in ways the paper can actually demonstrate. The authors claim improved realism but the empirical comparison to, say, Split-CIFAR or similar setups feels thin. After reading the experimental section twice I still wasn't sure what the key takeaway was supposed to be — is CALM harder? More discriminative between methods? The paper gestures at these things without landing cleanly.

The new metrics proposed in Section 4 are interesting but I think they need more justification. The forgetting metrics in particular — I'm not entirely sure the proposed formulations capture something meaningfully different from BWT (backward transfer) in the existing literature. The paper should engage more directly with Lopez-Paz et al. and the subsequent metrics literature. There's a real risk of reinventing the wheel here and the authors don't fully address it.

On the mixture-of-experts / Products of Experts baseline: I found this section honestly a bit muddled. The gating mechanism described around equation 3 (page 6 or so) seems like a fairly incremental addition to standard PoE approaches. The performance improvements in Table 2 are modest and I'm not confident the ablations are sufficient to isolate what's actually doing the work. Is it the gating, the architecture, the training procedure? Hard to tell.

I think the paper is trying to do three things simultaneously — propose a benchmark, propose metrics, and propose a model — and doesn't fully succeed at any of them. In my experience, papers that try to do all three end up with each contribution feeling undercooked. The benchmark contribution is probably the strongest, but even there I'd want more analysis of the dataset's properties (domain/language distribution, temporal autocorrelation structure, etc.).

The writing is generally clear but gets loose in the discussion section. Some claims about biological plausibility of the setup feel like padding.

I'm borderline on this one. The problem framing is good and I believe the community needs better OCL benchmarks. But as currently written the paper feels like a workshop paper that's been stretched. I'd encourage a major revision focused on sharpening what the actual core contribution is, and providing stronger empirical evidence that CALM reveals something existing benchmarks cannot.

This paper tackles demonstration retrieval for in-context learning by introducing linguistically-motivated criteria for selecting effective examples. The core idea — that good demonstrations should satisfy certain linguistic properties rather than being selected purely by learned similarity metrics — is interesting and the motivation is reasonable. I've spent a fair amount of time with retrieval-augmented prompting literature over the past few years, and I think the framing here is genuinely novel enough to warrant attention.

That said, I have pretty significant concerns about the execution and presentation.

The abstract promises a lot: explainability, robustness, transferability, bias analysis, AND state-of-the-art performance. After reading the paper twice, I felt like several of these claims were underdeveloped. The "explainability" angle in particular feels thin to me. Saying that linguistic criteria are inherently more interpretable than learned embeddings is somewhat circular — you've just defined your method as explainable by construction. I was hoping to see a user study or at least a more rigorous analysis of what the explanations actually communicate to practitioners. What's on page 6-7 doesn't really satisfy this.

The comparison against MetaICL is one of the headline results, but I'm not entirely sure the comparison is apples-to-apples. MetaICL involves explicit meta-training across tasks, and comparing it to a retrieval-at-inference method without controlling for the information each approach has access to feels potentially misleading. I might be wrong about the exact experimental setup — the paper could have addressed this somewhere I missed — but I didn't find a clear discussion of this.

The seven tasks are a reasonable breadth, but I'd want to see error bars or statistical significance tests. Table 2 shows some improvements that look marginal (like 0.8-1.2 points) and I genuinely can't tell if those are meaningful without variance estimates. This is a basic ask.

The linguistic principles themselves (Section 3, I believe) are described at a level of abstraction that made me uncertain how they'd generalize. Are these criteria manually defined? How sensitive is performance to the specific operationalization of each criterion? An ablation breaking down individual criterion contributions would have been really valuable here and is conspicuously absent.

I did appreciate the domain adaptation experiments — that felt like a genuinely useful contribution and the results there are more convincing to me than the main ICL results. The bias analysis section is interesting but brief.

Writing quality is generally okay but there are grammatical issues throughout ("Existing approaches suffers" appears literally in the abstract, which is not a great sign).

My overall take: there's a real idea here worth developing, but this version of the paper feels rushed. The claims outpace the evidence. I'd encourage the authors to tighten the scope, add ablations, fix the statistical reporting, and either substantiate the explainability claims properly or dial them back. Not ready in current form.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — multiscale modeling in molecular dynamics — and the high-level idea of combining Graph Fourier Transforms with Neural ODEs is conceptually appealing. I've seen a lot of work in this space over the past few years, and the motivation here is reasonable. That said, I have fairly significant concerns about the execution and the evaluation that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

Let me start with what I found genuinely interesting: the idea of decomposing molecular graphs into spatial frequency components and then coupling those to temporal dynamics at different scales is elegant in principle. The framework described around Section 3 has some nice ideas and I think there's a real paper here somewhere.

But here's my main problem: MD17 is not a challenging enough benchmark to support the claims being made. I mean this sincerely — MD17 has been essentially solved by several recent methods, and showing improvements on it, while nice, doesn't really demonstrate that you've cracked "multiscale" modeling in any deep sense. The molecules in MD17 are small, relatively rigid, and don't exhibit the kind of long-range or slow-timescale phenomena that would truly stress-test a multiscale approach. The authors gesture at drug discovery and materials science in the abstract, but then evaluate exclusively on aspirin, ethanol, and friends. This gap bothers me quite a bit.

On the irregular timestep sampling experiments — I found this the most compelling part, and Table 2 shows some nice numbers. But I'm not entirely sure the baselines are being treated fairly here. Were the baselines retrained under the irregular sampling conditions, or just evaluated zero-shot? This needs to be made crystal clear. After reading this section twice I still couldn't tell.

The ablation in Section 4.3 is appreciated but feels a bit thin. Removing the GFT component and the NODE component separately — sure, that confirms they both contribute. But I'd want to see more nuanced ablations, e.g., what happens with different numbers of frequency bands? The choice of K (number of graph Fourier components) seems pretty arbitrary and I didn't see sensitivity analysis anywhere.

Also, the comparison to methods like EGNN, NequIP, and Equiformer — I might be wrong, but I believe some of these baselines have been superseded by even stronger models that aren't cited here. The related work section feels like it stopped reading the literature around 2022.

Minor things: Figure 3 is genuinely hard to parse. The color scheme makes it nearly impossible to distinguish the curves, and I'd ask the authors to fix this. Equation 4 has a typo or at least a notational inconsistency with what's defined in equation 2.

Overall: the core idea has merit, the writing is generally clear, but the evaluation is the weak link. I lean toward rejection in the current form but would genuinely encourage a resubmission with stronger benchmarks and more careful experimental design.

This paper tackles an interesting theoretical question about what diffusion models actually learn in their first denoising step, and I found the core observation — that first-step inference approximates ZCA inverse whitening — genuinely surprising and worth investigating. The connection to ZCA is elegant and I don't think I've seen this framing before, so credit where it's due.

That said, I have some significant concerns that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

The theoretical analysis in Section 3 feels hand-wavy in places. The jump from Equation 4 to the claim about ZCA correspondence relies on assumptions that aren't clearly spelled out — specifically, I'm not convinced the Gaussian approximation of the image distribution is doing as little work as the authors imply. In my experience with this literature, these kinds of distributional assumptions can quietly carry a lot of the explanatory weight, and the paper doesn't really grapple with what happens when those assumptions break down. I'd want to see ablations on this.

The experimental results are... fine? The image variation and editing applications in Section 5 look reasonable, but I found myself asking "compared to what, exactly?" The baselines feel somewhat cherry-picked, and Table 2 in particular shows improvements that are modest enough that I'm not sure they clear the bar for a methods contribution. If the main contribution is theoretical insight, then maybe lean into that harder and be honest that the applications are illustrative rather than SOTA.

I also found the writing in the introduction a bit overconfident. Phrases like "fundamental elements present across different diffusion models" set expectations that the paper doesn't fully deliver on — the experiments cover a fairly narrow set of models and datasets.

One thing I genuinely liked: Figure 3 is quite illuminating and does a good job making the ZCA correspondence visually intuitive. More of this kind of visualization would strengthen the paper considerably.

I'm also not entirely sure the "model-agnostic" claim holds up. The method is evaluated on DDIM-based models, and I'd want to know how this extends to consistency models, flow matching approaches, etc. The claim is broader than the evidence.

Minor stuff: there are a few notation inconsistencies between Section 3 and Section 4 (the use of W vs. W_ZCA flip-flops), and some of the related work discussion around SDEdit and prompt-to-prompt feels perfunctory.

Overall, I think there's a real idea here that deserves to see the light of day, but in its current form the paper sits uncomfortably between a theory paper and an applications paper without fully succeeding as either. I'd encourage the authors to sharpen the theoretical claims, be more careful about what the assumptions buy them, and either strengthen the empirical evaluation or dial back the methods framing. Leaning toward weak reject but genuinely open to being convinced otherwise.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — unsupervised code translation — and the core idea is straightforward: use unit tests to filter back-translated pairs before using them for fine-tuning. The intuition is sound and honestly pretty obvious in retrospect, which I think is actually a mark in the paper's favor. Good ideas often seem obvious after the fact.

The empirical results are strong. A 16%+ improvement on Java→Python and 24% on Python→C++ are substantial gains, and I appreciate that the authors are careful to frame these in terms of error rate reduction as well. Table 2 (I think — the main results table) tells a clear story. I found the ablation studies reasonably convincing, though I wish there were more analysis of *why* the filtering helps so much. Is it primarily removing syntactically broken translations? Semantically wrong ones? The paper gestures at this but doesn't really dig in, and I think that's a missed opportunity.

My main concern is about the evaluation methodology. The paper relies on unit test pass rates, which is convenient but circular in a way that bothers me. The same unit test infrastructure used to filter training data is then used to evaluate — I'm not entirely sure this inflates results artificially, but it feels like it could, and the paper doesn't adequately address this concern. I'd want to see some human evaluation or alternative metrics on at least a subset of examples.

I also found the related work section a bit thin. There's a growing body of work on code generation and program synthesis that the authors don't engage with seriously. CodeBERT, Codex-adjacent work, etc. — I know some of this postdates the submission but some of it doesn't. The positioning of this work relative to recent LLM-based approaches feels like it's being deliberately avoided.

The writing is generally clear, though I found the description of the training procedure around page 5-6 somewhat hard to follow on first read. After going through it twice, I understood it, but the notation could be tightened up. Equation 2 in particular felt like it was doing a lot of work without enough surrounding explanation.

One thing I genuinely liked: the discussion of why code is different from natural language with respect to noise sensitivity. This is well-articulated and grounds the paper's motivation concretely. In my experience reviewing NLP papers, authors often handwave this kind of thing.

Overall I think this is a solid contribution. The idea is simple, it works well, and the experiments are mostly rigorous. But the circular evaluation issue is real and needs to be addressed, and I think the paper undersells its own analysis opportunities. Minor revision territory at minimum, but I lean toward accepting with revisions if the authors can address the evaluation concern directly. It's not a fatal flaw but it's the kind of thing that will nag at readers.

This paper presents a multi-agent soccer environment and demonstrates that competitive population-based training can lead to emergent cooperative behaviors. The core finding — that cooperation can emerge from competition without explicitly rewarding cooperative behavior — is genuinely interesting, and I appreciate the ambition here.

That said, I have significant concerns about the depth of the empirical analysis and the novelty of the claims.

The emergence of cooperation through competition isn't a new idea. OpenAI's hide-and-seek work, plus a long line of research going back to Axelrod and beyond, has explored this territory. I think the authors need to much more carefully position their contribution relative to existing work, particularly hide-and-seek (Baker et al. 2019) and the DeepMind football work. What does this paper actually add that we didn't already know? The continuous physics soccer setting is somewhat novel, but I'm not convinced the environment alone constitutes sufficient contribution.

The evaluation methodology using game-theoretic principles is actually the most interesting part of the paper to me, and I wish it received more attention. The Elo-style evaluation adapted for multi-agent settings is sensible, but after reading this section twice I still wasn't totally clear on how they handle the transitivity violations that are endemic to multi-agent evaluation. This needs more rigorous treatment.

On the "evidence of cooperation" — I found this frustratingly vague. Figure 3 (or whichever figure shows the behavioral progression) shows some qualitative trajectories, but honestly, how do we know this is genuine cooperation versus coincidental coordination? The distinction matters enormously and the paper doesn't fully address it. In my experience, what looks like cooperation in these environments often dissolves under closer analysis.

The reward shaping discussion is interesting but I wanted more ablations. What happens without the specific shaping rewards? The paper kind of gestures at this but doesn't deliver a clean ablation that would make the causal story convincing.

I'm also somewhat bothered by the scale of compute required. The authors mention large-scale training but are not particularly transparent about what this means practically. This raises reproducibility concerns.

Minor things: the abstract says "principals" when it should be "principles" — small thing but come on. Some of the figures are hard to read at print scale.

Look, I don't want to be entirely negative. The environment itself seems like a reasonable contribution to the community, the paper is clearly written for the most part, and the behavioral progression they document is genuinely interesting to watch. But the theoretical framing oversells what are ultimately empirical observations in one specific environment. I think this needs substantial revision to sharpen the claims and provide more rigorous evidence for the cooperation hypothesis specifically. Currently sitting at borderline reject, could be convinced otherwise with stronger rebuttal addressing the cooperation evidence question directly.

This paper proposes augmenting language model reasoning with physics simulation outputs from MuJoCo, feeding simulation results as context to help LMs answer physical reasoning questions. The core idea is straightforward and I actually think there's something genuinely appealing about it — the grounding motivation is well-articulated and the results look impressive at first glance.

That said, I have some fairly significant concerns that I couldn't shake after reading this twice.

The most glaring issue is the evaluation setup. The benchmark consists of 39 tasks that seem to have been specifically designed around scenarios that MuJoCo can simulate well. This feels circular to me. Of course a system that uses a physics engine will outperform a pure LM on physics engine-friendly tasks. The 27.9% and 46.0% improvements cited in the abstract are striking numbers, but I'd want to understand what happens on physical reasoning that *can't* be neatly simulated — friction of irregular surfaces, thermodynamics, biological physics, etc. The paper doesn't address this scope limitation seriously enough in my view.

The render-to-text pipeline (converting simulation states into natural language descriptions for the LM) is doing a lot of work here and I found the description of this component somewhat underspecified. How sensitive are results to the verbalization choices? Figure 3 gives some examples but I'm not convinced the ablation studies fully isolate this. Equation 1 is trivially simple and I wonder if the formalism is there mostly for decoration.

The claim that smaller models with Mind's Eye match models 100x larger is headline-grabbing but I'd want to see error bars and significance tests. Table 2 presents these comparisons but the variance across tasks is never properly addressed. In my experience with similar benchmarks, aggregate numbers can mask huge variance.

I also think the related work section underengages with the tool-use and program synthesis literature — things like Toolformer, or even older work on grounded language learning with simulators (e.g., work from the Chai group). The framing of this as primarily a "grounding" contribution feels a bit overstated given how much prior work exists on giving LMs access to external tools.

The writing is generally clear and the figures are decent. I appreciated the ablation in Section 4.3, though I wish it went further.

Honestly, I'm torn. The idea works, the results are real, and the paper is readable. But the evaluation feels too narrow to support the broad claims about grounding and physical reasoning. I'd want a much more honest discussion of what the system can and cannot do, and ideally evaluation on at least some out-of-distribution physical scenarios. As it stands, I lean toward weak reject, but I could be convinced otherwise if the authors can address the scope concerns directly.

This paper describes an industry system (ACVI) for natural language interaction with AI companions in an FPS game, deployed in Arena Breakout: Infinite. I'll be upfront: I have mixed feelings about this submission.

On the positive side, the problem is genuinely interesting and practically relevant. The observation that existing command interfaces (hotkeys, radial menus) are severely limited for conveying spatial and tactical nuance is well-motivated. The scale of the deployment — 17,000+ identifiable objects, real-time latency constraints — is impressive and I don't want to dismiss the engineering effort here.

That said, I have significant concerns about this as a scientific contribution to an ML conference. After reading this twice, I'm still not entirely clear on what the novel technical contribution is versus what is essentially a system integration paper. The "confidence-based selection framework" mentioned in the abstract is described in vague terms throughout — I couldn't find a precise formulation of how confidence scores are computed or how the selection threshold is determined. Equation 2 on page 5 is particularly hand-wavy. This matters because it's presented as a core contribution.

The evaluation is, frankly, weak. The authors report accuracy numbers on what appears to be an internally constructed benchmark with no details about how test cases were sampled or who annotated them. There's no comparison to any reasonable baseline — even a simple intent classification system or a vanilla LLM-based approach. I'm not asking for a full ablation study of every component, but some comparison is essential. The numbers in Table 2 look good but I have no way to know if they're meaningful without context.

I'm also bothered by the somewhat breathless writing style — "revolutionary," "most immersive experience," "first-ever" — this reads more like a product announcement than a research paper. I understand this is from an industry team and there's always some tension there, but the ML community deserves more measured claims. The related work section is thin and misses relevant work on grounded language instruction following (e.g., work from the Embodied AI community) and spatial reference resolution.

The multi-modal entity retrieval section is the most technically interesting part to me, and I wish the paper had leaned into this more heavily. How exactly are visual features from the game engine aligned with linguistic references? The details are buried and insufficient.

In my experience reviewing system papers, the bar is that they either present a genuinely novel technical method or provide unusually rigorous empirical analysis of a deployed system. This paper currently does neither convincingly. I think there's a real paper here — maybe in a deployment track or a workshop — but in its current form I can't recommend acceptance. The authors should significantly expand the technical depth and provide honest, properly controlled evaluation.

Leaning toward rejection, but I'd encourage revision and resubmission.

This paper proposes SAIR, a framework that combines semantic information with implicit neural representations for image inpainting. The core idea — using text-matched semantic embeddings alongside appearance features in an implicit representation framework — is reasonable, and I can see the intuition behind it. However, I have significant concerns about both the technical contribution and the experimental validation.

Let me start with what bothers me most. The abstract claims the method "surpasses state-of-the-art approaches by a significant margin," but after reading the experimental section twice, I'm not convinced the comparisons are fair or comprehensive. The baselines chosen feel somewhat dated, and I didn't see comparisons against several recent inpainting methods that are clearly relevant here (e.g., recent diffusion-based inpainting approaches that also leverage semantic understanding). This is a pretty serious omission in 2024.

The two-module design (SIR + AIR) is described at a high level in the abstract but the actual technical details in Sections 3 and 4 are, frankly, hard to follow. The explanation of how the text-matched embeddings are obtained and integrated is muddled. Figure 2 helps somewhat but I still found myself confused about the training procedure — specifically, how the SIR is trained jointly or separately from the AIR, and what the loss landscape looks like. Equation 3 seems to be doing a lot of work here but the notation isn't clearly defined before it appears.

I think the core idea of injecting semantic continuity into implicit representations is actually interesting and underexplored. That's a genuine contribution. But the paper doesn't adequately distinguish this from related work on semantic image inpainting (going back to Pathak et al. and more recent work). The related work section feels thin and doesn't engage seriously enough with the broader inpainting literature.

On the experiments — Table 2 shows improvements on Places2 and CelebA-HQ, and the numbers look decent, but the ablation study (Table 3) is insufficient. I want to understand specifically how much the semantic component contributes versus just having a better implicit representation architecture. The ablation removes components but doesn't isolate this cleanly. Also, the qualitative results in Figure 5 are cherry-picked — I'd bet they are, because the failure cases are completely absent. Real reviewers notice this.

Minor issues: writing quality is uneven throughout, some sentences in Section 2 are grammatically awkward, and "misses" in the abstract should probably be "are missing." These aren't dealbreakers but suggest the paper was rushed.

My overall feeling is that this work has a kernel of a good idea but isn't ready. The technical presentation needs significant clarification, the experimental comparisons need to be stronger and more current, and the claims in the abstract oversell what's actually demonstrated. I'd encourage the authors to resubmit with a substantially revised experimental section and clearer technical exposition.

Leaning toward rejection in current form.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely problem — the gain map format is becoming increasingly relevant with Apple/Adobe adoption, and I haven't seen much academic work specifically targeting GM estimation as a learning problem. So credit where it's due: the problem formulation is novel and practically motivated.

That said, I have a number of concerns that prevent me from recommending acceptance in the current form.

The core technical contribution feels thin to me. The dual-branch architecture (LCR + GLE) is reasonable, but after reading this twice I'm struggling to see what's genuinely novel here beyond applying fairly standard ideas (local/global feature extraction, attention mechanisms) to a new output target. The "global luminance estimation" branch in particular seems to just be predicting a scalar-ish quantity — I'm not entirely sure why this requires a dedicated branch rather than a simpler conditioning mechanism. The authors should be more explicit about what architectural choices are non-trivial.

My bigger concern is the evaluation setup. The synthetic dataset construction pipeline (Section 3, I believe) involves generating SDR via tone mapping operators and then computing the GM from the HDR-SDR pair. This creates a circular dependency that the authors don't adequately address — the network is essentially learning to invert a specific tone mapping process. How does this generalize to real-world SDR content that wasn't produced by those operators? The real-world dataset captured on mobile devices is a step in the right direction, but it's quite small from what I can tell, and the details of how ground truth GMs are obtained from mobile captures are unclear to me.

The comparison baselines also feel somewhat cherry-picked. Comparing against general ITM methods is fine, but I'd want to see comparison against simple heuristic GM construction methods (e.g., ratio-based approaches) to understand how much the learning actually adds. What's the gap between a naive baseline and GMNet? I couldn't find this anywhere in the paper.

Figure 4 is helpful visually but the compressed regions in the gain maps look suspiciously similar across methods — I wonder if the quantitative gains are actually perceptually meaningful. The PSNR/SSIM numbers in Table 1 show improvement but the margins aren't huge.

The writing is generally competent though some passages in the introduction are a bit overblown about the significance of the contribution. The claim that this enables "more effective up-conversion" should be quantified more carefully — effective compared to what, measured how, on what displays?

One small thing: the paper mentions the code/dataset is available but I couldn't verify dataset size and diversity from the paper itself. This needs to be documented properly.

In my experience, papers introducing new tasks need to work harder to establish the task itself is well-posed and the evaluation is rigorous. This paper gets partway there but not all the way. I lean toward rejection but would reconsider with a strong rebuttal addressing the evaluation concerns.

This paper proposes using conditional mutual information (CMI) as a regularization term for dataset distillation, arguing that existing methods like distribution matching produce synthetic datasets that are "too hard" to learn from. The core idea is to minimize CMI in the feature space of pretrained networks to control class-aware complexity. I've read through this twice now and have some significant concerns.

The motivation is actually somewhat compelling — the observation that synthetic datasets can become pathologically difficult is real and underexplored. But I think the paper doesn't fully convince me that CMI is the *right* tool here, as opposed to, say, simpler complexity measures. The connection between CMI minimization and "learnability" is asserted more than it's rigorously established. Equation 3 and surrounding discussion (around page 4) needs much more justification. Why CMI specifically? The paper gestures at class-awareness as the key advantage but never really shows a controlled comparison against simpler alternatives like variance regularization or intra-class distance penalties.

The empirical results are... fine? Table 1 and 2 show consistent improvements over baselines across IPC settings, which is good. But the gains are often modest — like 0.3-0.8% on CIFAR-10 — and I'm genuinely uncertain whether these differences are statistically meaningful. I don't see any standard deviation reporting over multiple runs in the main results tables, which is a serious omission for a method paper in this area. Dataset distillation results can be quite noisy in my experience.

I found the ablation in Section 4.3 underdeveloped. They remove the CMI term but I'd want to see sensitivity to the hyperparameter λ (the regularization weight). How brittle is this? The paper mentions it in passing but Figure 3 doesn't really resolve my concerns about tuning sensitivity.

One thing that genuinely bothered me: the computational overhead of estimating CMI from feature spaces is non-trivial, and the paper sort of buries this. They claim improved "training efficiency" in the abstract but I'm not sure that's fully justified when you account for the additional forward passes through pretrained networks needed for CMI estimation. This needs a clearer wall-clock comparison.

The related work section is adequate but misses some recent work on information-theoretic approaches to data selection/pruning that would be directly relevant.

Overall I think there's a real idea here but the execution feels rushed. The theoretical grounding is weak, the ablations are insufficient, and some claims in the abstract are oversold. I might be wrong about the significance of the empirical gains — maybe 0.5% consistently across multiple datasets and methods IS meaningful in this space — but I'd need the variance numbers to believe it.

My recommendation is weak reject, with the main path to acceptance being: (1) statistical significance analysis, (2) stronger ablations on λ sensitivity, (3) honest computational cost discussion, and (4) better theoretical justification for CMI over alternatives.

This paper presents Octopus, a vision-language model for embodied agents that can generate executable code from egocentric visual observations and natural language instructions. The core idea — using GPT-4 to bootstrap training data and then fine-tuning a smaller VLM with environmental feedback — is reasonable and timely given the current interest in embodied AI.

That said, I have significant concerns about this work that I think need to be addressed before it's ready for publication at a top venue.

The experimental evaluation feels thin to me. After reading through the results section twice, I kept looking for ablations that would help me understand *why* the design choices work. Table 2 shows performance numbers but I'm not convinced the baselines are strong enough. Comparing against GPT-4 directly (page 8) without fine-tuning seems like an unfair comparison in the authors' favor — of course a specialized fine-tuned model beats zero-shot GPT-4 on a narrow benchmark. What about recent work like SayCan, Code as Policies, or PaLM-E? The absence of these comparisons is conspicuous and frankly a bit suspicious.

The OctoVerse simulator — I'm genuinely unsure how much of the contribution this represents versus a wrapper around existing environments. The paper is vague on this. If it's mostly a lightweight wrapper, that's fine, but be honest about it.

I think the code generation framing is interesting and I've seen similar ideas in related work (Voyager comes to mind), but the novelty claim here feels overstated. The authors use phrases like "novel VLM" and "crucial stride" quite liberally in the abstract. In my experience, papers that lean this hard on hype language in the abstract are often compensating for something.

The feedback loop mechanism described in Section 3.2 is actually the most interesting part of this work, but it's underexplored. How sensitive is the system to the quality of environmental feedback signals? What happens when the feedback is sparse or noisy? I wanted to see a proper analysis here and got basically nothing.

Figure 4 is pretty but I'm not sure it's telling me anything I couldn't get from the text. The qualitative examples are cherry-picked — I'd want to see failure cases, which are conspicuously absent.

One more thing: the writing has some issues. The abstract reads like marketing copy ("ignite further innovation," "adeptly handle a wide spectrum"). It's a bit much. The technical sections are clearer but inconsistent in depth.

I'm not saying this is bad work — there's something here, and open-sourcing the model and simulator is genuinely valuable. But as submitted, the experimental rigor doesn't meet the bar for acceptance. The authors need stronger baselines, failure analysis, and a cleaner story about what's actually novel. Right now it reads more like a technical report than a conference paper.

Leaning toward rejection, but willing to reconsider if the authors can address the experimental gaps.

This paper tackles an important and genuinely hard problem — understanding SGD's implicit bias through the lens of noise structure and symmetry. The core idea, that rescaling symmetries in the loss landscape constrain SGD dynamics to a low-dimensional subspace and push toward "noise-balanced" solutions, is interesting and I think worth publishing in some form. But I have real concerns about the current presentation and the scope of the claims.

The theoretical results around diagonal linear networks are the strongest part of the paper. The derivation of a stationary distribution for a high-dimensional non-quadratic potential is, as the authors claim, genuinely novel to my knowledge, and I spent a good chunk of time checking the logic in Section 4. The phase transition phenomena and the loss of ergodicity in deep (vs. shallow) networks is the most compelling empirical finding here. Figure 3 in particular is striking, and if it holds up, it's a meaningful result.

That said, I found the paper frustrating to read in places. The transition from the general symmetry argument (Section 2-3) to the specific diagonal linear network analysis feels abrupt. I'm not entirely sure the general theory is doing as much work as the authors claim — it sometimes reads like the diagonal linear network is carrying almost all the weight, and the rescaling symmetry framing is a post-hoc organizing principle rather than a tight theoretical scaffold. I might be wrong about this, but the authors should address it more directly.

The connection to variational Bayesian inference in the final section feels rushed and frankly underdeveloped. I understand space is limited, but right now it reads like the authors wanted to claim practical relevance without really earning it. Either develop this properly or cut it — the paper is strong enough without it.

A few more specific issues: Equation 7 — I found the notation here genuinely confusing and had to re-read it several times. The "noise balance" condition needs a cleaner, more intuitive explanation earlier in the paper; right now it's introduced somewhat formally before the reader has enough context to appreciate why it matters. Also, the assumption of continuous-time stochastic gradient *flow* as a proxy for discrete SGD is doing a lot of work throughout, and I don't think the authors are sufficiently careful about when this approximation breaks down. This is a known issue in the literature (see e.g. work by Li et al. on the modified equations perspective) and deserves more than a brief acknowledgment.

The empirical validation is thin. The experiments on diagonal linear networks are fine as proof-of-concept, but given the generality of the theoretical claims, I'd want to see at least some evidence that the noise-balance phenomenon appears in realistic network training scenarios.

Overall: interesting theoretical contribution, somewhat oversold, needs tightening. I lean toward acceptance with revisions, but the authors need to be more honest about the gap between the general claims and what the theory actually delivers.

This paper addresses individual treatment effect estimation from observational data, proposing ESCFR which uses Sinkhorn discrepancy within a stochastic optimal transport framework. The two identified problems — mini-batch sampling effects and unobserved confounder effects — are real issues in the ITE literature, and I appreciate that the authors try to tackle both simultaneously.

That said, I have significant concerns.

The framing of "Mini-batch Sampling Effects" as a novel problem feels a bit oversold to me. Batch-level distribution mismatch has been discussed implicitly in CFRNet and related work, and I'm not convinced the authors adequately distinguish their contribution from, say, just using better batching strategies or reweighting. The novelty argument would be stronger with a more careful related work discussion.

The proximal factual outcome regularizer (Section 3.3, if I'm reading the structure right) is interesting but I found the motivation for why proximal outcomes specifically address unobserved confounders somewhat hand-wavy. The connection to proximal causal inference literature (Miao et al., Tchetgen Tchetgen et al.) is mentioned but not really developed. After reading this section twice I still wasn't clear on what identifying assumptions are actually being invoked. What confounders can this handle? All of them? Clearly not. The theoretical guarantees feel weaker than advertised.

On the experiments — Table 1 shows improvements on IHDP and Jobs, but the margins on some baselines are within what I'd consider noise territory given the variance of IHDP. I'd want to see more seeds and confidence intervals reported more carefully. Also, I notice the authors don't compare against some recent strong baselines like DR-CFR or DESCN. Why not? This feels like a gap.

The ablation in Table 2 is appreciated and does show each component contributing something, which is good. But I'm skeptical of the UCE experiments — the synthetic unobserved confounder setup in Section 4.3 is designed by the authors themselves, which makes it hard to evaluate fairly.

Equation 3 — I think there might be a notational inconsistency between how the relaxed mass constraint is defined there versus how it's used in the algorithm. I might be wrong but I had to reread this several times.

My broader concern is that the paper combines several existing tools (Sinkhorn distances, OT-based distribution matching, proximal methods) without a sufficiently clear theoretical story tying them together. In my experience, papers at this venue need either strong theory or very convincing empirical results, and I'm not sure this clears either bar in its current state.

I lean toward rejection but I'd genuinely reconsider if the authors could clarify the theoretical assumptions, add missing baselines, and be more precise about what the proximal regularizer actually buys you. The core idea isn't bad, it just needs more rigorous development.

This paper proposes DuRND, which uses two RND modules in parallel to generate both novelty-based and contribution-based rewards for sparse-reward RL problems. The core idea is fairly intuitive — use one network for the standard exploration bonus and another to measure something like task-relevant progress. I've read a lot of RND variants over the years, and I want to be honest: the conceptual contribution here feels incremental. That said, let me dig into specifics.

The experimental results across Atari, VizDoom, and MiniWorld do look reasonable, and I appreciate that the authors tested on multiple environment types rather than just cherry-picking one benchmark. Table 2 shows some nice gains on hard exploration Atari games like Montezuma's Revenge and Pitfall, which are the right places to look. But I'm not fully convinced by the ablations. The ablation study (Table 3, if I'm reading this right) removes one component at a time, but I'd want to see more analysis of *why* the contribution reward helps — is it actually capturing task-relevant structure, or is it just adding a second exploration signal with different timescales? The paper doesn't fully answer this, and I found section 4.3 a bit hand-wavy on this point.

My bigger concern is the novelty of the approach. The paper positions itself against both novelty-based and value-based methods, but the "contribution reward" concept feels underdeveloped theoretically. How is it formally different from, say, reward shaping methods like potential-based approaches? The relationship to prior work like NGU, Agent57, and RIDE is discussed but I think insufficiently. After reading this twice I still wasn't totally clear on what the fundamental technical distinction is versus just running two RND networks with different target signals.

The writing is mostly fine but could be tighter. There are some awkward phrases throughout and the motivation in the intro takes too long to get to the actual method. Figure 2 is helpful but the caption is too sparse to stand alone.

I'm also a bit skeptical about the "low computational overhead" claim. The paper mentions this in the abstract but I don't see a rigorous wall-clock time comparison. Two networks versus one network — yes, probably acceptable overhead, but show me the numbers properly rather than just asserting it.

To be fair, the method does seem to work empirically, and reproducibility looks okay given the implementation details. But I think the paper needs a stronger theoretical grounding for the contribution reward component and a more honest engagement with what makes this genuinely different from prior multi-signal intrinsic reward methods.

Overall I'm leaning weak reject. The idea isn't bad but the execution and positioning need more work. Not a clear reject — there's something here worth developing — but not ready in its current form.

This paper tackles zero-shot quantization, which is a genuinely important problem, and the three challenges it identifies (noisy synthetic data, off-target pattern predictions, and erroneous hard labels) are reasonable motivations. I've seen a lot of ZSQ papers come through in recent years, and the framework here is competent but I have some significant concerns I'll try to articulate.

The low-pass filtering idea (Section 3.1) is the most straightforward contribution here, and honestly it makes intuitive sense — high-frequency noise in synthetic images would naturally hurt quantization-aware training. But I found myself wanting a deeper theoretical justification beyond the intuition. Why exactly does a low-pass filter help specifically for *quantization* as opposed to just being a general image denoising trick that would help any training procedure? This distinction is never really made clear. Figure 3 is helpful but I'd want to see ablations that isolate this component more rigorously.

The CAM alignment idea (Section 3.2) is something I found more interesting, and I think this is probably the strongest contribution of the paper. The intuition that quantized models might latch onto background or spurious features — and that aligning class activation maps with the full-precision teacher is a principled way to address this — is genuinely clever. That said, after reading this section twice, I'm still a bit fuzzy on the computational overhead this introduces. Is the CAM computation done every iteration? This seems potentially expensive and deserves more discussion.

The soft label selection strategy in Section 3.3 is... fine. I'm not entirely sure this is novel enough to stand on its own as a contribution. Using soft labels for hard samples has been explored extensively in knowledge distillation literature (Hinton et al., and many follow-ups), and the framing here doesn't convincingly distinguish SynQ's approach from existing work. I might be wrong, but this feels like the weakest of the three contributions.

The experiments in Table 2 show strong numbers across ResNet and MobileNet architectures, which is good. But I notice the gains on MobileNetV2 at W4A4 are substantial — I'd want to understand *why* specifically the method helps more in this regime. Is it the filtering? The CAM alignment? The ablation in Table 4 helps somewhat but I don't think it fully answers this.

One thing that genuinely bothered me: the synthetic data generation method is not original to this paper — it borrows from prior work — and I think the paper undersells how much of its pipeline is inherited versus novel. The framing in the introduction implies a more end-to-end novel system than what's actually being presented.

Overall, I think this is solid incremental work that would be appropriate for a workshop or a conference with broader scope. The ideas are reasonable and the empirical results are convincing. But the novelty bar feels a little low for a top venue. I lean toward weak reject, but I'm open to being convinced otherwise.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant question: why does complete graph regularization (adding a constant τ to all entries of the adjacency matrix) improve spectral clustering performance? The theoretical framing around block models is clean, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to provide some principled understanding of a widely-used heuristic.

That said, I have significant concerns about the novelty and depth of the contribution. The block model analysis, while correct as far as I can tell, feels fairly incremental relative to existing work — Amini et al. (2013), Joseph & Yu (2016), and especially the Qin & Rohe (2013) regularized Laplacian paper already provide substantial theoretical grounding for regularization in spectral methods. I kept asking myself while reading: what does this paper give me that I couldn't piece together from those prior works? The answer, unfortunately, isn't entirely clear. The "focuses on largest blocks" interpretation is intuitive but I'm not sure it's surprising enough to anchor a full paper.

The theoretical results themselves (Section 3 I think, around equations 4-6) are presented somewhat informally. I found myself wanting more precise statements — are these exact eigenvalue characterizations or asymptotic ones? The conditions on τ relative to block sizes and edge probabilities feel underspecified. After reading this twice I still wasn't 100% sure what regime the main result operates in.

The experiments are my bigger concern honestly. The synthetic experiments on page 7-8 basically just confirm what the theory predicts, which is fine, but the real data results (Table 2) show pretty modest improvements from regularization in several cases, and the paper doesn't really grapple with when regularization *hurts* or is neutral. In my experience reviewing clustering papers, cherry-picked benchmarks where the proposed method wins are not compelling. I'd want to see a more systematic study.

I also think the "outlier/noise" interpretation, while appealing, needs to be made more rigorous. The claim that regularization makes embedding "less sensitive to outliers" is stated somewhat loosely — is there a formal sense in which this holds? A theorem would be much stronger than the current intuitive argument.

The writing is generally clear but there are some awkward moments, and I noticed "on both on both" in the abstract which suggests the manuscript hasn't been carefully proofread.

I might be wrong about the novelty gap — perhaps I'm missing something in the related work section — but as it stands I think this paper is better suited for a workshop than the main conference. The theoretical story is plausible and the intuition is nice, but the contribution feels narrow and the empirical validation isn't convincing enough to compensate for the modest theoretical advance. I'd encourage the authors to either significantly deepen the theory or substantially expand the experimental section before resubmitting.

Leaning reject, but open to discussion.

This paper tackles offline learning for the decoupling capacitor placement problem using what they call "collaborative symmetricity exploitation." The core idea — leveraging symmetry properties to both improve data efficiency and generalization — is reasonable and the application domain (hardware design) is genuinely underexplored in the ML community.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The writing is honestly quite difficult to follow in places. The term "symmetricity" is used throughout but I found the formal definition (Section 3.2 if I recall correctly) to arrive quite late and still felt a bit hand-wavy. I had to read the symmetry exploitation section twice before I understood what was actually being done mechanically. This needs to be tightened considerably.

My bigger issue is with the evaluation. The claim that CSE "greatly outperforms the expert method used to generate the offline data" (also repeated in the abstract) sounds impressive, but after digging into the experiments, I'm not entirely sure this is as remarkable as presented. If the expert data was generated by a relatively weak heuristic, then extrapolating beyond it isn't necessarily saying much. The paper needs to be more explicit about what the expert baseline actually is and how strong it is. Table 2 shows these results but the caption doesn't give me enough context about the expert method's provenance.

The self-exploitation component feels underdeveloped theoretically. I think the consistency learning objective (Eq. 6 or 7, I lose track) is essentially asking the model to be equivariant under known symmetry transformations of the input, which is a fairly standard data augmentation idea dressed up in new terminology. I might be wrong about this characterization, but the authors should directly address how this differs from or improves upon standard augmentation-based approaches.

I do think the problem setup is well-motivated and the DPP benchmark itself seems like a legitimate and hard problem. The scalability experiments in the later sections are actually pretty convincing and this is where the paper is strongest in my opinion.

The related work treatment of combinatorial optimization with neural methods is decent but misses some relevant recent work on symmetry in graph neural networks for CO problems — there's a body of work here the authors should engage with more carefully.

Minor things: Figure 3 is too small to read comfortably, some notation is introduced without definition (what is the context vector c exactly in Section 3.1?), and the paper runs long without always earning it.

Overall I'm leaning reject but would consider revising upward if the authors can clarify the theoretical novelty of self-exploitation versus augmentation, and better contextualize the strength of the expert baseline. The application is interesting enough that I want to see this work, but in its current form it oversells some of the contributions.

This paper proposes a multi-compartment spiking neuron model (PMSN) inspired by hippocampal pyramidal neurons, with the key selling point being both biological plausibility and GPU-parallelizable training. I've been working in the SNN space for several years and I think I have a reasonable handle on where this fits in the literature.

Let me start with what I think is genuinely interesting here: the parallelization angle. The sequential update bottleneck in LIF-based networks is a real pain point, and the authors' approach of reformulating the multi-compartment dynamics to enable parallel computation (Section 3.2, if I'm reading the structure correctly) is the most compelling technical contribution. The connection to the cable model is not new — Mainen & Sejnowski did foundational work here, and there's a whole lineage of compartmental models — but adapting it specifically for efficient GPU training is a reasonable contribution.

That said, I have significant concerns. The experimental evaluation feels incomplete to me. The sequential MNIST and other "standard" benchmarks in Table 2 are fine for a baseline check, but in 2024 these tasks are pretty saturated and don't tell us much about whether PMSN actually handles the "high temporal complexity" scenarios the abstract promises. Where are the speech or audio tasks? Where are the long-range dependency benchmarks like PathX or ListOps? The paper claims superiority for "multi-scale sequential modeling" but I don't see experiments that specifically stress-test multi-scale behavior. This is a significant gap.

The biological motivation in Section 2 is a bit hand-wavy. The authors invoke hippocampal pyramidal neurons but then make several simplifying assumptions that are fine for engineering but undermine the biological framing. I'm not sure the paper can have it both ways — either lean into the bio or just call it what it is: a structured state-space-ish recurrent model with spike nonlinearities. The framing felt inconsistent to me after reading this twice.

The comparison baselines also need work. PLIF, GLIF, and vanilla LIF are included but I noticed the paper doesn't compare against more recent SNN sequence modeling work — specifically I'm thinking of models like SpikFormer or recent S4-inspired SNN variants that are directly relevant. Missing these makes the "superior performance" claims harder to evaluate.

One more thing: the ablation in Table 4 is appreciated but it's pretty thin. I'd want to see clearer isolation of which compartment interactions are actually doing the work versus just having more parameters overall.

I might be wrong about the significance of some of these gaps — maybe the authors have good reasons for the benchmark choices I'm questioning. But as it stands, I think the paper needs a more rigorous experimental section and a cleaner story about what the core contribution actually is. The parallelization idea has merit. The rest needs tightening. I'm leaning toward rejection with encouragement to revise.

This paper proposes subspace regularization for few-shot class incremental learning (FSCIL), essentially encouraging weight vectors for new classes to lie near the subspace spanned by existing class weights during logistic regression training. The claimed 23% improvement over SOTA on miniImageNet is eye-catching, and I spent a fair amount of time with this paper trying to understand whether the improvement is real or an artifact of the comparison setup.

My biggest concern — and I want to be direct here — is that the baselines being compared against are using fundamentally different (and arguably weaker) feature extractors. In my experience with this literature, the choice of pretrained backbone accounts for the vast majority of performance differences, and I'm not convinced the authors have done enough to isolate their regularization contribution from the representation quality.
