The empirical evaluation is my bigger concern. The tabular experiments (Section 4.1 or so) show results on what appear to be fairly small, simple gridworlds. These are not convincing benchmarks in 2024. For Deep Q-learning, the Atari results (Table 2 if I recall correctly) are incomplete — only a handful of games, and the comparison baselines feel cherry-picked. Where is the comparison against bootstrapped DQN, NoisyNet, or even a properly tuned ε-greedy? The authors compare against some baselines but I'm not entirely sure the hyperparameter tuning was done fairly across methods.

I'm also bothered by the writing. The abstract says the paper is "proving its strong practical advantage" — this language is far too strong for what the experiments actually demonstrate. That's not proving anything. The paper would benefit from more careful claims.

That said, I don't think the paper is without merit. The core idea of leveraging prediction variance without explicit posterior maintenance is appealing from a scalability standpoint, and I think there's a real paper here. The connection to SAU is clean and the method is simple to implement — simplicity is a virtue. Figure 3 (the learning curves on the deep RL tasks) does show some promising results on a few games.

But in my experience, papers that extend bandit algorithms to RL need to be much more careful about what guarantees carry over and which don't. The theoretical analysis here is basically absent. Even a regret bound in the tabular case would substantially strengthen the submission.

My recommendation is rejection, but I want to be clear this is a revise-and-resubmit type situation rather than a fundamental flaw. Stronger baselines, honest claims, and at least some theoretical grounding would make this publishable. I might be wrong about the theoretical ask if the authors can point me to analysis I missed, but I don't think I am.

This paper proposes "Double Viterbi," an extension of Viterbi-based weight pruning that combines pruning with weight quantization to achieve both high compression ratios and parallelizable on-chip reconstruction. The core idea is to apply Viterbi encoding to both the sparsity pattern (positions) and the weight values separately, enabling parallel decoding of the full sparse matrix. I've read this twice and I think the basic idea is sound, but I have significant concerns about the evaluation and presentation.

Let me start with what I think works. The observation that existing Viterbi pruning still has sequential weight decoding is legitimate, and the combination with quantization to address this is at least conceptually clean. The 19x compression on LSTM/PTB is a headline number that sounds impressive.

That said, I have real problems with the experimental comparisons. The baselines chosen in Table 2 feel cherry-picked or outdated. In my experience, you need to compare against strong recent baselines like learned step-size quantization or more aggressive mixed-precision approaches. The authors are comparing against what looks like fairly vanilla pruning methods, and I'm not convinced the playing field is level. More importantly, the perplexity numbers — are these actually competitive? A 19x compression achieving what PPL exactly? The paper buries this somewhat and I found myself flipping back and forth between tables trying to reconcile the numbers.

The hardware simulation results (Section 5, the 20-106% speedup claim) rely on a custom simulation framework that I'm skeptical of. This is not a real chip implementation. The assumptions about memory bandwidth, on-chip buffer sizes, and PE utilization need much more careful justification. That 106% figure at the high end feels optimistic and I'd want to see where exactly this comes from — what configuration produces this? The range is suspiciously wide.

Figure 3 is confusing. After staring at it for a few minutes I think I understand the double encoding structure, but the caption is inadequate and the notation is inconsistent with what's defined in Section 3.2. This needs a rewrite.

I'm also not entirely sure the "parallelizability" claim holds under practical constraints. The authors argue decoding can happen in parallel, but the actual throughput depends on synchronization overhead that isn't modeled here.

The writing is functional but unpolished in places — there are grammatical issues throughout and some sentences in the introduction are genuinely hard to parse on first read.

Overall, I think this is a borderline paper. The idea has merit and the compression numbers are interesting, but the hardware claims need stronger grounding, the baselines need updating, and the presentation needs work. I lean toward rejection in current form, but I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit with a more rigorous hardware evaluation and clearer comparisons to 2023-era compression methods.

This paper tackles spectral risk minimization with a stochastic gradient approach, which is a worthwhile problem. I've seen a lot of work in this space over the past few years, and the motivation is solid — practitioners really do care about more than just average loss, and the gap between CVaR-style objectives and proper spectral risk minimization has been a real issue in the literature.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The claim of being "the first stochastic gradient-based algorithm with convergence guarantees for spectral risks minimization" is strong, and I'm not entirely sure it holds up under scrutiny. The authors need to be much more careful in distinguishing their contributions from existing work on CVaR optimization (e.g., Rockafellar & Uryasev, and follow-ups) and from recent work on distributionally robust optimization more broadly. The related work section feels a bit thin to me — after reading it twice I still wasn't confident the novelty claim was fully justified.

The theoretical result of O(1/sqrt(epsilon)) is fine but not surprising given the problem structure. I would have appreciated more discussion of the constants hidden in that tilde-O notation, because in my experience those matter enormously for whether the theory actually explains empirical behavior. The proof sketch in Section 4 is hard to follow — specifically around Lemma 3 and the handling of the dual representation. I found myself going back and forth between the appendix and the main text more than I should have had to.

On the experimental side, Table 2 is where I spent most of my time, and honestly the results are mixed. The authors say SOREL "outperforms existing ones in most cases" but some of the margins are within what I'd consider noise, and there's no statistical significance testing reported anywhere. That's a real weakness. Also, the baseline comparisons seem to exclude some recent relevant methods — I'd want to see comparisons against at least one more recent smoothing-based approach to make the empirical story convincing.

I'm also a bit puzzled by the computational overhead. The per-iteration cost seems non-trivial and the paper doesn't discuss this carefully enough relative to the baselines.

Minor things: Figure 3 is nearly impossible to read in grayscale, equation (7) has what looks like a typo (missing transpose?), and the writing in the introduction is occasionally awkward.

My overall take: the core idea is interesting and probably publishable somewhere, but as it stands the paper oversells its contributions, the experiments need more rigor, and the theoretical exposition needs cleaning up. I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form but would genuinely reconsider with a strong revision. This isn't a bad paper — it just isn't ready yet.

This paper addresses an interesting and practically relevant problem — safe MARL with zero-constraint thresholds — by extending the epigraph form from single-agent settings to the CTDE paradigm. I've spent a fair amount of time with constrained optimization approaches to RL, so I feel reasonably qualified to evaluate this.

The core idea is sensible. The authors correctly identify that CMDP-based MARL algorithms struggle when the constraint threshold is zero, which is a known issue in single-agent safe RL (as noted in works like LAMBDA and related Lagrangian methods). Extending the epigraph form to CTDE is a reasonable contribution, though I'd push back a bit on how novel this really is — the technical leap from single-agent epigraph methods to MARL feels somewhat incremental, and I think the paper somewhat oversells the difficulty of this extension in places.

My biggest concern is the empirical evaluation. The Multi-Particle Environments used are relatively simple, and while the Safe MuJoCo results are more convincing, I found the comparisons a bit cherry-picked. Specifically, Table 2 — the framing of "as safe as the safest baseline while achieving similar performance as the best-performing (unsafe) baseline" is doing a lot of rhetorical work. After reading this twice, I'm not convinced the differences are always statistically meaningful. Are the reported numbers averaged over multiple seeds? The paper mentions this somewhere but I couldn't find clear confidence intervals in all the relevant tables, which is a real problem.

The theoretical analysis in Section 4 is appreciated but feels thin. The convergence guarantees come with assumptions that are fairly strong and not well-justified for the experimental settings used. I might be wrong, but Assumption 3 in particular seems quite restrictive — is this actually satisfied in the MuJoCo environments? This deserves more discussion.

I also found the exposition in Section 3.2 somewhat hard to follow on first read. The transition from the centralized epigraph formulation to the distributed execution policy is not as clean as it could be. More intuition here would help readers who aren't already steeped in constrained optimization literature.

On the positive side: the motivation is clear, the problem is real, and the experimental setup covers a reasonable range of scenarios with varying agent counts. The ablation studies are useful. I genuinely think the zero-threshold safe MARL problem deserves more attention and this paper is a reasonable step.

Overall, I'm lukewarm. The contribution is real but feels modest, the theory needs shoring up, and the empirical claims need tighter statistical treatment. I'd lean toward weak reject in current form, but I think with revisions — particularly around the statistical rigor and theoretical discussion — this could be acceptable. Authors should also engage more seriously with recent single-agent epigraph work to better delineate what's new here.

This paper tackles grokking — a phenomenon I've found genuinely interesting since the Power et al. paper — and proposes using SVD-based reparameterization ("Decomposed Learning") to mitigate it. The core idea is straightforward: decompose weight matrices into U, Σ, V^T and train in this factored form, which the authors claim biases learning toward low-rank, generalizable solutions earlier in training.

I'll be honest: I wanted to like this paper more than I do. The idea is clean and the motivation is reasonable. But there are serious problems that prevent me from recommending acceptance.

The empirical evaluation is far too narrow. Everything is on modular division with p=97. That's it. Grokking has been demonstrated across a wide range of algorithmic tasks — other modular arithmetic operations, sparse parity, group compositions — and I have no idea whether Decomposed Learning works beyond this single setting. The authors acknowledge this briefly but wave it away. That's not acceptable. After reading this twice, I still couldn't find a convincing argument for why modular division specifically is representative enough to draw general conclusions about "transformer-based learning tasks" as the abstract claims.

The baselines are weak. The comparison to weight decay and other regularization approaches that are known to affect grokking (Nanda et al., Liu et al.) is superficial at best. I might be wrong, but I believe aggressive L2 regularization alone can produce similar acceleration of generalization in these settings, and the paper doesn't seriously engage with this. Why is SVD reparameterization better than just tuning weight decay more aggressively?

Figure 3 is central to the paper's claims but the variance across runs isn't shown consistently — some plots show single runs, others show averages. This makes it hard to assess reliability. The grokking phenomenon is known to be sensitive to random seeds.

I also found the theoretical justification underdeveloped. The argument around equation (3) that SVD promotes "compact representations" is intuitive but hand-wavy. There's no formal analysis of why this parameterization changes the loss landscape in a way that favors generalization, and in my experience, that's exactly the kind of thing reviewers at top venues will push back on.

On the positive side — the writing is generally clear, the phenomenon being studied is important, and the idea of using structured parameterization to influence the implicit bias of gradient descent is worth pursuing. Table 2 showing parameter reduction is mildly interesting.

But this reads more like a workshop paper or early-stage exploration than a complete contribution. The claims in the abstract significantly outpace what the experiments actually demonstrate. I'd encourage the authors to substantially broaden the empirical evaluation, engage more seriously with related regularization literature, and either provide theoretical grounding or significantly temper their claims. As it stands, I can't recommend acceptance.

This paper proposes using test-time augmentation (TTA) to reduce prediction set sizes in conformal prediction, which is a reasonable and practically motivated idea. I'll share my thoughts, though I'll say upfront that my overall reaction is somewhat mixed.

The core intuition is straightforward: by averaging softmax probabilities over augmented versions of a test image, you get a better-calibrated score, which in turn leads to smaller prediction sets at the same coverage level. This is not a shocking idea — TTA has been used to improve classifier calibration for years, and the connection to conformal efficiency feels somewhat incremental. That said, the empirical results are solid and the paper is mostly well-written, so I don't want to dismiss it entirely.

The theoretical analysis in Section 3 is where I spent the most time. The authors try to explain *why* large prediction sets persist even with methods like RAPS, and I found this section partially convincing but also a bit hand-wavy in places. The argument around Proposition 1 feels like it's building toward something more substantial than it delivers. I might be wrong, but I think the assumptions required are stronger than acknowledged and the connection to the empirical behavior isn't as tight as the authors suggest. I'd like to see this section tightened considerably.

The experiments cover three datasets and three models, which is reasonable, though I notice ImageNet-scale experiments are somewhat limited and the gains on harder datasets (Table 3, I believe) look less impressive. A 30% reduction headline sounds great but that number appears to be cherry-picked from the most favorable setting — in many configurations the improvement is more like 10-15%, which is still useful but less dramatic. I think the paper would be more trustworthy if it led with the average-case results rather than the best-case.

One thing I genuinely appreciated: the authors are honest about when TTA doesn't help much. That kind of intellectual honesty is refreshing and I wish more papers did this.

My main concern is novelty. TTA for improving classifier outputs is well-studied. Applying it inside a conformal wrapper is a natural engineering choice, but I'm not sure it rises to the level of a top-venue contribution on its own. The paper feels like it belongs at a workshop or a solid second-tier venue rather than a flagship conference. The theoretical contribution is thin, and the empirical contribution, while careful, isn't groundbreaking.

Minor things: the notation in Section 4 gets cluttered around equation (4) and I had to re-read it twice to follow. Also "probabilites" in the abstract should be "probabilities" — small thing but suggests the paper needs another proofread pass.

In summary: decent work, practically useful, but I'm not convinced the contribution is substantial enough for acceptance here. I'd encourage the authors to either strengthen the theory or broaden the empirical scope significantly.

This paper proposes a modular approach to visual reasoning where new task solvers are built compositionally on top of previously learned modules, motivated by analogy to how humans acquire skills progressively. The core idea is appealing and I think there's something genuinely interesting here, but I have significant concerns about the execution and evaluation that prevent me from recommending acceptance in the current form.

Let me start with what I liked. The high-level framing is intuitive, and the black-box communication protocol between modules (query + output only) is a clean design choice that I think deserves more attention in the paper than it gets. Figure 2 is actually quite helpful for understanding the architecture, though I had to read the caption three times before I understood what the dashed lines were meant to convey.

Now the problems. The experimental evaluation feels thin. The tasks are relatively simple visual reasoning tasks, and I'm not convinced the baseline comparisons are strong enough. An attention-based baseline is fine as a starting point, but in 2023/2024 there are much stronger compositional reasoning baselines — NMNs, NS-VQA, and variants thereof — that feel conspicuously absent. Why weren't these compared against? The authors claim improved performance across all tasks, but the margins reported in Table 2 are in several cases quite small and I don't see statistical significance reported anywhere. This is a real oversight.

The "no forgetting" claim is presented almost as a given (Section 3.2 I think) because of the modular architecture, but this deserves actual empirical verification. Just saying the architecture prevents forgetting by construction doesn't substitute for showing it. I'd want to see a continual learning experiment with a sequential baseline.

The human evaluation for interpretability (Section 4.3) is interesting but I found the methodology underdescribed. How many judges? What were the instructions exactly? What was the inter-annotator agreement? Without this, the interpretability claim is basically unverifiable. I might be wrong, but this section reads like it was added somewhat hastily.

I also found the related work treatment of program synthesis and neural module networks a bit dismissive. The connection to Andreas et al. (2016) and subsequent NMN literature needed a more careful treatment of what's actually novel here versus what's been done before.

To be blunt: the core contribution feels incremental relative to existing modular/compositional approaches, and the paper needs either stronger baselines, more rigorous evaluation, or a clearer articulation of what specifically is new. The writing is generally clear but the paper is not ready. I'd encourage the authors to resubmit with a substantially expanded experimental section.

Score: Reject (borderline). Happy to reconsider if the authors can address the baseline and evaluation concerns.

This paper proposes a stereo matching method combining grouped correlation aggregation with a propagation mechanism, drawing inspiration from traditional SGM-style approaches. The authors claim state-of-the-art zero-shot generalization and top ranking on ETH3D. I've spent a fair amount of time with this paper and have mixed feelings.

Let me start with what I think works. The core idea of integrating SGM-inspired aggregation into the iterative optimization framework is interesting and not something I've seen done quite this way. The contour-aware aggregation modification is conceptually appealing, and I can see why it might help at depth discontinuities — which have always been the Achilles heel of cost-volume approaches. The grouped window shifting mechanism also seems reasonable for improving correlation volume coverage.

That said, I have significant concerns. The experimental evaluation feels somewhat selective to me. Ranking 1st on ETH3D is highlighted prominently, but ETH3D is a relatively small benchmark and results there don't always generalize. The comparison on Middlebury and KITTI feels more buried, and after reading Table 2 twice I'm not convinced the improvements are as consistent as the abstract implies. Some of the gains over baselines like RAFT-Stereo and IGEV-Stereo are within what I'd consider noise range for some metrics.

The "efficient updater" description in Section 3.2 is honestly underdeveloped. The authors claim improved single-iteration performance but the ablation in Table 4 doesn't fully isolate this component — I might be wrong but it looks like the ablation conflates the updater design with other changes. This needs to be cleaner.

The video stream inference section feels tacked on. I appreciate the real-world deployment demonstration, but the simulator experiments for frame rate evaluation are not convincing — what simulator, under what conditions? This section reads like it was added late and doesn't meet the bar of the rest of the paper.

A minor but persistent irritation: the writing quality is uneven. Some sections are clear, others are genuinely hard to parse. The sentence on page 6 describing the propagation schedule is a good example — I read it three times and still couldn't fully reconstruct the intended algorithm without looking at the accompanying pseudocode.

In my experience reviewing stereo matching papers over the years, the field has become somewhat saturated with incremental improvements to RAFT-style frameworks. This paper does bring a somewhat fresh angle with the SGM integration, but the contribution needs to be argued more forcefully and evaluated more rigorously. The real-world deployment is a nice touch but doesn't substitute for thorough quantitative analysis.

I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form. The ideas have merit, but the paper needs stronger ablations, cleaner writing, and a more honest positioning of where it actually improves versus existing methods. I'd encourage the authors to resubmit after addressing these issues.

This paper tackles an interesting and important problem — analogical reasoning in neural networks — and I think the core insight about data presentation and contrastive training being more important than architecture is genuinely worth publishing. That said, I have some significant concerns.

The central claim, that "the critical factor for inducing such capacity is not an elaborate architecture, but rather, careful attention to the choice of data," is stated fairly boldly and I think the evidence, while suggestive, doesn't fully support the universality of this claim. The experiments are conducted on relatively constrained visual analogy domains (the PGM-style matrices and similar), and I'm not convinced this generalizes to the kinds of open-ended analogical reasoning that, say, Hofstadter and Mitchell were concerned with. The paper would benefit from being more careful about the scope of its claims.

On the contrastive training methodology itself — I found the description in Section 3 a bit hard to follow on first read. The way the negative examples are constructed feels underspecified. After reading it twice I still wasn't entirely clear whether the negatives are sampled at the batch level or constructed explicitly per-example, and the ablation in Table 2 (page 8 I think) doesn't quite isolate this factor cleanly. I might be wrong about this, but it seems like an important detail.

Figure 3 is actually quite compelling and I appreciate that the authors show qualitative examples. The generalization results to held-out relation types are the strongest part of the paper empirically. This is where the contribution feels most crisp.

I'm more skeptical about the comparison baselines. The architectures chosen as comparisons feel a bit like strawmen — I'd have liked to see comparison against something like a relation network or even a simple transformer baseline, both of which were available at the time and are relevant prior work. The claim that "even the simplest architectures" work feels somewhat oversold without this context.

Minor point but the writing in the introduction leans heavily on intellectual history (Gentner, Hofstadter, etc.) and while I appreciate the grounding, it goes on for almost two pages before we get to what the paper actually does. Busy readers will lose patience.

The symbolic generalization experiments are interesting but feel somewhat tacked on — like they were added to strengthen the paper after the fact. The connection between the visual and symbolic results could be drawn more explicitly.

Overall I think this is a solid contribution with a clear and reproducible methodology, and the core empirical finding is interesting. But the framing needs to be more careful, the baselines need work, and some of the experimental details need tightening. I'd lean toward weak accept contingent on the authors addressing the baseline comparisons and being more precise about what their contrastive method actually does at the implementation level.

This paper proposes GasketRAG, which inserts an intermediate "gasket" module between the retriever and LLM to jointly optimize retrieval ranking and document refinement. The core motivation — that existing retrievers aren't aligned with LLMs and retraining is expensive — is legitimate and well-understood in the community. But I have significant concerns about whether this paper actually delivers on its promises.

My biggest issue is with the evaluation. After reading the experimental section twice, I couldn't find a clear description of the baselines used and whether comparisons are truly "fair" as claimed in the abstract. The claim of "fair comparison against the latest RAG methods" is doing a lot of work here. Which methods exactly? What years? I noticed Table 2 includes some comparisons but the baseline descriptions in Section 4.2 feel thin — I'm not confident the strongest recent methods (e.g., SELF-RAG, FLARE, or more recent reranking approaches) are all represented. This matters a lot.

The "gasket" framing is clever marketing but I'm honestly not sure it adds conceptual clarity. The actual technical contribution seems to be a reranker + summarizer jointly trained with preference data, which... isn't entirely new? The preference data collection methodology described around Section 3.3 is the most interesting part of the paper to me, but it's also the least thoroughly explained. How sensitive are the results to the quality of this preference data? What happens when the preference signal is noisy? These questions go unanswered.

Figure 3 is quite hard to parse. I stared at it for a while and I think I understand the pipeline but the caption doesn't help much. This needs a serious revision.

The ablation study (Table 4 I think) is appreciated but feels incomplete. I'd want to see what happens when you remove just the ranking component vs. just the refinement component independently — the current ablations don't fully isolate these contributions in my reading.

I might be wrong about this, but the computational overhead of adding the gasket module seems like it could be non-trivial. I see no latency analysis anywhere. For a system paper making practical claims, this is a real omission.

Writing quality is generally okay but there are places where the prose is vague in ways that obscure whether something is a genuine contribution or a restatement of obvious things. The abstract in particular oversells.

Overall: the idea is reasonable and the empirical results look promising on the surface, but the paper as written doesn't give me enough confidence in the methodology or the comprehensiveness of the evaluation. I'd want to see stronger baselines, better ablations, a latency/cost analysis, and a cleaner explanation of the preference data pipeline before I'd feel comfortable accepting this. Not a reject necessarily, but needs substantial revision.

This paper proposes LinBridge, a framework for interpreting nonlinear neural encoding models by decomposing the nonlinear mapping into a "linear inherent component" and a "mapping bias" capturing sample-selective nonlinearity, using Jacobian analysis. The application domain is neural visual encoding with CLIP-ViT predicting fMRI responses.

I'll be honest — I had to read the core methodology section (around pages 4-5) twice before I felt like I understood what was actually being learned. The self-supervised learning strategy for extracting components from Jacobian matrices is the heart of the paper, but the exposition is muddled. Equation 3 in particular feels like it's doing a lot of work without sufficient justification for why this particular factorization is meaningful rather than just mathematically convenient. I'm not entirely sure the authors have made a compelling case that the "linear inherent component" is anything more than a PCA-like compression of the Jacobian matrices.

The experimental results in Table 2 are interesting and the ROI-level analysis of nonlinearity distribution is probably the most genuinely compelling part. The finding that higher visual areas show more sample-selective nonlinearity aligns with what I'd expect from the neuroscience literature (Yamins & DiCarlo, Scotti et al. etc.) and at least passes the sanity check. Figure 4 is reasonably clear.

That said, I have real concerns about the baselines. The comparison is mostly against linear encoding models, which feels like a somewhat low bar. There's a whole literature on kernel methods and nonlinear encoding models — why not compare against something like a Gaussian process regression approach or even a simple MLP baseline with explicit interpretation tools like LIME or SHAP? The authors mention in passing that existing interpretation tools aren't well-suited here but don't actually demonstrate this empirically. That's a significant gap.

The CLIP-ViT choice is also underexplored. I'd want to see whether LinBridge generalizes to other feature extractors — the paper's claims are about "nonlinear neural encoding models" generally, but the experiments are exclusively CLIP-ViT on one dataset (NSD, I believe). This significantly limits the scope of what can actually be concluded.

Minor things: Figure 3 caption is inadequate, the notation in Section 3.2 gets cluttered around the mapping bias definition, and I found the abstract's final sentence cut off, which is a sloppy oversight that shouldn't happen at submission.

I think there's a genuine idea here worth developing. The Jacobian-based analysis angle is reasonable and the neuroscience motivation is sound. But in its current form the paper feels like it's making broader claims than the experiments support, and the methodology needs clearer justification. I'm leaning toward rejection with encouragement to resubmit with stronger baselines and cleaner theoretical grounding. Not a clear reject, but not close to acceptance either.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically important problem — the instability of heteroscedastic neural network training via NLL maximization. The core observation (that high variance predictions can "explain away" poor mean estimates) is not entirely novel; I've seen related discussions in the uncertainty quantification literature and in work on robust regression, but the authors do a reasonable job framing it specifically for the deep learning context and proposing a concrete fix.

The synthetic example in Section 3 is actually quite effective at building intuition. I found Figure 1 particularly clear. My concern is that the theoretical analysis feels somewhat shallow — the conditions identified are described informally and I think the paper would benefit substantially from a more rigorous characterization of exactly when this failure mode occurs. The current treatment reads more like "here's a thing we noticed" than a principled analysis. That said, I don't think this kills the paper.

The β-NLL formulation is simple, which I appreciate. Equation 6 (or whatever the final loss formulation is numbered — I'll admit I was reading a printed version and lost track) is easy to implement and the authors show it helps. My worry is the sensitivity to β itself — you've replaced one potential failure mode with a hyperparameter that practitioners now need to tune. The experiments show robustness across β values in certain ranges, but I'm not fully convinced this holds generally. The ablations here feel a bit cherry-picked to me, though I might be wrong about that.

On the experiments: I think the evaluation is decent but not comprehensive. The domains covered are reasonable, but I'd really want to see results on something like molecular property prediction or physics simulations where heteroscedastic uncertainty actually matters in downstream decision-making. RMSE and NLL as evaluation criteria are fine but tell you nothing about calibration, which seems like the whole point. Where are the reliability diagrams? This feels like a significant omission for a paper specifically about uncertainty estimation.

The related work section is thin. There's a whole body of work on loss attenuation, on beta-divergences in robust statistics (which directly anticipates parts of this contribution), and on learning with noisy labels that should be engaged with more seriously. I'm a little surprised the connection to β-divergences isn't discussed more explicitly given the name of the proposed method.

Overall: I think this is a solid, practical contribution that will likely be useful to people training probabilistic networks. The writing is generally clear. But the theoretical depth is lacking, the calibration evaluation is missing, and the related work needs work. I'm leaning toward acceptance but I'd want to see the calibration analysis and a more serious engagement with the robust statistics literature before I'm fully comfortable. Not a groundbreaking paper, but useful. Borderline accept for me.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — zero-shot navigation to novel map layouts using abstract 2D occupancy maps as input — and the framing around "reading a paper map like a human" is compelling. I've spent a fair amount of time with model-based RL and world model papers over the years, so I feel reasonably positioned to evaluate this.

The core idea of using a hypermodel to generalize across map layouts is reasonable, and the n-step goal relabeling trick for denser feedback is a nice practical contribution. I found Figure 3 helpful for understanding the overall architecture, though I had to read the section around page 5-6 twice before the relationship between the hypermodel predictions and the planner became clear. The writing in that section could genuinely use tightening.

My bigger concern is the experimental evaluation. The authors test on DeepMind Lab, which is fine, but the baseline comparisons feel thin. Where's the comparison against something like a memory-augmented reactive policy or a recent goal-conditioned approach? The paper essentially argues that model-based > reactive for this setting, but the baselines chosen don't really stress-test that claim. I'm not entirely sure the performance gaps reported in Table 2 would survive against a stronger reactive baseline with the same observation budget. This feels like the central empirical gap in the paper.

The zero-shot transfer results are the headline claim, and they're promising — I'll give the authors that. But the number of novel maps used in evaluation is quite small, and I'd want to see statistical significance reported. Error bars are missing in several key figures (Figure 5 specifically). In my experience, navigation results in procedurally generated or limited-layout settings can have high variance.

The n-step goal relabeling (Section 4.3) — I think this is actually the most practically interesting contribution, but it's somewhat underplayed and the ablation in Table 3 doesn't fully isolate its effect from the hypermodel component. I'd want a cleaner ablation.

One thing that genuinely puzzles me: the claim about "small proportion of training maps" is mentioned in the abstract but I couldn't find a precise characterization of this proportion or a sensitivity analysis. How does performance degrade as training map diversity decreases? That seems crucial for the paper's core claims.

The related work section is decent but misses some relevant work on map-based navigation and spatial abstraction in RL — the Neural Map paper by Parisotto & Salakhutdinov and some of the more recent work on topological representations feel directly relevant and aren't discussed.

Overall, I think the paper has a genuinely interesting idea that isn't yet fully validated. The writing needs work, the baselines need strengthening, and several empirical questions are left open. I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, but I could see a revised version being competitive if the authors address the experimental gaps seriously.

This paper introduces StableSemantics, a large-scale synthetic dataset built on top of Stable Diffusion generations, pairing images with cross-attention maps at the noun-chunk level. The scale is impressive on paper — 224k prompts, 2M+ images, 10M attention maps — and I appreciate that the authors are trying to address a real gap in dense semantic understanding. That said, I have significant concerns about the actual contributions here.

My biggest issue is that the paper reads more like a dataset description paper than a research contribution. And that's fine — dataset papers have their place — but the bar for those is that the dataset needs to be demonstrably useful and the benchmarking needs to be thorough. Here, I'm not convinced either criterion is fully met. The benchmarking in Section 5 feels thin. Table 2 shows captioning metrics and Table 3 shows segmentation results, but there's no strong baseline comparison or ablation that would help me understand *why* this dataset is better or different from, say, just using COCO or something like LAION with existing segmentation annotations. What does training on StableSemantics actually buy you? I kept waiting for a downstream task where the dataset demonstrably helps and it never really arrived.

The cross-attention maps as semantic attributions — this is the core novelty claim and I think it needs much more scrutiny. It's been fairly well documented (Chefer et al., Tang et al., among others) that cross-attention maps in diffusion models are noisy, often poorly localized, and don't straightforwardly correspond to semantic segmentation masks. The authors acknowledge this somewhat but kind of brush past it. Figure 3 shows some nice-looking examples but I'd want to see failure cases. How often are the attention maps actually wrong or degenerate? Is there any validation against human annotation? Without this, I'm not sure I trust the 10M attention maps as reliable semantic labels.

I also found the discussion of the prompt curation process (Section 3.1) a bit vague. "Human-curated" is doing a lot of work here — are these prompts from a specific community, what was the filtering process, what biases might be introduced? The demographic and content bias analysis in Section 4 is appreciated but feels surface-level.

The paper is generally well-written and easy to follow, though a bit dry. I think the authors are building something that could genuinely be useful to the community — I don't want to dismiss that. But right now it feels like the paper is trying to claim more than it demonstrates. The contribution would be much stronger with (a) a real downstream task showing dataset utility, (b) honest quantitative analysis of attention map quality, and (c) clearer positioning against related synthetic datasets like DatasetDM or Grounded Diffusion work.

I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, but I think with significant revision this could be a solid dataset contribution.

This paper tackles zero-shot object detection on the YCB Video Dataset, which is at least an interesting application domain — robotic manipulation in manufacturing contexts is a real problem and I appreciate that motivation. However, I have significant concerns about this submission that I think need to be addressed before it could be considered for publication at a serious venue.

First, the technical contribution here is genuinely unclear to me. After reading this twice, I still couldn't identify what novel algorithmic contribution the authors are making. The abstract mentions "introducing" zero-shot detection to this domain, but novelty of application alone is rarely sufficient. What are they actually proposing? If they're adapting an existing ZSD framework (which ones? the paper doesn't make this clear in the abstract), the modifications need to be substantial and well-motivated.

The framing also bothers me. The claim that "zero-shot object detection is a subset of unsupervised learning" (abstract) is just... wrong? ZSD typically operates in a supervised setting with seen classes and transfers to unseen ones — that's not unsupervised learning. This kind of conceptual imprecision is a red flag for me about the rigor of the work overall.

On evaluation: 21 objects is a pretty small dataset by modern standards. I'm not entirely sure how the seen/unseen splits are constructed here, and from the abstract I can't tell if there's a meaningful experimental protocol with proper baselines. What are they comparing against? DELO? ZSD-YOLO? Some of the recent work from Rahman et al.? Without this it's impossible to judge the results.

The motivation around "high-mix-low-volume production" is actually compelling and I don't want to dismiss that entirely — this is a genuine industrial need. But the connection between that motivation and the specific technical choices made feels hand-wavy in the abstract. Why YCB specifically? The YCB dataset was designed for grasping benchmarks, not really for testing generalization in the zero-shot sense, so there's a legitimate question about whether it's the right testbed for the claimed contribution.

The writing needs work too. Phrases like "we discovered the necessity to introduce a hot concept" read as quite informal, and there are grammatical issues throughout ("does not change frequently" should be "do not"). I'd strongly recommend a thorough proofread.

I'm not in a position to recommend acceptance. The paper may have interesting empirical results buried in it — I genuinely can't tell from what's presented — but the contribution needs to be articulated much more precisely, the technical approach needs to be clearly described, and the experimental validation needs rigorous baselines. I'd encourage the authors to resubmit after substantially strengthening these aspects.

This paper proposes D5RL, a new benchmark for offline RL consisting of robotic manipulation and locomotion tasks with diverse data sources including human teleoperation data. I've been waiting for something like this — the D4RL saturation problem is real and the community needs harder benchmarks. That said, I have some significant concerns.

The core contribution is clear enough: more realistic robot simulation environments, human-collected data, image-based observations, and support for offline-to-online finetuning evaluation. I appreciate the breadth here. But after reading this twice, I'm left wondering whether the paper adequately justifies *why* these specific tasks and data collection protocols are the right choices. The argument essentially boils down to "D4RL is too easy, here's harder stuff" without a rigorous framework for what properties a benchmark *should* have. Section 3 gestures at this with the list of challenges (long-horizon, fine-grained control, etc.) but it reads more like a feature list than a principled analysis.

The experimental section is where things get genuinely interesting but also frustrating. The baseline results in Tables 2-4 show that most current offline RL algorithms struggle badly on these tasks, which is either a sign that the benchmark is appropriately challenging or that something is miscalibrated. I lean toward the latter being a concern — when IQL, TD3+BC, and CQL all score near zero on several tasks, it's hard to tell if the algorithms are failing because the problem is hard or because the benchmark has issues with reward shaping, dataset quality, or sim-to-real gaps that aren't the algorithm's fault. I think the authors need to provide stronger evidence that *some* reasonable approach can solve each task, otherwise reviewers and future users won't know if they're making progress or just fighting the benchmark.

I'm also not entirely sure the human teleoperation data is as valuable as claimed without more analysis. How suboptimal is it? What's the coverage like? Figure 4 shows some trajectory visualizations but this doesn't really answer the distributional questions I care about.

On the positive side, the environments look genuinely well-engineered and the inclusion of image-based observations with realistic visual variation is a real contribution. The franka kitchen variants in particular seem promising.

One more thing that bugged me: the paper is quite long and could be tightened considerably. The related work section rehashes D4RL, RoboMimic, etc. in a way that doesn't add much. Cut it in half.

My overall take: the community needs this kind of benchmark, and I think the authors have done real work here. But the paper needs a clearer conceptual framework for benchmark design and better evidence that the tasks are solvable-but-hard rather than just hard. Borderline accept for me, leaning toward accept if the authors can address the "are these tasks actually solvable" concern convincingly in the rebuttal.

This paper proposes SeaKR, which uses LLM internal states (specifically looking at token probability distributions / hidden states) to estimate uncertainty and drive adaptive retrieval decisions. The core idea — using the model's own uncertainty signal for when to retrieve, how to rank retrieved passages, and which reasoning strategy to employ — is reasonably clean and the experiments cover both simple and multi-hop QA settings.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The novelty claim feels overstated to me. Using internal model states or output probabilities to detect uncertainty for RAG isn't new — Self-RAG, FLARE, and several others have explored uncertainty-driven retrieval. The authors differentiate themselves mainly by applying this uncertainty signal to *three* components (retrieval trigger, re-ranking, reasoning strategy selection), but I'm not convinced this constitutes a fundamentally new contribution vs. a reasonable engineering combination. After reading the related work section twice, I still wasn't totally clear on what the *technical* distinction is between SeaKR's uncertainty extraction and, say, what's done in Self-RAG's token-level reflection. The paper needs to be much sharper about this.

The experimental results in Table 2 show improvements but they're often marginal — like 1-2 points on several benchmarks. I found myself wondering whether these differences are statistically significant. No variance/confidence intervals are reported anywhere that I can see, which is a real problem for claims of "outperforming existing methods." On 2WikiMultiHopQA in particular, the gains look more convincing, but on simpler datasets the story is murkier.

Section 3.3 (the reasoning strategy selection part) is honestly the most interesting contribution here, and I think it's underdeveloped. The idea of using uncertainty to choose between, say, iterative vs. one-shot retrieval is novel enough to deserve more ablation. Table 3 does an ablation but I found it hard to parse — what exactly is being held constant? The description on page 6 is unclear.

I'm also somewhat skeptical about the practical overhead. Extracting internal hidden states at inference time adds latency and requires white-box access to the LLM. The paper doesn't seriously discuss deployment constraints, and given that many practitioners use API-only models, this limits applicability in ways the abstract doesn't acknowledge.

Figure 2 is helpful but the caption is too sparse — I had to cross-reference with the text to understand what the uncertainty scores actually represent numerically.

Minor: some grammar issues throughout, particularly in the introduction. "alleviate hallucination" should probably be "alleviate hallucinations" or "reduce hallucination," and there are a few awkward phrasings in Section 4.

Overall, I think this is a decent paper with a reasonable idea, but it needs stronger differentiation from prior work, statistical rigor in results, and better exposition of the most interesting component (the strategy selection). Leaning toward weak reject, but I could be persuaded if the authors address the novelty and significance questions directly.

This paper proposes LIRS, a method for graph OOD generalization that takes an "indirect" approach — first learning spurious features and then subtracting them from ERM-learned representations to recover invariant features. The core intuition is interesting and I think the framing around "what invariant learning methods actually capture" is a legitimate research question that deserves attention.

That said, I have significant concerns about both the theoretical and empirical contributions.

On the theory side: the argument that removing spurious features leaves you with invariant features rests on assumptions that I don't think are carefully examined. The decomposition in Section 4 (around Eq. 3-5 if I'm reading correctly) essentially assumes that ERM features are a clean sum of spurious and invariant components. In my experience this kind of linear separability assumption is doing a lot of heavy lifting and the paper doesn't really interrogate it. What happens when spurious and invariant features are entangled? The theoretical guarantees feel hand-wavy to me — I read the relevant propositions twice and I'm still not convinced the proof sketch fully closes the argument.

The empirical results are where I have the most trouble. A 25.50% improvement over the second-best method, as advertised in the abstract, is an extraordinary claim. After reading Table 2 carefully, this number appears to come from a single dataset/setting where baselines perform quite poorly — it's not the average improvement. This is, frankly, misleading as an advertising number. The average improvement across benchmarks is much more modest and I think the paper should be upfront about this. I'd also want to see error bars and statistical significance tests more prominently — some of the margins in Table 3 look within noise to me.

I'm also not fully convinced by the spurious feature learning component. How do you ensure the "spurious feature extractor" doesn't just collapse to learning nothing, or alternatively absorbs invariant signal? The ablations in Section 5.3 help somewhat but don't directly address this failure mode. The connection to existing work on shortcut learning and feature disentanglement (e.g., work by Arjovsky et al. on IRM, and more recent graph-specific stuff like GSAT, DIR) could also be developed more — right now related work reads like a list rather than a positioning argument.

Minor things: Figure 3 is hard to parse at the resolution provided, and the notation in Section 4.2 gets noisy with subscripts. Page 6 has a paragraph that starts with "Notably" three sentences in a row which is distracting.

I might be wrong about the theoretical concerns — this isn't my primary specialty and maybe I'm missing something — but the empirical presentation issues feel unambiguous to me. The core idea has merit and I don't think this is a bad paper, but it needs a more honest empirical narrative and tighter theoretical grounding before I'd feel comfortable recommending acceptance.

Leaning weak reject, but willing to reconsider based on author response.

This paper proposes a three-stage pipeline (chunk, align, select) for handling long sequences with pre-trained transformers, targeting the well-known quadratic complexity bottleneck of self-attention. The general problem is well-motivated and practically important, but I have significant concerns about both the technical novelty and the empirical evaluation that I think need to be addressed before this could be accepted.

On the technical side: the chunking idea is not new (Longformer, BigBird, SLED, and many others do variants of this), and the "alignment" via start/end token embeddings feels like a minor variation on the [CLS] token propagation ideas that have appeared in prior work. I found myself asking after the second read — what's actually new here? The RL-based selection policy is probably the most interesting piece, but the description in Section 4.3 is honestly a bit muddled. The "dual updating scheme" with attention scores as rewards is conceptually interesting but I'm not entirely sure the reward formulation is well-justified. Why should high attention scores necessarily indicate representative hidden states? In my experience, attention scores are notoriously unreliable as importance proxies, and the paper doesn't really grapple with this criticism.

The empirical evaluation has some real problems. The comparisons in Table 2 and Table 3 feel cherry-picked — SLED and LED are included but I notice the authors don't compare against some very relevant recent baselines. Also, the improvements are modest in several cases (e.g., the gains on CNN/DailyMail in Table 2 are within what I'd consider noise range given the variance not reported). Speaking of which: where are the standard deviations? For RL-based methods especially, run-to-run variance can be substantial. This is a significant omission.

I also found the reading comprehension experiments (Section 5.2) somewhat unconvincing. The datasets used aren't the hardest benchmarks for long-context understanding — I'd want to see results on something like SCROLLS or QASPER to really believe the claims.

The writing is generally clear but there are places where precision suffers. The abstract says "selects the most representative hidden states" but what "representative" means is never formally defined until much later, and even then I found it a bit vague. Figure 2 is helpful but could be cleaner.

I want to be fair: the overall framework is coherent, the linear complexity claim appears valid, and the paper tackles a real problem. But in 2024 the long-sequence transformer space is crowded, and the bar for "simple framework" papers is that they need to be either strikingly simple AND clearly better, or offer genuine conceptual insight. This paper is somewhat simple and somewhat better, which I don't think clears the bar at this venue. I'd encourage the authors to sharpen the novelty claims, fix the evaluation gaps, and really stress-test the RL component. As it stands, I lean toward rejection but would reconsider with major revisions.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — extending fairness-aware domain generalization to handle arbitrary combinations of sensitive attributes at test time. The motivation is clear and the reduction from 2^n models to a single model is a compelling selling point. I'll be honest though: after reading this twice, I have significant concerns about both the technical contribution and the experimental validation.

On the technical side, the core idea of learning a "selective domain invariant representation" that aligns only domains sharing similar sensitive attributes is intuitive enough, but I found the formalization in Section 3 quite hand-wavy in places. The way the selective alignment loss is constructed in Eq. (4) — I'm not entirely sure the gradient dynamics here are actually doing what the authors claim. Specifically, how does the model avoid collapsing when domains with different sensitive attribute sets are being treated asymmetrically? This needs much more rigorous treatment. I kept waiting for a theoretical guarantee and it never came.

The experiments are my bigger concern. The benchmarks used feel a bit dated and narrow — I would have expected evaluation on something like CelebA across more diverse domain splits, or at minimum a more thorough ablation showing what happens as n (number of attributes) grows. The paper claims scalability to arbitrary n but the experiments mostly show n=2 or n=3. That's not convincing. Table 2 results also show improvements that are... modest. Some of the fairness metric gains are within what I'd consider noise range given the variance reported.

I also think the comparison to baselines is somewhat unfair (pun intended). The 2^n baseline is essentially a strawman — nobody actually trains 2^n models in practice. What about approaches that use attribute-conditioned representations or multi-task fairness methods? The related work section acknowledges some of these but they don't appear in the experimental comparison, which is a red flag.

The writing is generally okay but gets sloppy in Section 4.2, and Figure 3 is honestly hard to read — the color scheme makes it nearly impossible to distinguish the methods without printing in color.

I want to be fair (again, pun intended): the problem formulation is genuinely novel and I think there's a real paper here. The selective alignment idea has merit. But in its current state the contribution feels undercooked. The theoretical analysis is thin, the experiments don't fully stress-test the claimed scalability, and some design choices go unexplained.

My recommendation is reject with encouragement to resubmit. I'd want to see: (1) either theoretical guarantees or much more thorough empirical analysis of the selective alignment mechanism, (2) experiments with larger n and stronger baselines, and (3) a clearer discussion of failure modes. This feels like it needs another 3-4 months of work.

This paper proposes Acutum, an adaptive gradient method that claims to eliminate second moments from Adam while retaining both adaptivity and generalization. The core idea is interesting enough to warrant attention, but I have serious concerns about the execution and the claims being made.

Let me start with what I find genuinely intriguing. The observation that second moments in Adam might be separable from the "useful" adaptive behavior is a reasonable hypothesis, and the reinterpretation of momentum is at least thought-provoking. I've seen a few papers in this vein (e.g., work on AdaFactor, and various Adam variants) and the question of what second moments actually *do* for optimization is underexplored. So the motivation isn't crazy.

That said, I think the paper significantly oversells itself. The claim in the abstract that "Acutum is the first adaptive gradient method without second moments" is frankly not well-supported given the literature. AdaFactor (Shazeer & Stern, 2018) already substantially reduces or eliminates second moment storage in certain factored forms. The authors need to engage seriously with this and related work rather than making sweeping novelty claims.

The experimental section is where I have the most problems. The evaluations seem limited — I counted experiments on what appear to be a handful of standard benchmarks, and the comparisons don't include many strong modern baselines. Where's the comparison against AdamW with proper tuning? The learning rate sensitivity analysis feels incomplete. Figure 3 (if I'm reading it correctly) shows Acutum performing better than Adam on some training curves, but the differences look marginal in several cases, and the test performance gaps are not convincingly demonstrated. I'd want to see ImageNet-scale experiments or at least something more demanding than what's presented.

The convergence analysis in Section 4 — I read this twice and I'm not convinced the proof is tight. The assumptions made feel quite strong, and the resulting convergence rate doesn't obviously improve on what we already know about Adam/AMSGrad. What's the actual theoretical contribution here? The authors should be clearer about whether they're proving something genuinely new or just adapting existing proof techniques.

I also found the "novel point of view" on momentum (Section 3 I believe) to be somewhat vaguely stated. The intuition is described informally but the connection to the actual algorithm update rules feels hand-wavy. After reading this twice I still couldn't clearly articulate *why* removing second moments is justified beyond "it works empirically."

My honest assessment: this is a borderline paper with an interesting core idea that isn't developed rigorously enough. The writing needs work — there are awkward phrasings throughout and the abstract makes claims the body doesn't fully support. I'd want to see stronger experiments, a more honest literature comparison, and a cleaner theoretical story before recommending acceptance. Not there yet.

This paper addresses post-training quantization for DNNs, specifically targeting the scenario where you want to deploy to INT8 accelerators without fine-tuning and with limited calibration data. The problem is real and practically important, I'll grant that.

That said, I have significant concerns about the novelty and technical depth here. The core idea — recognizing channel-level distributions to reduce quantization error — isn't new. This is essentially per-channel quantization, which has been explored extensively. Krishnamoorthi (2018), Banner et al.'s ACIQ work, and several others have already established that per-channel scaling substantially outperforms per-tensor approaches for post-training quantization. I read through the method section (pages 4-6) twice and I'm honestly struggling to identify what is genuinely novel here beyond combining some known techniques. The authors should be much more explicit about what their specific contribution is relative to this prior work — and some of this prior work isn't even cited.

The experimental section covers eleven ImageNet networks plus a Pascal VOC detection network, which is reasonably thorough. Table 2 shows the accuracy numbers and they look... fine? Competitive with what I'd expect for 8-bit PTQ. But there's no comparison to contemporary PTQ methods. Without baselines like ACIQ or the Qualcomm per-channel quantization approach, I can't assess whether these results are actually good or just acceptable. This is a pretty serious omission for a methods paper.

On the calibration data question — the paper claims to minimize required image samples for profiling, which I found to be one of the more interesting angles. But the analysis of *how many* samples are needed (Figure 3, I think?) feels underdeveloped. In my experience, understanding the sample efficiency tradeoff is actually the hard and interesting problem here, and the paper kind of glosses over it.

The writing is serviceable but there are clarity issues. The description of the clipping strategy around equations 3-4 is confusing — I'm not entirely sure whether they're doing a fixed percentile clip or an optimization-based one. This needs to be stated more precisely.

I'm also a bit skeptical of the framing around "rapid deployment." This is really just post-training quantization. Calling it rapid deployment feels like marketing language dressed up as a technical contribution.

Bottom line: the paper tackles a relevant practical problem and the empirical results seem reasonable, but the technical novelty is thin, the related work coverage is inadequate, and the experimental comparison to existing PTQ methods is missing entirely. I'd need to see a clearer articulation of what's new, proper baselines, and a more rigorous treatment of the sample efficiency claims before I could recommend acceptance. As it stands, this reads more like a technical report than a research contribution.

Leaning toward rejection, though I acknowledge I might be wrong about the novelty if there's something subtle I missed in the method description.

This paper proposes using the Viterbi algorithm to generate structured sparse matrices with fixed-ratio index compression, which is an interesting angle on the weight pruning problem. I'll be honest — I found the core idea somewhat clever, but the presentation and experimental validation leave a lot to be desired.

Let me start with what I think is genuinely novel here: the insight that you can frame sparse pattern selection as a trellis-based search problem, allowing the index structure to be decoded with fixed complexity regardless of pruning rate. That's not nothing. In my experience reviewing compression papers, most work either ignores the index overhead entirely or treats it as an afterthought, so at least the authors are attacking a real problem.

That said, I have serious concerns. The experimental evaluation is, frankly, thin. MNIST and AlexNet? In 2024? After reading this twice I kept waiting for results on anything resembling a modern architecture — a ResNet, a transformer, anything. The authors demonstrate index compression ratios but the accuracy vs. compression tradeoff curves are not convincingly presented. Table 2 shows some numbers but I'm not confident I understand the baseline comparison conditions — are we comparing at the same sparsity level? The paper is unclear on this around page 6-7.

The claim of "similar pruning rate" achieving 85.2% index storage reduction is the headline result but I think it's undersold AND potentially misleading simultaneously. The comparison against magnitude-based pruning on MNIST is almost not worth including — that's a trivially easy dataset. I'd want to see this on ImageNet-scale models badly.

I'm also not entirely sure the hardware efficiency claims (Section 4, I think) are adequately substantiated. The authors describe the decoder as "highly parallelizable" but provide no actual hardware implementation results, no FPGA or ASIC numbers, no latency measurements. It reads as theoretical assertion rather than demonstrated advantage.

The related work section misses some fairly recent structured pruning and lottery ticket literature that would contextualize this better. The connection to convolutional code theory is interesting but I wonder if the authors are the first to notice this — I might be wrong but I feel like there's been some work in the information theory community touching on similar ideas that deserves citation.

Writing quality is acceptable but not polished. Several sentences in the abstract and introduction are awkward in ways that made me reread them.

My overall assessment: the core idea has merit and I think there's a publishable paper somewhere in here, but this version needs substantially stronger empirical validation on modern benchmarks and the hardware claims need to either be backed up or toned down significantly. I'm leaning toward rejection in current form, but I'd genuinely encourage the authors to resubmit with more thorough experiments. The Viterbi framing is creative enough that I don't want to dismiss it outright.

This paper tackles representation learning for model-based RL, specifically in the "learnable controllable embedding" framework. The core idea — using a policy iteration style algorithm rather than iLQR in the latent space, and weighting the representation loss by TD-error — is reasonable and the experiments show consistent improvements over baselines. I think the paper is generally solid but has some issues that prevent me from being fully enthusiastic.

Let me start with what I found compelling. The value-guided CARL variant is the most interesting contribution in my opinion. Weighting the representation loss by TD-error to focus on "important" transitions is a natural idea that I'm surprised hasn't been more thoroughly explored. The ablations in Table 2 do support that this helps, though I wish there was more analysis of *why* — for instance, are the high TD-error states clustered in particular regions of the state space? A simple visualization would go a long way here.

My bigger concern is with the framing and novelty claims. The paper positions the move from iLQR to soft actor-critic as a significant contribution, but honestly, this feels somewhat incremental. iLQR requires locally-linear dynamics assumptions that are obviously limiting, so it's not surprising that replacing it with a more general RL algorithm helps. The interesting question is *how* the representation should change to accommodate this, and I found the theoretical justification in Section 3 (around equations 4-6) a bit hand-wavy. After reading this twice I'm still not entirely convinced the CARL objective is doing something fundamentally different from prior LCE objectives beyond the algorithmic substitution.

The experimental section is fairly thorough — I appreciate the variety of tasks and the comparison against multiple baselines. However, I'm skeptical about some of the benchmark choices. Several tasks in Figure 3 look like they saturate quickly regardless of method, making it hard to distinguish approaches. Why not include harder tasks where the representation bottleneck is genuinely binding? In my experience, papers like this tend to cherry-pick environments where their method shines.

The online CARL section feels undercooked. The interleaving scheme described in Section 4.2 raises obvious questions about stability and sample efficiency that are not addressed empirically. Does this ever diverge? What are the practical hyperparameter sensitivities?

Minor things: the related work section is decent but misses some relevant work on bisimulation metrics (e.g., Zhang et al. 2021) which is directly related to learning task-relevant representations. The writing is generally clear though there are some awkward phrasings throughout — "amenable to the control problem at hand" appears almost verbatim three times.

Overall I'm on the fence. The empirical results are real and the value-guided variant has genuine novelty. But the theoretical contributions feel thin and the paper could do much more to explain *why* these design choices work. Borderline accept for me, leaning toward weak accept if the authors can address the theoretical clarity issues.

This paper proposes Dateformer, a transformer-based model for long-term time series forecasting that uses date-based patching and stores "global" information in time representations rather than relying solely on the lookback window. The core idea — encoding information into calendar-based representations that persist beyond the training window — is genuinely interesting, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to address a real limitation in how most forecasting models are deployed.

That said, I have significant concerns about this paper in its current form.

The biggest issue for me is the experimental evaluation. The claim of "33.6% relative improvement" in the abstract is striking, but after reading the results section twice I still couldn't figure out exactly where this number comes from or what the baseline is. Is this average across all datasets? Weighted somehow? This needs to be clearly stated. The comparison baselines in Table 2 (if I'm reading it correctly) seem to exclude some fairly obvious recent competitors — notably PatchTST, which is directly relevant since it also does patch-based processing. I might be missing something but this feels like a significant omission given how closely related it is.

The "patch by day" idea is the central technical contribution, and I think the motivation is reasonable, but the paper doesn't do enough work to separate the contribution of (a) daily patching versus (b) the time representation distillation. The ablation in Table 4 helps somewhat, but I'd want to see a version that uses daily patches without the global time representation, compared against PatchTST with similar patch sizes. Without this, I can't tell what's actually driving the gains.

I also found the writing in Section 3 quite hard to follow — the description of how time representations are constructed and updated during training is muddled. Equations 3-5 feel underspecified. What exactly is being "distilled"? Is this a learned embedding? A running average? The paper uses the word "distill" loosely and I think this obscures what is actually a fairly straightforward idea (if I understand it correctly).

The extension to half-year forecasting is presented as a major contribution, but the evaluation here is thin. One dataset, and the comparison is limited. In my experience, long-horizon evaluations need more careful treatment of how error accumulates and whether the model is actually capturing meaningful patterns versus just regressing to seasonal means.

Minor things: Figure 1 is cluttered and I found it more confusing than helpful. The related work section reads like a list and doesn't really engage with why prior approaches fail.

I think there's a real idea buried here, and the problem framing is good. But the paper needs substantially stronger empirical validation, clearer technical exposition, and honest engagement with closely related work before it's ready for publication at this venue. I lean toward rejection, though I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically important problem — diffusion models are slow, and the community needs better solutions. The core insight, that the Gaussian denoising assumption only holds for small steps and therefore forces many iterations, is reasonable and I think mostly correct. The proposed solution of using a conditional GAN to model each denoising step is clever, and I appreciate that the authors make a genuine theoretical argument for *why* their approach works rather than just empirically throwing GANs at the problem.

That said, I have some real concerns.

The experimental results (Table 1, CIFAR-10) look impressive — the 2000x speedup claim is eye-catching. But I found myself suspicious about the comparison setup. The baseline diffusion models being compared against use 1000 steps; it would strengthen the paper considerably to compare against more recent fast sampling methods like DDIM (Song et al.) and DPM-Solver, which can get decent quality in 20-50 steps. The authors mention DDIM briefly but the comparison feels somewhat underdeveloped. After reading this twice I still wasn't sure if the speedup claim holds up against *optimized* baselines, or just against the naive 1000-step setup.

The mode coverage analysis in Section 4 is appreciated but I found it a bit hand-wavy. The recall metric is used but in my experience recall estimates are notoriously noisy, especially on CIFAR-10. I'd want to see this more carefully validated, perhaps with some visualization of failure modes.

I'm also not entirely sure about the stability of the training procedure. GAN training is notoriously finicky, and now we're training a GAN *inside* a diffusion framework. The paper discusses this somewhat but I wanted more details — what happens when the inner GAN mode-collapses at a particular noise level? Is there any analysis of training stability across different timestep ranges? Figure 3 shows some qualitative results but doesn't really address this.

The writing is generally clear, though Section 3.2 gets a bit dense around equations (8)-(10) and could use a clearer intuitive explanation before diving into the math.

One thing I genuinely liked: the ablation in Table 2 is actually informative and tests the right things. More papers should do this.

My main hesitation for acceptance is the incomplete comparison with fast sampling baselines. The trilemma framing is nice marketing but it's not a new observation — people have noted this tension before. The novelty lies in the technical solution, which is solid but needs stronger empirical grounding. I'd lean toward weak accept if the authors can address the DDIM/fast sampler comparison more seriously. As it stands this is interesting work that I think will influence follow-up research, but the claims are slightly ahead of the evidence.

This paper proposes a receiver-to-receiver acoustic warping framework (SPEAR) that sidesteps the traditional source-to-receiver modeling pipeline. The core idea — warping audio from one receiver position to another rather than modeling full acoustic propagation — is genuinely interesting and I can see why it's appealing from a practical standpoint. The theoretical proof of existence of the warping field under the single-source condition is a nice contribution, and I appreciate that the authors tried to ground the network design in physical principles.

That said, I have several concerns that prevent me from recommending acceptance in the current form.

The "if and only if one audio source presents" condition is doing a lot of heavy lifting here, and I don't think the paper adequately addresses how sensitive the method is to this assumption in practice. Real acoustic environments almost never have a single clean source — there's background noise, reflections that interact with multiple sources, etc. The real-world experiments seem thin to me (I went back and read Section 4.3 twice and I'm still not convinced the real-world evaluation is rigorous enough to support the claims made). How was the single-source condition enforced or verified in those recordings?

The comparison baselines in Table 2 feel a bit cherry-picked. I'm familiar with several neural audio rendering approaches from the past couple years — stuff like NAF and similar implicit function methods — and I'm not sure the most competitive baselines are included. This is important because the claimed "superiority" in the title and abstract is quite strong language.

Figure 3 is honestly hard to parse. I spent too long trying to understand the warping field visualization and I think the authors could do much better here with clearer visual explanation of what the network is actually learning.

I'm also mildly bothered by the framing around "two robots independently recording." This sounds elegant but in practice what does this mean for deployment? The method still requires paired recordings at reference and target positions during training, which isn't obviously easier to obtain than other supervision signals. The practical accessibility argument needs to be made more carefully.

The writing is generally okay but there are places where the physics motivation feels hand-wavy — specifically around the three physical principles incorporated into the network design (Section 3.3 I think). The reciprocity principle application in particular — I might be wrong but I don't think the justification given fully holds for reverberant fields.

Minor: the ablation study is appreciated but I wish it were more thorough. Dropping one principle at a time doesn't tell us much about interactions.
