Overall: interesting idea with real novelty in the receiver-to-receiver framing, but the evaluation needs strengthening and some of the theoretical claims need tighter treatment. I'm on the fence, leaning weak reject, but I'd genuinely reconsider with a strong rebuttal addressing the baseline comparisons and real-world evaluation concerns.

This paper proposes SoftHash, a data-dependent hashing method inspired by the fly olfactory system that extends FlyHash with a soft Winner-Take-All (WTA) mechanism and Hebbian-like learning. The core idea is reasonable and I appreciate the biological motivation, but I have significant concerns about the paper as presented.

Let me start with what I think works. The soft WTA idea is intuitive — the argument that hard WTA fully suppresses weakly correlated data during learning is plausible, and the proposed relaxation makes biological and computational sense. The experiments span multiple datasets and include both image retrieval and word similarity tasks, which gives some breadth.

That said, I found the technical presentation frustratingly thin in places. The Hebbian learning rule is described somewhat loosely, and after reading Section 3 twice I'm still not entirely clear on the exact update equations and their convergence properties. Equation (4) or so — the weight update rule — needs more careful treatment. Is there a formal guarantee that this converges? What's the sensitivity to the softness hyperparameter? The ablation on this parameter (Table 3, I believe) shows some results but doesn't really tell me *why* certain values work better.

My bigger concern is the comparison to baselines. The paper compares against FlyHash and a handful of learning-based hashing methods, but notably absent are several recent strong baselines from 2021-2023. In my experience reviewing hashing papers, the field has moved fast, and the numbers here — especially on CIFAR and NUS-WIDE — feel like they might be cherry-picked against weaker competitors. Why isn't GreedyHash or HashNet included? The authors should justify these omissions explicitly.

The speed claims in the abstract are stated pretty boldly but Figure 4's runtime comparison is, honestly, not convincing. The wall-clock times seem measured under conditions that favor SoftHash, and there's no discussion of hardware, batch sizes, or whether the baselines were properly optimized. "Significantly outperforms" is a strong claim.

I'm also a bit skeptical about the biological motivation framing. It feels somewhat retrofitted — the paper doesn't really engage with the neuroscience literature beyond citing a couple of FlyHash papers. Either lean into the biology more seriously or dial back the claims about what this has to do with olfactory circuits.

Minor things: Figure 1 is useful but the caption is too sparse. Some notation in Section 3 is introduced without definition. The related work section is adequate but misses some relevant work on sparse coding and dictionary learning, which is conceptually related.

Overall, I think there's a publishable idea here but the paper needs a stronger empirical evaluation with up-to-date baselines, cleaner theoretical exposition, and more honest qualification of its claims. In its current form I lean toward rejection, though I'd be willing to reconsider with major revisions if the authors can address the baseline comparison issue seriously.

This paper tackles an interesting theoretical question about the expressive power of spiking neural networks (SNNs) compared to standard ReLU networks. The topic is timely given the renewed interest in neuromorphic computing, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to put SNNs on firmer theoretical footing. That said, I have significant concerns about the paper as presented.

The main claimed contribution — that a single spiking neuron can generate exponentially many linear regions with respect to input dimension — is striking if true. But I found the proof in Section 4 (around page 8-9) genuinely difficult to follow, and after reading it twice I'm still not confident I understand the argument fully. The analysis relies heavily on properties of the Spike Response Model with linear response functions, and I worry that this linearity assumption is doing a lot of work that the authors aren't being fully transparent about. How restrictive is this assumption in practice? Most biologically-motivated SRM formulations use alpha functions or exponential kernels, not linear ones. The paper should be much clearer about what is lost by making this simplification.

The comparison to ReLU networks in Theorem 3 feels a bit loose to me. The complexity notion used — "similar complexity" — isn't defined with the rigor I'd expect. What exactly does similar mean here? Same number of parameters? Same depth? I might be wrong but I think the comparison is not apples-to-apples and could mislead readers into thinking the emulation result is stronger than it actually is.

The related work coverage is okay but misses some important recent papers on SNN expressivity. The work by Maass on computational power of SNNs is cited, but there are more recent theoretical treatments (e.g., work on rate-coded SNNs and their VC dimension) that would strengthen the positioning of this paper. I'd also want to see engagement with the piecewise linear network literature more broadly — Montufar et al. 2014 is cited but the connection to their exponential bounds deserves more careful discussion since the authors are essentially claiming an analogous result for a different neuron model.

My biggest complaint is honestly about the practical implications section near the end. The claim that SNNs can replace ANNs "without any performance loss" is not supported by the theoretical results, which are about function approximation capacity, not about whether we can actually train SNNs to achieve this. That's a significant leap and borders on overselling.

The writing is generally clear though there are some awkward passages. Figure 2 is helpful for building intuition.

Overall, I think there's a real paper here but it needs substantial revision. The core technical contributions need cleaner proofs and more careful statements, the assumptions need better justification, and the authors should dial back the more ambitious practical claims. Not ready for publication in current form.

This paper tackles an important and timely problem — data selection for instruction fine-tuning of LLMs — and I appreciate that the authors attempt to handle multiple fine-tuning stages under a unified framework. That said, I have significant concerns about both the novelty claims and the experimental evaluation.

The core contribution is the "pairwise utility metric" described around equations 3-4, which essentially measures how much one sample reduces uncertainty/improves responses on another. After reading this twice, I'm honestly not convinced this is as novel as presented. This feels quite close to facility location and related submodular data selection ideas that have appeared in prior work (e.g., CRAIG, GRAD-MATCH, and particularly the SIMILAR framework which the authors do cite but I think understate the relationship to). The authors should be much more explicit about what is genuinely new here versus clever application of existing machinery.

On experiments: Table 2 is doing a lot of heavy lifting in this paper, and I find myself skeptical. The baselines feel cherry-picked or undertuned. In my experience, random subset selection at 30% of data is actually a surprisingly strong baseline for fine-tuning, and I'd want to see confidence intervals or multiple runs reported. The variance is never discussed. Also, the "up to 70% reduction" claim in the abstract is doing classic oversell — "up to" is doing a lot of work there, and the actual average reduction while maintaining performance parity seems to be more like 40-50% from what I can piece together from the tables. Minor thing but it bothers me.

The continual learning stage (Section 4.3 I believe) is the most interesting part to me and ironically gets the least attention. This feels like where the method has the clearest differentiation from prior work and the results there, while promising, are thin. I'd want to see this expanded significantly.

I also found the computational cost analysis unconvincing. The authors claim efficiency advantages over gradient-based methods, which is fair, but the actual wall-clock times reported in Table 4 seem to vary quite a bit depending on model size and the submodular optimization itself isn't free. The complexity analysis feels hand-wavy.

One thing I genuinely liked: the framing of the three stages as a unified problem is clean and the paper is generally well-written. Figure 2 is actually quite helpful for understanding the overall pipeline.

I'm not entirely sure about whether the submodular function choice (facility location vs. graph cut vs. log-determinant) is principled or post-hoc justified — the ablation in Section 5.2 hints at sensitivity but doesn't fully address this.

Overall: the paper addresses a real problem, has reasonable results, but the novelty is overstated and the experimental rigor needs work. I'm leaning toward rejection but I could be convinced otherwise if the authors can sharpen the novelty claims and provide stronger experimental validation with proper statistical testing.

This paper proposes DiagGCN, which extends standard GCN formulations by incorporating diagonal matrix operations to enable adaptive neighborhood aggregation. The core claim is that diagonal scaling of node representations improves expressiveness, and that GAT can be seen as a special case of this framework.

I'll be honest — I found the theoretical framing somewhat compelling at first, but after reading the relevant sections twice (particularly Section 3), I became less convinced the contribution is as substantial as the authors suggest. The idea of using diagonal matrices for adaptive scaling is not particularly novel in itself. Work on attention mechanisms, feature-wise transformations, and even some older signal processing literature on graph wavelets has touched on similar ideas. The authors do cite GAT and a few others, but I think the related work undersells how much prior art exists here. I might be wrong about some specific references, but this felt like a gap to me.

The reformulation of GAT as a special case (Section 4, around equation 8-ish) is interesting, but I'm not entirely sure the equivalence holds as cleanly as claimed. The authors seem to paper over some differences in how attention coefficients are computed versus how the diagonal entries are trained. I'd want to see this spelled out more rigorously.

On experiments — Table 2 shows improvements on several graph classification benchmarks, and the numbers look decent. But the gains are often marginal (within 1-2% on several datasets), and the variance isn't always reported consistently. PROTEINS and MUTAG results especially look like they could be within noise. In my experience reviewing graph learning papers, these benchmarks are notoriously sensitive to hyperparameter choices and data splits. The paper doesn't say enough about how hyperparameters were selected or how many runs were averaged.

Figure 3 is supposed to illustrate the adaptive aggregation behavior but honestly I found it hard to interpret. The caption is too brief.

The writing is generally acceptable but not great. There are several awkward phrasings throughout, and Section 2 (background) goes on longer than necessary — I almost fell asleep reading it. Some of that material could be moved to an appendix.

My bigger concern is the "so what" question. The theoretical contribution feels incremental — diagonal matrices add expressiveness, okay, but how much? Is there a formal characterization of the expressiveness gain relative to WL hierarchy or other established measures? The paper gestures at this but doesn't deliver anything rigorous.

I think there's a real idea here that's worth developing, but in its current form the paper reads more like a workshop submission than a full conference paper. The theoretical analysis needs strengthening, the experiments need more careful validation, and the relationship to prior work deserves more honest treatment. I'm leaning toward rejection, but I wouldn't be upset if the other reviewers see more value here than I did.

This paper presents MAESTRO, a self-supervised transformer-based method for learning set representations from cytometry data. The core idea — treating a patient sample as a *set* of cells and learning a representation at the sample level rather than the cell level — is reasonable and I think genuinely useful for downstream clinical tasks. That said, I have significant concerns about the paper as it currently stands.

Let me start with what I found compelling. The permutation invariance requirement for cytometry data is well-motivated, and the use of masked encoding with self-distillation (à la DINO/iBOT-style frameworks) applied to set-structured data is a nice conceptual contribution. Figure 3 is actually quite clear in illustrating the architecture.

But here's where things get frustrating. The experimental evaluation feels thin. The baselines are, frankly, not strong enough. Comparing against "existing cytometry approaches" is fine, but the claim that MAESTRO outperforms "other existing machine learning methods that have never been applied in cytometry" is doing a lot of rhetorical work without much substance. Which methods? Why were they chosen? I counted at least three places in Section 4 where the authors reference comparisons without clearly specifying the baseline configurations or hyperparameters. This makes reproducibility a real concern.

I'm also not entirely convinced by the ablations. Table 2 shows improvements from the self-distillation component, but the differences are often within what I'd consider noise range given the dataset sizes reported. Speaking of which — the datasets used are relatively small by modern deep learning standards, and I think the authors need to be more careful about overclaiming generalization.

The writing needs work. The abstract has a grammatical error ("Unlike previous studies only learn cell-level representations") and this kind of sloppiness carries through the paper. After reading this twice I was still unclear on some of the tokenization details in Section 3.2 — specifically, how the "online tokenizer" differs mechanistically from standard DINO-style momentum encoders. Is this actually novel or is it largely the same thing adapted to sets? The paper gestures at this distinction but never quite lands the explanation.

One more thing that bothered me: the clinical relevance claims. Showing improved disease diagnosis metrics on a couple of cytometry benchmarks is interesting, but the leap to "clinically relevant features" in the abstract overstates what's actually demonstrated. Real clinical utility requires much more rigorous validation.

I think there's a publishable paper somewhere in here — the problem setting is important, the approach is sensible, and cytometry analysis genuinely needs better representation learning tools. But in its current form, the evaluation is insufficiently rigorous and the novelty over existing self-distillation frameworks isn't clearly articulated. I'd recommend a major revision focusing on stronger baselines, clearer technical differentiation, and more conservative claims.

This paper tackles an interesting and relatively underexplored problem in federated learning — the hybrid setting where both feature and sample heterogeneity coexist. I'll give the authors credit for identifying a gap in the literature and attempting a unified formulation. The problem itself is well-motivated in the abstract.

That said, I have significant concerns about the technical contributions and the experimental evaluation that I think need to be addressed before this work is ready for publication.

Starting with the problem formulation: the mathematical model introduced in Section 3 (around equations 3-5 if I recall correctly from my reading) is presented as novel, but I found the connection to existing split learning and vertical FL literature somewhat hand-wavy. The claim that "this is the first formulation and algorithm developed for hybrid FL" is a strong one. I'm not entirely sure this is accurate — there are several works in the split FL and multi-task FL literature that handle overlapping feature/sample spaces, and the authors' related work discussion doesn't convincingly distinguish their approach from, say, work on multi-view federated learning. This needs a much more careful treatment.

The feature extractor / classifier decomposition idea is sensible but not particularly surprising. In my experience, this kind of modular decomposition has been explored extensively in transfer learning and domain adaptation contexts, and the novelty here feels incremental. The matching mechanism for aggregating heterogeneous extractors is where I expected to see the real technical meat, but the description felt rushed and I had to read it twice to understand what exactly was being matched and on what basis. Figure 2 helps somewhat but the caption is inadequate.

The experiments are my biggest concern. Testing only on image classification datasets (MNIST variants from what I can tell) is insufficient to support the broad claims being made. The baselines chosen are weak — comparing against vanilla FedAvg in a setting FedAvg wasn't designed for is not a fair or informative comparison. Where is the comparison against vertical FL methods adapted to this setting? The communication efficiency claims in particular need ablation studies that I don't see anywhere in the paper.

I also think the convergence analysis, if there is one, needs strengthening. I noticed the theoretical guarantees have fairly restrictive assumptions that aren't well-justified for the hybrid setting.

Look, the problem is real and worth solving. I don't want to be entirely dismissive. But as currently written, this reads more like a workshop paper than a full conference submission. The implementation section feels like it was added to pad contributions. I'd recommend a major revision with substantially stronger baselines, clearer novelty claims relative to prior work, and ideally experiments on non-image modalities to demonstrate the generality of the approach.

This paper tackles a genuinely interesting and practically relevant problem: how to select hyperparameter configurations that satisfy multiple simultaneous statistical guarantees while optimizing unconstrained objectives. The core idea of combining Pareto frontier construction with multiple hypothesis testing is clean and, I think, fairly well-motivated.

That said, I have some significant concerns that I'd like the authors to address.

The theoretical contribution feels somewhat incremental to me. The paper essentially applies existing risk control machinery (leaning heavily on the Learn then Test framework and related work) to a Pareto-filtered candidate set rather than the full grid. The key insight — that testing only Pareto-optimal configurations reduces the multiple testing burden — is sensible but not surprising, and I'm not entirely convinced the formal novelty clears the bar for this venue. The authors should be more explicit about exactly what is new theoretically versus what is a straightforward application of prior results. The paragraph around equations 4-6 (if I'm reading the theorem statements correctly) seems to be doing most of the heavy lifting, and it went by a bit fast for me.

On the empirical side: the NLP experiments with early exit and attention pruning are a reasonable testbed, but I found myself wanting more baselines. How does Pareto Testing compare against, say, just running risk control on a dense grid of configurations without the Pareto pre-filtering? The paper argues the Pareto stage improves power/efficiency, but Table 2 doesn't fully convince me — the gains in some configurations look modest, and I'd want to see statistical error bars more prominently. Also, the choice of Transformer acceleration as the sole application domain feels a bit limiting given how broadly the method is pitched in the abstract.

I'm also not fully satisfied with the discussion of computational cost. The paper emphasizes efficiency, but constructing the Pareto frontier itself requires evaluating many configurations on some dataset — how expensive is this in practice? This feels glossed over.

One thing I genuinely liked: Figure 3 does a nice job visualizing the tradeoff between the constrained and unconstrained objectives, and it made the method much more concrete. More of this kind of illustration would strengthen the paper.

Minor issues: the notation gets a bit heavy around Section 3.2, and after reading this twice I still wasn't fully clear on the distinction between the calibration set and the test set used for hypothesis testing. A clearer procedural description or pseudocode would help significantly.

Overall, this is a solid piece of work with a useful practical contribution, but the theoretical novelty feels overstated and the experiments need strengthening. I'm on the fence — leaning toward weak reject, but I'd be willing to reconsider if the authors can clarify the theoretical delta from prior work and provide stronger empirical comparisons. The problem is real and the approach is reasonable; it just needs sharper presentation and justification.

This paper tackles an important problem — establishing uniform-in-time propagation of chaos for mean-field gradient Langevin dynamics — and I think the technical contribution is real, though I have some concerns about presentation and the practical implications that are claimed.

Let me start with what I found genuinely valuable. The key novelty, as far as I can tell, is avoiding the "weak interaction" assumption that plagues most prior work in this space (Sznitman-type arguments, etc.). The authors instead leverage a log-Sobolev type condition, and this is a meaningful conceptual shift. In neural network optimization, the interaction strength can't generally be made small without trivializing the problem, so this is a legitimate gap being addressed. The O(1/N) rate uniform in time is a clean result.

That said, I found the paper somewhat difficult to parse in places. The discussion around Assumption 3 (the LSI-type condition) on page 8 felt rushed to me — after reading it twice I was still uncertain about which class of risk minimization problems actually satisfies this. The authors gesture at "regularized settings" but the examples provided feel a bit hand-wavy. How strong is the regularization required? Is this compatible with the scale of regularization used in any practical neural network training? I'm skeptical, and I think this deserves a more careful discussion. This is not a minor point — it affects whether the paper's claimed relevance to "practical finite-width models" holds up.

The comparison with Lacker (2023) and Chizat (2022) in the related work section could be sharper. I'm not entirely sure the paper adequately distinguishes its technical contribution from Lacker's entropy-based approach — there's some overlap in the proof strategy that isn't fully acknowledged.

Figure 1 is nice conceptually but doesn't add much technical content. I'd rather see a table comparing rates and assumptions across prior work — that would make the contribution much clearer at a glance.

I'm also a bit bothered by the framing around "practical finite-width models." The O(1/N) bound is uniform in time, yes, but the constants in the bound — do they depend exponentially on problem parameters? This is buried somewhat in the proofs and I think deserves explicit discussion. In my experience, these constants often make finite-N guarantees vacuous for any N you'd actually train.

The writing is generally competent but could use tightening. Some proof sketches in Section 4 are dense to the point of being unhelpful. I found myself wishing for a cleaner "proof strategy" paragraph before diving into the estimates.

Overall: I think this is a solid theoretical contribution that belongs in the conference, but the gap between the technical results and the practical claims needs to be addressed honestly. I'd ask the authors to clarify the LSI condition applicability, discuss the constant dependencies more explicitly, and sharpen the comparison with related work. Leaning toward acceptance contingent on these revisions.

This paper tackles an important and underappreciated problem — the sensitivity of LLMs to prompt formatting choices — and I think the community genuinely needs work like this. The 76 accuracy point swing on LLaMA-2-13B is striking, even if I suspect readers familiar with the prompting literature won't be entirely shocked by large variance numbers. The real contribution here is the systematic characterization rather than the discovery itself.

That said, I have some concerns. The FormatSpread algorithm (Section 4, if I'm reading the structure right) is presented as a key contribution, but after reading this twice I'm still not entirely clear on what's novel about the sampling procedure versus just... trying a bunch of formats. The framing around "without accessing model weights" is emphasized repeatedly but I'm not sure why this is the right desideratum — in practice, anyone running these evaluations has API access at minimum, and the more pressing issue is compute cost, not weight access. I might be wrong about the practical motivation here but it felt underexplained.

The finding that format performance correlates weakly across models (Figure 5 I think?) is actually the most important empirical result in the paper and I wish it received more attention. This directly undermines the standard benchmarking practice of using a single shared prompt across model comparisons, which is a genuinely damaging methodological critique. But the paper somewhat buries this. The discussion around it is thin compared to how much space is spent on the atomic perturbation analysis.

On the internal representation experiments — I found this section interesting but a bit underbaked. The probing results gesture at something meaningful about how models encode format-specific information, but the connection back to the behavioral sensitivity results isn't tightly drawn. It reads like a separate mini-paper that got stapled on.

My bigger gripe is the scope of models tested. LLaMA-2 variants and a couple others — in my experience these findings are likely to generalize, but the paper makes fairly strong methodological recommendations (report ranges of performance, not single-format numbers) that would benefit from demonstrating this sensitivity holds more broadly. The instruction-tuned models showing sensitivity is an important finding and I'm glad it's included.

The title is cute and I appreciate the Kubrick reference, but I wonder if it's doing the paper a disservice by making it seem lighter than it is.

Overall this is solid, publishable work with a clear message the community needs to hear. The recommendation to report performance intervals rather than point estimates is practical and I support it. I'd push the authors to tighten the FormatSpread motivation and give the cross-model correlation finding the prominence it deserves. Minor revision territory for me, leaning toward accept.

This paper proposes ReWOO, a system that separates the reasoning/planning phase of augmented language models from the actual tool-call observations, essentially generating a full plan upfront before executing any tools. The core idea is intuitive and the efficiency argument is straightforward — by not re-encoding the entire growing prompt context at each reasoning step, you save a lot of redundant computation.

I'll be honest: I found this paper reasonably compelling on a first read, but some cracks appear on closer inspection. The central claim of "5x token efficiency" on HotpotQA (highlighted in the abstract) is real but also somewhat cherry-picked — the gains vary considerably across benchmarks, and Table 2 shows much more modest improvements on several tasks. I'd appreciate a more nuanced discussion of *when* this approach actually helps versus when interleaving is genuinely necessary.

My bigger concern is about the fundamental assumption baked into this whole approach. ReWOO assumes you can plan all tool calls upfront without seeing intermediate observations. This works fine for certain retrieval-heavy QA tasks, but it seems like it would fail badly on tasks requiring adaptive tool use — where the result of tool call N determines whether you even need tool call N+1. The authors acknowledge this briefly but don't really stress-test it. The benchmarks chosen (HotpotQA, TriviaQA, etc.) are somewhat favorable to the approach by design. I'd want to see performance on something like ALFWorld or WebArena where adaptive decision-making is actually critical.

The finetuning section (Section 5) is interesting — distilling GPT-3.5 reasoning into 7B LLaMA is a genuinely useful contribution and I think this might actually be the most practically impactful part of the paper. But the evaluation here feels thin. Table 4 shows results on only a couple benchmarks and I'm not sure the comparison is entirely fair given differences in prompting setups.

A few smaller things: Figure 2 is helpful but the caption could do more work explaining what we're actually looking at. The "Solver" module description around page 5 was a bit unclear to me on first reading — specifically whether the solver sees the observations at inference time or not. I think it does, but the writing around equations 1-2 could be tightened. Also, the "robustness under tool failure" experiments are interesting but I found myself wanting more analysis of *why* ReWOO degrades more gracefully — is it just that the planner commits early and errors don't cascade, or something else?

Overall this is solid work. The idea is clean, the efficiency gains are real even if occasionally overstated, and the finetuning angle is genuinely novel. I'm leaning toward acceptance but I think the paper needs to be more honest about the limitations of upfront planning and should probably add at least one benchmark where adaptive reasoning matters. The writing is generally good though a few sections feel rushed.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — inferring emotional states from spatiotemporal behavioral data, with a particular focus on social activity patterns. The motivation is reasonable and the application to mental health monitoring is timely. That said, I have significant concerns about this submission that I think need to be addressed before it could be considered for publication.

First, the writing quality is quite poor throughout. The abstract alone contains redundancies ("social and social activity patterns" — what does this mean exactly?) and the framing is often vague to the point of obscuring the actual technical contribution. I had to read several sections twice and still wasn't entirely clear on what SADS actually does mechanistically. A cleaner description of the model architecture, ideally with a proper formalization, is badly needed. Figure 2 in particular is cluttered and I couldn't extract useful information from it without significant effort.

The experimental evaluation raises serious red flags for me. The datasets — SAPD22111510 and SAPD23031530 — are completely unknown to me, and I suspect to most readers. There's no reference to any public benchmark I'm aware of in this space. Are these proprietary datasets? How were they collected, what's the demographic, what are the ground truth emotional labels and how were they obtained? Self-reported? Clinical assessment? This matters enormously for interpreting the claimed 3.9% and 8.1% accuracy improvements. Without understanding the baseline and what "accuracy" even means here (classification accuracy? MAE on a continuous scale?), these numbers are essentially meaningless.

I'm also skeptical of the "first time" claim in the abstract. Work on inferring psychological states from mobility patterns and social behavior has been around for at least a decade — Saeb et al. (2015), Canzian & Musolesi (2015), and several others come to mind. The paper doesn't engage seriously with this literature. The related work section feels thin and mostly cites work that isn't directly comparable.

The theoretical framing around "multi-resolution" mapping between social activity sequences and emotional indices sounds interesting but is underdeveloped. What resolutions? How are they chosen? This felt like a promising idea that just... wasn't fleshed out.

I want to be fair — there may be something genuinely novel here, and the problem is worth studying. But in its current form the paper reads more like a draft or a workshop submission than a finished conference paper. The core contribution is unclear, the experimental setup is opaque, and the claims outpace the evidence. I'd encourage the authors to substantially revise with clearer technical exposition, proper dataset documentation, and a thorough comparison to the existing literature on mobility-based mental health inference.

Recommendation: Reject.

This paper proposes a domain adaptation method based on low-rank basis approximation, essentially finding a shared subspace representation to bridge source and target domains. I've read through this twice and I have to be honest — I came away with more questions than answers.

The core idea isn't bad. Subspace-based domain adaptation has a solid theoretical foundation and I appreciate that the authors are trying to address the practical concern of limited target domain data. That's a real problem. But the paper feels underdeveloped in almost every dimension that matters.

My biggest issue is the experimental evaluation. The results in Table 2 (and to some extent Table 3) are presented without sufficient statistical context — no confidence intervals, no significance tests, nothing. In my experience reviewing DA papers, cherry-picked benchmark results without error bars are essentially meaningless. The datasets used (I'm guessing Office-Caltech or similar standard benchmarks based on the abstract, though honestly the paper should be clearer about this much earlier) are also getting quite stale at this point. Where are comparisons against more recent baselines? The related work section reads like it stops around 2018-2019.

The claim that "efficient domain adaptation requires only a substantially smaller subset" is stated as if it's a theorem but never really proven rigorously. I might be wrong, but I don't see a formal bound anywhere that characterizes how much data you actually need. There's some intuition offered but the theoretical grounding is thin. This is the kind of claim that needs either solid theory or very convincing ablations — and I don't see either.

The writing is also a problem. Section 3 in particular I found confusing — the notation gets dense around equations 4-6 and I genuinely lost track of what the optimization objective was. A clearer exposition with maybe a worked example or diagram would help enormously. Figure 1 is supposed to illustrate the method but it's doing a lot of heavy lifting and not quite succeeding.

On the positive side, the computational complexity analysis is one of the stronger parts of the paper, and I do appreciate the authors' attention to the low-rank approximation rationale. That part feels technically sound.

But overall, the contribution feels incremental over existing subspace alignment methods (SA, GFK, etc.) and the paper doesn't do enough to convince me that the proposed approach offers meaningful advantages. The "real-world applicability" argument made in the abstract and introduction never really gets substantiated. Where are experiments with genuinely scarce target data? That's supposedly the whole motivation.

I'm recommending rejection. The paper needs substantially stronger experiments, clearer theoretical claims, and a more honest positioning relative to the current state of the field. This could potentially be a decent workshop paper but it's not ready for the main track.

This paper investigates whether relation decoding in transformer LMs can be approximated by a single linear transformation applied to subject representations. The core idea — constructing a first-order (Jacobian-based) approximation of the LM and using it as a "linear relational embedding" (LRE) — is reasonably elegant, and I appreciate that the authors are honest about the heterogeneity of results rather than overclaiming universality.

That said, I have some concerns I want to flag.

The evaluation methodology is where I kept getting stuck. The authors use "causally mediated accuracy" to filter which relations count as "linearly decodable," but this feels somewhat circular to me — you're essentially asking whether the linear approximation works well on cases where it already works well. I went back and forth on this while reading Section 4, and I'm still not fully convinced the filtering procedure doesn't inflate the reported linearity results. I might be wrong here, but the paper would benefit from a cleaner separation of these concepts.

The connection to prior work on linear representations (e.g., Mikolov's word2vec analogy work, and more recently work on probing classifiers and representation geometry) is acknowledged but I think undersold. The authors position this as a novel finding, but in my experience, the linearity hypothesis for factual relations has been floating around the community for years — what's new here is the specific mechanistic framing via the Jacobian approximation, and I wish that distinction were made crisper in the introduction.

Figure 3 is actually quite compelling and I think it's the strongest piece of evidence in the paper. The visualization of which relation types decompose linearly vs. not is genuinely informative. On the other hand, Table 2 is a mess — too many numbers, not enough guidance on what to actually look at.

The failure cases discussion is good and I'm glad it's there. The observation that some relations are accurately predicted but NOT linearly encoded is the most interesting finding in my opinion, and it's buried a bit late. This deserves more prominence and more analysis. Why does this happen? Is it a property of the relation type, the subject frequency, something about attention head routing? The paper gestures at this but doesn't really dig in.

One more thing: the claim that LREs can be used for "model editing" applications feels tacked on. The editing experiments in Section 6 are underdeveloped relative to actual model editing papers (ROME, MEMIT, etc.), and I'd honestly recommend either cutting this section or substantially expanding it.

Overall this is solid work with a clear contribution. The writing is generally good. I lean toward acceptance but with revisions — specifically around the evaluation methodology clarification and the failure case analysis. The paper is a bit long for what it delivers, and tightening it would help.

This paper tackles an interesting question — why do policy gradient methods seem to dominate in continuous/complex action spaces when there are known theoretical equivalences between PG and action-value methods in simpler settings? The core hypothesis is that the practical advantages of PG aren't intrinsic to the paradigm but arise from a few identifiable principles that can be ported over to Q-learning style methods. I find this framing genuinely compelling and worth pursuing.

That said, I have significant concerns about the execution.

The three "universal principles" identified (around page 4-5) feel somewhat post-hoc to me. After reading this section twice, I'm not fully convinced that the decomposition is principled rather than a reverse-engineering of what makes existing PG methods work. The authors present these as if they're derived from first principles, but I think the argument is more observational. I might be wrong here, but the paper would benefit from a cleaner theoretical justification of why exactly *these three* principles and not others.

QMLE itself — the instantiation — seems reasonable, and I appreciate that the authors are upfront about computational cost. The comparison to DMPO and D4PG on DMControl (Table 2, Figure 3) shows competitive performance, and I won't dismiss that. But the evaluation setup bothers me. The selection of environments feels somewhat cherry-picked, and I notice some of the harder sparse-reward or high-dimensional tasks are absent. In my experience reviewing continuous control papers, this is a common way to make a method look stronger than it is. Why not include results on Humanoid or the harder manipulation tasks?

The related work section is fine but glosses over some important prior work on action-value methods in continuous spaces — I'm thinking of work like QT-Opt and some of the cross-entropy method based approaches that also try to avoid policy gradients. The positioning relative to these isn't adequately addressed.

On the writing: the abstract is good, but Section 3 is genuinely dense and I found myself losing the thread around equation 6-8. A clearer worked example or illustration would help a lot.

One thing I genuinely liked: the discussion of controllable computational cost is more honest than most papers in this space. The authors don't pretend QMLE is free — they're explicit about the tradeoffs, which I respect.

Overall, I'm lukewarm. The idea is worth publishing somewhere, but in its current form the theoretical contribution feels thin and the empirical evaluation has gaps that matter. The paper is positioned as showing QMLE matches or beats state-of-the-art — that's a strong claim that requires stronger evidence. I'd want to see broader benchmarks and a more rigorous argument for why the three identified principles are the *right* abstraction before I'd feel comfortable accepting this.

Borderline reject for me, leaning toward major revision if the venue allows it.

This paper proposes ODNN, a framework that enforces orthogonality constraints between known physics components and a learned neural network to handle partially observable dynamical systems. The core idea is interesting — rather than adding physics as a soft regularizer (as PINNs typically do), the authors bake the constraint into the network architecture itself. I can see the appeal of this, and I've reviewed enough physics-ML papers to know that the regularization-vs-architecture debate is genuinely important.

That said, I have significant concerns.

The theoretical guarantees (Section 4, Theorem 1 and its corollaries) feel somewhat hollow to me. After reading this twice, I'm not convinced the "mild orthogonality assumptions" are actually mild in practice. The assumptions feel tailored to make the proofs work rather than reflecting realistic physical scenarios. The connection to the universal approximation theorem is invoked but the leap feels hand-wavy — I'd want to see a more careful treatment of approximation error under the orthogonality constraints, not just existence results.

The empirical evaluation spans eight datasets, which sounds impressive, but I found myself suspicious of the synthetic experiments. When you design the ground truth to have exactly the orthogonal structure your method assumes, of course you're going to recover it well. The real-world experiments (Section 6.3 I think?) are more interesting but the baselines feel weak. Why isn't there a comparison against, say, SINDy or Neural ODEs with physics constraints? These are the obvious competitors and their absence is conspicuous.

Figure 3 is honestly hard to read. The color coding doesn't hold up in grayscale and the caption doesn't adequately explain what we're looking at without hunting through the text.

I'm also not entirely sure the distinction between "explicit" and "implicit" orthogonality (the periodicity/convexity stuff) is as principled as the authors claim. Calling contrasting convexity a form of orthogonality feels like a stretch — I might be wrong here but it seems more like a heuristic that works in specific cases rather than a general principle. The paper would be stronger if this was either formalized rigorously or presented more honestly as a practical extension.

The writing is generally clear but the abstract oversells the theoretical contributions. Saying ODNN provides "strong guarantees" when the assumptions are quite restrictive is misleading.

One genuine strength: the idea of converting physical constraints directly into network architecture rather than the loss function is worth developing. If the authors could demonstrate this on a genuinely challenging real-world system where the orthogonality structure is not pre-specified, that would be compelling.

In my current assessment this is below the acceptance threshold, but not by a huge margin. Major revision needed — stronger baselines, more honest framing of theory, and at minimum one non-synthetic experiment where the orthogonality structure is discovered rather than assumed.

This paper tackles DGA detection using character-level deep learning, comparing CNN and RNN architectures on a 2M domain name dataset. The research question is reasonable and the application domain is relevant, but I have significant concerns about the contribution here.

Let me be blunt: the main finding — that architectures perform similarly and simpler is better — is not particularly surprising or novel. This kind of "we tried everything and it's all about the same" result can be publishable if the experimental setup is rigorous and the analysis is insightful, but I'm not convinced that's the case here. The authors seem to present this as a surprising finding ("surprisingly little difference"), but in my experience with text classification benchmarks, this is actually fairly well-established territory. The NLP community has been saying this for years.

My bigger concern is the dataset construction. The paper uses 2M domain names but I found the description of how benign domains were collected to be underspecified. Which DGA families are represented? The composition matters enormously here — some DGAs produce dictionary-word-based domains that are genuinely hard to distinguish from legitimate ones (e.g., Suppobox), while others produce obvious gibberish. Lumping these together without breakdown makes the reported accuracy numbers close to meaningless. Table 2 shows aggregate results but I really want per-family breakdowns.

The architecture descriptions in Section 3 feel like a literature review rather than a genuine contribution. I've read the Zhang et al. character-level CNN paper and the descriptions here are fairly thin restatements. What specific modifications, if any, did the authors make?

On the positive side, the paper is clearly written and the experimental methodology of comparing multiple architectures under identical conditions is sound in principle. The training/evaluation split appears reasonable.

I'm also not entirely sure the hyperparameter search was thorough enough to make the "no significant difference" claim. If one model was undertrained relative to another, of course they'd look similar. Was any statistical significance testing done on the accuracy differences? I don't see error bars or confidence intervals anywhere in the results tables.

The related work section misses several recent relevant papers — there's work by Woodbridge et al. and Saxe & Berlin that should really be discussed here given this is exactly the same problem space.

Overall I think this could be a workshop paper or a short empirical note, but as a full research contribution it doesn't clear the bar for me. The empirical comparison is useful to practitioners but the analysis doesn't go deep enough to extract genuinely actionable insights. Why do the architectures perform similarly? Is it a property of the domain (short strings, limited vocabulary), the specific dataset, or something else? That would be the interesting question to answer.

Recommendation: reject, with encouragement to resubmit with deeper analysis and more careful dataset characterization.

This paper proposes SFi-Former, a graph transformer that derives sparse attention patterns by framing attention as a network flow optimization problem with L1 regularization. The core idea is interesting — using flow-based sparsity rather than top-k truncation or learned gates is a reasonably novel angle, and I appreciate that the authors try to ground their sparsification in something principled rather than ad hoc.

That said, I have significant concerns about this work.

First, the theoretical motivation in Section 3 feels underdeveloped. The energy function in Eq. (3) is introduced somewhat abruptly, and I spent a while trying to understand why network flow is the right abstraction here rather than, say, optimal transport or any other structured sparsity approach. The connection between the flow conservation constraints and the desired inductive bias for graphs is asserted more than derived. I might be wrong, but I think the paper would benefit enormously from a theorem or at least a proposition showing that the minimizer of this energy function actually produces attention patterns with provably desirable properties (e.g., connectivity preservation, bounded approximation error relative to full attention).

Second, the experimental section is where I have the most trouble. The LRGB results in Table 2 look good, but the baselines are inconsistently tuned — it's not clear whether competing methods were run with the same number of parameters and training budget. In my experience this is exactly where these comparisons go wrong. The GNN Benchmark results in Table 1 are honestly underwhelming; "competitive" is doing a lot of heavy lifting there, and for a few datasets SFi-Former is just clearly worse than existing methods. The authors should be more upfront about this.

Third, the generalization gap analysis (Section 5.3, Figure 4 I believe) is presented as a major contribution but it's basically just showing train vs. test loss curves. This doesn't rigorously demonstrate reduced overfitting in any statistical sense. What are the error bars? Is the difference significant across multiple seeds?

I also found the writing in several places genuinely difficult to follow — the paragraph around lines 215-230 describing the sparse flow inference procedure reads like a first draft. After reading this twice I still wasn't entirely sure whether the flow optimization is solved exactly or approximately at each forward pass, and what the computational cost is relative to standard attention. Table 3 has ablations but doesn't address this runtime question head-on.

The idea has merit and I don't want to dismiss it entirely. Sparse attention for graph transformers is an important problem and the flow-based framing is at least fresh. But right now the paper feels like it's claiming more than it delivers — the theory needs sharpening, the experiments need more rigorous comparison, and the writing needs a serious pass. I lean toward rejection in the current form but would genuinely reconsider with substantial revisions.

This paper proposes a "Narrowing Information Bottleneck" (NIB) framework for interpreting multimodal image-text representations, specifically targeting CLIP-like models. I've spent a fair amount of time with this one and have fairly mixed feelings about it.

Let me start with what I think works. The motivation is reasonable — interpretability for multimodal models is genuinely underexplored compared to unimodal settings, and the authors are right that standard IB approaches carry baggage in the form of distributional assumptions and stochasticity. The connection to attribution axioms (completeness, sensitivity, etc.) is a nice theoretical angle, and I appreciated that they attempt to ground the method formally rather than just presenting it as a heuristic.

That said, I have significant concerns. The theoretical development in Section 3 feels rushed and at points I found it genuinely hard to follow — not because the ideas are deep, but because the notation is inconsistent. Around equations 4-6, the authors switch between using Z as a compressed representation and then as something closer to a mask without clearly distinguishing the two regimes. I read this section twice and I'm still not entirely confident I understand what's actually being optimized at inference time versus training time.

The empirical gains are... suspiciously large. 58.83% improvement in text interpretability is a remarkable claim, and I want to understand it better. Looking at Table 2, it's not clear to me that the evaluation metric they're using for text is truly capturing interpretability quality versus some proxy that happens to favor their method's output format. What baseline is this 58.83% relative to, exactly? The paper needs to be much more transparent here.

I'm also bothered by the comparison setup. Several of the baselines (particularly the IB-based ones) seem to be implemented by the authors themselves rather than taken from official codebases. In my experience, this introduces significant risk of unfavorable baseline implementations, even unintentionally. The authors should clarify this explicitly.

The healthcare application is mentioned prominently in the abstract but receives almost no treatment in the actual experiments. Either demonstrate it properly or drop the claim — it feels like a justification that isn't earned.

Figure 3 is helpful and I think actually does a decent job showing qualitative differences in attribution maps. The text attribution visualizations are genuinely more interpretable to me visually than the baselines shown. So there's something real here.

On the speed claim (63.95% faster) — faster than what exactly? If it's faster than a method that involves variational inference, that's not a particularly high bar.

Overall: I think there's a kernel of a good idea here, but the paper needs substantially more rigorous theoretical exposition, cleaner empirical comparisons with reproducible baselines, and honestly some toning down of the claims. The writing also needs another serious editing pass. I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, but I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit.

This paper tackles the unification of high-resolution ODEs for momentum-based optimization, which is a legitimate and interesting goal. I've been following this line of work since the Su-Boyd-Candès paper and the subsequent Shi et al. high-resolution ODE work, so I have some context here.

The central claim — that various HR-ODEs can be unified under a single parametric framework — is appealing in principle. And the use of IQC-based Lyapunov functions is a reasonable technical choice; I've seen this machinery used effectively in similar contexts (Lessard et al. comes to mind). But after reading this twice, I'm left with some frustration about how clearly the actual contributions are delineated.

The improvements for the triple momentum method ODE and QHM in discrete time are flagged as "notable" in the abstract, but when I got to the actual theorems (around Section 4-5), I found myself struggling to immediately see how significant the quantitative improvements actually are over prior bounds. The paper would benefit enormously from a table — even a simple one — directly comparing convergence rates old vs. new. Right now you have to hunt through the text to reconstruct what exactly changed, and that's not acceptable for a result-driven paper like this.

On the technical side, the Lyapunov construction via IQCs seems sound to me, though I'm not an expert in robust control and I might be missing subtleties there. What I do want to push back on is the discretization discussion. The gap between continuous and discrete convergence guarantees is often where the real difficulty lies, and I don't think the paper handles this transition with sufficient care in all cases. Specifically, the step-size conditions imposed feel somewhat restrictive, and I'd like to see a more thorough discussion of whether these are artifacts of the proof technique or fundamental.

The writing is generally clear but gets dense in Section 3. The general HR-ODE model is introduced with a lot of parameters and I found it hard to develop intuition for what varying each parameter "does." A simple figure showing trajectories for different parameter settings would help enormously here.

I'm also a bit skeptical of the framing around "unification." Showing that several ODEs are special cases of a more general one is useful, but it raises the question: does the general framework suggest *new* algorithms or just repackage existing ones? The paper doesn't fully convince me it does the former, which limits the practical impact somewhat.

Minor things: some notation is introduced before it's defined (noticed this around equation 8 or so), and the related work section undersells some relevant recent work on momentum methods from a dynamical systems perspective.

Overall I think there's a real contribution here but it's not yet presented in a way that makes the improvements crisp and compelling. I'd lean toward weak reject currently, with the expectation that clearer comparisons and better exposition could change my mind.

This paper proposes VRMPO, a variance-reduced policy gradient method that combines stochastic mirror descent with a variance reduction technique to achieve O(ε⁻³) sample complexity. I'll give my honest assessment here.

The theoretical result is... fine, but not particularly surprising at this point. We've seen O(ε⁻³) bounds from SVRPG, PAPG, STORM-based methods, and several others over the past few years. The authors claim this "matches the best-known sample complexity" which is technically true but framed in a way that oversells the contribution. After reading the theory section twice, I couldn't identify what's genuinely novel beyond combining mirror descent with a fairly standard SPIDER/STORM-style estimator. The proof techniques in the appendix look competent but derivative.

My bigger concern is the experimental section. The baselines chosen are somewhat dated — where is the comparison against more recent variance-reduced PG methods like STORM-PG or the methods from Papini et al. (2018) and Xu et al. (2020)? The environments tested (a handful of MuJoCo tasks) are standard but the performance gaps shown in Figure 2 and Figure 3 look suspiciously clean. I'd want to see more seeds, confidence intervals are shown but the variance looks artificially low in some plots. Also, the computational cost per iteration is higher than vanilla PPO or REINFORCE — the paper sort of buries this in Section 4 and doesn't give wall-clock comparisons, only sample complexity. That's a choice.

The writing is serviceable but there are places where it gets genuinely confusing. Equation (7) introduces the variance-reduced estimator but the notation isn't fully established until two pages later — I found this annoying on first read. Page 5 has a paragraph that seems to be saying two contradictory things about the batch size schedule; I might be wrong but I read it three times and still wasn't sure which regime the theory covers vs. what's implemented.

I think the mirror descent framing is actually the most interesting part of this work — the idea that the geometry of the policy space might interact beneficially with variance reduction is worth exploring. But the paper doesn't really lean into this. The theoretical analysis treats it almost as an afterthought and I don't see ablations isolating the contribution of the mirror descent component specifically. Does it actually help? Show me.

To be direct: this feels like an incremental combination of existing ideas with solid but not exceptional execution. The sample complexity result won't surprise anyone working in this area, and the experiments don't fully convince me of practical superiority. I'm leaning reject, but I could be moved if the authors can clarify the novelty over concurrent variance-reduced PG work more explicitly and provide stronger experimental evidence with proper baselines.

This paper proposes an interesting connection between machine unlearning and data attribution, framing unlearning as a problem of predicting and then matching the outputs of a retrained model. The core idea — using datamodels to simulate what retraining would produce, then fine-tuning to match those predictions — is reasonably elegant, and I appreciate that the authors ground it in a concrete meta-algorithm with both theoretical and empirical components.

That said, I have some real concerns.

The theoretical result in Section 4 (the convex setting analysis) feels a bit narrow to me. Showing that DMM provably outperforms iterative methods in a convex setting is... fine, but the whole motivation for this paper is that existing methods fail in *non-convex* settings. The theory doesn't really reach there, and honestly after reading this section twice I'm not sure it adds much beyond what's already known about gradient-based unlearning in convex problems. I think the authors should either strengthen this or be more upfront that it's illustrative rather than a core contribution.

The empirical evaluation is more convincing, though I have questions about the KL-divergence metric introduced in Section 5. I found the motivation for this metric a bit hand-wavy — why is KL divergence the right thing to measure here versus, say, something based on membership inference? The authors cite some prior evaluation frameworks (the Chundawat et al. and Hayes et al. lines of work) but I'm not totally satisfied that the new metric adds something the existing benchmarks don't already capture. Figure 3 is helpful but I'd want to see more ablation here.

My bigger concern is computational cost. Datamodel-based attribution is famously expensive — you need a large ensemble of models trained on random subsets to get reliable datamodel estimates. The paper acknowledges this (around page 7 I think) but somewhat brushes it off by saying future attribution methods will be cheaper. That's a bit of a punt. In practice, if DMM requires training thousands of subsampled models, it's not clear this is usable outside of academic toy settings. I might be wrong about how the authors are instantiating the datamodels here, but I didn't find the computational discussion fully satisfying.

The framing as a "meta-algorithm" is a double-edged sword. On one hand, yes, improvements in attribution flow through to unlearning — that's a nice property. On the other hand, it means DMM inherits all the limitations and failure modes of whatever attribution method you plug in, and those methods have known issues with distribution shift and model dependence.

Overall I think this is a solid contribution with a genuinely novel framing, but the gap between the theoretical setting and the practical non-convex setting is a real weakness, and the computational feasibility question needs a more direct answer. I'm leaning toward weak accept but I'd want the authors to address the attribution cost issue more seriously in revision.

This paper tackles text-to-motion generation by decomposing complex action descriptions into sub-sentences and using 2D pose priors as control signals. The general motivation is reasonable — multi-action descriptions are genuinely hard to learn from, and I've seen this cause failures in prior work like MDM and MotionDiffuse. However, I have significant concerns about both the technical novelty and the experimental validation.

The sentence decomposition idea (Section 3.1 or thereabouts) is honestly pretty straightforward NLP preprocessing — extracting verb phrases isn't novel in any meaningful sense. The authors present this as a core contribution but I'm not convinced it's more than an engineering choice. Similarly, the pose memory bank concept feels derivative of retrieval-augmented generation ideas that have been well-explored. The real question is whether these components interact in a principled way, and after reading this twice, I'm still not sure the paper makes that case convincingly.

The use of 2D pose priors from "static natural images" is interesting but raises questions I couldn't find answered. How are these images retrieved? What happens when the retrieved pose is semantically plausible but kinematically inconsistent with the surrounding motion context? The authors show qualitative results in Figure 4 that look reasonable but I'd want to see failure cases — real systems fail in predictable ways and hiding that is a red flag for me.

On the quantitative side, Table 1 shows improvements on FID, R-Precision, etc. on HumanML3D and KIT. The numbers look good but I'm skeptical. The improvements on KIT are marginal in some metrics and I'd want to know if these differences are statistically significant — there's no mention of variance or confidence intervals anywhere in the results section. In my experience, FID on these benchmarks can be quite sensitive to implementation details, and the paper doesn't provide enough detail about the evaluation protocol to fully trust the comparisons.

I found the ablation study (Table 2 I think?) somewhat helpful but it doesn't isolate contributions cleanly enough. Specifically, removing the pose prior guidance vs. removing the memory bank — these feel like they might have strong interaction effects that aren't discussed.

The writing is functional but not great. Some sentences in the introduction are genuinely hard to parse. The "various ways of expression in the forms of texts" framing in the abstract is clunky and I'm not sure the third challenge they identify is meaningfully different from standard text-to-X generalization challenges.

I'm not recommending acceptance in the current form. The paper might have something real here, but the contribution framing needs substantial work and the experimental validation needs tighter controls. I might be wrong about the novelty — maybe the specific combination of these ideas hasn't been done — but the burden is on the authors to make that case more clearly.

This paper proposes LayerShuffle, a training strategy for Vision Transformers that randomizes the execution order of attention modules during training to produce models that are robust to permuted or pruned layer orderings at test time. The core idea is simple and clearly presented, and I appreciate that the authors don't oversell it — the ~20% accuracy reduction is acknowledged upfront in the abstract, which I respect.

That said, I have some real concerns here.

The motivation is a bit scattered. The paper opens by citing distributed inference and fault-tolerant deployment as key motivators, but I never felt the connection between the theoretical motivation and the actual experiments was fully closed. How realistic is the scenario where transformer layers are executed out of order in a distributed system? I'd want to see this grounded in something more concrete — even a brief discussion of what actual distributed systems look like would help. Right now it reads more like a post-hoc justification for an interesting empirical observation.

The "Frankenstein" merging result (Section 4.x, I believe around page 7-8) is actually the most interesting contribution in my view, and I think it's somewhat buried. The fact that you can randomly merge trained models without catastrophic performance degradation is genuinely surprising and deserves more theoretical attention. Why does this work? The representational analysis is suggestive but hand-wavy. I found myself wanting a cleaner mechanistic explanation.

On the experimental side — the baseline comparisons feel thin. The paper compares against standard ViT training but I'm not seeing comparisons against other related robustness-oriented training schemes, like stochastic depth (Huang et al.) which is a pretty obvious point of comparison given it also randomly drops layers during training. The relationship to stochastic depth is discussed briefly but I think it deserves a dedicated comparison. In my experience this is exactly the kind of ablation that separates a solid paper from a good one.

Figure 3 is quite informative but the caption is too sparse — after reading it twice I wasn't sure what the color encoding represented without hunting through the text.

The accuracy drop of ~20% is... significant. I understand the paper is proposing a tradeoff, but I think the authors need to be more precise about whether this gap is fundamental to the approach or whether it could be reduced with better training recipes, more compute, larger models, etc. Table 2 shows some of this but the discussion is insufficient.

I'm also mildly skeptical about the claim that layers "learn to contribute differently based on their position." This feels like it could be a relatively trivial consequence of the training setup rather than a deep insight. The analysis in Section 4 is interesting but the conclusions feel slightly overclaimed.

Overall: interesting idea, clearly presented, but the paper needs stronger baselines, a tighter motivation, and more careful analysis of the Frankenstein merging behavior. I'm leaning toward weak reject but could be convinced otherwise.

This paper presents a method for building dynamic knowledge graphs from procedural text using MRC models, evaluated primarily on ProPara. The core idea is reasonable and the empirical results are solid, but I have several concerns that I think the authors need to address.

Starting with what works: the integration of MRC-style span extraction with graph construction is a natural fit for this kind of procedural tracking task, and I appreciate that the authors evaluated on both ProPara and Recipes rather than just optimizing for one benchmark. The state-of-the-art results on ProPara tasks 1 and 2 are convincing, though I notice task 3 results (Table 2) are less impressive — the authors somewhat gloss over this discrepancy and I'd like to see a more honest discussion of why the model struggles there.

My bigger concern is the knowledge graph component itself. After reading section 3 twice, I'm still not entirely clear on what the graph structure actually looks like at inference time. What are the nodes? What are the edge types? Figure 2 helps somewhat but honestly not enough. In my experience, papers that claim to build "knowledge graphs" often mean something much simpler — basically structured state vectors — and I suspect that's closer to what's happening here. If so, calling them knowledge graphs feels like it oversells the contribution. I'd appreciate either a more formal definition (something like a proper definition of G_t at each timestep) or more honest framing.

The ablation study is appreciated but feels incomplete. The authors remove components one at a time, but I want to know specifically how much the "dynamic" aspect — the recurrent graph updates — contributes versus just doing MRC over each sentence independently. Table 4 doesn't make this fully clear to me.

I'm also a bit skeptical about the Recipes generalization claim. The performance gap between ProPara and Recipes results suggests the model may be fitting somewhat to ProPara-specific patterns. I might be wrong about this, but the authors present the Recipes results almost as an afterthought rather than doing any serious analysis of where the model succeeds or fails on that dataset.

Minor things: the related work section is fine but doesn't engage seriously with prior graph neural network approaches to similar problems, which feels like an oversight given the framing. Also equation 4 on page 5 has a notation inconsistency that confused me briefly.

Overall: I think this is a decent paper with a real contribution. The MRC+graph framing is interesting and the ProPara results justify publication at some venue. But the presentation needs work — specifically around what "knowledge graph" actually means in this context — and the analysis could be deeper. I'm leaning weak accept but genuinely on the fence. The authors should not mistake state-of-the-art numbers for a free pass on clarity.

This paper proposes ZeroTS, a zero-shot time series forecasting framework that combines RAG-based retrieval with a reinforcement learning refinement loop. I'll share my honest thoughts after going through this twice.

The core idea is interesting — using retrieval-augmented generation to pull in both metadata and series-level information as context, combined with an RL feedback loop that treats ground truth as an environment signal. I can see the appeal conceptually. Zero-shot TSF is genuinely underexplored and the framing around "data-model interaction" is at least novel in how it's positioned.

That said, I have significant concerns about the technical substance and presentation.

First, the RL framework description is frustratingly vague. What exactly is the reward signal? How is the "error feedback" operationalized — is this just MSE? The phrase "treats ground-truth as environments" (abstract, also repeated around what I believe is page 4) is almost meaningless without a formal definition. In my experience reviewing RL-for-forecasting papers, this kind of hand-waving usually means the actual contribution is much simpler than it sounds. I want to see Equation-level clarity here, not metaphor.

Second, the TS-RAG component — how are retrieval candidates selected? What's the similarity metric over time series? The retrieval quality seems crucial to everything downstream, yet I found the description in Section 3 (I think?) surprisingly thin. Are you retrieving from a fixed corpus? How large? These are not minor details.

The experimental results in Table 1 and Table 2 show competitive numbers, and the efficiency claims (1/4 memory, 1/7 inference speed) are genuinely compelling if they hold up. But I'm skeptical of the zero-shot evaluation setup. Are you truly zero-shot, or is there implicit data leakage through the retrieval corpus? This distinction matters enormously and I don't think the paper handles it carefully enough.

I might be wrong, but the comparison baselines feel cherry-picked. Where are comparisons against recent strong zero-shot baselines like MOIRAI or TimesFM? The paper mentions LLM-based TSF methods but the actual table comparisons seem to favor older approaches.

The writing also needs work. The abstract is a bit of a mess — "harnessing the capabilities of LLMs" while simultaneously claiming efficiency gains feels contradictory without explanation. And "multi-party interactions" is used throughout without ever being precisely defined. Who are the parties? Data, model, and... what exactly?

Look, I don't think this is a bad paper — the problem is well-motivated and I genuinely believe there's something here worth developing. But in its current form it reads like a good idea that hasn't been fully worked out yet. The technical rigor needs to be substantially tightened before I could recommend acceptance at a top venue. Major revision at minimum.

This paper proposes applying sparse-to-sparse training to diffusion models, which is a reasonable direction to explore. The core idea — that you can train sparse diffusion models from scratch and match dense performance — is interesting, though I'll admit after reading this twice I'm not entirely convinced the contribution is as novel or impactful as the authors seem to think.

Let me start with what I found genuinely useful. The empirical coverage is decent — six datasets across Latent Diffusion and ChiroDiff, three sparsity methods (Static-DM, RigL-DM, MagRan-DM). The identification of "safe" sparsity regimes is practically useful and I appreciate that the authors didn't just cherry-pick the best results. Table 2 and Table 3 show some honest variability in performance, which I respect.

That said, I have several concerns. First, the novelty claim in the abstract — "for the first time, the paradigm of sparse-to-sparse training to DMs" — feels overstated. RigL and similar dynamic sparse training methods are well-established (Evci et al. 2020, and others), and applying them to a new architecture class is incremental work. The paper needs to be much clearer about what technical challenges are specific to diffusion models that required new solutions, rather than just plugging existing methods in. I honestly couldn't find a compelling answer to this in the paper.

Second, the evaluation metrics. FID scores are reported but I'm skeptical about the statistical significance of some of the "outperform" claims in the abstract. The differences in some rows of Table 3 look well within noise, and I don't see confidence intervals or multiple run statistics reported consistently. This is a real problem — claiming sparse models "sometimes outperform" dense counterparts based on point estimates is not rigorous.

Third — and this bothered me a lot — the paper focuses exclusively on unconditional generation. In my experience, this is the least practically relevant setting for diffusion models right now. The computational savings argument is strongest for large conditional models (text-to-image, etc.), but those are conspicuously absent. I understand this is scope management, but the gap between the claimed impact and the actual experimental setting is too wide.

The writing in Section 3 is a bit muddled. The distinction between the three methods could be explained more crisply — I found myself re-reading the method descriptions multiple times. Figure 2 helps but arrives too late.

I'm also not sure what to make of the ChiroDiff experiments. It's an interesting non-image domain but the connection to the broader claims feels thin.

Overall, this feels like solid empirical work that is slightly over-claiming its contributions. The "safe sparsity values" finding is probably the most actionable result. I'd lean toward rejection in current form, primarily because of the narrow evaluation scope and the insufficiently justified novelty claims, but this could be a reasonable workshop paper or a stronger submission with more rigorous evaluation and honest scoping.

This paper proposes combining local convolution and global attention within transformer heads for efficient NLP on mobile devices — a reasonable idea that the authors call Long-Short Range Attention (LSRA). I've read through this twice and have mixed feelings.

The core idea is not particularly novel. Combining convolution with attention has been explored in several concurrent and prior works (Wu et al.'s pay less attention, the convolution-attention hybrid work from Facebook, etc.), and I'm not entirely convinced the authors do enough to differentiate their specific contribution from this broader space. The "specialization" framing feels a bit post-hoc — why should we believe heads naturally want to specialize this way versus just being forced to? The ablation in Table 4 is helpful but doesn't fully resolve this question for me.

That said, the empirical results are solid. The WMT'14 En-Fr and En-De numbers look credible, and the comparison against Evolved Transformer is frankly the most interesting result in the paper — beating a 250 GPU-year search with a hand-designed component is a good story.
