I do wish the authors were more careful about the MAC-constrained comparison setup though. How sensitive are the conclusions to exactly where you draw the budget line? The 500M vs 100M MACs results in Table 1 both favor Lite Transformer, but I'd like to see more analysis of the full Pareto curve rather than just two operating points.

Figure 3 is helpful for understanding the architecture but the caption is too sparse. I had to read the surrounding text carefully to understand what was actually being depicted.

My bigger concern is the compression experiments in Section 4.4. The 18.2x compression claim combining pruning and quantization — I found this section rushed. Which pruning method? What quantization scheme? At what accuracy degradation? These feel like important implementation details that are either buried or missing. In my experience, these numbers can vary enormously depending on your baseline choices, and the paper doesn't give me enough to evaluate the claim critically.

The language modeling results (Table 3, ~1.8 perplexity improvement) are nice but the dataset is only WikiText-103, which feels thin. One dataset for an entire task claim is not convincing.

I think this is a decent paper with a legitimate practical contribution — deploying efficient transformers on edge devices matters, and the LSRA mechanism is simple enough to actually be useful. But the novelty bar is lower than what I'd hope for a top venue, and some of the supporting experiments feel undercooked. I'm leaning weak accept but could be convinced either way. The authors should also acknowledge the concurrent literature more thoroughly — as written, the related work undersells how crowded this space already was when this was written.

This paper tackles the level-of-manipulation problem in text augmentation, which is indeed a real and underappreciated issue in NLP. I've seen a lot of augmentation papers over the years and the core motivation here is reasonable — too little perturbation and you're just duplicating data, too much and your labels become unreliable. Fine. But I have significant concerns about execution and presentation.

The proposed method combines a "candidate pool adjustment" mechanism with a hybrid sentence embedding (sentence-BERT + doc2vec + XAI features) for pseudo-labeling. After reading this twice, I still couldn't get a clear picture of exactly how the candidate pool is parameterized. The description around equations 3-5 is vague — what exactly controls the "level" and how does a practitioner choose it? The paper claims systematic adjustability but I don't see a principled way to select the hyperparameter governing this. It feels like you've just shifted the problem, not solved it.

The XAI component is the part I find most confusing. Using SHAP/LIME-derived features in the embedding is novel-ish, but the justification is hand-wavy. Why would gradient-based attribution information improve pseudo-label reliability? The paper gestures at an intuition but never really makes the argument rigorously. I might be wrong, but this feels like it was added to make the method look more distinctive rather than because it was ablated and found necessary. Speaking of which — the ablation study in Table 4 is too coarse. Please show what happens when you remove JUST the XAI component.

The experiments are on relatively small benchmark datasets (SST-2, TREC, a few others), which is fine for a focused study, but the low-resource settings tested feel cherry-picked. Why these specific training sizes? The comparison baselines are reasonable — EDA, AEDA, BackTranslation — though I notice the paper doesn't compare against more recent augmentation methods like SSMBA or FlipDA, which seems like an oversight given this is 2024.

Table 2 shows improvements, sometimes quite small (0.3-0.8% on some datasets). I'm skeptical these differences are meaningful without significance testing, which is absent. This is a real problem.

The writing needs work. The abstract says "It introduced diversity" — basic grammar error, and there are several throughout. The related work section reads like a list rather than a coherent narrative. Figure 2 is genuinely helpful though, and I appreciated the visual breakdown of the pipeline.

My overall take: the problem is worth studying, there's a kernel of a decent idea here, but the paper in its current form feels undercooked. The theoretical justification is weak, the ablations are insufficient, and the empirical gains are modest and not statistically validated. I'd want a major revision at minimum — tighten the XAI motivation considerably, add significance tests, and compare against stronger recent baselines.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — what happens when you don't know precisely how similar your source and target tasks are? The minimax framing is a natural fit here, and I appreciate that the authors can get an analytical solution out of it. That's genuinely nice when it happens.

That said, I have some significant concerns after reading this twice.

The boundedness assumption on task similarity (Section 3, around Eq. 4-6 if I remember correctly) feels like it's doing a lot of heavy lifting without sufficient justification. The authors essentially assume the task distance lives in some known interval [0, δ_max], but in practice, where does δ_max come from? This is almost as hard to specify as the task similarity itself, which was the original motivation for the whole framework. I kept waiting for this to be addressed and it never really was. This feels like the core issue and the paper sort of sidesteps it.

The theoretical results are reasonably clean, and I do think the interpretation of how sample size, task distance, and model dimensionality interact (Section 4) is the strongest part of the paper. I found Table 2 helpful for building intuition. But I'm not entirely sure the bounds are tight — the authors don't discuss this much, and in my experience this matters quite a bit for whether the theory actually guides algorithm design or just provides post-hoc rationalization.

On the experimental side — image classification on what appear to be fairly standard benchmarks — the results are decent but not overwhelming. The "robust and competitive" framing in the abstract is doing some work here. Competitive with what exactly? The baselines chosen feel a bit dated, and I'd want to see comparison against more recent robust transfer methods. The random selection of training sets is a nice evaluation protocol, I'll give them that.

I also found the writing in Section 5 a bit muddled. The transition from theory to practical algorithm isn't as clean as it could be. After reading through it, I'm still not 100% clear on exactly what approximations are being made when moving from the analytical minimax solution to something you'd actually run on a GPU.

Minor thing: Figure 3 is referenced before Figure 2 in the text, which is just sloppy editing.

Overall, I think there's a real paper here — the minimax angle on robustness in transfer learning is worth pursuing — but in its current form it feels incomplete. The gap between the theoretical setup and practical usability needs serious attention. I'm leaning toward reject but would genuinely consider revising my score if the authors can address the δ_max specification issue convincingly. That's the crux of it for me.

This paper proposes GenCO, a framework combining deep generative models with embedded combinatorial solvers for generating diverse solutions to design problems. I've read through this twice and have mixed feelings about it.

The core idea is interesting — using differentiable combinatorial solvers embedded within a generative pipeline to enforce combinatorial constraints during training. I've seen related work in differentiable optimization (Elmachtoub & Grigas, Vlastelica et al.'s blackbox backprop, etc.) but the specific application to *diverse* solution generation for design tasks feels reasonably novel. The game level generation experiments are actually compelling, and Figure 4 shows visually distinct outputs that do seem to satisfy the stated constraints.

That said, I have several concerns. First, the related work section is thin on differentiable combinatorial optimization literature. Work like SurCo, DC3, and the broader predict-then-optimize line is either missing or mentioned only superficially. Given that the technical core of this paper sits squarely in that space, this feels like a significant omission — not just academically but because it makes it hard to understand what's genuinely new here.

Second, the experimental evaluation is where things get shaky for me. The baselines are weak. Comparing against vanilla VAEs and GANs without diversity-promoting objectives feels like cherry-picking. What about CCVAE, or methods that explicitly encourage diversity via quality-diversity algorithms (MAP-Elites comes to mind)? I'm not entirely sure all of these are directly applicable, but the authors should at least discuss them. The quantitative metrics in Table 2 — I found this confusing because it's unclear how "diversity" is being measured and whether the metric captures semantic diversity vs. surface-level variation.

The path planning experiments feel underdeveloped compared to the level generation ones. It almost reads like it was added to pad the paper. More analysis there would help, or honestly just cut it and go deeper on the stronger application.

On the technical side, equation 4 (the combined loss) introduces a weighting hyperparameter λ but there's essentially no ablation on this. In my experience this kind of balancing term is extremely sensitive and the paper's claims about robustness need to be backed up.

I also think the "combinatorial nature" framing in the abstract is doing a lot of heavy lifting. Some of the constraints being enforced feel more like structural/spatial constraints than truly combinatorial ones in the classical sense. The paper would benefit from being more precise here rather than using "combinatorial" as a catch-all.

The writing is generally clear though a bit verbose in places (the introduction runs long before getting to the actual contribution). 

Overall I think there's a genuine contribution here but the paper in its current form overpromises and underdelivers on the evaluation side. I'm leaning toward weak reject but would reconsider with stronger baselines and a more honest positioning relative to the differentiable CO literature.

This paper proposes combining a constellation model with standard CNNs for few-shot learning, essentially adding an attention-based part relationship branch on top of typical CNN features. The core idea is that explicit part-based representations should help when labeled data is scarce. I've seen a lot of few-shot learning papers over the years and I think the high-level motivation is reasonable, though not particularly novel at this point.

My biggest concern is the clarity of the technical presentation. After reading Section 3 twice, I'm still not entirely sure how the "cell feature clustering" works in practice — the description around equations (3)-(5) is vague enough that I couldn't confidently reimplement this. The term "constellation model" gets invoked repeatedly but the connection to the classical constellation models in computer vision (Fergus et al., Fischler & Elschlager) feels superficial. If you're going to claim this heritage, you need to be more explicit about what you're actually borrowing and what you're not.

The results on CIFAR-FS, FC100, and mini-ImageNet look competitive, but I'm skeptical about a few things. Table 2 comparisons — are all methods using the same backbone? I couldn't find a clear statement of this for every baseline listed. This matters enormously in few-shot learning and I've seen papers get away with murky comparisons before. The gains on FC100 are notably smaller than on the other benchmarks, and I think the authors should address why rather than just reporting numbers.

I found Figure 3 actually useful — it gives some intuition about what parts are being discovered. But Figure 4 feels like it's doing a lot of work to convince me the attention mechanism is doing something meaningful, and I'm not fully convinced. The visualizations are cherry-picked looking.

The ablation in Table 3 is appreciated but incomplete. What happens if you just use the clustering without the attention? And how sensitive is the method to the number of clusters K? I see K is set to some value but I don't recall seeing a proper sensitivity analysis, which would be important for understanding how much tuning was required to get these numbers.

The writing is functional but could be tighter. There are several places where the prose is redundant (the intro covers some of the same ground as the related work section). Minor thing but the abstract says "not expressive in the existing CNN frameworks" — this is an overstatement and the kind of claim that needs more careful qualification.

Overall I think there's a real idea here with some empirical support, but the paper needs stronger technical exposition and more rigorous experimental analysis before I'd be comfortable accepting it. I'm leaning toward weak reject, with the possibility of reconsideration if the authors can substantially clarify the method description and address the experimental concerns I've raised.

This paper tackles $\ell_0$ constrained optimization via hard-thresholding methods, framing various algorithmic variants through the lens of "biased gradient" perspectives. The core idea — that random hard-thresholding can be reinterpreted as a biased gradient algorithm, and that different bias types (memory vs. recursive) have different implications for convergence — is reasonable and has some novelty. But I have significant concerns about execution and presentation.

Let me start with what I think works. The unified framework for understanding hard-thresholding variants is genuinely useful, and the distinction between memory-biased and recursively-biased algorithms (Section 3) is clearly articulated. The extension to zeroth-order settings is timely given interest in black-box optimization. The convergence results for SARAHT appear to improve on prior work, and I was able to roughly verify the claimed rates against Table 1.

That said, I found the paper frustrating to read. The abstract is genuinely confusing — phrases like "recursively bias effectively counteracts some of the issues caused by Hard-Thresholding" are vague to the point of being meaningless without the full paper context, and even after reading the full paper twice I'm not sure this framing is as clean as the authors suggest. The writing throughout feels rushed. There are grammatical issues on nearly every page (e.g., "recursively bias" should be "recursive bias" consistently), and this made me question how carefully the technical content was checked.

My bigger concern is the experimental section. The black-box adversarial experiments are interesting, but I'm not convinced they're rigorous enough to support the claims. The comparisons in Figure 3 (if I'm reading it correctly) seem to cherry-pick favorable hyperparameter settings for the proposed methods. What's the sensitivity to the choice of sparsity parameter $s$? I'd want to see ablations. The ridge regression experiments feel like an afterthought — they don't really stress-test the zeroth-order methods in any meaningful way.

On the theory side: I might be wrong, but Theorem 4.2 seems to require assumptions that are quite restrictive (the restricted isometry-type conditions plus smoothness). In my experience with this literature, these are often simultaneously quite strong. The authors should be more upfront about when these assumptions actually hold in practice for the problem instances they consider.

The connection to SPIDER/SARAH-type variance reduction is acknowledged but I think undersold — SARAHT is essentially adapting known variance reduction tricks to this constrained setting, and the novelty there is incremental rather than fundamental. BVR-SZHT is more interesting to me as a contribution.

Overall, there's a publishable paper here, but it needs significant revision. Cleaner writing, more honest discussion of assumptions, and stronger experiments are all needed. I'm leaning toward weak reject in the current form, though I'd reconsider with substantial revisions to the experimental section and a more careful pass on the prose.

This paper proposes using Physics-Informed Neural Networks to solve the Feynman-Kac equation as a way to estimate expectations, positioning it as an alternative or complement to MCMC. The core idea — that you can sidestep Monte Carlo sampling by solving a PDE whose solution encodes the expectation of interest — is not new (this connection has been exploited in computational finance for decades), but combining it with modern diffusion bridge constructions and PINN solvers is at least a reasonable engineering contribution, if it works.

Unfortunately, I have serious concerns about this paper in its current form.

The theoretical claims are vague to the point of being unverifiable. The authors claim FKEE "significantly improves efficiency" and "substantially reduces variance" — but where are the formal variance reduction guarantees? I looked carefully through Section 3 and found no theorem that actually bounds the variance of the proposed estimator relative to vanilla MCMC. The word "substantially" appears multiple times without any formal backing. This is not acceptable for a methods paper at this venue.

The experiments are frankly weak. The Ising model experiment (Section 5, I think — the section numbering got confusing to me after Section 4) reports partition function estimates but the baselines are minimal. How does this compare against annealed importance sampling, or thermodynamic integration, which are the standard workhorses here? I've reviewed papers on partition function estimation for years and the absence of these comparisons is conspicuous. Table 1 shows some numbers but the error bars seem suspiciously small and the experimental setup description is insufficient to reproduce.

The curse of dimensionality issue is something I find almost contradictory in the paper. PINN methods for high-dimensional PDEs are themselves subject to severe approximation errors in high dimensions — the authors sort of acknowledge this but then wave it away. You can't claim to solve the curse of dimensionality by substituting one high-dimensional problem (sampling) for another (high-dimensional PDE solving). This needs to be addressed honestly.

Writing quality is a problem throughout. Sentences like "weakening the assumptions on the distribution of X and f in the general MCMC expectation estimator" are grammatically awkward and conceptually unclear. After reading this twice I'm still not entirely sure what assumptions are being weakened and why. The abstract promises a lot but the paper doesn't fully deliver on those promises.

I might be wrong about the novelty claim — maybe I'm missing a specific angle here — but I don't think so. The diffusion bridge matching described in Section 3.2 feels underdeveloped as a theoretical contribution.

I want to be clear: I think the underlying idea has some merit and I wouldn't want to completely dismiss it. But in its current state this paper needs substantially more rigorous theoretical analysis, stronger empirical comparisons, and a much more careful discussion of when and why this approach would be preferred over existing methods. Not ready for publication.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning of LLMs — and proposes a two-stage approach (ProMoT) that first does prompt tuning to capture format-specific information, then fine-tunes the base model with the soft prompt attached. The core intuition is reasonable: if you can offload the "format learning" into soft prompt parameters that get removed later, the base model weights are less contaminated by superficial dataset-specific patterns. I get it. But I have some significant concerns.

First, the paper's central claim about "format specialization" being a primary driver of catastrophic forgetting is asserted more than rigorously demonstrated. The analysis in Section 3 is suggestive but thin. Showing that forgetting happens early in training is interesting, but this alone doesn't establish that format is the *cause* rather than, say, the model simply overfitting to the training distribution more broadly. I'd want to see more controlled ablations here — e.g., what happens if you fine-tune on reformatted versions of the same data? The authors gesture at this but never really nail it down.

Second, and this bothers me more, the baseline comparisons feel incomplete. There's a whole literature on mitigating forgetting during fine-tuning — EWC, replay methods, LoRA-based approaches, various regularization techniques. The paper compares against standard fine-tuning and multi-task fine-tuning but largely ignores parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods that are arguably the more relevant comparison point in 2024. Why isn't LoRA a baseline? I think this is a real gap.

The experiments on 8 in-context evaluation tasks are reasonable in scope, and I'll admit the results on cross-lingual transfer (En-Fr → other language pairs) are genuinely interesting and probably the strongest empirical contribution here. The NLI → summarization result is intriguing but the effect size is modest enough that I'm not fully convinced. Table 2 shows mixed results that the authors spin somewhat optimistically in the text.

The method itself is simple, which I generally appreciate. But the simplicity also raises the question: why does this work? The explanation offered is "format specialization gets absorbed by the soft prompt" but this is basically a restatement of the hypothesis, not a mechanistic explanation. Some probing experiments or attention analysis would strengthen this considerably.

Writing is generally clear though there are some grammatical issues ("across a board range" in the abstract should be "broad range") and the related work section is a bit sparse given how much relevant work exists on parameter-efficient fine-tuning and forgetting in neural networks.

Overall I'm leaning toward rejection but I think this is borderline. The core idea has merit and the cross-lingual experiments are genuinely interesting. But the missing baselines and the somewhat hand-wavy mechanistic story make me hesitant. I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit with stronger baselines and more rigorous analysis of *why* the method works.

This paper tackles an important practical problem — speeding up Monte Carlo simulations for collider physics using ML regressors — and I appreciate that the authors are targeting a genuinely hard precision regime (sub-1% relative error). The application is well-motivated and the computational speedups reported (10^3 to 10^6x) are impressive if they hold up.

That said, I have significant concerns about the technical depth and novelty here.

The core contribution seems to be: (1) a ResNet-style DNN with skip connections, (2) BDTs for lower-dimensional problems, and (3) exploiting physics symmetries to reduce the number of required regressors. None of these are individually novel. ResNets are decades old, BDTs are standard, and using symmetry arguments in particle physics emulation has been explored — I'm thinking of work like the NNPDF collaboration's neural network fits and various matrix element emulation papers from the past several years. The authors don't adequately situate themselves in this existing literature. The related work section feels thin to me.

On the experimental side, I found the evaluation somewhat unsatisfying. The paper shows relative errors across the domain, which is good, but I kept wondering about the tails. In my experience with HEP simulations, the hard part isn't getting sub-1% error on average — it's avoiding catastrophic failures in low-probability regions of phase space that nonetheless matter for final observables. Figure 3 (I think?) shows error distributions but doesn't really address this corner-case behavior in a convincing way. What happens at the edges of phase space?

The claim about BDTs outperforming NNs at lower dimensions is interesting and I found myself wanting more analysis here. Why does this happen? Is it the training data efficiency? The inductive bias? The authors gesture at this but don't really dig in, and honestly this might be the most scientifically interesting finding in the paper.

I'm also not entirely sure the "order of magnitude reduction in regressors" from symmetry arguments is being compared fairly — what's the baseline exactly? This felt underspecified.

Writing quality is fine, though somewhat dry. The paper reads more like a technical report than a research paper with a clear thesis. After reading this twice I still wasn't sure what the single headline result is supposed to be.

The speedup numbers need more careful presentation. 10^3-10^6 is a very wide range — what drives the variation? Without understanding this, it's hard to assess the practical impact.

I think this work could be publishable with significant revision, but right now it reads like preliminary results without sufficient depth of analysis or engagement with prior work. I'd want to see stronger baselines, more careful error analysis in difficult phase space regions, and a clearer articulation of what's genuinely new here versus engineering application of known techniques.

Leaning toward reject but open to being convinced otherwise.

This paper proposes using RL to generate instruction datasets for fine-tuning LLMs, framing it as an alternative to both RLHF and self-instruct style approaches. I've read this twice and I have significant concerns.

The core idea — using RL to curate/generate instruction data rather than to directly optimize the model — is actually somewhat interesting in principle. I'll give credit there. But the execution and presentation leave a lot to be desired.

My biggest issue is clarity. After reading the abstract and intro multiple times, I'm still not entirely sure what the actual RL formulation looks like. What are the states, actions, rewards? The paper mentions "a suite of textual operations and rules" but this feels hand-wavy. When I got to the methodology section, I found the reward signal description particularly underspecified — if the whole approach hinges on a well-designed reward, I need to understand it much better than what's presented here. Figure 2 is supposed to clarify the pipeline but honestly I found it more confusing than the text description.

The experimental comparisons worry me too. The baselines selected seem convenient rather than comprehensive. Where's the comparison against more recent self-instruct variants, like Evol-Instruct or similar work? In my experience, papers in this space often cherry-pick baselines that make their numbers look good, and I can't rule that out here. Table 2 shows improvements on some benchmarks but the margins are modest and I'd want to see statistical significance reported — it's absent as far as I can tell.

The claim about "negating the need for subsequent RLHF stages" is strong and I don't think it's fully substantiated. The paper doesn't really ablate whether a subsequent RLHF stage on top of their approach would further improve things, so the claim feels more like an assumption than a demonstrated result.

I might be wrong about this, but the framing also seems to oversell novelty. Using RL for data generation/selection isn't brand new — there's prior work on RL for curriculum learning and data augmentation that deserves acknowledgment and comparison. The related work section feels thin.

The writing itself is a problem. Phrases like "paving the way for" and "underscore some key advantages" are fluffy. Be direct about what you showed.

On the positive side, the motivation is real — reducing dependence on GPT-4 queries and human annotation is a genuine practical concern, and if the method actually works as described, that's valuable. I also think the diversity-focused objective for instruction generation is a reasonable design choice.

But overall, in the paper's current form, the technical specification is too vague, the experimental validation is insufficient, and the claims outrun the evidence. I'd want a major revision at minimum before this is publishable at a top venue.

This paper tackles a reasonably interesting problem — how to select samples for few-shot learning — and the empirical scope is genuinely impressive. 20 strategies, 5 FSL approaches, 8 image + 6 text datasets. I'll give credit where it's due: the sheer breadth of experimentation here is commendable and must have taken considerable effort.

That said, I have some real concerns about the paper that I think need addressing before this is publishable at a top venue.

My biggest issue is with ACSESS itself. After reading the method section twice, I'm still not entirely clear on what's novel here beyond a fairly straightforward combination/ensemble of existing strategies. The optimization procedure described around equation 3-4 feels like a weighted selection mechanism that, in my experience, resembles AutoML-style portfolio approaches that have been around for years. The authors don't do nearly enough work situating their contribution against, say, algorithm selection literature or ensemble-based active learning. This feels like a significant gap.

The results in Table 2 and Table 3 show ACSESS consistently winning, but the margins are often quite small — sometimes within what I'd expect from variance in the few-shot sampling process itself. I didn't see sufficient statistical significance testing. With few-shot learning, variance is notoriously high, and reporting means without confidence intervals across multiple runs is, frankly, not acceptable by current standards. How many seeds? This needs to be crystal clear.

I also found the "regression to random selection" finding (Section 5.4 or thereabouts) genuinely interesting — probably the most interesting empirical finding in the paper — but it's treated almost as an afterthought. This deserves more analysis. Why does this happen? Is it a saturation effect? Are diverse strategies converging to the data manifold? I want more here.

The writing is serviceable but occasionally sloppy. There are places (around page 6-7) where the experimental setup description assumes familiarity with implementation details that aren't introduced until the appendix. Reorganize this.

One more thing that bothers me: the claim about "modality dependence" in the abstract is stated as a contribution but it's really just an observation from the tables. Calling it a finding requires more systematic analysis than "strategy X works better on images than text."

I'm also skeptical of including in-context learning alongside meta-learning and fine-tuning as if they're comparable paradigms for this analysis. The sample selection mechanism operates very differently in ICL — the prompt construction itself is part of the strategy. This conflation deserves at least a serious discussion.

Overall: the paper has value as an empirical study, and I think there's a publishable paper somewhere in here. But the methodological contribution of ACSESS needs stronger framing and justification, the statistical rigor needs improvement, and some of the more interesting findings are currently buried. I'd lean reject at this stage, with encouragement to revise substantially.

This paper attempts to provide a theoretical foundation for adversarial examples by connecting them to near-zero eigenvalues in the empirical kernel of neural networks. The core claim — that adversarial examples correspond to near-zero Mercer eigenvalues — is an interesting one, and I appreciate the ambition to give a unified mathematical treatment of a phenomenon that has largely been studied empirically.

That said, I have serious concerns about this paper in its current form.

The theoretical framework, while mathematically dressed, feels like it may be circular in places. The characterization in Section 3 (I think around page 5-6) essentially defines adversarial examples in terms of a property of the kernel and then "proves" they have that property. I read through the main theorem twice and I'm not entirely convinced the assumptions are mild in any meaningful practical sense — particularly the assumptions on the activation function. The authors say "mild assumptions" in the abstract but I'd push back on that characterization. What does this actually cover? ReLU networks? The paper is vague here and this matters enormously for any claim of generality.

The experimental section is where my patience really wore thin. MNIST. In 2024. DeepFool on MNIST. This is not sufficient validation of a theoretical framework that claims to be "unified." I understand that proof-of-concept experiments exist, but the eigenvalue shift shown in Figure 3 is visually modest and the statistical analysis is thin. There's no comparison against clean examples in a rigorous distributional sense, and no attempt to show this holds on CIFAR-10, ImageNet, or even with other attack methods beyond DeepFool. The claim in the abstract that results are "in strong agreement" with theory feels oversold relative to what's actually demonstrated.

I also think the connection to Goodfellow's original conjecture deserves more careful treatment. Goodfellow's linearity hypothesis is referenced somewhat loosely here, and in my reading of his 2014 paper, the conjecture about rarity is more nuanced than the authors suggest. The paper should engage more critically with this rather than using it as rhetorical support.

There are genuine moments of interesting mathematics here — the kernel perspective is not entirely without merit and I've seen related ideas in work on neural tangent kernels — but the bridge between the theory and practical adversarial robustness is underdeveloped. Why should a practitioner care about this characterization? Does it suggest new defenses? New attacks? The paper doesn't really answer this.

Minor point: the writing needs work. Several sentences in the introduction are convoluted and I had to reread them to extract meaning.

I think this paper needs substantial revision before it's ready for publication at a top venue. The theoretical contribution needs cleaner exposition and genuinely mild assumptions, and the experiments need to move well beyond MNIST to support the claims being made. Not ready in current form.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — optimizing tail performance in collaborative filtering rather than just average performance. The CVaR framing is natural here, and I appreciate that the authors ground this in real user satisfaction concerns rather than just presenting a theoretical exercise.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The connection to CVaR is well-motivated in the intro, but I found the transition from the optimization objective to the actual iALS extension (Section 4 I think — my copy's page numbers got garbled in the PDF) somewhat abrupt. The authors essentially claim the iterative reweighting scheme converges to the CVaR optimum, but the theoretical justification felt thin to me. I might be wrong, but I don't think the convergence guarantees carry over cleanly to the non-convex matrix factorization setting, and the paper doesn't really address this gap honestly. A remark or even just an acknowledgment would go a long way.

The empirical evaluation is... okay. The datasets used are fairly standard (MovieLens, etc.) which is fine, but I'd want to see results on at least one truly large-scale dataset given the paper's repeated claims about "web-scale" applicability. Table 2 shows the tail NDCG improvements, and yes they're real, but the magnitude is modest in several cases. The authors should be more upfront about this rather than consistently framing results as "excellent." In my experience, reviewers and practitioners notice when authors oversell modest gains.

I found Figure 3 genuinely useful — it does a nice job illustrating how the user loss distribution shifts. More of this kind of visualization would strengthen the paper.

One thing that bothers me: the baseline comparison feels incomplete. There's related work on group fairness in recommendation and on distributionally robust optimization for RecSys that seems directly relevant but gets short shrift. Specifically, I'd expect comparisons against something like the work on exposure fairness or re-ranking approaches. Are those not applicable here? If not, explain why.

The computational efficiency claims are somewhat validated by the wall-clock experiments, though I'd want to know more about sensitivity to the CVaR threshold α. The ablation in the appendix touches on this but doesn't go far enough — what happens at really extreme α values?

Minor things: the abstract uses "excellent" twice which reads awkwardly. Several equations in Section 3 lack sufficient explanation of notation on first use, which slowed me down.

Overall, I think this is a solid contribution with a clear practical motivation, but it needs more rigorous theoretical discussion of convergence, stronger empirical baselines, and more honest framing of the results. I'm leaning weak accept but could be pushed either direction depending on the rebuttal.

This paper tackles the problem of generalized-smooth nonconvex optimization, proposing an independently-normalized SGD variant and analyzing convergence under both standard and generalized PL conditions. The topic is timely and I think the motivation is reasonable — generalized smoothness does come up in practice with transformers and certain loss landscapes.

That said, I have significant concerns about the novelty and technical contributions here.

The core algorithmic idea — combining gradient normalization with clipping — isn't particularly fresh. Normalized gradient methods and clipped SGD have both been studied extensively (Zhang et al. 2020, Cutkosky & Mehta 2020, among others), and "independently-normalized" feels like a relatively minor variation. The authors need to do a much better job in the related work section (around pages 3-4) distinguishing what is genuinely new here versus what is a straightforward combination of existing techniques. Right now I found the discussion there somewhat hand-wavy about why independent sampling is essential rather than just convenient for the analysis.

On the convergence analysis: the O(ε^{-4}) rate in Theorem 3 (if I'm reading the main results correctly around page 7) matches what you'd expect for nonconvex SGD, so there's no improvement in the asymptotic rate. The paper's claim seems to be about achieving this under *relaxed* assumptions. Fine, but I'd like to see a more careful comparison table — Table 1 exists but honestly I found it a bit hard to parse, particularly which rows are directly comparable in terms of what assumptions are being made. The footnotes are doing too much work there.

The generalized PL analysis in Section 3 is actually the part I found most interesting and potentially the stronger contribution. The interplay between normalization and the PL geometry is non-obvious. But after reading this section twice, I still wasn't fully clear on whether the convergence rate here is tight or whether there's slack in the analysis. Some discussion of lower bounds would strengthen the paper considerably.

Experiments are... fine. Figure 2 shows faster convergence on some language model tasks, but the baselines feel cherry-picked. Where's a comparison against AdaGrad or Adam with gradient clipping? In my experience, these are the natural practical competitors for this kind of algorithm, and their absence is conspicuous.

Minor things: Equation 4 has a notational inconsistency with what's defined on page 5 (the conditioning on the sigma-algebra appears and disappears inconsistently). Also the proof of Lemma 2 in the appendix seems to skip a non-trivial step around the handling of the cross terms — I might be wrong but this should be spelled out more carefully.

Overall, I think there's a decent paper buried here, but in its current form the contributions feel incremental and the presentation needs work. I lean toward rejection but would reconsider if the authors can sharpen the novelty claims and address the experimental gaps.

This paper proposes a method for improving gradient estimation in evolutionary strategies by incorporating surrogate gradient information from past descent directions. The core idea is to project the ES gradient estimate onto a subspace that guarantees improvement over the surrogate, which is a reasonably clean theoretical contribution. I'll share my thoughts, though I have mixed feelings overall.

The theoretical result in Section 3 is the strongest part of the paper. The guarantee that the method always finds a direction at least as good as the surrogate gradient is appealing, and the proof for linear functions is clean. However, the gap between "works provably for linear functions" and "works well in practice for neural network optimization" is enormous, and I don't think the paper adequately bridges this. The authors acknowledge this with the "simplifying assumptions" caveat in the abstract, but after reading Section 4 twice, I'm still not convinced the theoretical intuitions survive into the nonlinear regime in any meaningful sense. This feels like a significant weakness that the authors somewhat paper over.

On the empirical side — MNIST and some RL tasks is honestly pretty weak evaluation for a paper appearing in 2024. MNIST is essentially a solved benchmark and tells us little about whether this scales. I'd want to see results on something more challenging: continuous control benchmarks with higher dimensionality, or at least MuJoCo tasks with proper comparisons against CMA-ES and recent ES variants like OpenAI ES or ARS. Table 2 shows improvements but the variance across seeds isn't always reported consistently, which makes it hard to assess statistical significance. I might be wrong, but some of those improvements look like they could be within noise.

The "no extra computational cost" claim in the abstract is technically true but a bit misleading — you're reusing previous gradient estimates, which does mean you carry additional memory and there are implicit assumptions about stationarity of the landscape between steps that aren't really discussed.

I found the related work section somewhat thin. The connection to quasi-Newton methods and BFGS-style curvature estimation feels relevant but is barely touched on. Work on guided ES (Maheswaranathan et al.) is cited but I don't think the comparison is deep enough — their approach also uses surrogate gradient information and a more thorough experimental comparison would strengthen the paper considerably.

One more thing: the writing in Section 5 gets quite dense and I lost the thread of the argument around equation 8. A figure illustrating the geometric intuition of the projection would help enormously here.

In my experience reviewing optimization papers, the ones that stick are the ones with either very strong theory or very compelling empirical results. This paper has decent theory but limited empirical scope. I'm leaning toward weak reject, though I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit with stronger baselines and broader experiments. The core idea has merit.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — finding lottery tickets in GNNs — and proposes a dual perspective framework (DGLT) that combines regularization-based network pruning with hierarchical graph sparsification. I've spent a fair amount of time with this one and have mixed feelings.

The motivation is reasonable. The critique of GLT requiring full pretraining is valid, and I appreciate that the authors attempt to ground their approach in graph information bottleneck theory. The theoretical analysis in Section 4 is one of the stronger parts of the paper, and I found the proof sketch connecting their sparsification to noise elimination genuinely interesting, though I'd want to see the full proofs verified more carefully — some steps felt hand-wavy to me.

That said, I have several concerns that prevent me from enthusiastically recommending acceptance.

First, the experimental evaluation feels thin in places. The baselines are somewhat dated, and I'm not convinced the authors are comparing against the strongest recent pruning and graph sparsification methods. Table 2 in particular — the performance numbers for some datasets look suspiciously close, and the claimed "triple-win" of high sparsity + performance + explainability isn't rigorously demonstrated. How is explainability actually being measured here? The paper gestures at this but never operationalizes it convincingly.

Second, the hierarchical graph sparsification component (Section 3.2 if I recall correctly) is described in somewhat vague terms. After reading this twice I still wasn't entirely sure how the hierarchy is constructed in practice and what the computational cost is. The claim that this avoids "massive information loss" compared to GLT's sampling is asserted more than demonstrated.

Third — and this is maybe my biggest issue — the "dual perspective" framing feels a bit overclaimed. Transforming a random ticket into a lottery ticket versus finding one through iterative pruning are complementary approaches, sure, but I'm not sure this constitutes a genuinely dual perspective in any formal sense. It reads more like a different optimization strategy than a fundamentally new conceptual lens.

The writing is generally okay but has some rough patches, particularly in the introduction where the motivation feels repetitive. The related work section is adequate.

I'm not saying this is bad work. The GIB-based analysis is a genuine contribution and the empirical results are generally positive. But the paper is trying to claim too many things at once — better sparsity, better performance, AND explainability — and doesn't fully deliver on all of them. In my experience, papers that claim triple wins usually end up with mixed wins at best.

I'd lean toward weak reject. The core ideas have merit but need sharper formalization, stronger baselines, and a more honest treatment of the explainability claims. Happy to revise this assessment if the authors can address the evaluation concerns.

This paper proposes using Wasserstein autoencoders as a principled framework for imposing structural constraints like fairness and conditional independence in latent representations. The core claim — that WAEs are "more principled" than VAE-based approaches because penalties arise naturally from the WAE functional constraints rather than being ad hoc — is the central selling point here.

I'll be honest: I found the theoretical exposition in Section 3 somewhat compelling on first read, less so on second. The argument that WAE's constraint-based formulation naturally induces encoder structure is interesting, but I'm not entirely sure the paper makes a convincing case that this is fundamentally different from what you get with a carefully designed VAE penalty. The authors wave at this distinction repeatedly but I felt it was never made rigorous enough to really land. Equation (6) in particular — the derivation of the induced penalty — felt like it was doing a lot of work without sufficient justification of why the specific decomposition chosen is the natural one.

The experimental section is where I have the most concerns. Table 1 and Table 2 show results on standard fairness benchmarks (Adult, German credit), but the margins over baselines like FFVAE and CVAE are modest and I'm genuinely unsure whether the differences are statistically significant — no confidence intervals are reported anywhere. This is a serious issue. In my experience reviewing fairness papers, the choice of demographic parity vs equalized odds metric can completely change which method "wins," and the paper doesn't sufficiently interrogate this sensitivity. The qualitative generation results in Figure 3 look fine but are cherry-picked in a way that's hard to evaluate.

I also think the related work section undersells some relevant recent work on invariant representations — papers from the causal representation learning literature (Schölkopf et al. direction) feel conspicuously absent given the framing around structural constraints and conditional independence.

The writing is generally clear, though the introduction is a bit long-winded and takes too long to get to the actual contribution. Page 4 has a paragraph starting "In this paper, we demonstrate..." that basically repeats the abstract verbatim.

My bigger picture concern is novelty. Combining WAEs with fairness constraints feels incremental — the WAE framework is well-established (Tolstikhin et al. 2018 as the authors acknowledge), and fairness-aware autoencoders have been studied extensively. The "principled" framing needs to be demonstrated more forcefully, ideally with an ablation showing that the induced penalties actually matter versus simpler baselines.

I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form. The ideas aren't bad, but the paper needs stronger theoretical justification for the "principled" claim, statistical rigor in experiments, and a more honest comparison with the broader literature. With significant revision this could be a solid contribution.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically important problem — extending the context window of LLMs without blowing up memory and compute. The recurrent compression idea is not entirely new (it echoes work like AutoCompressors, ICAE, and to some extent Mamba-style recurrent processing), but the specific formulation and the instruction reconstruction component do add something to the conversation.

That said, I have significant concerns about the empirical evaluation that I couldn't shake even after reading the paper a second time.

The passkey retrieval results (nearly 100% at 1M tokens) sound impressive in the abstract, but honestly, passkey retrieval is a pretty synthetic benchmark at this point. I'd argue it tells us very little about whether the model actually *understands* long contexts versus memorizing positional patterns during training. The BLEU-4 of 0.95 on text reconstruction is also hard to interpret without knowing what baseline compression methods score — is 0.95 good? Against what? The paper is somewhat self-congratulatory here without adequate comparison.

The long-text QA results are where I'd focus most attention, and frankly the numbers in Table 2 (if I'm reading it right) show the method is "comparable" but in several cases notably below the uncompressed baseline. The authors wave this away with the compression rate justification, which is fair to some extent, but I think the framing in the abstract is a bit misleading. "Comparable" is doing a lot of heavy lifting.

I'm also not entirely convinced by the instruction reconstruction methodology. The motivation is clear — compression degrades instruction-following — but the proposed fix feels somewhat ad hoc. Why does reconstructing the instruction specifically help? Is it the reconstruction loss during training, the inference procedure, or something else? The ablation in Section 4.x touches on this but doesn't cleanly isolate the contribution. I might be wrong here, but after reading that section twice I still wasn't sure what the key mechanism was.

The compression rate of 32x is impressive on paper but I'd want to see wall-clock time comparisons and actual memory profiles, not just theoretical calculations. In my experience these numbers can be misleading depending on implementation details.

Writing quality is okay but not great. Several sentences in the introduction are awkward ("delve into the prevalent issue" appears twice in slightly different forms), and the related work section reads like a list rather than a coherent positioning of this work. Figure 1 is helpful but Figure 3 is nearly unreadable at print size.

Bottom line: the core idea has merit and the 1M token compression result is eye-catching, but the paper needs stronger baselines, cleaner ablations, and more honest framing of where it falls short. I'm leaning toward rejection in current form, but I think this is fixable with a serious revision.

This paper addresses an interesting problem — how to properly integrate permutation-based pretext tasks into end-to-end self-supervised learning without the usual discretization hack of treating it as classification over a fixed set of permutations. The core idea of using differentiable ranking (specifically building on NeuralSort or similar work, I assume) to backpropagate through the inversion step is technically sound and I appreciate that the authors are tackling something that's been an acknowledged limitation in the field for a while.

That said, I have several concerns after reading this twice.

The most significant issue for me is the comparison with modern SSL baselines. The experiments seem to validate the approach against somewhat dated or narrow baselines. Given that DINO, MAE, and similar methods have largely moved the conversation away from permutation/jigsaw-style pretext tasks, I think the authors need to be much more explicit about where this work fits in the landscape. Is the claim that permutation pretext tasks are competitive with contrastive/masking approaches in general, or just that differentiable ranking improves *within* this family? I found the framing slightly misleading in places — the abstract implies broad improvements that the experimental results in Tables 2-3 don't quite deliver at the scale modern readers expect.

The multi-modal experiments (music, video) are genuinely interesting and I think that's actually the stronger selling point of the paper. The frequency-space permutation for spectrograms is a neat idea and I'd have liked to see this explored more deeply rather than treated somewhat briefly. The video results are reasonable but not striking.

I'm also not entirely sure the ablation in Section 4.x (I'm going from memory on the exact section) sufficiently disentangles the benefit of "more permutations" from "better gradient signal through differentiable ranking." These seem like two separate contributions that could be affecting results in entangled ways, and I think the paper would be stronger if they were more carefully separated.

On the writing — it's generally clear but gets a bit hand-wavy in the theoretical justification for why more permutations necessarily leads to better representations. The intuition makes sense, but I'd want something a bit more rigorous or at least an empirical analysis that more carefully controls for the number of permutations versus the differentiability aspect.

Minor point: Figure 3 is a bit hard to parse at small size, and I had to zoom in significantly to understand what was being illustrated about the patch types.

Overall, I think this is decent work with a genuine technical contribution, but the positioning needs rethinking and the empirical validation could be stronger given how fast this subfield moves. I'm leaning borderline. Not a rejection, but needs revision to sharpen the claims and situate itself more honestly relative to the current state of SSL.

This paper proposes SALT, a hybrid architecture that shares attention weights between transformer layers and linear layers for tabular data. The core idea is relatively straightforward — reuse the attention matrix computed in the transformer block to also weight a parallel linear pathway — and I'll admit the intuition is reasonable. But I have some significant concerns.

First, the empirical evaluation. Table 2 shows comparisons across several benchmarks, but I found the dataset selection somewhat cherry-picked or at least underexplained. Why these particular datasets? The tabular deep learning community has been burned repeatedly by evaluation protocols that favor certain architectures on certain splits. After reading this twice, I couldn't find a clear description of how hyperparameters were tuned across competing methods — this matters enormously when comparing against XGBoost or CatBoost, which are quite sensitive to tuning. If the baselines were run with default parameters while SALT was carefully tuned, the comparisons are basically meaningless.

The technical contribution itself — I'm genuinely uncertain how novel this really is. FT-Transformer, TabNet, and several other architectures have explored various forms of attention-weighted feature interactions. The authors claim the "sharing" is novel, but I think they need to engage more seriously with related work. The description around equations (3)-(5) is where the key idea lives, and honestly it felt underexplained. I had to re-read it several times and I'm still not 100% sure I understand the gradient flow implications of sharing the attention matrix. A diagram showing this more clearly would help significantly — Figure 1 is too high-level.

The semi-supervised and pre-training experiments in Section 4.3 feel tacked on. The improvements look modest and the experimental setup is thin. Why these specific missing-data ratios? What's the baseline for the pre-training scenario?

One thing I did appreciate: the ablation in Table 4 is actually informative and shows the sharing mechanism does contribute positively over a pure transformer baseline. That's good. It's the kind of result that makes me think there's something real here, even if I'm skeptical of the magnitude of the claimed improvements.

The writing needs work. There are grammatical issues throughout ("it leads to improved performance and robustness" appears almost verbatim in multiple places), and some claims are stated with more confidence than the evidence warrants. The claim that SALT "outperforms tree-based ensemble models" is stated pretty baldly in the abstract but the actual margins in the tables are often within noise.

My overall assessment: there's a potentially interesting idea here but the paper in its current form isn't ready. The evaluation methodology needs to be much more rigorous — I'd point the authors to the Grinsztajn et al. (2022) benchmark paper as an example of how to do this properly. The core technical contribution needs clearer exposition. I lean toward rejection but would be willing to reconsider with major revisions addressing the evaluation concerns specifically.

The paper proposes InstructProtein, an LLM framework that attempts to bridge protein sequences and natural language through instruction tuning, with a knowledge graph-based instruction generation component. I've read this twice and have mixed feelings.

Let me start with what I think is genuinely interesting here: the KG-based instruction generation framework is the most novel contribution in my view, and the motivation for addressing annotation imbalance in protein-text corpora is well-founded. Anyone who has worked with Swiss-Prot or similar databases knows this is a real and frustrating problem. The chain-of-thought analogy for protein function reasoning is cute, though I think the authors somewhat oversell how tightly connected their approach actually is to CoT reasoning in NLP.

That said, I have several significant concerns. First, the evaluation metrics. For protein sequence generation, the paper relies heavily on sequence similarity and some functional metrics, but I'm not convinced these adequately capture whether the *generated* proteins are actually functionally coherent. In my experience, you can get reasonable sequence-level scores while producing something biologically meaningless. The authors should discuss this limitation more honestly — it's buried if it's there at all.

Second, the baselines feel cherry-picked or at least underspecified. How exactly were models like ProteinBERT or ESM adapted for the generative tasks here? This matters a lot for the "outperforms by large margins" claim in the abstract, which is frankly oversold language. Large margins compared to what, exactly? Table 2 (if I'm reading this right) shows improvements that are real but not uniformly dramatic.

The pre-training setup is described somewhat vaguely around pages 4-5. I'd want to know more about the data mixture ratios, how the model handles the tokenization boundary between protein sequences and natural language, and whether the observed gains come primarily from pre-training or from instruction tuning. This ablation feels incomplete.

I'm also not entirely sure the KG-derived instructions actually improve reasoning in the way claimed, versus simply adding more training signal. The ablation in Table 4 helps but doesn't fully disentangle these effects. Is the improvement from the KG structure itself, or just more data coverage?

Minor things: Figure 3 is hard to read, the caption doesn't explain the color coding well enough, and there are a few awkward phrasings throughout suggesting non-native English that copy editing would fix.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a real contribution, but the writing needs to be more careful about what is actually demonstrated versus what is aspirational. The "pioneering step" and "revolutionized" language in the abstract is the kind of thing that makes reviewers skeptical before even reading the method. Tone it down. The core ideas are interesting enough to stand without the hype.

I'm leaning weak accept, but the authors need to address the evaluation validity concern substantively in their response.

This paper tackles few-shot intent detection by reframing it as a QA retrieval problem, which is a reasonably interesting angle. I've seen a number of papers try creative reformulations of intent detection recently, and the core idea here isn't entirely novel (there's a growing body of work on cross-encoder/bi-encoder architectures for similar classification-as-retrieval tasks), but the specific combination proposed is at least worth discussing.

My main concern is with the clarity of the technical contribution. After reading sections 3 and 4 twice, I'm still not entirely sure what the architectural novelty is beyond combining existing components. The two-stage training (pre-training with self-supervised contrastive loss, then fine-tuning with token-level similarity) feels incremental. Equation 2 in particular — I found this confusing. The notation around the token-level similarity scoring isn't well-defined, and I couldn't tell whether this was a new scoring function or an adaptation of ColBERT-style MaxSim. If it's the latter, that should be stated explicitly and the delta from prior work made clear.

The experimental results on the three benchmarks (SNIPS, CLINC150, and NLU-Benchmark based on Table 2) look strong, and I appreciate that they include multiple K-shot settings. But I'm skeptical of some comparisons. Several baselines in Table 2 appear to use different backbone models — I might be wrong but I don't think this is adequately controlled for. When you swap in a stronger pre-trained model, performance differences can easily be explained by the backbone rather than the method. This is a real problem and needs to be addressed directly.

The ablation in Table 3 is helpful but incomplete. I'd want to see what happens when you remove the QA reformulation entirely and just train a standard bi-encoder with the same contrastive losses. That's the critical ablation. Without it, I can't tell if the QA framing is doing real work or if you're just getting gains from the training recipe.

I found the related work section a bit thin on the retrieval-augmented and dense retrieval side — DPR, ANCE, and similar work feel relevant and aren't discussed. Given that the whole architecture is essentially a dense retrieval system, this seems like an oversight.

Writing quality is okay but uneven. The abstract is fine, but around page 5-6 the prose gets dense and hard to follow. Some sentences in section 4.2 are doing too much work.

Overall: the results are promising and the idea is interesting, but the paper needs stronger justification of novelty, better-controlled baselines, and a cleaner ablation story. In its current form I think it's borderline. I'd lean toward weak reject but I'm genuinely uncertain — if the authors can address the ablation gap and the baseline fairness issue, this could be a solid contribution.

This paper introduces MMMT-IF, a benchmark for evaluating instruction following in multimodal multi-turn dialogues, along with the PIF metric family. I'll share my thoughts on this work, which I think has some genuinely useful contributions but also some notable gaps.

The core idea is solid and addresses a real problem — existing benchmarks don't adequately stress-test models' ability to maintain instruction compliance over long, multi-turn dialogues with interleaved images. The observation about LLM judges being biased toward outputs from the same model family is interesting and I'd actually like to see this explored more rigorously. It feels almost like a throwaway finding when it deserves its own analysis.

The PIF metric is clean and I appreciate that it's grounded in code execution rather than model-based judgment. That's the right call. However, the 60% correlation with human ratings (mentioned in the abstract) is... honestly not that impressive? I've seen similar papers claim this is sufficient, but in my experience, 60% leaves a lot of room for the metric to miss things humans care about. The authors should be more forthcoming about what the remaining 40% disagreement looks like — are there systematic failure modes? Is the metric consistently wrong about certain instruction types?

The experimental findings themselves are interesting. The drop from 0.81 to 0.64 PIF across turns 1-20 is meaningful, and the "append instructions to end of context" ablation showing 22.3 point improvement is probably the most practically useful finding in the paper. This clearly implicates retrieval over long contexts as a key bottleneck, which I think is an important insight.

A few things that genuinely bothered me: First, the dataset construction methodology feels underspecified. How were the global instructions selected? What's the distribution of instruction types? I couldn't find a clear breakdown — maybe I missed it but after reading through the paper twice I still wasn't sure. Second, the benchmark only covers image-based Q&A. Why not video or other modalities? The "multimodal" framing feels a bit oversold for something that's really just image+text. Third, I'm not entirely sure the PIF-N-K family of metrics adds as much as the authors claim — the presentation around equations in that section is convoluted and I think a cleaner formulation with a concrete worked example would help enormously.

The comparison across only three commercial models (Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) is limiting. I understand access issues, but including even one open-source model like LLaMA or InternVL would strengthen the paper considerably.

Overall I think this is decent work with a useful benchmark, but the paper needs tighter writing, better justification of design choices, and more thorough analysis of the metric's failure cases. I'd lean toward weak accept contingent on revisions.

This paper proposes a "Local VAE" that attempts to handle datasets with locality structure by combining VAEs with gradient-based meta-learning (essentially MAML-style adaptation) to learn separate parameters for local neighborhoods. I've read through this twice and I have fairly significant concerns.

The core idea — that real-world data has local structure and we should have location-dependent model parameters — is reasonable enough in principle. But I think the paper severely undersells the connection to existing work. The relationship to MAML (Finn et al., 2017) and its many variants is acknowledged but not really grappled with seriously. This is essentially meta-learning applied to a generative model, and the literature on that is already quite rich (e.g., VAMPRIOR, various conditional VAE approaches, hierarchical VAEs). The novelty claim feels thin to me.

The experimental section is where I have the most trouble. The authors primarily demonstrate results on 3D Shapes, which is a synthetic, relatively clean dataset specifically designed to have disentangled factors. I'm not entirely sure this is the best showcase for "multi-scale structured datasets" — it almost feels like cherry-picking a dataset where you'd expect this kind of approach to work. Where are the results on something messier? CelebA? Something with genuinely complex local structure? The FID scores reported in Table 2 are better than baseline VAE, sure, but the margins aren't always convincing, and there's no statistical significance testing across runs.

Figure 3 is honestly hard to interpret. The generated samples look reasonable but I can't tell from the visualization alone whether the model is actually capturing locality or just learning a better global representation. More ablations here would really help — what happens when you remove the meta-learning component and just fine-tune locally? That baseline seems conspicuously absent.

The writing is also a problem. Several passages in the related work section (around page 3-4) are quite dense and at times confusing — "the embedding model for the other local structure as a weak form of supervision" took me a few readings to parse. The abstract similarly uses the term "locality" in a way that I think means something specific to the authors but won't land cleanly for most readers.

I'm also skeptical about scalability. The per-neighborhood parameter approach with meta-learning is computationally expensive, and the paper doesn't really address this honestly. The training times mentioned in passing suggest this doesn't scale well.

My overall assessment: the idea has some merit and I don't think it's wrong, exactly, but in its current form the paper feels incomplete. The experiments need to be substantially stronger, the baselines need to be more carefully chosen, and the theoretical justification for why this specific formulation is the right one is largely missing. I'd want to see this revised pretty substantially before it appears at a top venue.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely question — whether attention mechanisms are strictly necessary for in-context learning, or whether alternative architectures can match or exceed transformer performance on ICL tasks. I appreciate the breadth of the empirical sweep here; evaluating thirteen architectures across synthetic ICL benchmarks is genuinely useful for the community.

That said, I have real concerns about the depth of the conclusions. After reading this twice, I kept coming back to the same issue: the synthetic tasks (linear regression, classification variants, etc.) may simply not be representative enough to make strong claims. The abstract says "all considered architectures can perform ICL under a wider range of conditions than previously documented" — but wider than what baseline? The comparison point is underspecified in the intro and I had to dig into Section 3 to understand what prior work was actually being challenged. That framing should be front-loaded more clearly.

The memorization vs. in-context generalization experiment (Section 4.3 I think, around page 7-8) is actually the most interesting contribution here, in my opinion. The idea of measuring each architecture's "predisposition" toward memorization rather than leveraging context is novel and I found myself wanting much more of this analysis. Instead it feels somewhat rushed. Why do some architectures preferentially memorize? Is this a capacity issue, an inductive bias issue? The paper raises the question and then kind of... moves on.

On the architecture side — I'm not entirely sure the selection of 13 models is as principled as the paper implies. Some of these architectures (particularly some of the SSM variants) have quite different parameter counts and training dynamics, and I didn't feel the ablations adequately controlled for this. Table 2 shows some stark differences but the caption doesn't clarify whether these are matched by parameter count or FLOPs. This matters a lot for the conclusions.

The finding that "no single architecture demonstrates consistency across all tasks" is fine but honestly feels a bit like a non-result dressed up as a finding. I'd push back on presenting this as a primary contribution.

The writing is generally clear, though I found Figure 3 difficult to parse on first read — the color scheme between architectures is too similar for several of the lines.

My overall take: this is a solid empirical paper with a good research question, but the execution is uneven. The memorization angle deserves to be the centerpiece, not buried. The claims in the abstract occasionally outrun what the experiments actually demonstrate. In my experience, papers like this live or die on whether the benchmark design is convincing, and I'm not fully persuaded the synthetic tasks here are rich enough to support architecture-level conclusions. I'd lean toward weak reject but could be convinced otherwise if the authors address the controlled comparison issue and expand the memorization analysis.

This paper tackles an interesting angle on the NVAS problem — instead of trying to synthesize full RIRs directly, it reformulates the task as estimating spatially distributed acoustic parameters (T60, C50, etc.) via an image-to-image translation from floormaps to parameter heatmaps. I appreciate the framing and think there's a real contribution here, but I have significant concerns about the evaluation and some of the methodological choices that I think need to be addressed.

Let me start with what I found genuinely interesting. The dataset of 1000 multi-room apartment scenes is a meaningful contribution — most prior work (AV-NeRF, SoundSpaces, etc.) really does suffer from the single-room limitation, and I've been frustrated by this myself when thinking about practical AR applications. The beamformed parameter prediction extension is also a nice touch that I wasn't expecting from the abstract.

That said, I have real problems with the evaluation. The baselines in Table 2 feel thin. Comparing against "statistical baselines" is not convincing to me in 2024. Why not compare against a method that predicts acoustic parameters from geometry more directly, or at minimum ablate against a stronger learned baseline? I found myself after reading this twice still not convinced that the image-to-image translation approach is doing anything substantially better than what a well-calibrated room-volume estimator might achieve. The relationship between floormap complexity and prediction accuracy is never really explored, which feels like a significant gap.

The conditioning mechanism for the reverberator (Section 4.2 if I recall correctly) is underspecified. How sensitive is the final perceptual quality to errors in the estimated parameters? The paper claims perceptual plausibility but the listening tests, if I'm reading Table 3 correctly, seem limited in scope. How many listeners? What stimuli?

I'm also not entirely sure about the claim that only "a single RIR at inference time" is required — this feels like it's doing a lot of heavy lifting and the practical implications aren't fully worked out. What happens when that single RIR is measured in a geometrically atypical location? The paper doesn't seem to address robustness here.

The writing is generally clear but Figure 4 is honestly pretty hard to read at print size and the colormap choice for the heatmaps makes it difficult to compare predicted vs. ground truth distributions.

Look, I think the core idea is sound and publishable in some form. The multi-room dataset alone might justify acceptance at a workshop. But for a main track paper, the evaluation needs to be substantially strengthened, the reverberator integration needs more rigorous perceptual validation, and the authors need to engage more seriously with failure cases. Right now it reads like a promising system paper that stopped short of doing the hard work of convincing me it actually works well.

Borderline reject, leaning toward weak accept if the authors can address the evaluation concerns in a rebuttal.

This paper proposes using normalizing flows as a drop-in replacement for isotonic regression in the calibration/recalibration pipeline. The core idea is framing recalibration as MLE, which I think is actually a pretty clean insight, and then leveraging the structure of normalizing flows to get something that's both calibrated AND analytically tractable (means, correlations, etc.). The multivariate extension is the part I find most compelling — isotonic regression really does fall apart there, and I've seen people just ignore multivariate calibration entirely in practice.

That said, I have significant concerns.

The experimental section is thin. Table 2 shows comparisons on a handful of regression benchmarks, but the baselines feel cherry-picked. Temperature scaling and isotonic regression are there, but where's the comparison against GP-based recalibration methods, or against something like CRUDE (Chung et al. 2021)? I'm not entirely sure those are exact apples-to-apples comparisons, but the authors should at least discuss them. After reading this twice I still couldn't tell if the gains in Table 2 are statistically significant — there are no error bars or confidence intervals reported, which is a pretty glaring omission.

The theoretical contribution feels a bit undercooked. The claim in Section 3 that the MLE framing is "equivalent" to minimizing the calibration error — I found this confusing and I'm not convinced the proof sketch in the appendix is complete. Specifically, the step around equation (7) seems to assume the validation set is drawn i.i.d. from the same distribution as training, which is a non-trivial assumption that gets one sentence of acknowledgment.

On the flow architecture side — the paper uses RealNVP throughout but never really justifies this choice. Would autoregressive flows work better for the multivariate case? This feels like an obvious ablation that's missing.

Figure 3 (the Q-Q plot extension) is actually the clearest part of the paper and I appreciated the clean visual. Though calling it a "simple extension" in the abstract might be underselling it, or... maybe it is that simple and I'm overthinking it. Hard to tell.

My bigger picture worry is the overfitting issue the authors themselves flag. They mention it briefly but don't really provide guidance on how to choose flow capacity or regularize in practice. This matters a lot for the practitioner who would actually use this. In my experience, recalibration on small validation sets is already fragile with isotonic regression — adding a more expressive model without clear regularization guidance seems like it could make things worse in realistic settings.

I think there's a real paper here. The framing is novel enough and the multivariate angle is genuinely useful. But in its current form the empirical evaluation doesn't support the claims being made.
