I'd want to see stronger baselines, error bars, and a more careful treatment of the theoretical claims before recommending acceptance.

Borderline reject, leaning toward weak reject.

This paper proposes using graph coarsening as a privacy mechanism in federated learning settings for graph-level tasks, arguing that coarsened graph representations inherently conceal sensitive information and thus can replace or supplement differential privacy approaches. I've spent considerable time with this paper and have significant concerns.

The core claim — that graph coarsening "alone can suffice for privacy guarantees" — is stated boldly in the abstract but I never felt the authors actually proved this in any rigorous sense. Where is the formal privacy guarantee? DP has epsilon-delta guarantees. What does coarsening give you? The paper gestures toward empirical resistance to attacks, which is not the same thing as a provable privacy bound. This is a fundamental gap, not a minor one. After reading sections 3 and 4 twice, I still couldn't find anything resembling a formal theorem about what information is actually concealed and under what attack model. I might be missing something but if I am, the presentation is at fault.

The experimental evaluation compares against DP baselines, but the comparison feels somewhat cherry-picked. DP with very tight epsilon is of course going to hurt utility — that's expected. The authors are essentially showing that a lossy compression technique preserves more utility than very strong privacy guarantees, which is... not surprising? What I want to know is: at equivalent empirical privacy leakage levels, how does coarsening compare to DP? Table 2 doesn't answer this clearly.

I also found the attack evaluation unconvincing. Testing against a gradient inversion attack and showing coarsening makes reconstruction harder is fine, but the threat model is narrow. What about membership inference? Property inference? The claim of comprehensive privacy protection from a single attack type feels premature.

On the positive side — the method itself is reasonably well-described and the coarsening formulation in Section 3.2 is clear. The computational overhead analysis is a genuine contribution and I believe the efficiency claims. The writing is generally okay, though there are awkward passages around lines 180-200 that could use editing.

The federated learning integration feels somewhat underexplored honestly. Most of the technical content is about the coarsening itself, and the FL aspects (aggregation, communication rounds, heterogeneity across clients) get limited attention. Figure 1 is helpful but raises questions that aren't answered — what happens when different clients have very differently structured graphs?

In my experience, papers making privacy claims without formal guarantees tend to get found out eventually. I'm not saying the empirical results are wrong, but the positioning of this work as a privacy-preserving method without formal backing is problematic for a venue that takes security seriously. The authors should either scope down their claims significantly or do the hard work of establishing theoretical guarantees.

Leaning toward rejection in current form. The idea isn't without merit and I'd encourage revision, but the mismatch between the claims and what's actually demonstrated is too large to overlook.

This paper tackles the domain bias problem in generalized zero-shot learning using a relation-based approach rather than the now-dominant synthesis-based methods. I appreciate the motivation — synthesis-based methods are indeed expensive and the community has perhaps over-invested in that direction. The core idea of using a domain discriminator on a shared VAE embedding space to counteract seen-class bias is reasonable and fairly intuitive.

That said, I have significant concerns about both the technical novelty and the experimental rigor.

On the technical side, the combination of VAEs with adversarial domain discriminators is not new — this has been explored in domain adaptation literature extensively, and several GZSL papers (e.g., CADA-VAE, f-CLSWGAN variants) already leverage alignment objectives in latent spaces. I think the authors need to be much more precise about what exactly is novel here. The "mixture model" combination described in the abstract sounds promising but after reading the relevant section twice I found myself struggling to understand the actual inference procedure. The formulation around the discriminator output as "prior knowledge" (I believe this is around Section 3.3?) is hand-wavy. Is this a proper probabilistic model? What are the theoretical guarantees, if any? The math needs to be tightened considerably.

The experimental results are my bigger concern. The paper claims "almost equal accuracy to high-cost synthesis-based methods" but looking at Table 1 (and Table 2 for CUB/SUN/AWA datasets), the harmonic mean H scores are consistently below state-of-the-art synthesis methods by non-trivial margins in several cases — 2-4 points on CUB, which in my experience is actually quite significant. Calling this "almost equal" feels like overselling. I'd also want to see error bars across multiple runs, which are absent entirely.

The ablation study is appreciated but thin. I want to understand the contribution of each component more carefully — specifically, how much of the gain comes from the VAE architecture choices versus the discriminator versus the mixture model combination. Right now it's hard to disentangle these.

Writing quality is honestly subpar in places. There are grammatical issues throughout and some sentences on pages 4-5 are genuinely hard to parse. I might be wrong but some passages read like they were translated from another language without sufficient editing. This needs a thorough proofread.

I'm not entirely sure the authors fully engage with recent work from 2022-2023 in the related work section — the literature review feels dated.

Overall, the idea has merit and I think there's a paper here eventually, but in its current form the contribution feels incremental, the claims are overstated, and the presentation needs substantial work. I lean toward rejection but would genuinely reconsider with a strong revision addressing the technical clarity and experimental honesty issues.

This paper tackles video affective computing, specifically valence/arousal prediction, using a multimodal fusion approach with what the authors call a "long-short temporal context" mechanism. I've been working adjacent to this area for a while and have seen quite a few papers on this problem, so I'll try to be direct about where I think this lands.

The core idea — using separate temporal scales for intra-clip and inter-clip modeling — is reasonable and not entirely novel, but the specific implementation choices seem sensible. The residual-based progressive training strategy is probably the most interesting contribution here, though I found the explanation in Section 3.3 somewhat hard to follow on first read. After going through it twice I think I understand what they're doing, but the writing needs significant work. Figure 2 helped but the caption is too terse.

My bigger concern is the evaluation. The "large margin" claim in the abstract is bold, and when I looked at Table 2, the improvements are real but I wouldn't call all of them "large." On some metrics the gains are modest. The authors should either temper this language or provide statistical significance testing — which is conspicuously absent throughout. In my experience, papers in affective computing often cherry-pick the metrics where they look best, and I'm a bit suspicious here.

The modality selection process described in Section 3.1 is presented somewhat casually. They say they "select the most suitable modalities for valence and arousal tasks respectively" but the justification feels post-hoc. Was this selection done on the test set? I couldn't tell from the paper, and this is actually a fairly important methodological question.

I also think the related work section is thin. There are several relevant papers on multimodal temporal modeling for affective video analysis — including some work on transformer-based approaches from the last two years — that aren't discussed at all. This makes it harder to contextualize the contribution properly.

On the positive side: the overall framework is clearly motivated, the ablation study (Table 3, I think?) is genuinely informative and I appreciated that they tested components individually. The visual features extraction choices seem well-considered.

Minor things: the writing throughout has grammar issues that suggest non-native English, which is fine, but some sentences are genuinely unclear (page 5, the paragraph beginning "To combine the complementary..."). Also "with a large margin" appears in both the abstract and conclusion — this kind of self-congratulatory phrasing reads poorly.

Overall I think there's a publishable paper somewhere in here, but in its current form it needs clearer writing, stronger experimental rigor (significance tests, cleaner ablations), and better positioning against recent work. I'm leaning toward weak reject with encouragement to resubmit after revision. The ideas aren't bad, the execution just isn't there yet.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — how to constrain deployed LLMs to specific use cases rather than letting them respond to arbitrary queries. The framing of "scoping" is reasonable and I appreciate that the authors distinguish between simple refusal of harmful content (which has been studied extensively) and the more nuanced problem of restricting models to intended task domains.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The core technical contribution feels thin. The paper essentially adapts Circuit Breakers (Zou et al.) to a new application setting. I don't see a fundamental methodological innovation here — it's more of an empirical study of existing methods applied to a new problem definition. That's not necessarily disqualifying for a venue like this, but the empirical evaluation needs to be really thorough and convincing to carry the paper on its own, and I'm not sure it is.

On the evaluation side: the choice of "sentiment analysis" and "summarization" as the target scoped tasks feels somewhat arbitrary and easy. These are clean, well-defined NLP tasks. Real deployments (the paper mentions documentation Q&A and coding assistants in the abstract) are messier. The gap between the motivating examples in the introduction and the actual experimental setup bothered me after reading the paper twice. Why not evaluate on something closer to a real RAG or documentation assistant scenario? The finer-grained "news article summarization" experiment is a step in the right direction but still feels controlled.

I'm also concerned about the adversarial robustness claims. The paper shows CB is more robust to adversarial prompting than fine-tuning, which is interesting, but the adversarial attacks evaluated seem relatively weak. In my experience, adaptive attacks specifically designed against representation-space interventions like CB can be quite effective (see e.g. work on bypassing activation-space defenses). I'd want to see stronger adaptive baselines before believing the robustness story.

The SFT+CB combination result (Section 5 or thereabouts) is probably the most practically useful finding and I think it deserves more analysis. Why does this combination work? Is CB preventing the SFT from generalizing too broadly, or is something else happening mechanistically? Right now it reads as an empirical observation without much explanation.

The writing is generally clear but the related work section undersells some relevant literature on task-specific fine-tuning and constitutional AI approaches that seem directly relevant. I might be wrong but I feel like there's a body of work on "guardrails" systems (NeMo Guardrails, etc.) that deserves more serious comparison rather than just a passing mention.

Overall: the problem is real and worth studying, the results are somewhat interesting, but I don't think the paper makes a sufficiently strong contribution at its current state. The empirical gaps and shallow mechanistic understanding hold it back. I lean toward rejection but would be willing to reconsider with a stronger evaluation and clearer positioning of what's actually novel here.

This paper proposes MamBEV, a Mamba/SSM-based architecture for multi-camera BEV perception in autonomous driving. The core idea is to replace the quadratic attention mechanisms in transformer-based BEV methods (like BEVFormer) with linear SSM-based operations, which is a reasonable and timely research direction given the recent interest in Mamba for vision tasks.

That said, I have significant concerns about this submission.

The experimental results are underwhelming. Looking at Table 1 and Table 2, MamBEV doesn't actually outperform the transformer baselines on the main metrics — the authors frame this as "promising performance" but honestly that's generous. The NDS and mAP numbers on nuScenes lag behind BEVFormer and several other baselines. I understand the argument is about efficiency, but the efficiency gains shown in Figure 3 (the scaling curves) while real, don't feel large enough to justify the performance tradeoff in practical settings. Autonomous driving is a safety-critical application — you don't get to say "well it's 15% faster" when your detection numbers are noticeably lower.

The SSM cross-attention formulation described around equations 3-5 is the most technically interesting contribution, but I found the explanation genuinely confusing after reading it twice. The mapping from standard cross-attention to the SSM formulation isn't made rigorous. What are the theoretical guarantees here? How does the SSM "select" relevant image features in the way deformable attention does spatially? This needed much more careful treatment.

I'm also not entirely convinced the comparison is fair. BEVFormer uses deformable attention which is already quite efficient — it's not vanilla O(n²) attention. The paper should be comparing against this more carefully rather than implying the baseline is some naive quadratic transformer.

The ablation studies (Table 3 I think?) are appreciated but feel thin. What happens if you just replace the SSM blocks with linear attention? Is the specific Mamba formulation crucial or is it just the linearity that helps? I'd want to see this teased apart.

One more thing — the writing needs work. There are several places where the motivation jumps around and I found myself re-reading paragraphs. The related work section is particularly rushed.

On the positive side, I do think the research direction is valid and the problem setup is well-motivated. The BEV perception + SSM combination makes intuitive sense and I haven't seen it done quite this way before. The computational scaling experiments are a genuine contribution.

But in my experience, a paper at this venue needs to either (a) show competitive or superior performance OR (b) show dramatic efficiency improvements with only modest performance loss. MamBEV currently sits in an uncomfortable middle ground. I'd encourage the authors to revise substantially — either push the performance numbers harder or make a cleaner, more honest efficiency-focused argument with stronger theoretical grounding. As it stands, I lean toward rejection.

This paper tackles symbolic regression using an RNN trained with policy gradients — the core idea being to use a large model to search for small models (mathematical expressions). I've read this paper twice now and I think the framing is clever and mostly well-executed, though I have some reservations I'll get to.

The risk-seeking policy gradient is probably the most interesting technical contribution here. The idea of optimizing for best-case rather than expected performance makes intuitive sense for this problem — you don't care about the average quality of sampled expressions, you care whether *any* of them nail the ground truth. Equation 8 and the surrounding derivation in Section 3.2 are clear. That said, I'd like to see a more thorough theoretical treatment of convergence. The authors gesture at this but don't really nail it down. I'm not entirely sure the epsilon-quantile formulation is as novel as claimed — there's related work in CVaR optimization and risk-sensitive RL that I think deserves more discussion.

The experimental results against Eureqa are compelling on the surface, but I found the benchmark setup a bit too convenient. The Nguyen benchmark suite (Table 1) has been criticized elsewhere for being relatively easy — low-dimensional, clean functional forms. How does this scale to messier real-world datasets? The noise experiments are appreciated but feel somewhat tacked on. I'd really want to see this tested on something like the Feynman symbolic regression benchmark or similar.

I'm also skeptical about the computational cost story. Training the RNN takes significant resources, and the comparison to GP-based methods like Eureqa doesn't adequately account for wall-clock time. In my experience, Eureqa is remarkably fast in practice. Table 2 shows recovery rates but I don't see a fair apples-to-apples runtime comparison anywhere that I could find — if it's there and I missed it, it needs to be more prominent.

The presentation is generally solid. Figure 2 showing expression trees is helpful for readers unfamiliar with the representation. The writing is clear throughout Section 2 and 3. Section 4 gets a bit dense.

One thing I genuinely liked: the in-situ constraint incorporation is practically very useful and the authors demonstrate it convincingly. This feels like a real advantage over prior work.

Minor stuff: the abstract says "underexplored" which I think is slightly overstated given prior GP and GP+NN hybrid work. The related work section handles this okay but could be more generous to prior deep learning approaches.

Overall I think this is a good paper with a genuine contribution. The risk-seeking gradient formulation is interesting beyond symbolic regression. But the benchmarking needs strengthening — particularly runtime comparisons and harder benchmark suites — before I'd feel fully confident recommending acceptance. Leaning weak accept, conditionally on the authors addressing the computational overhead question more directly.

This paper tackles an interesting and relevant question about functional organization in V1 — specifically whether neurons cluster into distinct cell types analogous to retinal ganglion cell types. The rotation-equivariant CNN approach as a backbone for this analysis is a reasonable choice given recent work in this space (Ecker et al., Lurz et al., and the broader line of work from Bethge/Sinz groups).

That said, I have significant concerns about the paper in its current form.

The core methodological contribution feels somewhat thin to me. The "rotation-invariant clustering" idea is conceptually straightforward — essentially you're factoring out orientation preference before clustering in the CNN feature space — and I'm not entirely convinced this required a full paper to present. After reading the methods section twice I'm still fuzzy on some of the details. Specifically, how sensitive are the resulting clusters to the choice of clustering algorithm and the number of clusters? I see results for a particular configuration but there's no real ablation here. In my experience, these kinds of unsupervised clustering analyses can be extremely sensitive to hyperparameter choices and the authors don't adequately address this.

The validation of the clusters is weak. What's the evidence that these clusters are "real" in any meaningful biological sense rather than artifacts of the CNN architecture itself? This is the elephant in the room and I don't think the paper handles it satisfactorily. The preferred stimuli visualizations (Figures 3-4 I think) are visually compelling but also somewhat uninterpretable — what am I supposed to conclude from them exactly?

The dataset of 6000 neurons sounds impressive but I'd want to know more about data quality, signal-to-noise, and whether neurons with poor model fits are handled appropriately or just silently influence the clustering.

I'm also not sure the framing around "cell types" is entirely appropriate here. Functional clustering in response space is quite different from establishing cell types in the retinal sense, and the paper occasionally conflates these. The retina comparison in the abstract sets up an analogy that, frankly, the paper doesn't fully deliver on.

On the positive side — the paper is clearly written, the problem is well-motivated, and the use of rotation-equivariant representations is genuinely clever for this application. The mouse V1 dataset is substantial.

But overall I think this is preliminary work that needs more rigorous validation and a more careful treatment of what these clusters actually mean. The authors should engage more seriously with alternative explanations and with the sensitivity of their results. I'd also strongly encourage comparison with simpler baselines — does naive clustering without the rotation-invariant trick give substantially different results, and if so, which is more biologically meaningful?

I'm leaning toward rejection in current form, but I think there's a real paper here if the authors do the harder work of validating what they've found.

This paper proposes using a nonparametric scoring test to assess connection importance in DNNs and prune insignificant connections. The core idea is framed as hypothesis testing over connection relevance, which is a reasonable angle — I've seen related ideas in the Bayesian pruning literature, but the specific nonparametric framing is at least somewhat distinct.

That said, I have serious concerns about this submission in its current form.

The abstract already contains a typo ("a mount") and "nonparemtric" is misspelled. This is the abstract — if the authors haven't proofread the abstract, I'm not confident the technical content has been carefully checked either. After reading the paper twice, I found the writing throughout to be rough and imprecise in ways that make it hard to evaluate the actual technical contribution.

The core technical contribution is underspecified. The "nonparametric scoring test" is never rigorously defined in a way I found satisfying. Which test exactly? What are the null and alternative hypotheses formally? Equation 3 (if I'm reading the notation correctly) seems to conflate the test statistic with the p-value threshold, which is either a notational problem or a conceptual one — I'm not sure which, and that uncertainty itself is a problem. The "lossless" claim in the title is doing a lot of heavy lifting. Lossless with respect to what metric, under what conditions? This needs to be stated precisely or dropped.

The experiments are weak. Comparisons are made against a small set of baselines, and I don't see comparisons against more recent structured pruning methods or magnitude-based pruning with retraining, which is a strong and embarrassingly simple baseline that many fancy methods fail to beat. The datasets used (I'm assuming standard benchmarks based on the results tables) are fine, but the compression ratio improvements over baselines look marginal in several cases — the authors should do significance testing given the variance across runs.

I think the probabilistic framing is potentially interesting and I don't want to dismiss the idea entirely. In my experience, hypothesis-testing perspectives on pruning can yield principled sparsity control, and there's genuine value in that. But the current paper doesn't deliver on that promise rigorously enough.

The related work section is thin. Papers like Molchanov et al. on variational dropout and Louizos et al. on L0 regularization feel directly relevant and are either missing or underengaged with.

My overall assessment: this is not ready for publication. The idea has a kernel of interest but the execution — technically, experimentally, and in terms of writing quality — is not at the level expected here. I'd encourage a significant revision with clearer formalism, stronger baselines, and frankly a more careful proofread before resubmission.

This paper proposes using MLLMs to assist VLMs in grounding tasks by providing semantic guidance, evaluated on camouflage detection, anomaly detection, and medical image segmentation. I'll be direct: in its current form, this paper has significant issues that prevent me from recommending acceptance.

The core idea isn't unreasonably bad — using language models to provide semantic context for challenging segmentation scenarios is a reasonable direction. But the execution and presentation are both quite weak. After reading this twice I still couldn't fully reconstruct the actual technical contribution. What exactly is the "formatting instruction-based guidance" mentioned in the abstract? This seems like it should be central to the method but is described so vaguely I genuinely couldn't tell what was novel versus what was just prompting an off-the-shelf MLLM and feeding text to a VLM. Is there any learned component here at all?

The experimental section is where my concerns really pile up. Comparing against "zero-shot baselines" and showing 10%+ improvement is... not very convincing. Zero-shot baselines are a low bar. Where are the comparisons against task-specific fine-tuned methods? For camouflage object detection alone there's a substantial literature (COD10K, SINet, etc.) that this work seems to largely sidestep. I might be wrong but I don't think the authors are claiming SOTA — if that's intentional they should be much clearer about what niche they're actually filling.

I found the figures confusing. Without seeing the actual paper in full detail I can't point to specific figure numbers, but the abstract suggests a generalizable framework and I'd want to see clear architectural diagrams showing exactly how MLLM outputs are formatted and consumed by the VLM.

The framing also bothers me a bit. The paper calls them "M-LLMs" and "VLMs" somewhat interchangeably with inconsistent terminology. GPT-4V is both a VLM and an MLLM by most definitions — the distinction the authors are drawing isn't cleanly established.

In my experience reviewing grounding papers, the interesting question isn't whether adding semantic context helps (it usually does, obviously), but WHEN it helps, HOW MUCH it costs at inference time, and whether the gains justify the added complexity and latency of chaining two large models. None of these questions are addressed.

The 10%+ improvement number in the abstract with no context for what metric, what dataset split, or what baseline is a red flag for me. That kind of vague quantitative claim in an abstract suggests the paper may be cherry-picking its most favorable result.

I think there's potentially a publishable idea buried here but it needs substantially more rigorous evaluation, clearer technical description, and honest comparison with competitive methods. As submitted, I cannot recommend acceptance. Major revision at minimum.

This paper tackles an important question in quantum machine learning — specifically, whether quantum kernel methods retain meaningful predictive power under realistic noise conditions. The theoretical results around concentration of noisy quantum kernels under global depolarizing noise are interesting, and I think the community needs more of this kind of critical analysis rather than optimistic hype.

That said, I have significant concerns about the scope and novelty of the contributions.

The main result essentially shows that noisy quantum kernels concentrate their predictions toward a fixed value, which feels like a restatement (perhaps with more quantitative precision) of what's already known about barren plateaus and noise-induced concentration phenomena. The connection to prior work by Thanasilp et al. and others studying kernel concentration is not adequately distinguished. After reading Section 3 twice, I couldn't clearly identify what's genuinely new here versus what's a corollary of existing concentration results. The authors need to be much more explicit about this.

The restriction to global depolarization noise is a real limitation that the abstract somewhat glosses over. In practice, noise is local, gate-dependent, and highly structured. I'm not entirely sure how much the conclusions change under more realistic noise models, but the paper doesn't really grapple with this. One or two sentences of handwaving in the discussion section doesn't cut it for a theoretical paper making broad claims about "noisy quantum kernels."

The convergence rate characterization in terms of number of qubits, layers, shots, etc. (Section 4 I believe, around Theorem 2) is the most technically interesting part. But the presentation is dense and the dependence on training sample size n feels underexplored — specifically, whether there's a regime where you can compensate for noise by increasing samples. The authors seem to suggest no, but this deserves more careful treatment.

I found the writing somewhat inconsistent in quality. The introduction reads well, but certain proof sketches in the appendix are hard to follow. Some notation is introduced without adequate definition — equation (7) particularly confused me on first reading.

There's also a missing discussion of fault-tolerant regimes. The paper frames itself as a warning about near-term devices, which is fair, but it would strengthen the work considerably to discuss where the noise threshold needs to be for quantum kernels to recover meaningful power.

In my experience, papers that claim to provide "crucial warnings" to the community need to be especially rigorous about distinguishing what's truly new. Right now this feels like an incremental contribution that's dressed up as more foundational than it actually is. I'd recommend major revision — tighten the novelty claims, broaden the noise model discussion even if only theoretically, and improve the readability of the technical sections. The core idea is worth publishing somewhere, but not in the current form.

This paper proposes UO-Explainer, a framework that uses graphlets and their orbits as interpretable units for explaining GNN predictions at both model and instance levels. The core idea is interesting — graphlets are indeed well-established in network biology and other domains, so grounding explanations in these structures has intuitive appeal. I've seen orbit-based approaches used in feature engineering contexts before, but applying them specifically to decompose GNN predictions in this way is a reasonable contribution.

That said, I have significant concerns about this work.

The theoretical justification for why orbit decomposition yields *faithful* explanations is underdeveloped. The paper spends considerable effort describing the mechanics of the approach but I couldn't find a rigorous argument (around Section 3-4, from what I could tell) explaining why decomposing GNN weights into orbit units actually captures what the model is doing, rather than just producing a post-hoc rationalization. This is the central question for any explainability method and it's glossed over. After reading this twice I'm still not fully satisfied with the faithfulness guarantees being offered.

The experimental section has issues too. The baselines selected feel somewhat dated — GNNExplainer, SubgraphX, etc. are fine but there are more recent methods that should be included. More importantly, the evaluation metrics for "human interpretability" are weak. Calling something human-interpretable and then evaluating it primarily on fidelity and sparsity metrics is somewhat circular. Where's the user study? The authors claim interpretability is a key motivation but provide no human evaluation whatsoever. In my experience, this is a fatal flaw for a paper centered on this specific selling point.

The "unified framework" claim in the title also feels a bit oversold. Model-level and instance-level explanations are both present, sure, but the connection between them isn't as tight as the framing implies — they feel more like two separate components that share the orbit vocabulary rather than a genuinely unified theoretical framework.

On the positive side, the graphlet-based decomposition is reasonably well-motivated for domains like biology and chemistry where graphlets have established meaning. Figure 3 is actually quite clear and I thought the visualization of orbit patterns was one of the stronger parts of the paper. The scalability discussion is appreciated, though I'd want to see more rigorous runtime analysis on larger graphs.

I'm also not entirely sure about the claim that orbits are "universal across graph domains" — this feels too strong. Social networks and molecular graphs have very different structural properties and what constitutes a meaningful substructure differs substantially.

Overall, the paper presents an idea with genuine potential but in its current form it lacks theoretical rigor, the experimental evaluation doesn't support the interpretability claims adequately, and some claims are overstated. I lean toward rejection, though I think with substantial revision — particularly adding a user study and tightening the theoretical analysis — this could become a solid contribution.

This paper proposes GEVRM, a closed-loop VLA framework that borrows the internal model control (IMC) principle from control theory to handle perturbations during robot manipulation. The core idea — using video generation to produce future visual goals and then using prototype contrastive learning to distinguish perturbations via "internal embeddings" — is interesting, and I appreciate the authors trying to ground their work in classical control theory. That said, I have significant concerns.

The motivation from IMC is compelling on the surface, but after reading section 3 twice, I'm honestly not convinced the analogy is rigorous. In classical IMC, the internal model explicitly inverts the plant dynamics. Here, the "internal model" is essentially a video generation module, and the connection feels hand-wavy. The authors should either tighten this theoretical framing considerably or tone down the IMC framing. As written it feels like retrofitting a theoretical justification onto an engineering system.

The experimental results on CALVIN are strong — Table 1 shows clear improvements over GR-2 and SuSIE, which are reasonable baselines. But I'm troubled by the perturbation evaluation setup. What exactly counts as a "perturbed" CALVIN benchmark? The paper describes lighting changes and camera jitter (around page 6-7), but the severity levels feel arbitrarily chosen. I've seen papers in this space inflate results by tuning robustness evaluations to their own method's strengths, and I can't rule that out here.

The video generation component — I think this is actually the most technically interesting part — is underexplained. Figure 3 shows the architecture but I found the description of how the text-conditioned latents feed into the action policy genuinely confusing. Specifically, does the policy condition on raw video frames or on some compressed representation? Equation 4 suggests the latter but the ablation in Table 3 doesn't cleanly isolate this choice.

The prototype contrastive learning for perturbation inference is a reasonable idea but not novel. Methods like BYOL and various contrastive approaches have done similar things. The authors don't engage seriously with this literature, which is a problem.

Real robot experiments (Section 5.3) are appreciated but thin. Five tasks, unclear number of trials per task, no statistical significance reported. In my experience, 20 trials per task is a minimum for these kinds of claims.

Minor things: the writing is occasionally awkward (English is clearly not the authors' first language, which is fine, but some sentences in the intro are hard to parse). The related work section undersells the connection to world-model-based RL approaches like DreamerV3, which seem very relevant.

Overall, I'm leaning reject but I think there's a real paper here if the authors tighten the theory, clarify the architecture, and strengthen the real-robot evaluation. The core idea has merit and the CALVIN numbers are genuinely good. I'd encourage a revision.

This paper attempts to provide a theoretical grounding for why depth matters in neural language models, using the concept of separation rank borrowed from tensor decomposition literature (similar to the work by Cohen et al. on convolutional networks). The motivation is reasonable — we do need more principled understanding of why certain architectural choices work — and I appreciate that the authors are trying to connect theory to practice.

That said, I have significant concerns.

The theoretical contributions feel incremental at best. The separation rank analysis largely follows the framework established in prior tensor network work, and I'm not convinced the adaptation to language modeling introduces enough novelty. The bounds derived in Section 3 are not tight, and the authors acknowledge this somewhat obliquely on page 6, but I think they undersell how loose these bounds actually are. A lower bound that doesn't track actual model behavior closely is of limited practical utility. I found myself asking: what do we *do* with Theorem 2? The answer the paper gives is "build an adaptive depth network," but the connection between the theorem and the architectural choice feels hand-wavy.

The ACT-inspired adaptive recurrent network is the practical contribution here, and honestly it's the more interesting part of the paper to me. But the experimental evaluation is underwhelming. Comparing against bidirectional LSTM on sentence classification is a fairly low bar in 2023 (or whenever this was submitted). Where are the comparisons against transformers, or at minimum against other adaptive computation approaches? Table 2 shows modest improvements that I'm not fully convinced are statistically significant — are those numbers averaged over multiple runs? What's the variance? This is not mentioned anywhere that I could find.

Figure 3 is supposed to illustrate the adaptive depth behavior but I found it confusing. After reading this twice I still wasn't sure what the x-axis represents in the bottom subplot.

I'm also skeptical of some of the claims about depth vs. width in Section 3.2. The argument that depth is categorically more efficient than width for capturing contextual dependencies is presented somewhat dogmatically. There's a reasonable body of work suggesting this relationship is more nuanced and task-dependent — the authors cite some of this but don't really engage with the counterarguments.

Minor issues: the writing needs another pass. There are grammatical errors throughout ("the ability to represent the contextual dependency" is used almost as a fixed phrase, repeated maybe a dozen times). Some notation in Section 3 is introduced without adequate definition.

I think there's a kernel of a good paper here — the theoretical framing is interesting and the adaptive network idea has merit. But as it stands the theory feels disconnected from the experiments, the empirical evaluation is insufficient, and I'm not sure the claimed contributions fully materialize. I lean toward rejection but would be open to a major revision if the authors can tighten the theory-practice connection and do more rigorous experiments.

This paper studies weighted ERM for transfer learning via importance sampling, proving that plugging in estimated importance weights preserves generalization bounds up to lower-order terms. The theoretical setup is clean and the linearization argument is the core technical contribution.

Let me start with what I think works. The paper is fairly well-written and the problem formulation in Section 2 is clear. The observation that in several practically relevant cases Φ takes a tractable parametric form is useful, and I appreciate that the authors ground this in concrete scenarios rather than hand-waving. The numerical experiments do support the theory reasonably well.

That said, I have significant concerns.

The main theoretical result — that estimation error in Φ doesn't kill your generalization bound — feels incremental to me. After reading this twice, I'm honestly not sure this is far enough beyond what's already known in the covariate shift literature. Sugiyama et al. (2007, 2008), Cortes et al. (2010), and the line of work on kernel mean matching all deal with estimated importance weights, and the authors don't do enough to distinguish their contribution from this body of work. The related work section is too brief and somewhat dismissive of prior art. I'd want to see a more careful comparison, ideally with explicit statements of what the previous bounds look like versus Theorem 3 (or whichever is the main result — the numbering felt slightly off to me by page 12).

The linearization technique in Section 4 — I'm not entirely sure the conditions under which it's valid are clearly stated. There's an assumption about the estimator of Φ being "regular" in some sense, but I found the precise conditions buried and hard to parse. This is important because if those conditions are hard to verify in practice, the theorem is less useful than it looks.

On the experiments: Figure 3 is quite hard to read in grayscale, and I'm not confident the baselines are strong enough. In my experience, a well-tuned direct ERM on reweighted data with a simple logistic density ratio estimator often performs surprisingly well, and I don't see that comparison made carefully.

Minor things: the abstract says "one may straightforwardly extends" which is a grammatical error, and there are a handful of similar issues throughout. Not a dealbreaker but suggests the manuscript could use another careful proofread.

My overall take: there's a legitimate contribution here, probably, but it needs to be positioned much more carefully against prior work, and the theoretical conditions need to be stated more transparently. As it stands I think this is borderline. I'm leaning toward rejection but I'd genuinely reconsider if the authors can sharpen the comparison to Cortes et al. and the kernel mean matching literature and clarify the regularity conditions. The topic is important and the paper isn't bad — it just doesn't yet clearly articulate why we need it given what already exists.

This paper tackles a real and somewhat underappreciated problem in multi-ControlNet applications — the interference caused by "silent" control signals (essentially empty/background regions in edge maps and similar conditions) suppressing texture generation in areas that should be freely generated. I've seen this artifact myself when trying to combine, say, a depth map with a Canny edge map, and the paper correctly diagnoses why it happens.

That said, I have pretty significant concerns about the paper as presented.

The three proposed strategies — balanced dataset construction, balanced feature injection, and the Jacobian asymmetry correction — feel somewhat loosely connected to each other. Reading Section 3, I kept wondering whether all three are necessary, or whether one of them is doing most of the heavy lifting. The ablation in Table 2 helps somewhat, but I found the margins surprisingly close in some configurations, and the authors don't really discuss this. Is the Jacobian fix actually contributing meaningfully? I'm not entirely sure the theoretical motivation in Section 3.3 is tight enough to justify it as a standalone contribution — the connection between score function Jacobian asymmetry and the observed visual artifacts felt hand-wavy to me on first read, and honestly still felt that way after reading it twice.

The dataset balancing strategy (Section 3.1) is probably the most practically interesting contribution, but the description is vague. How exactly are "balanced" samples defined and selected? What's the distribution before and after? Figure 3 shows some examples but I wanted actual statistics.

On the experimental side — I appreciate that the authors show qualitative results across several control combinations, and Figure 5 and Figure 6 do demonstrate visible improvements. But the quantitative metrics are weak. FID and a user study with (I think?) 30 participants is not compelling for a paper claiming state-of-the-art multi-control generation. Where are comparisons against more recent methods like Uni-ControlNet or ControlNet++? The baseline comparisons feel cherry-picked.

The writing is okay but could use another pass. Some sentences in the abstract and introduction are awkward in ways that suggest non-native English writing that wasn't fully edited. Minor thing, but at a top venue it matters.

I also want to flag that the claim of "Minimal Impact" is a bit misleading in places — the goal isn't minimal impact broadly, it's *selective* minimal impact in silent regions while preserving control elsewhere. This distinction matters and the paper blurs it occasionally.

My overall take: the problem is real and worth solving, and there are genuine ideas here. But the paper isn't ready. The theoretical justification needs tightening, the experiments need stronger baselines and more rigorous evaluation, and the ablations need deeper analysis. I'd encourage the authors to resubmit after addressing these, particularly the experimental gaps.

Leaning toward rejection in current form.

This paper proposes AlignNet, a self-supervised module for object tracking/alignment that draws inspiration from the "sticky indices" concept from psychology. The core idea is to learn to assign and reassign indices to objects across time using a graph network over a bipartite graph structure. I'll be honest — I found the motivation compelling and the psychological framing refreshing, but the paper has some significant issues that need to be addressed.

The bipartite graph formulation is clean and I appreciated that the authors explicitly handle the case of unmatched objects (appearance/disappearance). That said, I think the technical novelty here is somewhat limited. Graph-based assignment problems have been studied extensively — Hungarian algorithm variants, deep learning approaches to multi-object tracking (e.g., Braso & Leal-Taixe, 2020), and various slot-based methods all occupy similar territory. The paper doesn't do enough to differentiate itself from these. After reading the related work section twice, I'm still not entirely sure what AlignNet does that a well-tuned MOT baseline couldn't. The authors need to be much more explicit about this.

My bigger concern is the experiments. The question-answering evaluation is interesting conceptually but feels somewhat toy-like. The environments described are quite controlled, and I worry the results don't tell us much about real-world viability. Table 2 shows improvements but the baselines feel weak — where's a comparison against state-of-the-art tracking methods applied to the same QA setup? I might be missing something, but the experimental section reads like it was designed to make AlignNet look good rather than to rigorously stress-test it.

The self-supervised training signal is described around equations 3-5 and I found this genuinely confusing on first pass. The contrastive-style objective makes sense in principle but the negative sampling strategy isn't clearly justified. Why those specific negatives? This could matter a lot for performance.

Figure 3 is helpful but Figure 4 is honestly hard to parse — the caption doesn't do enough work. I spent a few minutes staring at it.

One thing I genuinely liked: the persistent memory mechanism is conceptually elegant and the reappearance experiments (Section 4.2 if I recall correctly) are the strongest part of the paper. This is where AlignNet seems to offer something real.

In my experience reviewing perception/memory papers, the gap between a nice idea and a convincing paper often comes down to ablations, and this paper's ablations feel incomplete. What happens if you remove the memory entirely? What's the sensitivity to the graph construction parameters?

Overall, I think this is an interesting paper with a real idea at its core, but it needs stronger baselines, clearer technical differentiation, and more thorough ablation studies. I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, though I'd genuinely like to see a revised version. The direction is worth pursuing.

This paper proposes "Adversarial Masking," a hypothesis that the robustness-accuracy trade-off in adversarial training stems from feature masking effects arising from the interaction between batch normalization rescaling and ReLU activations. The authors build on this to propose RobMask, which learns perturbation-specific feature maskings. Let me share my thoughts.

I'll start with what I think is the core problem: the central hypothesis feels underspecified and, frankly, a bit hand-wavy. The claim that BN rescaling + ReLU acts as a "feature masking layer" isn't really novel — this is essentially redescribing well-known phenomena about how BN and ReLU interact to gate activations. I've read several papers on this going back to at least 2018-2019, and I'm not convinced the framing here adds meaningful new insight. The authors need to much more rigorously distinguish their hypothesis from prior work on robust vs. non-robust features (Ilyas et al. 2019), which they somewhat dismissively set aside without convincing argumentation.

The theoretical grounding is weak. Around equations 3-5, the authors make several leaps that I found difficult to follow even after reading twice. The connection between the masked feature maps and generalization improvement is asserted more than derived. I might be wrong, but I don't think the theoretical claims actually support the strength of the conclusions being drawn.

On the experimental side — Table 2 shows improvements on clean accuracy, which looks nice on paper, but I'm concerned about cherry-picking. The robustness numbers against strong attacks (AutoAttack specifically) seem either missing or buried. For a paper fundamentally about the robustness-accuracy trade-off, you cannot just report clean accuracy improvements and call it a day. Where is the comprehensive evaluation under PGD-100 and AutoAttack across all datasets? This is a significant gap.

The writing also needs work. Several sentences in Section 3 are genuinely hard to parse — the phrase "sufficiently, adversarial training can be treated as an effective regularizer" appears in the abstract and I still don't understand what "sufficiently" is doing there. Small thing but it signals careless editing throughout.

I do think there's a potentially interesting empirical observation buried in here about how different attack types induce different activation patterns, and Figure 3 is actually kind of compelling if you stare at it long enough. But the paper over-promises on the theoretical side and under-delivers on rigorous empirical validation.

My honest assessment is that this needs significant revision. The idea isn't hopeless but the current version reads like it was rushed. The hypothesis needs sharper formalization, the experiments need to be more comprehensive (especially robustness evaluations), and the related work engagement needs to be more serious. I'd lean toward rejection at this stage.

This paper tackles the important problem of label noise robustness by investigating a symmetrization procedure for multi-class loss functions. The core idea — decomposing any loss into a symmetric component plus a class-insensitive term — is elegant and the connection to Dirichlet priors is a nice observation that I hadn't seen spelled out quite this way before.

That said, I have significant reservations about the paper in its current form.

The theoretical contributions feel somewhat incremental to me. The symmetrization approach builds heavily on prior work (Ghosh et al., van Rooyen et al., and related), and I think the authors undersell the extent to which the multi-class unhinged loss was already implicit or explicit in earlier literature. The claimed uniqueness result for the convex multi-class symmetric loss (I'm looking at the theorem around section 4) is interesting, but the proof relies on assumptions that feel somewhat restrictive — I'd want to see a more careful discussion of when these assumptions actually hold in practice. Are they satisfied for typical network outputs? This is never really addressed.

The approximation result about cross-entropy being "approximately symmetric" near the uniform prediction point is, frankly, not that surprising once you think about it. A Taylor expansion argument around a symmetric point will naturally produce symmetric leading terms. I'm not sure this rises to the level of "remarkable" as claimed in the abstract. It's a cute observation but I'd temper the enthusiasm.

My biggest issue is with the experiments. CIFAR with synthetic noise is fine for a proof of concept, but in 2024 this feels thin. Where are comparisons on CIFAR-N or other real-world noisy label benchmarks? The baselines chosen seem somewhat dated, and the performance differences in Table 2 (if I'm reading it correctly) are often within what I'd consider noise given the variance across runs. I found the experimental section genuinely underwhelming relative to the theoretical ambition of the paper.

The writing is generally clear but the title is unwieldy — almost comically long — and the abstract buries the lede. I had to read the paper twice to fully understand what the actual algorithmic contribution is versus what's purely theoretical characterization.

I also found the connection to Dirichlet regularization (Section 3 I believe) somewhat handwavy. The claim that symmetrization "follows from" Dirichlet priors deserves more rigorous treatment or should be softened.

To be clear, I don't think the paper is bad. The theoretical framework is reasonably clean and the unhinged loss extension is a legitimate contribution. But the gap between the ambitious framing and what's actually delivered — both experimentally and in terms of novelty — is too large for acceptance in the current form. I'd encourage the authors to either strengthen the experimental evaluation substantially or reframe the paper more modestly as a theoretical note. Right now it's trying to be both and not fully succeeding at either.

Leaning toward reject, but would reconsider with a major revision addressing the experimental concerns.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely problem — improving chain-of-thought reasoning in vision-language models — but after reading it twice I have some significant concerns about both the novelty and the experimental rigor.

Let me start with what I think works. The two-stage approach (GPT-4o distillation followed by DPO-based preference optimization) is reasonable and the motivation is clear enough. The observation that training on short annotations hurts reasoning generalization is well-taken, though honestly not particularly surprising to anyone who has followed the instruction tuning literature over the past two years.

My main issue is novelty. Knowledge distillation from stronger models to weaker ones for CoT improvement has been done extensively — see works like Magister et al., Ho et al., and frankly much of the recent LLM reasoning literature. The DPO component is similarly well-established. I'm not seeing a clear technical contribution beyond assembling these existing pieces together. The authors need to make a much stronger case for what is genuinely new here. The abstract promises a "two-fold approach" but both folds feel like fairly standard recipes at this point.

On the experimental side — I found the evaluation somewhat thin. Which benchmarks exactly? The abstract is vague and I had to dig through the paper to piece this together. The comparison baselines also feel incomplete. Where are comparisons against other distillation approaches or RLHF variants? Table 2 (if I'm remembering the numbering correctly) shows improvements but without enough ablation I can't tell whether the gains are coming from the DPO stage, the distillation stage, or just the additional data volume. This is a critical confound that the paper doesn't adequately address.

I'm also skeptical about using short answer correctness as the signal for constructing positive/negative reasoning pairs. A model can arrive at the correct answer through flawed reasoning, and vice versa. This is a known problem in the process vs. outcome reward literature and the authors don't really engage with it seriously.

The writing is generally clear but there are several places where the argument feels hand-wavy. The claim about "interpretability and trustworthiness" in the abstract is invoked but never really cashed out empirically. How are you measuring trustworthiness? I think the paper would be stronger if it dropped these broader claims and stayed focused on the concrete reasoning quality improvements.

Overall, I think this is a reasonable engineering paper with some solid empirical work, but it reads more like a technical report than a research contribution with a clear scientific thesis. The ML conference bar requires more — either a stronger theoretical insight or a much more thorough empirical investigation. In my experience papers like this tend to work better at workshops or as components of larger system papers. I'd encourage the authors to sharpen the claimed contribution significantly before resubmission.

Leaning toward rejection, but I'd be willing to revisit if the authors can address the novelty and ablation concerns directly.

This paper tackles an interesting and important question: what is the implicit bias of adversarial training? The theoretical framework draws on the line of work on implicit bias of gradient descent (Soudry et al., Ji & Telgarsky, etc.) and extends it to the adversarial training setting. I think this is a worthwhile direction and the results, if correct, are meaningful.

That said, I have significant concerns about both the technical contributions and the clarity of the presentation.

On the positive side, Theorem 1 (deep linear networks, linearly separable case) feels like the most solid result in the paper. The proof sketch is at least followable and the connection to max-margin is clean. I've read similar arguments for standard gradient descent and the extension to adversarial perturbations here is non-trivial enough to be interesting.

However — and this is my main concern — the generalization to nonlinear homogeneous networks in Section 4 feels much weaker than advertised. The conclusion is convergence to a KKT point of some margin-maximization problem, but KKT points can be arbitrarily bad saddle points or local minima. This is a well-known issue with KKT-based characterizations and the authors don't really grapple with it. Saying "converges to a KKT point" without any discussion of which KKT point, or under what conditions it might be a global optimum, feels like the result is somewhat hollow. I might be wrong but I don't think this actually justifies the "longstanding conjecture" claim in the abstract as strongly as the authors suggest.

The assumptions also need more scrutiny. The homogeneity assumption is stated but its practical implications for modern architectures (BatchNorm, residual connections) are barely discussed. After reading this twice I still wasn't sure which common architectures this actually applies to cleanly.

Figure 2 is helpful but the experimental validation feels thin — a few small-scale experiments don't really stress-test the theory. I'd want to see whether the KKT characterization is tight in practice or whether it's just technically true but vacuous.

Minor things: the notation in equations (7)-(9) is dense and I found it hard to follow without a cleaner setup earlier in the paper. Page 6 in particular felt rushed.

I also think the related work undersells some recent papers on implicit bias in non-convex settings that seem directly relevant. The authors should engage more carefully with that literature.

Overall: the linear network result is solid, the nonlinear generalization is technically correct but arguably weak in what it actually tells us, and the writing needs tightening. I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, but I think with a more honest framing of the limitations and a stronger discussion of what the KKT result does and doesn't imply, this could be a reasonable contribution. The core idea deserves to be published somewhere — I'm just not convinced the current version is ready.

This paper tackles an interesting and genuinely underexplored problem — learned simulation of rigid body dynamics with complex geometry. I'll say upfront that I found the core idea compelling: computing interactions at the face level rather than node level is a natural and well-motivated solution to the obvious failure mode of node-based GNNs when contacts occur mid-face. That much makes intuitive sense, and I appreciated that the motivation was laid out clearly in the introduction.

That said, I have a number of concerns after reading through this carefully.

The computational efficiency claims (8x more efficient than particle-based methods, ~4x more accurate) are headline numbers that I found hard to fully evaluate. Table 2 presents these comparisons but the baselines — particularly the particle-based one — feel like they may be somewhat strawmanned. Is the particle density for the dense baseline actually optimized, or just set to some reasonable default? I'd want to see a proper ablation on particle count vs. accuracy to understand the tradeoff curve before accepting the efficiency claim at face value.

The real-world data experiments are the most exciting part of the paper to me, and honestly I wish there was more of it. The result that FIGNet can outperform analytical solvers given modest training data is a strong claim, and Figure 6 provides some evidence, but I'm not entirely convinced the comparison is fair — which analytical solver is used, and what are its parameter settings? Real rigid body simulators have lots of tunable parameters and "analytical solver" is not a single thing. I might be wrong, but this feels underspecified.

I also found the description of the face-face interaction computation (Section 3.2, around equations 4-6) somewhat dense and had to read it twice. The notation gets a bit heavy and I think the paper would benefit from a clearer diagram walking through a single interaction step — Figure 2 helps but doesn't quite get there for me.

One thing I genuinely liked: the discussion of how FIGNet handles non-convex shapes and the examples shown in Figure 4 are convincing. This is where node-based methods really struggle and the qualitative results look good.

My bigger concern is generalization. The experiments are somewhat limited in shape diversity — I'd want to know how FIGNet handles highly non-convex objects (think L-shaped or toroidal geometry) or scenes with many simultaneous contacts. The current evaluation feels like it covers relatively clean collision scenarios.

The writing is generally good and the paper is well-organized, though the related work section is a bit thin on the robotics simulation learning literature, which feels like an oversight given the stated relevance to robotics.

Overall I think this is a solid contribution with a clean core idea. The evidence is reasonably convincing but I think some claims — especially around the real-world solver comparison — need more careful treatment. I lean toward acceptance with revisions, but the authors need to tighten those experimental comparisons.

This paper tackles the problem of offline policy evaluation with partial coverage, proposing an interval-based approach that characterizes and achieves the "minimax bias." The core theoretical contribution — showing that MIS achieves minimax bias with appropriate importance weights — is interesting, but I have significant concerns about the paper's execution and positioning.

Let me start with what I think works. The formalization of "minimax bias" as the irreducible component of estimation error under insufficient coverage is conceptually clean, and I appreciate that the authors don't sweep the coverage problem under the rug the way a lot of OPE papers do. The connection between the importance weight function and the tightness of the resulting interval is genuinely nontrivial.

That said, I found the paper frustrating to read. After going through it twice, I'm still not entirely clear on the relationship between the assumptions made in Theorem 2 and those in Theorem 4. The realizability condition is introduced somewhat informally and then I'm not sure it's actually dropped cleanly — the title promises "without sufficient exploration OR realizability" but the treatment of the realizability relaxation felt rushed and underspecified to me. Can the authors clarify exactly what regime each theorem applies to?

The experimental section is, frankly, thin. The environments tested feel like toy settings, and I'd want to see evaluation on something closer to D4RL benchmarks or similar standard OPE testbeds. In my experience, interval-based OPE methods can look great on small MDPs and then the intervals explode or become vacuous on even moderately complex problems. I don't see convincing evidence this method avoids that failure mode.

I'm also not convinced the comparison to existing partial coverage OPE work (e.g., work building on Rashidinejad et al., or the more recent pessimism-based approaches) is thorough enough. The related work section gestures at these connections but doesn't really engage with them technically. How does the minimax bias characterization here compare quantitatively to the suboptimality bounds in those pessimism-based frameworks?

Minor things: Figure 3 is too small to read, several equations in Section 4 use notation that isn't defined until Section 5 (or I missed the definition — either way, bad sign), and the abstract says "given decision-making policy" which is slightly awkward phrasing.

My overall take: there's a potentially solid contribution here around the minimax bias characterization, but the paper needs substantially more work on (1) clarity of assumptions, (2) honest comparison to related theoretical frameworks, and (3) empirical validation on realistic settings. I'm leaning toward rejection in its current form, though I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit. The core idea isn't bad — it's just not ready.

This paper tackles an interesting statistical problem — MLE under the multivariate hypergeometric distribution when both N (total population) and the category counts are unknown. The problem is well-motivated and the connection to single-cell genomics is timely. That said, I have significant concerns about the paper that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

Let me start with what I think works. The core insight that you can maximize the hypergeometric likelihood to recover unknown population sizes even under severe undersampling is clean and the theoretical motivation makes sense to me. The VAE extension is natural, and I appreciate that the authors try to validate on simulated data before moving to the genomics application.

But honestly, after reading this twice, I found the theoretical guarantees quite thin. The identifiability question is never really answered rigorously. I might be wrong, but I don't see a proof that the MLE is consistent here, and given the severity of undersampling the authors themselves acknowledge, this seems like a non-trivial concern. In my experience with similar latent variable models, identifiability issues have a way of hiding in the empirical results — things look fine on simulated data but for the wrong reasons.

The simulated validation (Section 4, if I'm reading the structure right) feels somewhat circular. The authors generate data from the hypergeometric model and then recover it with a hypergeometric-based estimator. Of course it works. I'd want to see robustness checks where the data generating process is misspecified — what happens when cells have more complex capture processes? The single-cell field has moved toward quite sophisticated noise models (see e.g. work on ambient RNA contamination, doublets, etc.) and I don't see the proposed method benchmarked seriously against existing approaches like scVI or even simpler negative binomial-based methods.

The writing in the methods section is dense and I found equation (I'm guessing around eq. 3-5, the ELBO derivation) hard to follow without more annotation. Some of the notation is introduced informally and I had to re-read several paragraphs.

The genomics results are intriguing but I'm skeptical. How sensitive are the recovered transcript counts to the choice of prior? This is never really addressed. The comparison to ground truth — what IS the ground truth here? This needs to be explained much more carefully because "true number of transcripts" is itself not directly observable in most protocols.

I'm also a bit puzzled by the novelty framing. The authors claim the hypergeometric MLE problem is unaddressed in the literature, but there's related work in capture-recapture and ecological abundance estimation that seems relevant and isn't cited.

I lean toward rejection in the current form, but I think there's a real paper here if the authors tighten the theory, do honest benchmarking, and address the identifiability question head-on.

This paper tackles an interesting and genuinely important question — why do diffusion models generalize at all given that the theoretical optimum of DSM is the empirical score function? The "generalization through variance" framing is clever and I think conceptually correct at a high level. The path integral approach is technically sophisticated and I appreciated the connection to physics-inspired methods.

That said, I have real concerns about the gap between the theoretical setup and practical diffusion models as deployed. The analysis focuses on fairly paradigmatic/toy settings — underparameterized linear models and specific overparameterized cases — and I found myself repeatedly asking how much of this carries over to, say, a U-Net trained on CIFAR-10 or ImageNet. The authors gesture at this but never really close the gap. After reading the theoretical sections twice I'm still not convinced the covariance structure argument (Section 4, around equation 12 if I recall correctly) generalizes cleanly beyond the Gaussian or near-Gaussian regimes they study. I might be wrong here, but this feels like it needs more explicit justification.

The "gaps filled in" intuition is nice and Figure 2 does illustrate it compellingly for the toy cases. But I think the paper somewhat oversells this result. The phenomenon they describe — that noise during training acts as a regularizer that smooths the learned distribution — is related to, and in my view not sufficiently distinguished from, prior work on score function regularization and the role of noise in energy-based models. The related work section acknowledges some of this but I felt it was a bit too breezy in differentiating the contribution.

The interaction between variance-induced bias and feature-related inductive biases (Section 5 or so) is actually the most interesting part to me, and somewhat underdeveloped. This feels like it could be its own paper. The current treatment felt rushed.

A few smaller things: the writing is generally good but there are passages in the intro that are vague to the point of being unhelpful. Phrases like "resemble their training distributions but with gaps filled in" need crisper formalization earlier. Also, I'd want to see more empirical validation even if the paper is primarily theoretical — the experiments feel thin and somewhat cherry-picked to confirm the theory rather than probe its limits.

To be blunt: this is a solid theory paper with a genuine insight, but it currently reads like the authors found a beautiful mathematical framework and then worked backwards to figure out what claim it proves. The result is that the empirical scope feels narrow and the practical implications are underspecified. I'm leaning toward acceptance given the venue values theoretical contributions, but the authors need to be much more honest about the limitations and assumptions baked into their analysis. Revisions addressing the scope of the theory and the relationship to prior smoothing/regularization literature are necessary.

This paper tackles GAN compression, which is a reasonably interesting problem that hasn't received as much attention as classification/detection compression. The core observation — that naive weight pruning breaks GANs — is intuitive and I think most practitioners would nod along, but the paper deserves credit for actually demonstrating this empirically rather than just asserting it.

The proposed approach of using the discriminator as a supervisory signal for the compressed generator is clever. It's one of those ideas that feels obvious in retrospect, which is usually a good sign. My concern is that the paper doesn't fully interrogate *why* this works as well as it does. Is it because the discriminator provides a richer perceptual loss? Or is it fundamentally about keeping the generator-discriminator relationship intact during compression? I kept waiting for a deeper analysis and it never quite arrived. The ablations in Section 4 help somewhat but feel a bit thin.

On the experiments: the results look decent, but I'm not entirely convinced by the evaluation setup. FID scores are reported but image quality metrics for GANs are notoriously finicky, and I'd have liked to see more discussion of variance across runs. I also found the comparison baselines somewhat underwhelming — after reading this twice I'm still not sure the authors are comparing against the strongest possible alternatives. Knowledge distillation approaches for GANs exist (GAN-KD and related work), and their absence feels like a gap.

The generalization claims made in the abstract ("generalizes well to new tasks and models") are, in my opinion, overstated relative to what the experiments actually show. Two or three architectures is not strong evidence of broad generalization. I've seen this pattern in compression papers before and it's a bit frustrating.

Figure 3 is useful but the caption is doing too much work — I had to cross-reference the main text multiple times to understand what I was looking at. Minor thing but it slows down reading considerably.

I'm not entirely sure about the theoretical grounding here. The paper is largely empirical, which is fine, but some of the claims in Section 3 about why discriminator supervision prevents mode collapse during pruning feel hand-wavy. A more careful treatment would strengthen the paper significantly.

The writing is generally clear, though there are a few places in Section 2 where the related work discussion feels rushed, particularly around structured vs. unstructured pruning. The distinction matters a lot for understanding the contribution.

Overall: this is a solid if not spectacular contribution. The core idea is sound and the experiments support the basic claims, even if I'd push back on some of the stronger assertions. I think it's borderline for acceptance. The paper would benefit substantially from stronger baselines, more rigorous ablations, and toning down some of the generalization claims. I'm leaning weak accept but could be convinced either way.

This paper addresses an interesting and practically relevant problem — how to distill knowledge from black-box model APIs without access to training data and while minimizing the number of queries. The core idea in IDEAL is to decouple data generation from distillation such that the generator produces synthetic samples without any teacher queries, and then each synthetic sample gets queried exactly once during distillation. The 0.02x query budget claim compared to DFME is eye-catching and I understand why the authors lead with it.

That said, I have some significant concerns after reading this paper twice.

First, the data generation stage. The paper claims to generate useful training data without any teacher queries, which sounds almost too good. I kept looking for a clear explanation of what signal guides the generator in this stage — if not the teacher, then what? The explanation around Section 3.2 is hand-wavy in my opinion. Using a pre-trained generative model or auxiliary data distribution as a prior is fine, but the paper needs to be more upfront that this essentially *displaces* rather than eliminates the information requirement. This isn't a fatal flaw but it feels like the framing is slightly misleading.

Second, the comparison baselines. DFME is the main comparison throughout, and I think the authors cherry-pick CIFAR10 where the improvement looks dramatic. Table 2 tells a more complicated story — on some harder datasets the gains shrink considerably, and I'd want to understand why. Also, I'm not entirely sure why more recent black-box KD methods (there were several published in 2022-2023 that I'm aware of) aren't included. The related work section mentions them but the experimental comparison doesn't.

The query efficiency argument is compelling on its face, but I think the paper conflates query count with actual cost. One query with a complex synthetic image that requires expensive generation might not be cheaper than multiple queries with cheap random noise. There's no analysis of the computational cost of the generation stage itself, which feels like an important omission.

Figure 3 is helpful for intuition but I found the ablation in Section 4.4 somewhat superficial. Removing components one at a time is standard, but the interactions between the generation and distillation stages seem worth investigating more carefully.

Writing is generally clear, though there are grammatical issues scattered throughout — "query-effIcient" in the title is an awkward forced acronym and the capitalization in IDEAL is honestly a bit cringe-worthy.

I think there's a genuine contribution here, and the idea of single-query-per-sample distillation is clean and worth publishing somewhere. But the current submission oversells the novelty and undersells the assumptions. I'd want to see more honest framing of what the generator actually relies on, stronger baselines, and cost analysis before accepting this. Leaning toward rejection but open to being convinced otherwise by the authors' response.

This paper studies a general class of Markov chains (called "Itô chains") that can be viewed as Euler-Maruyama discretizations of SDEs, and derives W2 bounds between the law of the chain and the corresponding SDE solution. The unification angle is the main selling point — covering SGLD, SGD, and stochastic gradient boosting under one roof.

Let me start with what I think is genuinely valuable here. The generality is real.
