Is the mutual information objective replacing the task reward entirely, or is it combined with it? Figure 2 is supposed to clarify this but I found it more confusing than helpful. The caption does almost no work.

The experimental evaluation is where I have the most serious concerns. The environments tested feel fairly limited — I'd want to see this compared against POET with a proper reward function as a strong baseline, and against other unsupervised RL methods like DIAYN or APT running without the open-ended curriculum. Without these comparisons I genuinely cannot tell whether the gains (to the extent they exist) come from the open-ended curriculum, the unsupervised objective, or their combination. This is a fundamental ablation that's missing.

The quantitative results in Table 1 are hard to interpret. What does "identifiable skills" mean precisely? This feels like a somewhat hand-wavy evaluation criterion. I might be wrong, but in my experience reviewers at top venues will push hard on this. The downstream task evaluation is a step in the right direction but the tasks chosen seem cherry-picked, and the variance across seeds is not reported as far as I can tell.

I think there's a real paper here. The combination of POET-style curriculum generation with intrinsic motivation is underexplored and worth investigating. But right now the contribution feels more like a preliminary workshop paper than a full conference submission. The theoretical justification for why the POET selection/mutation mechanism is compatible with a non-stationary unsupervised reward is essentially absent — this seems like a non-trivial issue that deserves serious treatment.

Minor things: the related work section misses several relevant papers on environment-conditioned skill discovery (e.g., work on LEXA, LSD, and some of the contrastive RL literature). The hyperparameter choices are not well justified anywhere.

In summary: interesting direction, weak execution. I'd encourage the authors to substantially strengthen the baselines, clarify the algorithm description with pseudocode, and tighten the evaluation criteria before resubmission. Not ready in current form.

This paper proposes InterpGN, a framework combining shapelet-based interpretable models with deep neural networks via a gating mechanism. The core idea is that when the interpretable expert is confident, it handles the prediction; otherwise, a deep model takes over. I've seen similar mixture-of-experts ideas before, but the specific framing around confidence-based gating for interpretability preservation is a reasonably interesting angle.

My biggest concern is the novelty and clarity of the gating mechanism itself. The confidence-based gate is described around equations 3-5, but I found myself rereading this section multiple times and still uncertain about exactly how "confidence" is operationalized during training versus inference. Is this a soft gate throughout training? How does the gradient flow through here? The paper needs a much cleaner technical exposition of this central component. Figure 2 helps somewhat but not enough.

The experimental results in Table 2 show "comparable" performance to SOTA deep models, but I think the authors are being a bit generous to themselves here. On several UCR datasets the gap is non-trivial, and "comparable" is doing a lot of work. I'd rather see the authors be honest about where the interpretability tradeoff costs accuracy and frame it as such — that's a legitimate and interesting result in itself, not a weakness to hide.

I actually found the shapelet quality metrics (Section 5.3) the most compelling part of the paper. The comparison against existing shapelet learning methods on interpretability metrics is genuinely useful and I think underemphasized relative to the pure classification benchmarks.

The MIMIC-III experiments are appreciated — real-world validation matters — but the setup is described too briefly. What's the prediction task exactly? What's the train/test split? I had to dig through the appendix and still wasn't entirely sure.

A few smaller things that bothered me: the related work section doesn't engage seriously with post-hoc explanation methods. I understand the authors are proposing an intrinsically interpretable model, but reviewers and readers will ask "why not just use SHAP/LIME on a good deep model?" — this needs a more direct response. Also, in my experience, shapelet-based methods are notoriously sensitive to hyperparameters (shapelet length, number of shapelets), and I didn't see adequate ablations on this.

The writing is generally clear but occasionally vague. "Significant" appears frequently without quantification.

Overall, I think this is decent work with a reasonable contribution, but it needs more rigorous experimental analysis and cleaner technical writing before I'd feel comfortable recommending acceptance. The idea has merit. The execution needs tightening. I'm currently leaning toward weak reject, but I'd be willing to revise upward if the authors can address the gating mechanism clarity and the honest treatment of the accuracy-interpretability tradeoff.

This paper presents Gaga, a system for segmenting 3D Gaussian scenes using a "3D-aware memory bank" to handle inconsistent 2D mask predictions across views. The core idea is reasonable and the problem is well-motivated — inconsistent mask IDs from zero-shot segmenters like SAM across different viewpoints is genuinely a pain point in this area, and I've seen multiple papers struggle with it.

That said, I have real concerns here.

The technical contribution feels incremental to me. After reading this twice, the memory bank mechanism described in Section 3 essentially projects 3D Gaussian centers into 2D, does IoU-based matching, and maintains running ID assignments. I might be wrong about some details, but this is... not far from what several tracking-based methods already do, just with 3D geometry as the glue instead of temporal continuity. The authors claim this is fundamentally different from video tracking approaches, but the distinction feels overstated in places. The 3D-awareness comes primarily from using Gaussian positions for projection — I'd like to see a stronger argument for why this is architecturally novel rather than an engineering choice.

The experiments compare against a reasonable set of baselines (Table 1, Table 2), and the numbers do look favorable. But I'm skeptical of the evaluation setup. The scenes used in quantitative evaluation — are these truly challenging for pose variation? In my experience with NeRF/Gaussian splatting benchmarks, many standard datasets have relatively smooth camera trajectories anyway, which would partially undermine the claimed advantage for "sparsely sampled images." The sparse-view experiment in Section 4.3 is appreciated but feels a bit cherry-picked with only a handful of scenes shown.

Figure 4 is actually pretty compelling visually, I'll give them that. The qualitative results on the in-the-wild scenes look good.

I found the ablation study (Table 3) somewhat thin. They ablate the memory bank components but I don't see a clear ablation of the 3D projection mechanism itself versus a simpler 2D overlap baseline. That comparison would be the most informative thing to include, honestly.

The writing is generally clear but the abstract oversells robustness a bit. Phrases like "significantly enhancing its versatility" appear multiple times and read like boilerplate. Minor thing but it affects the impression of rigor.

One thing I genuinely can't tell from the paper: what's the runtime compared to baselines? Maintaining and querying a memory bank over thousands of Gaussians has to have some overhead, and I don't see wall-clock comparisons anywhere obvious.

Overall this is a competent paper with decent results, but I'm not convinced the contribution clears the bar for novelty at a top venue. It reads more like a well-executed system paper than something that advances the conceptual frontier. I'd lean toward weak reject, but I'm open to being persuaded otherwise if the authors can better articulate what's truly new here versus careful engineering.

This paper tackles an important and timely problem — gender bias in neural language generation — and proposes an architectural solution that decouples representation learning from memory management. I appreciate the motivation, and the application to news article generation is well-chosen given how consequential that domain is. That said, I have significant reservations about the work as presented.

My biggest concern is the novelty and clarity of the proposed architecture. After reading the relevant sections twice, I still don't have a crisp understanding of what the "memory module" actually is and how the "equal ratio across gender types" update mechanism works in practice. The description in the architecture section feels hand-wavy — I think the authors need to be much more precise here, ideally with a clearer diagram and formal description of the update rule. As it stands, I can't tell if this is genuinely novel or a fairly straightforward modification of existing memory-augmented networks.

The evaluation also worries me. Perplexity is used as the main quality metric, but in my experience perplexity alone is a pretty weak signal for generation quality, especially when you're explicitly modifying what the model memorizes. The authors claim "similar perplexity values" as a positive result, but the gap reported in Table 2 (if I'm reading it correctly) is non-trivial — a couple PPL points can matter quite a bit in practice. I'd want to see human evaluation of generation quality, or at minimum BLEU/BERTScore comparisons.

On the bias measurement side — I'm not entirely sure the metrics being used are capturing what the authors think they're capturing. The gender bias amplification metric borrowed from image captioning literature doesn't straightforwardly transfer to open-ended text generation, and I think this needs more justification. What counts as a "biased" generation here? The paper never quite pins this down rigorously.

The related work section is okay but misses some important recent work on debiasing at the data level (e.g., counterfactual data augmentation approaches) and at the decoding level. Without situating this against those baselines, it's hard to know if the architectural approach is actually necessary.

I also found the writing somewhat rough throughout — there are grammatical issues and some sentences in the abstract/introduction that are genuinely confusing on first read ("its unintended memorization" is an odd phrase).

Look, I think the core idea might have merit, and the problem is definitely worth solving. But the execution needs substantial work. The architecture description needs to be made rigorous, the evaluation needs to be strengthened considerably, and the bias metrics need better justification. In its current state I can't recommend acceptance. I'd encourage the authors to revise significantly and resubmit — this could become a solid paper with another round of work.

This paper tackles OOD detection using auxiliary outliers and proposes ProMix, which applies mixup to increase outlier diversity during training. The core motivation — that diversity in auxiliary outliers leads to better generalization — is reasonable and the theoretical framing is a nice touch. That said, I have some significant concerns that need to be addressed before I'd feel comfortable recommending acceptance.

Starting with the positives: the generalization bound analysis (Section 3, around Theorem 1) is genuinely interesting, and I appreciated the attempt to formally connect outlier diversity to detection performance. This is more rigorous than most papers in this space. The improvements on CIFAR-100 (31.5% FPR95 relative improvement) are striking if they hold up.

But here's where I start getting skeptical. The mixup idea itself is... not novel? Mixup has been around since Zhang et al. 2018 and has been applied in semi-supervised and OOD contexts before. The paper needs to do a much better job distinguishing ProMix from simply "we applied mixup to outliers." What is the actual technical contribution beyond this? I read Section 4 twice and I'm still not entirely sure. The "provable" in the name suggests the theoretical guarantees are the key contribution, but after reading the theorem and its proof sketch, I'm not convinced the bounds are tight enough to be meaningful in practice — the gap between the theoretical setup and the actual experimental conditions seems large.

The experiments worry me. Table 2 shows impressive numbers but I notice the comparison set doesn't include some recent strong baselines from 2023. Specifically, methods like NPOS or recent energy-based approaches with outlier synthesis seem conspicuously absent. The authors cherry-pick favorable comparisons, which undermines confidence in the claimed improvements. Also, the ablation in Table 3 is too sparse — I'd want to see sensitivity to the mixup coefficient α more carefully analyzed. What happens at the extremes?

The claim in the abstract that "our method achieves excellent performance on different metrics in almost all datasets" is embarrassingly vague and reads like it was written to hedge against failures. Pick a lane.

One more thing: the computational overhead analysis is basically nonexistent. Mixup is cheap, sure, but training with diverse synthetic outliers has costs that should be reported honestly.

I'm also mildly confused by Figure 3 — the visualization is supposed to show the diversity improvement but the axes aren't clearly labeled and I couldn't connect it back to the theoretical claims directly.

Overall, this is a decent paper with a reasonable idea, but the novelty bar feels low and the experimental rigor needs work. I'd lean toward weak reject but I'm genuinely open to being convinced otherwise if the authors can address the baseline comparison issue and clarify the technical differentiation from standard mixup augmentation.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — automating concept bank generation for concept bottleneck models using LLMs. I've been following the CBM literature since Koh et al.'s original work, and the pain point of manual concept annotation is real, so I appreciate the motivation here.

That said, I have significant concerns about the technical depth and novelty of the contribution.

The core idea — prompt an LLM, evaluate with CLIP-style similarity, compare to ConceptNet — is fairly straightforward, maybe even obvious in retrospect. I kept waiting for the methodological surprise that never came. The proposed "multimodal evaluation metric" (Section 3, if I'm reading the structure correctly) essentially amounts to using CLIP embeddings to measure concept-image alignment, which feels like an incremental engineering contribution rather than a genuine research advance. I might be wrong about how novel this framing is, but I've seen similar ideas in concurrent work on automated concept discovery (e.g., LaBo, which the authors do cite but I feel they undersell the comparison to).

The experiments are limited to CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. These are toy benchmarks. The entire motivation in the abstract mentions healthcare and finance as target domains, but there's no evaluation there whatsoever. This gap is glaring. If your pitch is interpretability for sensitive domains, you need at least one domain-appropriate dataset. CUB-200 would have been a natural fit given prior CBM work.

Table 2 shows the quantitative comparisons and the numbers do favor the LLM-generated concepts, but I found myself questioning what these scores actually mean for downstream task performance. Do better concepts (by this metric) actually lead to better CBM accuracy or human interpretability ratings? There's no end-to-end evaluation. The metric is validated... against itself, essentially.

I also found the writing somewhat loose in places. The abstract uses "epicenter" in a way that's slightly off, and the framing of the three research questions feels artificially constructed to make the contribution seem broader than it is. After reading this twice, I'm still not entirely sure what the precise claim is — is it the metric, the generation pipeline, or both?

One thing I did appreciate: the analysis comparing unimodal vs. multimodal evaluation (Section 4.3 or thereabouts) is genuinely useful and the intuition that text similarity alone undersells visual concept alignment makes sense. That part felt like a real insight.

Overall, I think this is a borderline-to-weak submission. The problem is worth solving, the direction is sensible, but the execution is thin. No downstream CBM evaluation, narrow benchmarks, and a metric that lacks independent validation are together too much to overlook. I'd encourage the authors to substantially expand the empirical scope and demonstrate that their metric actually correlates with something practitioners care about before resubmitting.

This paper tackles an interesting and somewhat underexplored problem — representing molecular chirality in a way that's actually useful for downstream tasks. I'll say upfront that I found this more compelling than I expected going in.

The core contribution is an SE(3)-invariant model (ChIRo) that works with torsion angles rather than Cartesian coordinates, and crucially incorporates invariance to bond rotations explicitly. This is clever. Most 3D GNNs I've seen either ignore chirality entirely or handle it sloppily through atom-level tags. The authors are right to call this out, and the theoretical motivation in Section 3 is generally sound, though I found myself re-reading the invariance arguments around Eq. 4-6 a couple of times before feeling satisfied. Could be clearer.

The four benchmarks are reasonable, though I have some concerns. The contrastive learning task feels somewhat constructed — I'm not sure how directly this maps to anything practically important. The R/S classification task is cleaner as a proof of concept, but let's be honest, this is a fairly easy task once you have the right representation. The docking score ranking task (Section 5.4) is actually the most interesting to me and I wish there were more analysis here. The performance gaps over baselines look meaningful but Table 4 needs more context about variance across protein targets.

My bigger concern is the conformer generation setup. The paper claims invariance to bond rotations mitigates the need for multi-conformer augmentation, which is a strong claim. But in practice, I'm not convinced the ablations in Section 5.5 fully support this. The comparison uses a fixed number of conformers — what happens with more? Does ChIRo's advantage shrink? This feels like a gap.

I'm also a bit skeptical about the comparison to E(3)-invariant baselines like DimeNet++ and SchNet. These models aren't really designed for chirality-sensitive tasks, so beating them isn't a huge surprise. A fairer comparison might include 2D models with explicit CIP stereo tags, which are used in practice. The authors do include some 2D baselines but I think this deserves more attention.

The writing is generally good. Figure 2 is helpful. I found the related work section a bit thin on recent conformer-based methods — there's been relevant work on torsional diffusion and related geometric representations that isn't discussed.

Overall I think this is a solid, well-motivated paper with genuine novelty in how it handles the invariance structure. The empirical results are mostly convincing. My main reservations are about the depth of ablations and whether the baselines are truly the right ones to compare against. I'd want to see the authors address the conformer count ablation more carefully and strengthen the practical motivation for the contrastive task. Leaning toward acceptance but these are real issues, not just nitpicks.

This paper proposes using Mamba-based architectures as retrievers for long-document QA, training on synthetically generated data via a "link-based method." The core idea is appealing — using SSMs to avoid the quadratic cost of attention while still capturing global context that chunked RAG misses. I've been following the SSM/Mamba literature closely and think this is a reasonable application space.

That said, I have significant concerns about the evaluation setup and some of the claims made.

The claim that the models "outperform state-of-the-art embedding models across 41 long-document Q&A benchmarks" is stated pretty boldly in the abstract, but after reading the results tables carefully, I noticed that the margins on many benchmarks are quite small, and it's not always clear the comparisons are apples-to-apples. What embedding models exactly? BM25? BGE? E5-mistral? The baselines need to be much more carefully specified. I found Table 2 particularly hard to parse — the column headers are ambiguous and I had to read the caption twice to understand what "chunk-based" vs "full-context" means in this context.

The comparison to GPT-4o is honestly a bit much. Saying performance is "comparable to GPT-4o on long documents over 256k tokens" — comparable how? On what subset of tasks? GPT-4o with 256k context is doing full reading comprehension; your model is doing retrieval and then handing off to something else downstream. These are different tasks and presenting them as equivalent feels misleading, or at minimum needs much more careful framing.

The link-based synthetic data generation is actually the most interesting contribution here, and I think it's undersold relative to the architectural choice. The ablation in Section 4.3 is appreciated but feels thin. I'd want to see more analysis of *why* link-based training helps — is it because it creates harder negatives? Because it exposes the model to longer-range dependencies during training? The current explanation is hand-wavy.

I'm also not entirely sure the 130M vs 1.3B comparison tells us what the authors think it tells us. The scaling behavior shown in Figure 3 (I think?) doesn't obviously demonstrate that this is a Mamba-specific advantage vs just a parameter count advantage.

One practical concern: inference speed benchmarks are given but memory benchmarks are not shown comprehensively. For practitioners in resource-constrained settings (which the paper explicitly targets), peak GPU memory during inference over 256k token documents is crucial information.

The release of code and checkpoints is genuinely good and I appreciate that.

Overall, I think there's a real paper here but it needs tighter claims, better baselines, and more honest comparison framing. The writing is generally clear but some sections feel rushed. I'd lean toward reject at this stage but would reconsider with significant revisions to the evaluation and claims.

This paper tackles an interesting extension of the "hidden subnetworks" line of work (Ramanujan et al., 2020), introducing what the authors call "disguised subnetworks" — essentially subnetworks that require a learned weight transformation (the "unmasking" step) in addition to mask selection. The core idea is intuitive enough, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to push beyond the somewhat disappointing performance ceiling of pure mask-based approaches like edge-popup.

That said, I have some real concerns here. The theoretical justification for why disguised subnetworks should exist and be findable feels thin. The paper spends a lot of space on the algorithmic description but the theoretical grounding — particularly around Proposition 1 and the surrounding discussion on pages 4-5 — feels more like hand-waving than rigorous argument. I've read this section twice and I'm still not fully convinced the "unmasking" transformation is as principled as the authors claim. It sometimes reads like they've just added a learned scaling/shifting operation and are wrapping it in elaborate new terminology.

The experimental results (Tables 2-4) do show improvements over edge-popup, which is good, and I found the ImageNet experiments somewhat convincing. But the baselines feel cherry-picked. Where's a comparison to simply fine-tuning the mask with a lightweight training budget? The "efficient alternatives" to edge-popup (random masks, magnitude-based selection) that they claim work well due to unmasking — this is actually the most interesting claim in the paper, and I think it deserves much more rigorous ablation. Figure 3 touches on this but doesn't go far enough.

I'm also a bit bothered by the framing. The authors position this as fundamentally different from conventional sparse training, but in my experience, once you allow learned weight transformations on the surviving weights, you're getting closer to standard sparse training with extra steps. The distinction feels somewhat artificial. What exactly is being preserved from the "no training" spirit of the original lottery ticket / hidden subnetwork literature?

The writing is generally okay but gets sloppy in places — particularly Section 3.2 which I found confusing on first read. The "Peek-a-Boo" branding is cute but I'll admit it made me slightly skeptical about whether the novelty is being oversold.

Minor things: Figure 2 is hard to parse, the caption needs work. Some notation in Section 3 is introduced inconsistently.

Overall, I think there's a real idea here worth publishing, but the paper needs to either strengthen its theoretical story considerably or be more honest about what the method actually is. As it stands it feels like it's trying to have it both ways — claiming the principled elegance of the hidden subnetwork framework while quietly doing something much closer to partial fine-tuning. Lean into one or the other. I'm borderline on this — weak reject leaning toward accept if the authors can address the baseline and theoretical concerns.

This paper tackles regularization for tensor decomposition-based knowledge graph completion models, proposing to regularize intermediate variables rather than just the embeddings themselves. The core idea is reasonable and the theoretical grounding connecting the proposed regularization to trace norm minimization is a nice contribution. I've read this twice now and I think the paper makes a genuine, if somewhat incremental, contribution to the area.

Let me start with what I think works. The unification of existing TDB models under a general form (Section 3) is useful and reasonably well-executed. This kind of synthesis is genuinely helpful for the community even if it's not technically novel in the strictest sense. The theoretical analysis in Section 4 connecting intermediate variable regularization to low trace norm of the predicted tensor is the strongest part of the paper — I found this convincing, though I'd note the proof sketch in the appendix could use more detail around Lemma 2. I wasn't fully satisfied that the bound is tight enough to be practically meaningful, not just asymptotically nice.

My main concern is empirical. The improvements on FB15k-237 and WN18RR (Tables 2 and 3) are... fine, but marginal in many cases. On several metrics the gains are within what I'd consider noise given that many of these papers don't report variance across multiple runs. Do the authors run multiple seeds? I don't see this reported anywhere and in my experience with KGC benchmarks, variance can easily swamp differences of 0.001-0.003 in MRR. This significantly undermines the strength of the experimental claims.

I'm also a bit skeptical of the comparison setup. The baselines use their own original regularization schemes — but are hyperparameters re-tuned for the new regularization? This matters a lot. If you're just adding your regularization on top with a well-tuned lambda while the baselines use suboptimal settings, the comparison isn't clean.

The related work section feels thin. There's a body of work on tensor nuclear norm minimization and matrix/tensor completion theory (Tomioka et al., Candes & Recht, etc.) that the authors should engage with more seriously. The connection to nuclear norm regularization in matrix factorization is not new and the paper somewhat undersells how this connects to prior theoretical work.

Section 5.2 on the ablation study is appreciated but I found Figure 3 somewhat hard to read — the color choices make it difficult to distinguish curves, at least in print.

One thing I genuinely liked: the discussion about why minimizing embedding norms alone is suboptimal is intuitive and well-motivated in Section 2. I think this framing is the paper's best selling point and should be foregrounded more aggressively in the intro.

Overall: I think this is borderline. The idea is solid, the theory is a genuine contribution, but the experiments need more rigor (variance reporting, cleaner baselines). I'd lean toward weak accept if these issues are addressed, but as submitted it's not quite there.

This paper tackles an interesting theoretical question about prefix learning / prompt tuning, specifically trying to understand what happens as prefix length grows and whether one can approximate an "infinite-length" prefix efficiently. The motivation is reasonable and the topic is timely given the popularity of PEFT methods.

That said, I have significant concerns about both the theoretical contributions and the experimental validation.

On the theory side: the NTK analysis in Section 3 feels fairly standard — applying NTK convergence results to this setting isn't particularly surprising, and I'm not convinced the "ultra-long prefix" setting they analyze is actually the interesting regime in practice. The connection between the theoretical result (convergence of gradient descent on a stylized overparameterized network) and the actual algorithm they propose feels tenuous at best. After reading Section 4 twice, I still couldn't see a clean derivation showing why the specific parameterization they propose in Eq. (7) follows from the theory rather than just being a reasonable heuristic. This is a significant gap. The phrase "based on this strong theoretical guarantee" in the abstract is doing a lot of heavy lifting for what is essentially a separate design choice.

The experimental results are described as "preliminary" by the authors themselves — which is somewhat disarming but also a red flag. Table 2 shows comparisons on a handful of tasks, but the variance across seeds isn't reported consistently, and some baselines feel cherry-picked. I'd want to see results on standard GLUE/SuperGLUE benchmarks with proper ablations before making claims about being "superior or competitive" with LoRA, which has been validated much more extensively. The math dataset experiments (Section 5.3) are interesting but underdeveloped.

I also found the related work discussion a bit thin on the approximation theory side. There's a body of work on low-rank approximations of attention and on theoretical properties of soft prompts that deserves engagement — Akyürek et al., for instance, and some of the expressiveness results from Wei et al. The authors seem unaware of or chose to ignore these connections.

Minor things: Figure 3 is hard to read at print size, and some notation in Section 3 (particularly around Eq. 4-5) is introduced inconsistently. The writing in the introduction is fine but gets noticeably rougher in the technical sections.

My overall take: there's a potentially interesting paper here, but right now the theory and the algorithm feel like two separate contributions that haven't been properly unified, and the experiments aren't mature enough to support the claims being made. I think this needs substantial revision — either lean harder into making the theoretical contribution rigorous and self-contained, or reframe this more honestly as an empirically motivated method with theoretical inspiration. The current hybrid doesn't fully succeed as either. Leaning toward rejection, but I'd be willing to reconsider with major revisions.

This paper tackles backdoor purification through the lens of loss landscape geometry, arguing that backdoor models sit in sharper minima than clean models and that pushing toward flatter minima can serve as a purification mechanism. The core intuition is reasonable — I've seen related ideas in the sharpness-aware optimization literature and catastrophic forgetting work, so this isn't coming out of nowhere. The proposed Smooth Fine-Tuning (SFT) method combines FIM-guided re-optimization with a regularizer to preserve clean accuracy, plus a Fast SFT variant that reduces computational overhead.

Let me start with what I think is the paper's biggest weakness, because it's been bothering me since page 3: the central empirical claim that backdoor models converge to sharper minima needs much stronger justification. Figure 2 shows some sharpness comparisons, but the analysis feels cherry-picked. Is this consistent across all 14 attacks? Across all architectures? I'm skeptical that a WaNet-poisoned model and a Dynamic-attack model will exhibit the same sharpness characteristics, and the paper doesn't really grapple with this heterogeneity. The theoretical grounding in Section 3 is thin — closer to motivation than proof.

The regularizer design (Equation 6, I think?) is interesting but I found the explanation somewhat circular. You want to remember the clean distribution, but you're doing so using a subset of the potentially-compromised training data. The paper acknowledges this briefly but I don't think the response is satisfying. How sensitive is performance to the size of this clean subset? This feels like it could be a critical hyperparameter that's being swept under the rug.

On the experimental side — the breadth is genuinely impressive. Four tasks, 14 attacks, multiple architectures including ViTs. I appreciate this. Table 2 and Table 3 show competitive or superior ASR reduction compared to baselines like ANP and Fine-Pruning. That said, I'm a bit suspicious of some of the cleaner accuracy numbers. After reading this twice, I'm not sure the comparison against FT-SAM (which is the most directly comparable baseline) is being done with equivalent computational budgets. The 5x speedup claim for Fast SFT needs clearer ablation — Table 5 helps but doesn't fully convince me.

Minor things: the writing in Section 4.2 gets quite dense and could use a clearer exposition. The title is oddly self-deprecating ("naive exploitation") in a way that feels like false modesty. The related work section undersells some relevant connections to model merging and mode connectivity literature.

Overall, I think this is borderline. The idea has merit and the empirical scope is strong, but the theoretical motivation has gaps and some experimental choices feel underexplained. I'd want the authors to significantly strengthen the sharpness characterization analysis and clarify the clean-data regularization sensitivity before I'd feel comfortable recommending acceptance.

This paper investigates an interesting phenomenon where LLMs can apparently handle multiple ICL tasks simultaneously in a single forward pass — what the authors call "task superposition." The movie reference in the title is cute, I suppose, though it starts to feel a bit gimmicky by the end.

I'll be honest: I found the core empirical finding genuinely surprising and worth publishing in some form. The experiments across different model families (Section 4) are reasonably thorough, and Figure 3 in particular tells a compelling story about how performance scales with model size. That said, I have significant concerns about how the claims are framed and supported.

My biggest issue is with the theoretical section. The authors claim to offer "theoretical explanations" for why this capability is within the expressive power of transformers, but what's actually presented is closer to a proof-of-concept construction showing that *some* transformer *could* do this in principle. This is not the same as explaining why *trained* LLMs develop this behavior. After reading this section twice I still wasn't convinced it adds much beyond "transformers are universal approximators, so of course." The gap between the constructive proof and the empirical phenomenon is never really bridged.

The "task vectors" analysis in Section 5 borrows heavily from prior work (Hendel et al., Todd et al.) and I think the authors undersell how much they're building on those findings versus contributing something new. I'd like to see a clearer delineation here.

On the experimental side — what exactly counts as "success" in the superposition setting? The threshold choices feel somewhat arbitrary, and I didn't see a sensitivity analysis. Also, the tasks chosen are fairly simple classification/translation type things. I'm skeptical the findings generalize to more complex reasoning tasks, and this limitation deserves more than a single paragraph of discussion. In my experience, phenomena like this often look impressive on toy tasks and then fade on anything harder.

The "superposition of simulators" framing in the discussion feels speculative to the point of being unfalsifiable. I understand the authors want to connect to broader theoretical discussions about LLMs, but this section reads more like blog post material than scientific argument.

Minor things: Figure 2 is confusing on first read — the caption doesn't do enough work. The writing in Section 3.2 is dense and could use reorganization. Table 2 results for smaller models are pretty weak and I think the authors are a bit too optimistic in how they characterize them.

Overall: interesting phenomenon, real empirical contribution, but the theoretical framing is oversold and some experimental choices need justification. I'm leaning weak accept — the core finding is novel enough to merit publication, but the paper needs to be more honest about what it has and hasn't explained. A revision addressing the theoretical overclaiming and adding robustness checks would make this considerably stronger.

This paper tackles an interesting theoretical question about KL-regularized policy optimization in contextual bandits and RLHF settings. The central claim — that KL regularization yields O(1/ε) sample complexity versus O(1/ε²) without it — is genuinely interesting if correct, and I think the high-level intuition is sound. The geometric properties of KL divergence do create a kind of implicit variance reduction that one would expect to manifest in sample complexity improvements. So the motivation is there.

That said, I have real concerns about the technical contributions here. The proof techniques in Section 4 feel quite similar to standard analyses of self-normalized estimators and importance-weighted regression, and after reading this twice I wasn't convinced the sharp O(1/ε) result requires fundamentally new machinery. The authors claim novelty in their analysis approach on page 7 but I found this claim somewhat overstated. In my experience, once you properly account for the local geometry induced by KL regularization (essentially exploiting local strong convexity near the reference policy), the improved rate is not particularly surprising. The paper would benefit from a clearer articulation of what specifically is technically hard here.

The two-stage mixed sampling strategy in Section 5 is actually the part I found most interesting and potentially most useful in practice. The additive versus multiplicative dependence on the coverage coefficient is a meaningful distinction. However, I'm not entirely sure the comparison to prior work is fair — the authors seem to be comparing against results that make strictly weaker assumptions, so of course their bounds look better. Table 1 on page 3 needs a more careful footnote explaining the assumption differences. As written it's a bit misleading.

A few specific issues: Theorem 3.2 requires ε to be "sufficiently small" but the paper is somewhat cagey about how small is sufficiently small as a function of the problem parameters. This matters a lot for understanding when the O(1/ε) regime actually kicks in versus the O(1/ε²) regime. I'd want to see this threshold made explicit. Also, the assumption in Equation (6) — I might be wrong but this looks like it implicitly assumes bounded density ratios, which is essentially a coverage condition being smuggled in through the back door in the online setting.

The writing is generally clear though there are some rough patches around pages 9-10 where the notation gets heavy. Minor point but the repeated use of "predominant" in the intro sounds slightly off.

Overall: the paper addresses a relevant problem and the main results are likely correct. But I think the contribution is somewhat incremental given existing literature on variance reduction via regularization, and some of the comparisons to prior work need to be made more carefully. I'm on the fence about acceptance — this feels like a solid workshop paper that needs more work to be a full conference contribution. I'd want to see the authors address the "sufficiently small ε" issue and be more honest in their comparison table before I'd feel comfortable recommending acceptance.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem in consistency models — the gap between consistency distillation (CD) and consistency training (CT) arising from the Monte Carlo estimation error in the velocity field. I found the core motivation fairly compelling, and the theoretical analysis in Section 3 that formalizes this discrepancy even in the continuous-time limit is one of the stronger contributions here.

That said, I have several concerns.

The proposed generator-induced flow is conceptually intuitive — using the currently trained model's outputs to construct a proxy flow rather than relying on the noisy single-sample estimate. But I'm not entirely sure the theoretical justification fully supports the empirical gains. The argument that this flow approximates the "true" velocity field better relies on assumptions about the model quality at intermediate training stages, which are never really examined carefully. What happens early in training when the generator is poor? This seems like it could actually make things worse initially, and I didn't see ablations addressing this specific concern.

The comparison to optimal transport (OT) based consistency models (specifically referencing the work by Pooladian et al. and related minibatch OT approaches) is a highlight of the paper, and I appreciate that they provide both theoretical and empirical evidence here. The transport cost argument in Section 4 is interesting, though I found some of the notation around equation (6) a bit hard to follow — I had to re-read that section twice. The claim that generator-induced flows *surpass* OT-based methods is a strong one and I think the empirical evidence, while suggestive, isn't overwhelming given the relatively narrow margins in Table 2.

The experiments are on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet 64x64, which is fine but honestly feels somewhat limited for 2024. I'd want to see results on at least 256x256 resolution to be convinced this scales. FID numbers reported in Table 1 show improvement but the gains over the baseline CT are modest in some configurations, and the paper doesn't do enough to characterize when/why the method helps most.

Writing is generally clear but could be tightened. The introduction takes a while to get to the actual contribution. Figure 1 is helpful but the caption is underdescriptive.

One thing I genuinely liked: the connection to self-consistency as a training signal is underexplored in the literature and this paper moves that needle. I think there's a real idea here worth developing.

My main reservation for acceptance is that the empirical evidence doesn't quite match the theoretical ambition of the paper. The method is sound, the motivation is good, but the "surpasses OT" claim needs stronger support. I'm on the fence — leaning weak accept if the authors can address the scaling question and provide the early-training ablation I mentioned.

This paper proposes DeepInteract, an end-to-end pipeline for protein interface contact prediction using a "Geometric Transformer" that aims to be rotation and translation invariant. The core idea is reasonable and the problem is certainly important — interface contact prediction has real downstream value for docking and drug discovery workflows. That said, I have some significant concerns about both the technical contributions and the empirical evaluation.

Starting with what I think works: the framing is clear, and the geometric invariance motivation is well-grounded. The authors do a decent job explaining why naive coordinate-based approaches fail under rigid body transformations. The overall pipeline architecture (Figure 2) is readable, though I wish they'd been more explicit about the exact dimensions and computational complexity at each stage — this felt hand-wavy in places.

Now for my concerns, which are more substantial. The 14% top L/5 precision on CASP-CAPRI targets — I genuinely don't know how to interpret this number without more context. Is this good? The 1.1% on DB5 sounds quite poor, and while I understand the benchmark is challenging, the paper could do much more to contextualize these results. After reading this twice I still wasn't sure whether DeepInteract represents a meaningful practical advance or a modest incremental one. The comparison baselines in Table 2 feel somewhat cherry-picked, or at minimum, I'd want to see comparison against more recent learned methods including some of the ESM-based approaches that were emerging around this time.

My bigger concern is the novelty of the Geometric Transformer itself. The combination of attention mechanisms with geometric features on protein graphs is... not new. I'm thinking of work like GVP-GNN, SE(3)-Transformers, and related literature. The authors cite some of these but I don't think they adequately distinguish their technical contribution. What specifically does the "geometry-evolving" aspect add over, say, a standard equivariant network? The ablations in Table 3 help somewhat but don't fully answer this question in my view.

I also found the description of the edge feature construction (Section 3.2 I think, around page 5) genuinely confusing on first read. The notation gets dense and I'm not entirely sure the geometric features they define are actually invariant as claimed — I'd want to see a more rigorous proof or at least a clearer argument.

One more thing: the training details feel underspecified. Reproducibility is a real concern here. What hardware? How sensitive are results to the specific training split? The fact that proteins share evolutionary relationships makes data leakage a legitimate worry that isn't addressed carefully enough.

I'm not recommending rejection outright — there's real work here and the problem matters. But in my experience, this paper needs a sharper articulation of its technical novelty and stronger empirical grounding before it's ready. Major revision at minimum.

This paper tackles an interesting phenomenon in neural network learning dynamics — the early convergence to an "optimal constant solution" (OCS) before any input-discriminative learning occurs. The theoretical contribution for deep linear networks is reasonably clean, and I appreciated the attempt to connect computational findings to human behavioral data. That said, I have several concerns that I think need to be addressed before this is ready for publication.

Starting with what I found compelling: the derivation of exact learning dynamics in deep linear networks with bias terms (Section 3, equations around Eq. 8-12 if I recall correctly) is the strongest part of the paper. The insight that even zero-initialized biases can substantially alter early dynamics is genuinely interesting, and I think underappreciated in the field. I've read a fair amount on loss of plasticity and early learning dynamics (Saxe et al., Advani & Saxe, etc.) and this feels like a meaningful addition to that conversation.

However, I have real problems with the human learning section. The jump from "networks do this" to "humans do this too" is presented with what I think is unwarranted confidence. The behavioral signature they identify — elevated true negative rates early in training — is interesting, but frankly there are many explanations for this pattern that have nothing to do with an OCS mechanism. Response bias, conservative criterion setting, simple fatigue or confusion during day 1 of a novel task... the authors don't adequately rule these out. Three days of training with what appears to be a relatively small N feels thin for the conclusions being drawn. I'd want to see the actual subject counts and more careful analysis of individual differences before buying the "humans implement OCS" narrative.

The nonlinear CNN results (Section 5 I think) feel a bit hand-wavy to me. Showing that signatures of OCS appear in CNNs trained on MNIST/CIFAR10 is nice, but the connection to the theoretical results is informal. The theory is for deep linear networks — the extension to nonlinear networks is described more as empirical observation than principled extension, and I think that gap deserves more honest treatment.

I'm also not entirely sure the final section on input correlations driving OCS-like behavior without biases fully coheres with the rest of the paper. It feels like it was added somewhat separately and the integration is a bit loose.

Minor: some figures (particularly Figure 3) are quite dense and the captions don't do enough work explaining what I'm supposed to take away. I had to read the figure twice before understanding the axes.

Overall: the core theoretical result is solid and publishable, but the human learning claims are overclaimed relative to the evidence provided, and the paper would be stronger with either stronger behavioral methodology or more hedged conclusions in that section. I lean toward major revisions.

This paper tackles an interesting and underexplored problem: providing worst-case guarantees on PINN residual errors rather than relying on point-wise evaluations. The motivation is sound — I've seen too many PINN papers declare victory based on a handful of test points, so having a certification framework is genuinely useful.

The core technical contribution is ∂-CROWN, which adapts the CROWN/α-CROWN bounding framework to handle the higher-order derivative computations that appear in PDE residuals. I think this is non-trivial and the paper deserves credit for working out the details. Computing bounds through automatic differentiation operations is not straightforward, and the authors seem to handle this carefully, though I had to read Section 3 twice before the propagation rules for the derivative terms really clicked. Equation (7) in particular could use more intuitive explanation — I'm not entirely sure the notation is as clean as it could be.

My main concern is the experimental scope. The paper evaluates on Burgers', Schrödinger, Allen-Cahn, and Diffusion-Sorption equations. These are reasonable benchmarks, but they're all relatively low-dimensional (1D or 2D spatial domains if I'm reading Table 1 correctly). The scalability claims in the abstract feel somewhat overstated given this. What happens with 3D or higher-dimensional PDEs? This is where PINNs arguably offer their biggest advantage over classical solvers, and it's precisely where I'd expect the bounding approach to struggle. The paper doesn't address this at all, which feels like a significant gap.

The comparison with interval arithmetic and naive IBP baselines in Table 2 is good and shows ∂-CROWN produces meaningfully tighter bounds. But I'm missing comparison with any sampling-based or mixed approaches. Is there no related certification work from the formal verification side that applies here? The related work section feels a bit thin on the verification/certification literature — works like those using SMT solvers or branch-and-bound for neural network verification seem relevant.

One thing I found genuinely confusing: the paper introduces "tolerance-based correctness conditions" in Section 2 but the connection between these conditions and practical PDE solver tolerances is never made quantitative. What tolerance values are typical in practice? The certificates obtained in the experiments — are these actually tight enough to be useful for any real application, or are they still orders of magnitude looser than what an engineer would need? This is the critical question and the paper dances around it.

Minor things: "Allan-Cahn" should be "Allen-Cahn" throughout (appears to be a consistent typo), and Figure 3 is quite small and hard to read in print.

Overall, I think this is solid work with a clear contribution, but the scalability story needs to be more honest and the practical utility of the obtained certificate values needs direct discussion. Leaning toward acceptance if these concerns can be addressed, but the paper as written oversells itself a bit.

This paper proposes Moonwalk, a gradient computation method for invertible/right-invertible networks that combines forward-mode differentiation with inverse Jacobian products to achieve linear (rather than quadratic) time complexity while reducing memory overhead compared to standard backpropagation. The core idea is clever — using the invertibility structure to avoid materializing the full Jacobian — and I appreciate that the authors are tackling a real problem. Memory during training is genuinely a bottleneck, and forward-mode alternatives have been underexplored.

That said, I have significant concerns about the paper in its current form.

The theoretical contributions in Section 3 feel rushed. The derivation around equations (4)-(6) glossed over several steps that I had to reconstruct myself, and after reading this twice I'm still not 100% confident the formulation handles non-square Jacobians correctly in the right-invertible case. The authors claim this generalizes cleanly but the proof sketch in the appendix doesn't fully convince me. I might be wrong here, but I think there's a gap.

The empirical evaluation is where this paper really struggles. The experiments are limited to relatively small architectures — I see ResNet variants and some flow-based models — and the comparison baselines feel cherry-picked. Where is the comparison against gradient checkpointing? This is the obvious practical baseline that any practitioner would reach for, and it's conspicuously absent from Table 2. The memory vs. time tradeoff curves in Figure 3 are interesting but the axes aren't labeled consistently with the text, which drove me nuts. Also, "several orders of magnitude" in the abstract is a serious overclaim based on what's actually shown in the results.

The hybrid reverse+forward variant (Section 4.2) is arguably the most practically useful contribution, but it feels underdeveloped. How do you decide where to split the network? Is there a principled way to choose this, or is it just tuned per architecture? This matters a lot for practitioners.

I also think the related work section undersells some relevant prior work. Specifically, the connection to RevNet-style methods and to the work on memory-efficient backprop through ODEs (Pontryagin adjoint methods) deserves more careful treatment. The positioning of Moonwalk as "the first forward-based method to compute true gradients... in computation time comparable to backpropagation" is a strong claim and I'm not sure it survives scrutiny given what's already in the literature.

In my experience, papers like this — good idea, incomplete execution — benefit enormously from a revision cycle. The core insight is publishable, but right now the paper is trying to do too much and doing none of it with sufficient rigor or breadth. I'd want to see the gradient checkpointing baseline, tighter proofs, larger-scale experiments, and a more honest abstract before recommending acceptance. Borderline reject, leaning toward rejection in current form.

This paper proposes a new decoding strategy for LLMs called Permute-and-Flip (PF), which the authors claim achieves better quality-stability tradeoffs than standard sampling, along with a compatible watermarking scheme. I'll share my thoughts after going through this carefully.

The core idea is actually pretty elegant. By permuting tokens according to their probabilities and then flipping through them sequentially, you get a decoder that provably dominates sampling in a certain quality-stability sense. Theorem 1 and the surrounding analysis in Section 3 are the technical heart of the paper, and I think the proof is correct — though I had to read it twice to convince myself the coupling argument goes through cleanly. The claim that PF is "never worse" than any other decoder under their stability metric is strong, and I'd want the authors to be more careful about exactly what class of decoders this holds over. Is this over all randomized decoders? The statement on page 5 felt a bit loose to me.

The watermarking extension is natural and I appreciate that it maintains the output distribution exactly — that's genuinely useful from a practical standpoint. The connection to Aaronson's Gumbel watermark is clearly articulated. That said, I'm not entirely sure the theoretical guarantees in Section 4 are meaningfully stronger than what you'd get from just applying existing watermarking analysis to this new decoder. The authors should be more explicit about what's *new* in the watermarking theory versus what's inherited.

My bigger concern is with the experiments. The perplexity comparisons in Table 2 show improvements, but perplexity is a weird metric to use when you're evaluating a *decoder* — you're essentially measuring how close the decoder's output distribution is to the model's, which by construction PF should do well on. I'd want to see more downstream task evaluations or human preference studies. The stability experiments are fine but feel a bit synthetic. Also, the baselines are somewhat limited — why not compare against top-k or nucleus sampling variants more thoroughly?

I also found the related work treatment of other watermarking approaches (Kirchenbauer et al., Christ et al.) a bit thin. These are the papers everyone in this space is benchmarking against now, and the comparison feels rushed.

Minor things: Figure 3 is hard to read at print size, and there are a few places (around line 340ish) where the notation gets sloppy with indexing.

Overall, I think this is a solid contribution with a genuinely interesting theoretical result. The practical improvements are modest but real. My main ask is that the authors sharpen the experimental section and be more precise about the scope of their theoretical claims. I'm leaning toward acceptance but this needs revision — the current draft oversells certain aspects and the experiments don't fully support the enthusiasm in the abstract.

This paper proposes APCtrl, a conditional control method for diffusion models based on alternating projections onto a "denoising set" and a "condition set." The geometric framing is appealing at first glance, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to ground their approach in a principled mathematical framework rather than just proposing another adapter architecture empirically.

That said, I have significant concerns after reading this twice.

The theoretical justification is the paper's main selling point, but I found it surprisingly thin on rigor. The definition of the "denoising set" D_t is somewhat hand-wavy — in what sense exactly is z_t a "projection" onto this set? The DDPM/DDIM update is not literally a projection in the Hilbert space sense unless you make very specific assumptions that I don't see stated anywhere. The connection to classical alternating projections (Dykstra, von Neumann) requires the sets to have certain convexity properties. Are D_t and C_t convex? I'm not sure the authors have established this, and honestly I suspect they haven't because the condition sets induced by a neural network are almost certainly not convex in any meaningful sense. This feels like geometric language being used to dress up what is essentially a residual guidance mechanism.

On the experimental side — Table 2 shows improvements over ControlNet on several metrics, but the margins are modest and I'm not convinced the evaluation is comprehensive enough. The FID differences in some cases are within what I'd consider noise territory given typical variance in these evaluations. Also, I'd really want to see a proper user study here, not just automated metrics, especially for a conditional generation paper where perceptual quality matters a lot.

The "model and sampling agnosticism" claim is stated prominently but I think it's oversold. In practice, the method requires training a latent control network A_theta, which means it's not plug-and-play with arbitrary models without retraining. The comparison to T2I-Adapter and ControlNet is fair, but the claim of agnosticism needs to be qualified.

Multi-condition generation results (Section 4.3 if I recall correctly) look visually reasonable, but again — where's the ablation showing that the alternating projection formulation is actually doing the heavy lifting versus just the architecture choices? This is my biggest complaint: the ablation studies are insufficient to isolate the contribution of the projection framework specifically.

I found Figure 3 helpful for understanding the method intuitively, but the caption is too sparse.

Minor thing: the abstract has a typo — "denosing" should be "denoising."

Overall, I think the idea has some merit and the paper is readable, but the gap between the theoretical claims and what's actually proven/demonstrated is too large for acceptance in its current form. Needs substantially stronger theoretical grounding or the authors should be more honest about the heuristic nature of the projection framing. Leaning toward rejection but willing to reconsider with major revisions.

This paper presents DOMiNO, an RL approach for discovering diverse policies while maintaining near-optimality, using state-action occupancy measures as the basis for a diversity intrinsic reward. The Lagrange multiplier formulation for balancing quality and diversity is clean, and I appreciate that the method is designed to plug into any existing RL algorithm without architectural changes.

That said, I have some significant concerns about the paper as presented.

The theoretical grounding is the strongest part — the derivation of the intrinsic reward from occupancy measures is principled and I found Section 3 reasonably convincing on first read. However, after reading it twice, I'm not entirely sure the "near optimality" claim in the name is actually backed up rigorously. The Lagrange multiplier approach gives you a constraint in expectation, but the paper doesn't really prove tight bounds on how far discovered policies can deviate from the optimum. This feels like it should be Theorem 1 territory, not just an empirical observation.

On the experiments: the control domains (MuJoCo locomotion tasks) are fine but honestly a bit tired at this point. Everyone uses humanoid stand/walk. I think the paper would be substantially stronger with even one environment where the behavioral diversity is harder to interpret or where the reward function is genuinely ambiguous. The comparison against baselines (Table 2, page 7) shows favorable numbers, but I'm skeptical of the DIAYN and PEP comparisons specifically — the hyperparameter sensitivity of those methods is notorious, and I'd want to know whether the baselines were tuned with similar care as DOMiNO.

Figure 3 is actually pretty compelling visually, I'll admit. Seeing qualitatively distinct locomotion behaviors emerge is satisfying. But the robustness experiments in Section 5.4 feel rushed — what perturbations exactly? The description is vague enough that I can't evaluate whether this is a meaningful stress test.

The claim of "simple reward function" appears repeatedly but I'm not sure this is as distinctive as the authors think. Lots of diversity methods work with simple rewards. This framing feels a bit like motivated marketing.

Minor things: the writing in the introduction is a bit meandering and could be tightened. Equation 4 has a notation conflict with the variable used earlier in Equation 2 — I might be wrong but the subscript indexing seems inconsistent. Also "Near Optimally" in the acronym expansion should probably be "Near Optimality" — it's in the title correctly but the abstract expansion is wrong.

Overall: I think this is a solid paper with a genuine contribution, but it's not quite there yet. The theoretical claims need more rigorous support, the experimental section needs either stronger baselines or more diverse environments, and some of the framing needs to be toned down. Leaning toward weak reject, but I'd reconsider with a strong rebuttal addressing the optimality gap question specifically.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — node representation learning when attributes are partially missing — using a Wasserstein-based diffusion framework. I appreciate that the authors are trying to do something genuinely different from the typical GNN imputation approaches, and the geometric flavor of the method is appealing. That said, I have some fairly significant concerns about both the presentation and the experimental validation.

The core idea (Section 3, roughly) of mapping node features to discrete distributions in a lower-dimensional space and then doing diffusion in Wasserstein space is clever, but I found the exposition quite dense and sometimes hard to follow. After reading sections 3.2 and 3.3 twice, I'm still not entirely sure how the "pull-back" to the original space is computed in practice. The description around equation (7) or so feels hand-wavy — how sensitive is this step to the choice of projection? The authors should be much more explicit here. Related to this, the computational complexity discussion is brief to the point of being almost useless. Wasserstein distances are expensive to compute in general, and I'd want to see concrete wall-clock comparisons, not just asymptotic claims.

On the experimental side, the results in Table 2 for node classification are... fine, but not particularly convincing. The gains over baselines like GRAPE or feature propagation methods are often within what I'd consider noise margins, and the authors don't report confidence intervals or standard deviations for most results, which in my experience is a red flag. Which seeds were used? How many runs? This needs to be clarified. Also, the choice of baselines feels a bit dated — there are more recent missing-attribute GNN methods from 2022-2023 that should probably be included for a fair comparison.

I'm also not totally convinced by the matrix completion experiments. The connection between the Wasserstein diffusion framework and matrix completion feels somewhat bolted on — like the authors needed a second application to justify the generality claim in the abstract. The theoretical justification for why this approach should work well for matrix completion specifically is thin.

One thing I do think is genuinely nice: the non-parametric nature of the approach means there's no training instability, and the framework is fairly interpretable compared to black-box GNN approaches. The visualization in Figure 3 is actually quite informative and I think undersells itself in the caption.

Minor gripe: the abstract says "we designed two algorithms" which sounds oddly informal for a paper at this venue.

Bottom line: the paper has a legitimate idea but needs substantially more work on clarity of exposition, computational analysis, and experimental rigor. I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, but I'd genuinely encourage the authors to revise and resubmit — the core contribution isn't without merit. I'd want to see the complexity analysis fleshed out and proper statistical reporting before reconsidering.

This paper tackles visible-infrared person re-ID using a masked pre-training approach, which is a reasonable direction. I'll share my thoughts after going through this twice.

The core idea — using random channel exchangeable augmentation to generate color-irrelevant images and then doing shared-mask reconstruction — is intuitive enough. I appreciate that the authors are trying to bring masked autoencoding ideas (clearly inspired by MAE) into the cross-modal Re-ID space. That said, I'm not entirely convinced the contribution here is as significant as the paper claims.

My main concern is the performance gains. +1.87% and +1.24% mAP on SYSU-MM01 and RegDB respectively... these are pretty modest numbers. In my experience reviewing Re-ID papers, gains of this magnitude are often within the noise of different experimental runs, and I don't see any statistical significance analysis anywhere in the paper. How many runs were averaged? This feels important given the claim of "stable improvement" in the abstract.

The channel exchangeable augmentation idea is the most interesting part to me, but I felt the explanation around equation — I think it's around page 4-5 — was rushed. Why does randomly exchanging channels specifically minimize modality difference in a meaningful way? The intuition is hand-wavy. There's related work on style transfer and channel statistics for domain adaptation that the authors seem unaware of, which would have strengthened the motivation considerably.

I also found the decoder design choice confusing. The paper says they only reconstruct visible and generated images (not infrared) due to "misalignment" — but this asymmetry needs much more justification. If the infrared images are misaligned, why does shared masking still make sense? These seem like contradictory design choices that aren't adequately resolved.

Table 2 (ablation) is helpful but the baseline numbers feel oddly chosen. I'd want to see what happens if you apply standard MAE pre-training without the cross-modality components — is the gain coming from pre-training in general or specifically from the cross-modal design? This experiment seems conspicuously absent.

The writing is mostly fine but there are grammatical issues throughout that distracted me. "The visible together with the generated image, and the infrared image are masked" — this sentence structure appears in the abstract itself, which is not a great sign.

I might be wrong, but I think this paper is solving a somewhat narrow problem in a way that won't generalize much beyond the specific benchmarks tested. The approach feels incremental relative to existing masked pre-training literature. I'd lean toward rejection in current form, but I think there's a salvageable paper here if the authors can (1) run more rigorous experiments, (2) better justify the asymmetric reconstruction design, and (3) situate the work more carefully against MAE variants and existing cross-modal pre-training methods. The gains need to be more convincing.

This paper proposes SiReRAG, a RAG indexing method that combines similarity-based and relatedness-based tree structures for multihop QA. The core idea is intuitive — existing methods like RAPTOR focus on semantic clustering while missing entity-grounded relational structure, and combining both should help with multihop reasoning. I get the motivation, and I've seen this gap myself when working with hierarchical RAG systems.

That said, I have some real concerns here.

The improvements are modest. 1.9% average F1 improvement is... fine? But table 2 shows the gains are quite uneven across datasets — the numbers on HotpotQA are underwhelming compared to MuSiQue. I'd want a more honest discussion of why the method helps more in some settings than others rather than just reporting averages that obscure this heterogeneity.

The relatedness tree construction relies on proposition extraction and entity grouping, which sounds clean in the abstract but I found the description in Section 3.2 somewhat underspecified. Specifically, how are entity co-occurrence thresholds chosen? What happens with highly ambiguous entities (common names, etc.)? After reading this section twice I still wasn't fully clear on the edge cases. The pipeline has a lot of moving parts — proposition extraction, entity linking, grouping, then recursive summarization — and the ablations in Table 3 help somewhat, but I think the paper would benefit from an error analysis showing where this breaks down.

I'm also a bit skeptical of the efficiency claims. The paper says SiReRAG is "reasonably efficient" but building two separate tree structures with LLM-based summarization at each level is genuinely expensive. Table 4 shows the index construction times and they're... not great, honestly. The framing of this as efficient feels like spin to me. I'd rather the authors just be upfront about the cost and argue that the quality tradeoff is worth it.

The 7.8% improvement with reranking (highlighted in the abstract) is the most compelling result to me, and I think the paper actually undersells what's happening there. Why does SiReRAG synergize so well with reranking? Is it because the relatedness tree provides candidates that similarity search misses, and reranking can then surface the right ones? This mechanism deserves more analysis.

One thing I might be wrong about — I initially thought the similarity tree contribution was trivial since it's basically RAPTOR with minor modifications, but the ablations do show it matters. Fair enough.

Minor stuff: Figure 2 is helpful but a bit cluttered. The related work section is adequate but doesn't engage deeply enough with recent entity-centric retrieval work (e.g., graph-based methods like HippoRAG which is cited but somewhat dismissed too quickly).

Overall this is decent work with a sensible idea and solid empirical evaluation on standard benchmarks. The gains are real but modest, and some of the mechanistic story needs tightening. I lean toward weak accept but wouldn't fight hard for it.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely problem — understanding memorization in CLIP models. The authors propose CLIPMem, a formal definition of memorization adapted to the contrastive multimodal setting, and run a fairly comprehensive set of experiments. I'll say upfront that I found the core idea compelling and the paper is generally well-written, but I have some significant concerns about the framing and certain experimental choices.

The definition of CLIPMem (Section 3, around equation 2-3) is reasonable, but I'm not entirely sure the analogy to prior memorization definitions (e.g., Feldman's long-tail theory, or the influence-function-based notions) is as tight as the authors claim. The contrastive loss creates fundamentally different dynamics than cross-entropy — the "leave-one-out" style counterfactual is harder to interpret when the negative samples in a batch are themselves data-dependent. I think the authors should be more careful here, or at least acknowledge this more explicitly. After reading Section 3 twice I still wasn't fully convinced the definition captures what we intuitively mean by memorization in this setting.

The finding about mis-captioned samples exhibiting highest memorization is interesting and I believe it — it aligns with intuitions from noisy label literature. But Table 2 feels undercooked. What's the variance across runs? These numbers need error bars, especially given the relatively small-scale experiments.

The claim that the text encoder contributes *more* to memorization than the image encoder is probably the most novel empirical finding here, and I found the ablations in Section 5 somewhat convincing. But I'd want to see this validated on a larger model (ViT-L or bigger). All the experiments seem to be on ViT-B/32 which, in my experience, may not generalize cleanly to the scales people actually care about in practice.

The mitigation strategies section is where the paper gets exciting — claiming you can *reduce* memorization while *improving* utility is a strong result that, if true, is genuinely surprising. The authors are right that this hasn't been shown before for traditional paradigms. However, I'm a bit skeptical. The utility improvements are modest (Figure 4), and I wonder whether this is partly an artifact of the baseline being poorly tuned rather than a fundamental property of the proposed approach. I'm not trying to be uncharitable, but this needs to be addressed more carefully.

Figure 3 is honestly hard to read. The color scheme doesn't work well in grayscale and the caption doesn't explain what the shaded regions represent until you dig into the appendix.

Minor thing: the related work section largely ignores the growing literature on data attribution and influence functions for large models (Ilyas et al., DataModels work from Engstrom et al.), which seems directly relevant.

Overall — this is solid, publishable-quality work that makes a real contribution. I lean toward acceptance but the authors need to address the definitional concerns, add variance estimates, and tone down some of the stronger claims unless they can back them up at larger scale.

This paper proposes a "forget-and-relearn" framework that attempts to unify several existing iterative training methods under a common theoretical lens. The core claim is that selective forgetting of "undesirable" information followed by relearning can improve neural network training, and that this lens explains the success of methods like winning ticket pruning, data augmentation curricula, and certain language emergence algorithms.

I'll be honest — I found the framing more compelling than the technical contributions. The idea that forgetting can be beneficial is not new (this has been discussed in the continual learning literature for years, and there's obvious connections to work on lottery tickets, dropout, and even older work on weight perturbation), and I think the authors undersell how much prior art there is here. The related work section feels a bit thin in places, particularly around catastrophic forgetting literature where "beneficial forgetting" has been explicitly studied.

That said, I think the unification angle is genuinely interesting. Seeing Table 2 (if I'm reading it correctly) lay out how different methods fit into the forget-and-relearn taxonomy is useful and I haven't seen it presented quite this way before. My concern is whether the unification is deep or mostly taxonomic. After reading this twice I'm still not sure the framework makes predictions that go beyond post-hoc explanation. The authors claim it "offers a clear path towards performance improvements" but the empirical improvements shown in Section 4 are modest and not always consistent across settings.

The experiments on image classification are fine but not particularly convincing. The language emergence experiments are more interesting to me personally, but the baselines feel a bit dated. I'd want to see this compared against stronger recent methods before I'm fully sold on the practical value.

My biggest technical concern is around the operationalization of "undesirable information" — this is doing a lot of work in the paper and I'm not sure it's ever rigorously defined.
