The authors gesture at it in Section 3 but the definition feels circular in places: undesirable information is what gets forgotten, and what gets forgotten is undesirable. This needs tightening considerably.

The writing is generally clear, though Section 3.2 is dense and I think could be reorganized. Figure 3 is helpful but the caption is too sparse.

Overall: interesting perspective, somewhat incremental empirical contribution, and a theoretical framing that needs sharper formalization. I'm not opposed to this being published but it needs significant revision. The authors should either strengthen the empirical story or commit more fully to a theoretical treatment with proper definitions and proofs. Right now it sits uncomfortably between the two. Leaning toward weak reject, but I could be persuaded if the authors address the circularity issue and show me I'm wrong about the novelty concerns.

This paper tackles an interesting and (I think) genuinely underexplored question: how do ReLU networks and polynomial kernels compare when the data has sparse latent structure, rather than in the worst-case approximation setting that dominates the classical literature. The framing is well-motivated and I found the abstract compelling enough that I read this fairly carefully.

That said, I have significant concerns.

The theoretical contributions hinge on complex-analytic techniques that the authors claim are novel. After reading the relevant sections twice, I'm still not fully convinced the key lemmas in Section 4 are as tight as advertised. The "almost-tight" claim in the abstract is doing a lot of work — the gaps between upper and lower bounds in Theorem 3 (if I'm reading Table 1 correctly) are not trivial, and calling this almost-tight feels optimistic. I might be wrong here, but the authors should be much more explicit about exactly where the tightness fails and why they believe the bounds can't be improved further.

The experimental section feels like an afterthought. Figures 3 and 4 show simulations on what appear to be fairly small-scale synthetic problems, and there's no real attempt to connect the empirical numbers back to the theoretical rates. In my experience, this kind of paper lives or dies on whether the theory actually predicts the right qualitative behavior, and I couldn't convince myself it does here. What are the actual latent dimensions being tested? The caption on Figure 3 is genuinely unclear.

I also think the related work section undersells some relevant prior work. The connection to the statistical query lower bounds literature (Kearns, and more recently work by Goel et al. on superpolynomial separations) feels underexplored. These aren't identical settings but the relationship deserves more than a passing sentence.

On the positive side — the problem formulation is clean, the motivation for moving beyond worst-case analysis is compelling, and there are moments in Section 3 where the writing is genuinely elegant. The core idea of exploiting sparsity in the latent structure to get a more refined comparison is good, and I think there's a real paper here.

But honestly, in its current form this feels like it's missing a section. The gap between the theoretical claims and the experimental validation is too wide. The "may be of independent interest" comment about the complex-analytic techniques needs to be backed up — either develop those techniques more fully or be more modest about the claim.

Minor things: Equation 7 has a typo (missing exponent I think), and the paper exceeds the page limit if you count the appendix the way the submission guidelines specify, though I might be misreading the formatting rules.

Leaning toward weak reject, but I'd genuinely reconsider if the authors can tighten the experimental section and clarify the tightness claims. The core idea is worth publishing somewhere.

This paper proposes a graph neural network framework that incorporates clustering via optimal transport into the message passing mechanism. The core idea is to introduce "cluster-nodes" that interact with regular nodes on a bipartite graph, with the optimization steps themselves being cast as message passing operations. I've read this twice and I think the high-level motivation is reasonable, but I have significant concerns about the execution and presentation.

The connection to optimal transport is interesting, but honestly equation 3 onwards gets quite dense and I'm not fully convinced the derivation is as clean as the authors claim. The phrase "simple yet elegant" in the abstract is doing a lot of heavy lifting — after working through the math, I wouldn't call it simple. I might be wrong, but some of the steps feel hand-wavy, particularly around how the alternating optimization is guaranteed to converge and whether the gradients actually flow properly through the cluster assignment steps in practice.

On the experiments: the results in Table 2 look decent on heterophilous benchmarks, but I'm troubled by the dataset selection. We're in 2024 and papers are still evaluating primarily on Cora, Citeseer, Cornell, Texas? These datasets are tiny and have well-documented issues. The heterophily community has largely moved on to larger and more challenging benchmarks. The improvements over baselines like FAGCN and GPR-GNN seem marginal in several cases, and I don't see error bars reported consistently — this makes it hard to assess statistical significance.

The long-range experiments are thin. Claiming the method handles long-range propagation is a strong claim that really demands evaluation on something like LRGB benchmarks (Dwivedi et al. 2022). As it stands, this claim feels more like motivation than demonstrated fact.

One thing I did find genuinely interesting is the bipartite graph formulation — framing the cluster assignment updates as message passing is elegant and has practical implementation benefits. This is probably the strongest contribution. But I think the paper tries to do too much: long-range AND heterophily AND an OT framework AND scalability arguments. In my experience papers that try to address everything often fully address nothing.

The related work section misses some relevant work on pooling-based approaches (DiffPool, MinCut Pool) that are clearly related. The distinction from these methods is discussed briefly but deserves more careful treatment.

Presentation is generally okay but there are notation inconsistencies between sections 3 and 4 that I found confusing — specifically around how cluster assignments are denoted.

Bottom line: the core idea has merit and I can see a path to a publishable paper here, but in its current form the experimental evaluation is insufficient for the claims being made, the theoretical analysis needs tightening, and the scope needs to be narrowed or the experiments need to be substantially expanded. I lean toward rejection but would reconsider with major revisions.

This paper tackles an interesting theoretical question about CycleGAN — specifically, characterizing the solution space and showing it has the structure of a principal homogeneous space (torsor) under the group of automorphisms of the underlying probability spaces. I think the core mathematical contribution is genuinely interesting and the connection to group theory is elegant. The main theorem showing that automorphisms act freely and transitively on exact solutions is clean.

That said, I have some real concerns.

The paper spends a lot of time on the pure CycleGAN loss case (Section 3) before moving to the extended loss with identity terms (Section 4), and I found the transition somewhat abrupt. The perturbation bounds in Section 4 feel underdeveloped compared to the exactcase — the constants in the bounds are not carefully analyzed and I'm not convinced the bounds are tight enough to be practically informative. After reading this twice I still wasn't sure whether Theorem 4.2 is giving us something quantitatively useful or just qualitatively reassuring.

The empirical section is, frankly, weak. Showing that neural networks "can learn automorphisms" with a few cherry-picked examples on what looks like a fairly simple domain doesn't strongly support the broader claim in the abstract that hidden symmetries can "render the result useless." I'd want to see this demonstrated on standard CycleGAN benchmarks (horse↔zebra, apple↔orange, etc.) rather than the toy setting used in Figures 3-4. The gap between the theoretical contribution and the experimental evidence is too large.

I'm also not entirely sure the assumption that exact minimizers of the CycleGAN loss are achievable in practice is doing a lot of unacknowledged work here. In my experience, neural network training never reaches these exact optima, so the relevance of characterizing the exact solution space depends heavily on the perturbation analysis holding up — which brings me back to my concern about Section 4.

The related work discussion is decent but I think the paper undersells its own novelty. The connection to measure-theoretic automorphisms and the torsor structure hasn't been made explicit before in this context, and the authors are too modest about it. On the other hand, they should engage more seriously with the literature on mode collapse and solution uniqueness in GANs — there's relevant work by Bau et al. and others that isn't cited.

Minor things: the notation in Section 3 gets quite heavy around equations (8)-(11) and a brief summary sentence or two would help readability. Figure 2 caption is cryptic.

Overall — I think this is a solid theoretical paper with an interesting core result, but the experimental validation needs significant work and the practical implications are overstated given what's actually demonstrated. I'm leaning toward weak accept, contingent on strengthening Section 4 and the experiments. The math is probably right but I'd want the reviewers with stronger measure theory backgrounds to verify Theorem 3.1 carefully.

This paper tackles an interesting problem — the sim-to-real gap in world model-based RL — and the core idea is simple enough that I can summarize it in one sentence: use dropout at inference time in a world model to implicitly perform domain randomization, then train a policy in these stochastically varied "dream" environments to improve transfer to reality. Fine. The idea is intuitive and I appreciate that it doesn't require massive engineering overhead.

That said, I have real concerns about this work that I think need to be addressed before it's ready for publication.

The connection to domain randomization feels somewhat superficial to me. Domain randomization in traditional sim-to-real (Tobin et al., OpenAI) involves explicitly varying semantically meaningful parameters — lighting, friction, mass, etc. What dropout is doing here is introducing structured noise into the latent dynamics of a neural network. The paper never really convincingly argues that these are equivalent in any meaningful sense, or that the diversity induced by MC dropout is the *right kind* of diversity for bridging the reality gap. I found this gap in the theoretical motivation pretty frustrating after reading the paper twice. There's a claim on what I believe is around page 4 that the stochastic environments "approximate" the true distribution of possible environments, but this is essentially asserted rather than demonstrated.

The experiments are my bigger concern. The evaluation is conducted on what appear to be relatively simple environments, and I'm not entirely sure the "reality gap" being bridged here is actually significant enough to make this a compelling result. How much does the baseline (no dropout) actually fail? If the gap is small to begin with, closing it doesn't tell us much. Table 2 shows improvements but the variance is high enough in several conditions that I'd want statistical significance reported more carefully.

I'm also puzzled by the ablation design. The paper claims "extensive" ablation studies but in my reading they mostly vary dropout rate. That's not extensive — that's a hyperparameter sweep. What about architectural choices? What about comparing against explicit noise injection or other regularization approaches?

The writing is okay but there are places where it gets hand-wavy. Phrases like "nearly infinite number of different dream environments" in the abstract are technically true of any stochastic process and don't mean much.

I don't want to be entirely dismissive — I think there's a real kernel of a good idea here, and the simplicity is genuinely appealing. In my experience, simple techniques that work are worth publishing. But the paper needs stronger empirical validation on harder transfer tasks, a more rigorous treatment of *why* dropout diversity helps (not just that it does), and comparison against more meaningful baselines. As it stands it reads more like a workshop paper than a full conference submission.

Leaning toward rejection, but I'd be willing to reconsider with major revisions.

This paper proposes a key-based protection scheme for collaborative learning aimed at defending against GAN-based attacks from active adversaries. The core idea is to use class-specific keys to obfuscate the output layer so that an adversary participating in the learning process cannot mount a successful GAN attack. I appreciate that the authors are tackling a genuinely important problem — GAN attacks on federated/collaborative learning are a real threat and there's been growing interest in this since the Hitaj et al. work.

That said, I have significant concerns about the technical depth and experimental validation here.

First, the threat model feels underspecified. The paper assumes a particular GAN attack setup but doesn't rigorously characterize what the adversary knows, what their computational budget is, or whether the proposed key scheme is secure under adaptive attacks. An adversary who participates in training could potentially try to recover the keys through repeated queries or gradient information. I didn't see a formal security analysis — the argument essentially amounts to "the adversary doesn't know the keys, therefore the attack fails," which is intuitive but not convincing as a security proof. What happens if the adversary attempts to brute-force or approximate the keys? This is never addressed.

Second, the experimental evaluation is quite weak. MNIST and AT&T Olivetti Faces are both very small, relatively simple benchmarks. After reading this twice, I couldn't find experiments on anything resembling a modern realistic collaborative learning scenario. The face dataset in particular has only 40 subjects — this is not representative of the scale at which real privacy threats emerge. No comparison against other defense mechanisms (e.g., gradient perturbation, secure aggregation, or more recent defenses) is provided in a systematic way.

Third — and this bothers me more than I expected — the claim in the abstract that the approach "does not inherently feature a trade-off between model accuracy and data privacy" is stated very boldly but the empirical evidence supporting this is thin. Table results (I'm looking at Table 2 specifically) show accuracy numbers but the baseline comparisons aren't comprehensive enough to make this claim confidently.

I'm also not entirely sure the novelty framing holds up. The idea of key-based or transformation-based obfuscation at the output layer isn't entirely new in related security literature, and the paper's related work section doesn't engage deeply enough with adjacent work on output perturbation and one-way transformations.

There's genuine potential here and I think the core intuition is interesting. But in my experience reviewing security-flavored ML papers, the bar for "this defense works" needs to be much higher — you need adaptive adversaries, formal analysis or at least rigorous empirical red-teaming, and realistic datasets. As currently presented, I don't think this is ready for publication at a top venue. I'd encourage the authors to substantially expand the threat model analysis and experimental scope before resubmission.

This paper tackles the Schrödinger bridge problem by proposing a stochastic action minimization approach, framing the solution via a stochastic Lagrangian. The core idea is interesting enough — using variational principles from classical mechanics and extending them to the stochastic setting is a natural and somewhat elegant direction. But I have significant concerns about both the technical depth and the empirical evaluation that prevent me from recommending acceptance.

Let me start with what I think is the fundamental issue: the paper doesn't adequately situate itself relative to existing work. The connection between Schrödinger bridges and stochastic optimal control is well-established (see Léonard 2013, Chen et al. 2021, and the line of work by De Bortoli et al.), and the variational / action-minimization perspective isn't exactly novel territory. The authors claim in the abstract that this is a "viable alternative" but never convincingly demonstrate *why* it's better or even comparably good. Better at what, exactly? Computational cost? Sample quality? Convergence speed? I found myself re-reading sections 3 and 4 multiple times trying to extract a crisp technical contribution and came away unsatisfied.

The experiments are, frankly, thin. The paper shows results on what appear to be low-dimensional toy distributions and maybe one or two slightly more interesting cases, but there's no comparison against DSBM, I²SB, or other recent competitive baselines on standard benchmarks. For a paper to be published at a top venue in 2024, this just isn't enough. Table 1 (if I'm reading it correctly) reports some metric but the experimental setup is described so briefly I'm not confident I understand what's being measured.

On the technical side — I'm not entirely sure the treatment of the boundary conditions in equation (6) is correct, or at least it's presented in a way that glosses over some non-trivial measure-theoretic details. The claim that "all vital physical constraints" can be incorporated directly into the Lagrangian sounds appealing but feels hand-wavy without a proper theorem or at least a more rigorous proposition. Where is the convergence guarantee?

I think there's a kernel of a good idea here. The stochastic Lagrangian framing could potentially lead to cleaner training objectives, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to make the loss function more interpretable. That's a legitimate goal. But the paper reads like an early draft — the writing in Section 2 is quite rough, some notation is introduced without definition, and the overall narrative needs tightening considerably.

My recommendation is rejection. I'd encourage the authors to substantially expand the experimental section, properly prove (or at least carefully state) the theoretical guarantees, and give a much sharper account of how this differs from and improves upon the existing IPF and score-matching based approaches that currently dominate this space.

This paper proposes a "Batch Confusion Norm" (BCN) regularization to handle both fine-grained visual classification and long-tailed distributions simultaneously. The core idea is that confusion between classes — measured at the batch level — can serve as an adaptive signal to regularize training differently depending on whether inter-class similarity is the dominant challenge or class imbalance is.

I'll be honest: I found the motivation section somewhat hard to follow on first read. The abstract promises a unified treatment of FGVC and long-tailed recognition, which is genuinely an interesting problem setup, but after reading sections 3 and 4 twice I'm still not fully convinced the two problems are being addressed in a principled unified way versus just... both being addressed by the same loss term somewhat incidentally. The connection feels hand-wavy in places. Equation (3) especially — I think the notation could be clarified significantly. What exactly is being summed over, and how does the batch-wise nature of BCN interact with standard mini-batch SGD training dynamics? This matters for reproducibility.

The experimental results on CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, and iNaturalist2018 are solid and I don't doubt the numbers. Table 2 shows consistent improvements. But I have real concerns about the baselines chosen. Several of the comparisons are against methods that don't use the same backbone or the same resolution, and the paper doesn't always make this crystal clear. In my experience this is where FGVC papers can be misleading, and I'd want to see a more controlled comparison. The iNaturalist2018 results in Table 4 are competitive but not clearly state-of-the-art — the claims in the abstract are a bit stronger than what the tables actually show.

The ablation study (Table 5 I believe) is appreciated but feels thin. I want to understand what happens to performance as batch size changes, since BCN is explicitly batch-dependent. This seems like an obvious experiment that's missing. Does the method become unstable with small batches? That's a practical concern for anyone trying to use this.

Writing quality is inconsistent throughout. "Depart from the conventional approaches" in the abstract is grammatically off, and there are similar issues scattered through the paper that suggest it was not carefully proofread. This isn't a dealbreaker but it does affect readability.

I think the core idea here has merit — using confusion statistics as an adaptive regularizer is clever and I haven't seen it framed quite this way before. But the paper needs stronger theoretical grounding for why BCN behaves differently in the fine-grained vs. long-tailed regime, more careful experimental controls, and the missing batch size sensitivity analysis is a genuine gap. In its current form I lean toward rejection, but I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit. The idea is worth pursuing.

This paper tackles the challenging problem of inverse rendering for indoor scenes, specifically focusing on learning a prior over surface emission to better constrain the decomposition of lighting and materials from multi-view observations. The core idea — using a neural field parameterized by emitting direction and a local latent code in a voxel grid, trained as a prior and then fit at test time via differentiable path tracing — is reasonable and I think the motivation is sound.

That said, I have several concerns after reading this twice.

The comparison to baselines feels somewhat cherry-picked to me. Looking at Table 1, the quantitative gains are modest in several metrics, and I'd want to understand better whether the improvement comes from the learned prior itself or simply from the additional semantic supervision signal. The ablation in Section 4.3 partially addresses this but I don't think it's thorough enough — specifically, what happens if you give the baselines the same semantic information? That experiment seems conspicuously absent.

The claim about requiring "fewer views than state-of-the-art" is made prominently in the abstract but the experiments on this (Figure 6 I think?) only show results at a couple of data points. In my experience, a proper few-view analysis needs more systematic evaluation across a range of view counts, not just "5 views vs. full." This feels underbaked.

I'm also not entirely sure about the voxel grid resolution choice and how sensitive the method is to it. There's a brief mention of this hyperparameter but no sensitivity analysis. For a method that stores local latent codes in a grid, this seems like a fairly critical design decision.

The writing in Section 3.2 is dense and I found myself confused about the exact training procedure for the prior versus the test-time optimization. Specifically, the relationship between the training dataset and the local latent codes isn't spelled out clearly enough. I had to re-read this section several times and I'm still not 100% certain I understand the gradient flow correctly.

The qualitative results look reasonable — Figure 4 and 5 show plausible relighting — but I worry about generalization. The method was trained and evaluated on what appears to be fairly similar indoor scene distributions. How does it handle unusual lighting configurations like colored accent lights or very low-contrast scenes?

One more thing: the failure cases section is almost nonexistent. A single paragraph at the end of the experiments is not sufficient for a method with this many moving parts.

I want to be fair: this is a solid piece of work and the problem is genuinely hard. The idea of learning emission priors is useful and I think the paper makes a real contribution. But in its current form the experimental validation has gaps that need to be addressed before I'd feel comfortable accepting it. I'm leaning weak reject, with the possibility of changing my mind if the authors can address the few-view evaluation and the ablation concerns specifically.

This paper proposes using implicit neural representations for time series, specifically adapting SIREN architectures and wrapping them in a hypernetwork framework for generation tasks. The core idea is interesting and I think there's something genuinely novel here — INRs haven't been extensively explored for time series and the connection to spectral properties is a reasonable motivation.

That said, I have significant concerns about the execution and evaluation.

The iSIREN architecture (Section 3 I believe) modifies the standard SIREN with what amounts to fairly incremental changes. After reading this twice I'm still not entirely convinced the architectural novelty justifies a separate name. The authors should be more upfront about exactly what differentiates iSIREN from vanilla SIREN beyond the initialization scheme and the additional frequency terms. Figure 2 helps somewhat but the ablations are incomplete — we don't really get a clean story about which component is doing the heavy lifting.

The FFT-based loss is probably the most interesting contribution here. In my experience, getting generative models to match spectral distributions is genuinely hard and the approach is sensible. But the loss weighting (equation 6 or thereabouts) feels underexplored — how sensitive are results to this hyperparameter? There's basically no analysis of this, which is a glaring omission.

On evaluation: the fidelity metrics used are fine but the comparison feels cherry-picked. TimeGAN is included but some more recent baselines are missing — I'm thinking of approaches from 2022-2023 that have shown strong performance on exactly these benchmarks. The authors claim to "exceed state-of-the-art" but I'm not sure that claim holds up against the full landscape of current methods. Table 2 shows improvements that are sometimes marginal and the variance across runs isn't always reported consistently.

The iHyperTime interpretability story in the later sections is honestly underdeveloped. It reads like it was added late in the writing process. The "prior knowledge constraints" idea is interesting but the experiments don't really demonstrate this compellingly — we get one or two toy examples that don't make me confident this scales.

I found the writing generally clear but there's a density problem around Section 4 where a lot of notation gets introduced quickly. Page 7-8 in particular could use restructuring.

I'm genuinely uncertain about the multivariate results — the gains there look more compelling to me than the univariate case, but this receives less discussion than it deserves.

Overall this is a borderline paper. The core idea is interesting, the spectral loss contribution is real, and the INR framing is fresh for this domain. But the evaluation has gaps, some claims are overclaimed, and the interpretability section needs substantially more work. I'd want to see stronger baselines and more rigorous ablations before accepting. Leaning toward weak reject but could be convinced otherwise.

This paper tackles the problem of causal representation learning, specifically trying to identify latent causal models with instantaneous effects. The theoretical contribution centers on characterizing three "intrinsic properties" (transitivity, permutation indeterminacy, scaling indeterminacy) and arguing that transitivity is the key obstacle to identifiability. The proposed solution — conditioning causal coefficients and noise variances on an observed auxiliary variable — is then used to derive identifiability up to permutation and scaling.

I have mixed feelings about this work. The problem is genuinely important and I appreciate that the authors are trying to tackle instantaneous causal relations, which many prior works sidestep. But I found the theoretical development harder to follow than it needed to be, and I'm not fully convinced the contribution is as novel as claimed.

My main concern is the relationship to prior work on nonlinear ICA and related identifiability results (Khemakhem et al., iVAE; Hyvarinen & Morioka; Lachapelle et al.). The linear-Gaussian model with auxiliary-variable-modulated parameters feels quite close to iVAE-style assumptions, and the authors don't do enough work in my view to delineate what's genuinely new here versus what follows fairly directly from existing machinery. The "transitivity" framing in Section 3 is interesting but I found it somewhat vague — after reading it twice I'm still not sure this is a formal property in the same sense as the other two indeterminacies, or more of an informal observation.

The SAUVAE model (the acronym is a bit tortured, honestly) seems reasonable but the architecture choices in Section 5 are underspecified. How sensitive are results to the number of layers, the parameterization of the auxiliary-variable dependence, etc.? Table 2 shows improvements on real data but the margins are modest and I'd want to see confidence intervals — are these differences statistically significant?

On the theory side, Theorem 1 is the main result, but the proof sketch in the appendix relies on some steps I found hand-wavy around the injectivity of the mixing function. I might be wrong about this, but Assumption 4 feels quite strong in practice and I'd like to see a more explicit discussion of when it fails and what happens to identifiability in those cases.

The synthetic experiments are convincing enough. Figure 3 is clear. The real-data section on the biological dataset is less so — I'm not sure what ground truth we're comparing against and the evaluation metric feels circular.

Overall, I think this is a solid if not spectacular contribution. The problem framing is good, the experiments are adequate, but the novelty relative to prior work needs to be argued more carefully and the theoretical presentation needs tightening. I lean toward borderline reject but could be persuaded otherwise if the authors address the relationship to iVAE-style results and clarify the injectivity assumptions. Not a bad paper, just needs another pass.

This paper tackles coreference resolution by adding a context-aware attention mechanism on top of the Lee et al. end-to-end model. The core motivation — that identical surface-form mentions get similar representations and this causes errors — is reasonable and I've seen this issue discussed in the literature before. But I have significant concerns about both the technical contribution and the empirical evaluation.

Let me start with what bothers me most: the improvement over the baseline is modest (the paper reports 72.84% F1 on test vs. the Lee et al. 2018 baseline of around 73% — wait, actually after reading this twice I'm confused about which exact baseline they're comparing to). Table 2 needs to be clearer about this. The numbers are actually competitive but the paper doesn't do a great job situating itself in the timeline of published results. By the time this reaches publication, these scores may already be surpassed.

The attention mechanism described in Section 3 is... fine, I guess. It's not particularly novel. Applying attention to reweight contextual features is something that's been done extensively in NLP since 2015. I think the authors need to more forcefully argue why their specific instantiation is non-obvious. Equations 3-5 look like fairly standard additive attention — what's the actual innovation here beyond applying a known technique to this particular problem? I'm not trying to be harsh, but this needs a stronger justification.

I found the ablation study (Table 3) helpful but underdeveloped. The different attention variants are compared but there's not enough discussion of *why* one variant wins. More qualitative analysis would strengthen the paper considerably. The error analysis in Section 5 is too brief — give me actual examples where the model succeeds and fails.

The writing is generally clear though there are grammatical issues throughout ("the verbatim same mentions" in the abstract is awkward). Section 2 could be condensed significantly, it reads like a textbook summary.

One thing I genuinely liked: the motivation example they provide is concrete and makes the problem tangible for readers less familiar with coreference. That's good scientific communication.

My main verdict: the paper makes an incremental but potentially valid contribution. However, the experimental section needs strengthening — I'd want statistical significance tests, more analysis of what the attention is actually learning (attention visualization?), and a clearer story about why this specific attention formulation is the right one. In my experience, papers that just bolt attention onto existing systems without deeper analysis tend not to hold up well over time.

Borderline reject leaning toward weak accept if the authors can address the empirical concerns. The foundation is there but it needs more work.

This paper tackles an interesting and underappreciated problem — the fact that existing corruption benchmarks like ImageNet-C may be redundant in what they measure, leading to biased estimates of model robustness. The core idea of removing "overlapping" corruptions to improve benchmark coverage and balance is intuitive and I think genuinely useful for the community.

That said, I have some significant concerns about the technical execution.

The proposed overlap metric (Eq. 3-4, if I'm reading it correctly) essentially measures correlation between model rankings across corruptions. This is reasonable, but I'm not entirely sure this is the right operationalization of "overlap." Two corruptions could produce correlated robustness rankings simply because they share a common difficulty axis (e.g., both are very hard for all models), not because they're measuring the same underlying phenomenon. The authors don't really address this confound convincingly, and after reading this section twice I still felt the theoretical grounding was thin. I'd want to see a more careful justification of why rank correlation specifically is the right measure here rather than, say, something based on feature space similarity or corruption type taxonomy.

The experimental validation in Section 4 is where things get shakier. The set of models used to compute the overlap metric is relatively small — around 20-something models if I recall — and I worry quite a bit about whether the resulting benchmark is stable to this choice. What happens if you compute overlaps on a different set of models? Is ImageNet-NOC robust to that variation? This feels like a critical ablation that's missing. In my experience with benchmarking work, these kinds of choices end up mattering enormously in practice.

I do think Figure 3 is actually pretty compelling and probably the paper's strongest empirical contribution. The visualization showing that ImageNet-NOC covers corruption types that are systematically absent from ImageNet-C is convincing, and I found myself genuinely persuaded that current benchmarks are skewed toward certain corruption families.

The writing is generally clear but occasionally sloppy. There are places where the distinction between "coverage" and "balance" gets muddled — these are presented as related but distinct goals, and I think the paper would benefit from a crisper definition upfront rather than the somewhat circular definitions given around page 3.

One thing I found confusing: the relationship between the greedy algorithm and the theoretical properties claimed for the resulting benchmark. The algorithm is heuristic, fine, but what guarantees do we have about how far it is from optimal? Nothing is said about this.

Overall, I think this is a useful contribution with a clear motivation, but the methodology needs more rigorous justification and the stability of the proposed benchmark needs to be demonstrated more carefully. I'm leaning toward weak reject, but I could be convinced otherwise if the authors address the model set sensitivity issue and tighten the theoretical framing of the overlap metric. The idea is worth pursuing.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant question — does language supervision actually help, or are we just benefiting from better/more data? I've seen a lot of papers in this space make strong claims without doing the careful controlled analysis, so I appreciate the attempt here.

The core finding, that CLIP benefits most when captions are descriptive and low-variability, is intuitive in retrospect but I think genuinely useful to have documented carefully. The counterintuitive result (Section 4 or so) showing that captions can actually *hurt* in certain data regimes is, I think, the most interesting contribution of the paper, and honestly I wish the authors had leaned into this more aggressively rather than burying it a bit.

That said, I have real concerns. The notion of "caption variability" feels underspecified to me. How exactly is this operationalized? The metric used (I found the definition around equation 2 a bit slippery after reading it twice) needs to be more carefully justified. Why this measure and not alternatives? I'm not convinced the authors have fully ruled out confounds here — it's possible that "low variability" captions are also just higher quality captions in other ways that aren't being controlled for.

The interventions proposed in the latter half of the paper feel somewhat tacked on. They work, fine, but the connection back to the theoretical story is loose. I might be wrong but it reads like the authors found some tricks that help and then reverse-engineered an explanation. The ablations in Table 3 are helpful but don't fully convince me.

On the image-only vs. CLIP comparison — I want to make sure the compute/data budget comparisons are truly fair. In my experience, these comparisons are extremely sensitive to training details and it's easy to inadvertently favor one method. The appendix has some discussion of this but I don't think it's thorough enough, particularly around augmentation strategies which can interact strongly with the supervision signal.

The writing is generally clear, though the abstract oversells slightly — "carefully controlled comparison" is doing a lot of work and I'd push back on that framing given the concerns above.

Overall I think this is a solid empirical paper with a genuine contribution, but it needs more rigorous treatment of the variability metric and a more honest discussion of what the proposed interventions are actually doing. The scope of claims should probably be tightened. I'm leaning weak accept — the question is important and the experiments are largely well-executed — but I'd want the authors to address the confound issue seriously before I'd feel comfortable recommending acceptance. If that ends up being unresolvable I could see dropping to reject.

This paper proposes a density estimation approach using root Sobolev norm regularization, targeting anomaly detection as a downstream application. I'll be honest — I came into this review somewhat skeptical, because the area of kernel-based and regularized density estimation is quite mature, and the bar for novelty is high. After reading the paper twice, my feelings are mixed.

The core idea is reasonable: regularize the density estimate using a Sobolev norm, which gives a cleaner inductive bias than KDE. I appreciate that the authors are upfront that there's no closed-form kernel and that they resort to sampling-based approximation. That's an honest acknowledgment. The connection to Fisher divergence / score matching for cross-validation (since you can't use log-likelihood with unnormalized densities) is actually the most interesting technical contribution to me, and I wish the paper had spent more time here instead of burying it toward the end.

My main concern is the optimization story, which feels undercooked. The authors say "standard gradient methods do not perform well" and then introduce natural gradients with a specific initialization as a fix. But I found the justification for *why* this works theoretically quite thin. Pages 6-7 go through the mechanics but I came away not really understanding the landscape well enough to trust this isn't just empirical tinkering. Is there any convergence guarantee? Even an informal one? In my experience, when you're selling a method partly on its optimization behavior, you need to be much more rigorous here or at least provide more ablation.

The ADBench evaluation is fair — it's a reasonable benchmark — but ranking "second best among 15+ algorithms" sounds better than it is. What's first? By how much? Table 3 (I think it's Table 3, the main results table) doesn't make this easy to parse quickly. I'd want to see confidence intervals or statistical tests given how noisy anomaly detection benchmarks tend to be. Also, 15 algorithms isn't actually that many in this space.

I'm not entirely sure the "interpretable inductive bias" claim holds up under scrutiny. The paper claims this as an advantage over KDE, but the Sobolev smoothness assumption is not obviously more interpretable to a practitioner. This feels a bit like marketing.

The related work section is thin. There's a body of work on regularized density estimation and Stein discrepancy methods that I think deserves more engagement. The connection to Hyvarinen's score matching work is mentioned but somewhat superficially.

That said, the paper is clearly written, the experiments are real, and the problem framing is sensible. I don't think this is a bad paper. But as it stands, it reads more like a promising preliminary report than a finished contribution. The theoretical foundations need strengthening, particularly around optimization guarantees, and the empirical claims need more careful statistical treatment.

I'm leaning toward rejection in current form, but I think this is revisable. A major revision addressing the optimization theory and evaluation rigor could get there.

This paper tackles an interesting and somewhat provocative question — can a carefully optimized single tree match or beat ensemble methods? I appreciate the ambition here, and the core idea of reformulating tree training as differentiable optimization using a scaled sigmoid approximation is technically interesting. That said, I have some significant concerns.

The claim in the abstract that the optimized tree "outperforms random forests by an average of 2.03%" is stated very boldly, and after reading the experimental section twice, I'm not fully convinced this generalizes. Sixteen datasets is... okay, but the selection criteria aren't entirely clear to me. Were these chosen before or after method development? This matters a lot for the credibility of the comparison. I've seen too many papers where dataset selection quietly does a lot of work.

On the technical side, the scaled sigmoid approximation strategy (Section 3, equations around eq. 4-6 if I recall correctly) is reasonable but not particularly novel in isolation — similar relaxations appear in soft decision tree literature going back to Irsoy et al. and Jordan & Jacobs. The authors should be more explicit about what's genuinely new versus what's a recombination. The subtree polish strategy is the most interesting piece to me, and I wish it got more careful treatment. How sensitive is the final accuracy to when and how polishing is applied? I found Figure 3 helpful but it raises more questions than it answers.

My bigger concern is the comparison baseline. Random forests with default hyperparameters? Or tuned? Because in my experience, a properly tuned RF is quite hard to beat with a single model on tabular data, full stop. The paper needs to be clearer about this. Table 2 shows some impressive numbers but I want to know what happens when you give the RF the same computational budget for tuning that you give your tree.

The interpretability argument — which is presumably a key motivation — gets mentioned in the introduction and then basically dropped. If you're going to argue for single trees on interpretability grounds, show me that the resulting trees are actually interpretable. How deep are these oblique trees in practice? Oblique splits are notoriously hard for humans to read.

I'm also a bit skeptical of the numerical stability claims. The sequence of increasingly accurate approximations sounds reasonable in principle, but the paper doesn't provide enough ablation evidence that this is actually necessary versus just adding complexity.

I think there's a real paper here, but right now it oversells. The title is catchy but the answer to the paper's own question is "sometimes, on some datasets, under certain conditions" — which is fine! That's still interesting. Just say that. Tone down the claims, strengthen the baselines, and add the interpretability analysis that the motivation promises. Currently I lean toward rejection, but I'd genuinely like to see a revision.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant question: can deep tabular models benefit from transfer learning in the same way that vision and NLP models do? The medical diagnosis testbed is a reasonable choice, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to address a real gap in the literature. That said, I have some significant concerns.

The experimental setup feels narrow to me. The entire empirical story rests on a single medical domain (or closely related domains within it), and I think this severely limits the generalizability of the conclusions. The abstract claims "definitive advantage over gradient boosted decision tree methods" but after reading the results tables (Tables 2 and 3) carefully, the margins in several settings are not what I'd call definitive — they're real but modest, and the variance across seeds is sometimes uncomfortably large. That word "definitive" is doing a lot of heavy lifting here and I'd push back on it.

The pseudo-feature method is probably the most novel contribution, and honestly I wish the paper had leaned into this more. The idea of handling mismatched feature sets between upstream and downstream tasks is genuinely a tabular-specific problem that nobody has addressed cleanly. But the current treatment feels undercooked — the ablations in Section 4.3 are helpful but I'm not convinced the proposed approach is robust. What happens with very large feature set mismatches? The experiments seem to test relatively mild cases.

I'm also somewhat skeptical of the self-supervised pretraining comparisons. The authors compare against SCARF and similar approaches, but the hyperparameter tuning story isn't entirely clear to me. Self-supervised methods for tabular data are notoriously sensitive to corruption rates and masking strategies, and I'd want to see more careful analysis here rather than what reads like a somewhat surface-level comparison.

On the writing: the related work section is decent but I think the authors underengage with some relevant work on meta-learning for tabular data (e.g., the TabPFN line of work) which is at least spiritually related. I might be wrong that this is directly comparable, but at minimum a discussion seems warranted.

Figure 2 is genuinely informative and I think represents the paper at its best — showing the data efficiency curve for fine-tuned models vs. GBDTs is exactly the kind of analysis that makes the case for this approach. More of this, please.

My overall take: the paper is exploring a worthwhile direction and has some useful empirical findings, but the contribution feels incremental and the experimental validation is too narrow to support the broad claims being made. The pseudo-feature idea is interesting but needs more development. In its current form I think this is below the bar for acceptance, but not by a huge margin. A more focused version of this paper — perhaps specifically centered on the pseudo-feature problem with stronger experiments — could be quite good.

This paper proposes supervised and semi-supervised extensions of Diffusion Maps by treating labels as a second "view" and constructing a multiplicative interpolation between a data kernel and a label kernel. The core idea is reasonably clean and I appreciate that the authors ground it in multi-view learning theory. That said, I have some significant concerns.

The technical contribution, while presented as novel, feels incremental to me. Incorporating label information into kernel construction for diffusion-based methods has been explored before — the connection to kernel alignment and co-training frameworks is real but the authors handle it somewhat superficially. The multiplicative interpolation scheme (Eq. 6 or thereabouts) is the heart of the paper, but I found the justification for *multiplicative* rather than additive or convex combination to be underdeveloped. There's a brief argument about extracting "common structure" but I wasn't fully convinced after reading it twice. Why does multiplication specifically achieve this? The connection to multi-view CCA-type methods deserved more careful treatment.

The experiments are on standard benchmark datasets and the numbers look good. But I'm skeptical of the baselines chosen. UMAP, t-SNE, and standard DM appear, but I didn't see comparisons against SSDR methods that are more directly related — e.g., kernel-based semi-supervised approaches or more recent graph-based label propagation methods combined with dimensionality reduction. The claim in the abstract that SDM/SSDM outperform "existing unsupervised, supervised, and semi-supervised nonlinear dimension reduction methods" is too strong given the baseline selection.

Figure 3 is helpful and probably the clearest illustration of what's happening geometrically. I wish the authors had spent more time here and less on the theoretical motivation sections which, frankly, get a bit dense without paying off proportionally.

The semi-supervised case (SSDM) feels like an afterthought compared to SDM. The handling of missing labels seems like a practical but not particularly principled extension, and the ablation on label fraction (if there is one — I may have missed it) wasn't convincing enough to me that the method degrades gracefully.

One thing I'll give the authors credit for: the parameter sensitivity analysis is present, which many papers skip. Still, the hyperparameter α controlling the kernel interpolation seems quite sensitive based on Table 3, which is a practical concern they don't adequately address.

Minor stuff: the notation gets cluttered around Section 3.2, and there are a few places where the writing is awkward ("whose purpose is twofold" in the abstract is a bit clunky). Page 7 has a paragraph that repeats almost verbatim something from the introduction.

Overall I'm leaning toward rejection but it's not a clear-cut case. The idea is interesting enough that I'd encourage the authors to revise — specifically beef up the theoretical justification for the multiplicative scheme, expand the baselines, and give SSDM more careful treatment. With significant revision this could be a solid contribution.

This paper addresses an interesting and underexplored problem — how link prediction models behave under distribution shift. The framing is reasonable and the motivation is clear enough. That said, I have significant concerns about the depth of the contribution and the execution.

The core contribution, LPShift, is essentially a splitting strategy that uses structural properties (degree, triangle participation, etc.) to create train/test splits with different distributions. I think this is a sensible idea, but after reading the paper twice I'm left wondering: is this really enough for a full paper? The splitting heuristic itself is not particularly novel — stratified splitting based on node/edge properties has appeared in related work on graph OOD generalization, and the authors don't do enough to distinguish their approach from e.g. GOOD benchmark or the work by Gui et al. on graph OOD. The related work section feels thin here.

The empirical findings in Table 2 and Table 3 are interesting — I'll grant that. Seeing GNN4LP methods degrade substantially relative to simple heuristics like CN or AA under distribution shift is a genuinely useful observation. But I'm not convinced the authors fully explain *why* this happens. Section 5 gestures at structural feature analysis but it reads more like post-hoc description than mechanistic insight. The title promises "towards understanding" but the understanding delivered is fairly shallow. What is it specifically about the inductive bias of SEAL or NBFNet that makes them brittle here? The paper doesn't really answer this.

I'm also skeptical about the choice to restrict experiments to OGB datasets only. 16 splits sounds impressive but it's really just 4 datasets × 4 splitting criteria. More diverse datasets would strengthen the claims considerably. And the OGB datasets are already pretty well-studied — I'd want to see this on some more heterogeneous or temporally-structured graphs where distribution shift is even more natural.

Minor things: Figure 3 is hard to read at print size. The notation in Section 3 is somewhat heavy for what is ultimately a fairly straightforward splitting procedure. Equation 2 in particular could be simplified or just explained in prose.

I'm also not sure the "generalization techniques" baseline (Section 4.3) is set up fairly. Dropping in things like mixup or data augmentation without careful hyperparameter tuning for this specific shift type may be stacking the deck against those methods.

Overall: the problem is real and worth studying, the empirical observations are somewhat interesting, but the paper feels like it's reporting results without delivering the deeper understanding it promises. I think this needs more mechanistic analysis and a broader experimental scope before it's ready. Borderline reject for me, leaning toward reject unless the authors can substantially beef up Section 5 and address the novelty gap relative to existing OOD graph benchmarks.

This paper proposes COLLAB, a mixture-of-agents approach to controlled decoding for LLM alignment. The core idea is to dynamically switch between multiple pre-aligned LLMs at the token level during decoding, guided by a long-term utility metric. I've read through this twice and have mixed feelings.

The motivation is reasonable — single-model controlled decoding has obvious limitations, and leveraging multiple off-the-shelf aligned models is a pragmatic direction. The token-level switching mechanism is interesting and I appreciate that there's some theoretical backing. However, I think the paper oversells some of its contributions.

My biggest concern is computational cost. Switching between models at every token step presumably requires maintaining multiple model states simultaneously. The paper doesn't adequately address the inference-time overhead this introduces. How many agents are being used in practice? What's the latency increase over single-agent baselines? The abstract emphasizes avoiding the cost of RLHF retraining, but if your inference cost is 3-5x higher, that's not obviously a win. I found this aspect frustratingly underexplored throughout.

The theoretical analysis (Section 3 or 4, I believe) establishes optimality guarantees but under assumptions that feel quite strong. I might be wrong, but the gap between the theoretical setting and the empirical setup seems significant. What does "optimal performance" actually mean here given that you're constrained to a fixed pool of off-the-shelf models? The bound feels vacuous unless the pool is rich enough.

The 1.56x average reward improvement and 71.89% GPT-4 win-tie rate numbers are prominently featured, but these numbers depend entirely on which baselines you're comparing against and how the reward model is set up. In my experience, GPT-4 evaluation metrics can be gamed by stylistic differences rather than genuine quality improvements. I'd want to see more ablations on the composition of the agent pool — does COLLAB still win when all agents are similar? What's the marginal gain from each additional agent?

The empirical evaluation covers diverse tasks which I appreciate, but I found Table 2 (or whichever table has the main results) somewhat hard to parse. The variance across tasks is quite high and some individual task results look less impressive than the headline numbers suggest.

One genuine strength: the policy-switching mechanism framed as a sequential decision problem is clean and the connection to prior controlled decoding work (like ARGS and related methods) is handled reasonably well.

Overall I think this is an interesting idea with real potential, but the paper in its current form doesn't fully address the practical limitations. The computational overhead question alone would likely limit adoption. I'd be more enthusiastic if the authors provided a proper efficiency analysis and tighter ablations. Leaning toward weak reject, though I'd reconsider if these concerns are addressed.

This paper proposes a modification to the standard contrastive loss that incorporates cross-sample similarities into the learning objective — essentially moving from a binary positive/negative structure to a weighted similarity graph. The core idea is straightforward and honestly kind of obvious in retrospect, which is sometimes the sign of a good idea.

I think the paper is reasonably well-written and the motivation is clear. The framing in terms of "similarity graphs" is helpful. That said, I found myself wanting more theoretical grounding for *why* this particular formulation is the right way to encode cross-sample relationships. The authors essentially propose a soft-labeling scheme over the contrastive distribution, but there's a long history of this kind of thing (knowledge distillation, label smoothing, soft nearest-neighbor loss) that I feel is underacknowledged. The relationship to NNCLR and to methods like CLIP with soft targets or SigLIP deserves more explicit discussion.

The empirical results are the main selling point here, and they're genuinely interesting — especially the CC3M gains which are striking (17%+ over CLIP). I do wonder though whether some of this is just because the baseline CLIP at 3M samples is quite weak and small improvements in the objective disproportionately help in low-data regimes. The CC12M gains are much more modest (~0.6%), which makes me slightly skeptical about how this scales. I'd want to see results on larger datasets before getting too excited.

The ImageNet9 results (Table... I believe it's Table 3?) are actually the most interesting to me. If the method genuinely helps separate objects from attributes and backgrounds, that's a substantive and non-obvious benefit. But the evaluation feels a bit thin — three metrics on one dataset. I think the authors could have done more here to really nail down *why* this happens mechanistically.

A few specific concerns: the construction of the similarity graph using class labels vs. text captions is treated somewhat uniformly but I think these are actually quite different settings with different assumptions. I found the description in Section 3 a bit hard to follow the first time through, particularly around Equation 4 — I had to re-read it a couple times to understand the normalization. Also, the computational overhead of constructing these cross-sample similarity graphs is mentioned briefly but I'd like to see a more careful analysis. In my experience, methods that look great on paper sometimes have hidden costs that make them impractical.

Overall: I think this is a solid contribution. The idea is clean, the results are mostly convincing, and the writing is good. My main complaints are around depth of analysis and engagement with related work. I'm not entirely sure this clears the bar for a top venue without some additional experiments or theoretical analysis, but it's close. I'd lean toward weak accept, contingent on the authors addressing the scaling story more carefully and beefing up the related work discussion.

This paper proposes Chain of Images (CoI), a method that generates intermediate visual representations during reasoning to help LLMs solve problems that benefit from spatial/visual intuition. The core idea is genuinely interesting — humans do sketch things out when problem-solving, and there's something compelling about the hypothesis that visual intermediate representations could help.

That said, I have serious concerns about this work that I don't think can be resolved with minor revisions.

The central technical contribution, SyMLM, feels underspecified. After reading the relevant sections twice, I still don't have a clear picture of how exactly it generates images "strictly based on language instructions." Is this a fine-tuned model? A constrained decoder? The symbolic aspect is mentioned but the description of the architecture (Section 3, roughly) is vague to the point where I couldn't reproduce this. In my experience reviewing systems papers, this level of underspecification is a red flag.

The experimental setup also troubles me. The evaluation on geometry and chess tasks shows improvement over CoT baselines, but the baselines feel cherry-picked. There's no comparison to tool-augmented LLMs (e.g., methods that invoke a chess engine or geometry solver), which would be the obvious competitive approach. The gains look impressive in Table 2 but I suspect a well-prompted GPT-4 with access to standard tools would close this gap considerably. The "common sense" tasks feel somewhat tacked on and the results there are less convincing.

I'm also not entirely sure the authors have engaged with the relevant literature seriously enough. Work on visual program synthesis, Scratchpad reasoning, and multimodal chain-of-thought (Zhang et al. 2023) seems directly relevant but is either missing or mentioned only briefly. The framing of CoI as novel doesn't fully hold up given these prior works.

The claim in point 3 of the abstract — that images "help eliminate textual hallucination" — is a strong one that I don't think the experiments actually demonstrate. Showing improvement on a benchmark is not the same as demonstrating a hallucination reduction mechanism. This kind of overclaiming weakens the paper.

There are also presentation issues. Figure 3 is genuinely hard to read at print size, and some of the chess board visualizations in the supplementary look incorrect to me (I play chess casually, and a couple of the positions shown don't correspond to the described game states).

Look, the core idea here has real merit and I think there's a publishable paper somewhere in this work. Visual reasoning chains are worth studying. But right now this reads more like a workshop paper — the technical contribution needs to be much more precisely specified, the baselines need to be stronger, and the claims need to be calibrated to what the experiments actually show. I'd encourage the authors to resubmit after significant revision.

This paper tackles an interesting theoretical question about rectified flows — specifically, how many rectifications are needed to achieve straight trajectories and what the Wasserstein convergence rate looks like. These are genuinely important open questions, and I was initially excited to read this.

After reading this twice, I have mixed feelings. The two main contributions are (1) a Wasserstein error bound characterized by discretization steps and a "stronger" straightness measure, and (2) a proof that 2 rectifications suffice for Gaussian-to-general-target flows under mild regularity. Contribution (2) is the more interesting one to me, and I think it does provide theoretical backing for what Liu et al. (2023b) observed empirically. That said, I'm not entirely sure how tight the regularity assumptions are in practice — the "finite first moment" condition sounds mild, but the proof seems to quietly rely on additional smoothness that deserves more explicit discussion. The assumption on page 7 (Assumption 3.2 or thereabouts) feels underexplained. How restrictive is this really for, say, multimodal distributions with well-separated modes?

On contribution (1), the Wasserstein bound — I found the connection to the new straightness formulation somewhat circular. You're essentially defining a quantity that makes your bound look clean, but then the question becomes: can you actually bound *that* quantity independently? The paper does gesture at this but I don't feel it's fully resolved. In my experience, this kind of thing can hide the real difficulty.

The empirical section is fine but honestly feels a bit thin for a theory paper trying to validate its claims. The mixture of Gaussians experiments in Figure 3 are suggestive, but I'd want to see whether the Wasserstein distance actually scales with NFEs in the way the theory predicts — not just qualitative trajectory plots.

I think the writing is generally clear but gets sloppy in Section 4. Some of the proof sketches are hard to follow without flipping back and forth to the appendix constantly. Real reviewers shouldn't have to do this much reconstruction work. The related work section also undersells the connection to Albergo et al. and flow matching literature — these feel like important comparisons that are kind of brushed past.

Minor: the abstract says "first theoretical analysis of Wasserstein distance" for RF, but I'm not 100% sure this is accurate given recent concurrent work. The authors should be more careful with such claims or explicitly address concurrent submissions.

Overall: this is a solid theoretical contribution with a clear story, but it needs sharper treatment of its assumptions and a more convincing empirical validation. I'm leaning weak accept — the 2-rectifications result is genuinely useful for the community — but I want the authors to address the regularity assumption question directly in their response.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — combining Byzantine resilience with differential privacy in federated learning via a gradient compression scheme. The core idea of β-stochastic sign SGD is reasonable and I appreciate that the authors identify a genuine gap in the literature (no compressor simultaneously achieving both properties in a quantifiable way).

That said, I have some fairly significant concerns.

First, the theoretical contributions feel somewhat incremental. The jump from stochastic sign SGD (Jin et al. 2020) to the β variant is not enormous — you're essentially injecting calibrated noise into the sign computation. I think the authors need to do more work situating this against existing Byzantine-robust FL methods like Bulyan, Krum, or the coordinate-wise median approaches. The claim in the abstract that "no gradient compressors simultaneously achieve quantitative Byzantine resilience and privacy preservation" is strong, and I'm not entirely convinced the related work section adequately defends it. There are papers combining DP with robust aggregation that I'd want to see addressed more carefully.

The byproduct result about simple mean, trimmed mean, median, and majority vote being equivalent when clients report sign messages is actually the most interesting thing in the paper to me, and it feels buried. I found myself rereading Section 4 (or wherever this appears) thinking "wait, this is actually a pretty clean observation that deserves more prominence." It has real practical implications for system design.

On the experimental side — MNIST and CIFAR-10 in 2024 feel underwhelming. I understand this is theory-heavy work but the empirical validation is thin. The Byzantine attack models tested seem fairly standard (e.g., sign-flipping, Gaussian noise attacks), and I'd want to see evaluation against more sophisticated attacks. Also, the figures are honestly a bit hard to read — the color choices in what I believe is Figure 3 make the curves hard to distinguish.

The convergence analysis requires assumptions that I think deserve more scrutiny. The bounded gradient assumption and the specific form of data heterogeneity assumed — are these standard in the FL literature or are the authors cherry-picking conditions favorable to their method? I might be wrong but the proof of Theorem 2 seems to lean heavily on assumptions that may not hold in practice.

Mobile Byzantine faults are handled, which is good, but the partial participation analysis feels somewhat mechanical — I didn't see strong intuition for why the bounds behave as they do with varying participation rates.

Minor things: some notation is introduced late and the paper could use a tighter pass for English clarity in places. Equation numbering jumps around in a way I found mildly confusing.

Overall, I'm leaning toward weak reject. The paper has merit and addresses a real problem, but the contributions need sharper positioning, the experiments need strengthening, and some theoretical assumptions need better justification. I'd encourage the authors to revise and resubmit.

This paper tackles an interesting and important problem — comparing neural network robustness to human perception across a continuous range of corruption intensities rather than the discrete severity levels used in benchmarks like ImageNet-C. The core motivation is sound, and I appreciate that the authors went through the substantial effort of collecting data from 7,718 human participants. That's genuinely impressive and not something you see often.

That said, I have some real concerns about the paper.

The proposed metrics (described around Section 3) feel somewhat ad hoc to me. After reading this section twice, I'm still not fully convinced that the "human-aware" framing adds as much as claimed. The metrics essentially measure area under a performance-vs-corruption-intensity curve, which is... not new? I might be wrong, but I feel like there's prior work in psychophysics and even in ML robustness literature (Geirhos et al., Hendrycks et al.) that has touched on continuous degradation in ways the authors don't sufficiently distinguish themselves from. The novelty claim needs sharper justification.

The finding that "the gap between NN and human robustness is larger than previously known" (finding #2) is interesting but I found myself wanting more nuance here. Which corruptions? Which models? The aggregate framing in the abstract glosses over what I think is actually the most interesting part of the story. Figure 4 starts to get at this but feels rushed. The variance across corruption types seems enormous and I think that deserves more careful treatment rather than collapsing everything into a summary claim.

Finding #3 — that some corruptions have similar impact on humans and thus can be grouped for cost-effective evaluation — is honestly the most novel and practically useful contribution in my opinion, but it gets the least attention. This feels backwards. I'd love to see this developed more rigorously, maybe with a proper clustering analysis and validation.

On the experimental side: the model selection is reasonable, covering CNNs and ViTs with different training regimes. But I'm curious why certain recent robust models were excluded — was this a compute issue? The paper doesn't really justify the selection criteria clearly.

Minor issues: the writing in Section 2 is quite dense and could be trimmed. Some figures (particularly Figure 2) are hard to read at normal paper size. Page 7 has a few grammatical oddities that survived editing.

Overall, I think this is useful work with a genuine empirical contribution (the human data alone has value for the community), but the paper is trying to make too many claims and the theoretical framing around the metrics doesn't fully hold up. The toolbox/benchmark framing is a good angle and probably the strongest pitch. I'd encourage the authors to focus the narrative, be more precise about where their metrics add value over existing approaches, and give finding #3 the attention it deserves.

Leaning toward weak accept, but the revision needs to be substantive.

This paper tackles an interesting problem with DETR-style detectors — the fixed query count issue is a real limitation that I've thought about before, and I appreciate that the authors are taking it seriously. However, after reading this twice, I have significant concerns about both the technical contributions and the empirical evaluation.

The core idea of AFQS (dynamically selecting queries from encoder tokens) is intuitive, but I found the description in Section 3 surprisingly vague. How exactly are queries selected and pruned during inference? The algorithm description left me with more questions than answers. I'm not entirely sure the method handles edge cases well — what happens in extremely dense scenes where the encoder itself may not produce enough high-confidence tokens? This needs more analysis.

The "detecting ambiguity" problem is, frankly, oversold. The authors claim that combining one-to-one and one-to-many matching introduces ambiguity, but this has been discussed in prior work (e.g., Group DETR, Hybrid DETR) and the framing here feels like it exaggerates a known tradeoff rather than identifying a novel problem. I'd like to see a more rigorous definition of this "ambiguity" — right now it reads more like a motivation paragraph than a scientific claim.

The Location-Deduplication Decoder is the most interesting piece to me, though I found Figure 3 hard to parse on first reading. The idea of separating regression (via CA) from classification/deduplication (via SA) is clever, but I worry about the computational overhead. The paper doesn't give a thorough latency analysis — Table 2 shows AP numbers but where are the FLOPs and inference time comparisons? This matters enormously for a method that claims practical utility.

The PoCoo loss is fine but feels somewhat disconnected from the rest of the paper. Small object detection improvement is nice, but this reads like an add-on rather than a core contribution. In my experience, papers that pile on loss functions without strong ablations often obscure where the real gains are coming from. The ablation in Table 4 helps somewhat but doesn't fully convince me.

Results on COCO look competitive, but the abstract is literally cut off mid-sentence, which is a bad sign for the overall polish of the submission. That's a pretty basic oversight.

My bigger concern: the comparison baselines feel selectively chosen. Where is the comparison against DAB-DETR, Anchor DETR, or more recent efficient DETR variants? The gains over DINO across five backbones are promising, but DINO is not the only relevant baseline here.

Overall, I think there's a real paper hiding in here, but it needs tighter writing, more rigorous analysis, and a cleaner story. The fixed-query problem is worth solving — I just don't think this submission has fully solved it yet. I'm leaning toward rejection but would reconsider with major revisions.

This paper proposes adapting behavioral item embeddings into a pre-trained LLM (PaLM2) using a Q-Former style architecture with an added contrastive loss. The general motivation is reasonable — there's clearly a gap between collaborative filtering representations and language models, and bridging this is a worthwhile problem. That said, I have significant concerns about this submission in its current form.

Let me start with what I found genuinely interesting: the idea of treating item embeddings as a kind of "foreign language" that needs translation into the LLM's representational space is intuitive, and the contrastive loss addition to the Q-Former framework is at least a sensible architectural choice. I also appreciate that the authors are working with real-scale systems (PaLM2 is not a toy model).

However, the experimental evaluation is where this paper really falls apart for me. The baselines are weak and poorly motivated. Comparing against "behavioral embeddings directly as input to PaLM2" is not a rigorous baseline — where is the comparison to other alignment approaches? There's no comparison to something like LLaRA, or even the simpler prefix-tuning approaches that have been explored for item representation in language models. After reading the results section twice, I honestly couldn't tell whether the improvements shown in Table 2 are statistically significant or just noise. No variance numbers, no significance tests. This is a serious omission.

The ablation study (Section 4.3 if I'm reading correctly) is too thin. The contrastive loss is presented as a key contribution but the ablation doesn't convincingly isolate its effect from other design choices. I might be wrong, but the performance differences look quite small — we're talking about marginal gains that could plausibly be explained by hyperparameter sensitivity.

The writing also needs substantial work. The abstract is vague to the point of being unhelpful, and I found the methodology section genuinely hard to follow on first read. Equation notation is inconsistent throughout. Figure 1 is doing a lot of heavy lifting but is not explained clearly enough in the caption or surrounding text.

My bigger concern is novelty. Q-Former adapters for multimodal LLMs are well-established (BLIP-2, InstructBLIP, etc.), and applying this to recommendation embeddings, while practically useful, feels more like an engineering contribution than a research one. The paper needs a much stronger articulation of what is *technically* novel here beyond the application domain.

I think there's a real paper somewhere in this work — the problem is genuinely important and the approach is plausible — but in its current state this reads like an internal technical report rather than a polished research contribution. I'd encourage a major revision with stronger baselines, proper statistical analysis, and a clearer positioning against the existing multimodal alignment literature.

Recommendation: Reject (with encouragement to resubmit after significant revision).

This paper proposes BAM (Bayes with Adaptive Memory), a framework for Bayesian online learning in non-stationary environments that selectively retains or discards past observations. The core idea is reasonable and the motivation is clear enough — standard Bayesian updating struggles in non-stationary settings, and simple forgetting mechanisms don't exploit the structure of environments where states recur. I get it.

That said, I have significant concerns about the novelty here.
