After reading this twice, I'm not convinced the authors have sufficiently differentiated BAM from existing work on particle filters, Bayesian forgetting factors, and switching state-space models. The claim in the abstract that "BAM generalizes many popular Bayesian update rules" is doing a lot of heavy lifting — when I looked at the actual theoretical results, this generalization feels somewhat trivially constructed. If you parameterize the memory selection broadly enough, of course you recover existing methods as special cases. That's not the same as providing new insight.

The experiments are my bigger concern. The tasks feel somewhat toy-ish, and I'm not sure the comparisons are fair. Specifically, in the contextual bandit experiments (Section 4.2 if I'm reading the numbering right), the baselines don't seem to be tuned with the same care as BAM. How sensitive is BAM's performance to its own hyperparameters? I didn't see an ablation on this, which is a glaring omission. The method presumably has its own memory selection parameters that need tuning — if those require as much effort as tuning the forgetting factor in simpler methods, the practical advantage is less obvious than advertised.

I also found the presentation in Section 3 genuinely confusing. The notation gets quite heavy around equations (6)-(8) and I had to re-read the memory selection mechanism several times. I think there's a cleaner way to present this. The connection between the "adaptive memory" framing and the actual computational procedure isn't made explicit enough until much later in the paper.

I'll be honest — I think the intuition here is appealing and there might be something worth publishing, but the current draft reads like the authors found a nice unifying perspective and then didn't push hard enough on either the theory or the empirical side to make a compelling case. The scalability question is basically unaddressed. Real non-stationary environments are high-dimensional; how does the memory selection procedure scale? I suspect not well, but I might be wrong.

Minor point: Figure 3 is quite small and the color scheme doesn't reproduce well in grayscale.

In my experience, papers that claim to "generalize" existing methods need to really demonstrate that the generalization buys you something concrete — either theoretical guarantees that weren't available before, or substantially better empirical performance. Right now, BAM doesn't fully deliver on either front. I'm leaning toward rejection but would consider revising my opinion if the authors can provide stronger empirical evidence and address the scalability concerns directly.

This paper tackles the problem of transferability estimation, which I agree is an important and underexplored area. The core idea — using harder subsets of target data to estimate transferability more reliably — is intuitive and the paper presents it reasonably clearly. That said, I have a number of concerns that prevent me from being enthusiastic about acceptance.

The central claim is that HASTE improves existing transferability metrics by focusing on harder examples. I found this plausible on first read, but after going through the experiments more carefully (Tables 2 and 3), the improvements are often marginal or inconsistent across datasets. For instance, some HASTE-modified metrics barely outperform their base counterparts, and the variance across seeds isn't always reported in a way that lets me judge statistical significance. I'd want to see proper significance testing here — in my experience, these kinds of small Kendall-tau improvements can easily be noise.

The theoretical analysis in Section 4 is the part I spent the most time on, and honestly I'm not entirely sure what to make of it. The bounds relating HASTE to optimal log-likelihood and negative conditional entropy feel a bit loose and I'm not convinced they provide genuine insight into *why* hard subsets work. It reads more like post-hoc justification than a principled derivation. I might be wrong, but the assumptions needed for Proposition 1 seem quite strong and are never empirically validated — they're just asserted.

The two variants (class-agnostic vs class-specific) are a reasonable design choice, but I found the motivation in Section 3.2 hand-wavy. Why exactly does the class-specific version help in certain regimes? Figure 3 is supposed to illustrate this but I had to read the caption three times before I understood what was being shown. Better visualization would help a lot.

My bigger concern is novelty. Using hard or uncertain examples to diagnose model behavior is a well-established idea — curriculum learning, active learning, and even prior transferability work (LogME, LEEP, etc.) have touched on data difficulty in various ways. The paper doesn't do a thorough enough job distinguishing HASTE from simply "use the hard examples" in a more naive sense. What's the non-obvious contribution here beyond the implementation details?

The experiments do cover a decent range of architectures and datasets, which I appreciate. The ImageNet experiments are relevant and the inclusion of NLP tasks is a plus. But the comparison baselines feel slightly cherry-picked and I notice H-Score is missing from several tables without explanation.

One small thing — the writing is generally fine but Section 2 is too long and could be cut by half without losing anything.

Overall: interesting idea, but the theoretical contributions feel overstated and the empirical gains are modest. I lean towards rejection in the current form, but I think a revised version with tighter theory and stronger empirical validation could be a solid paper.

This paper proposes a training-free guidance approach for conditional generation using diffusion models, framed as a generalization of inpainting tasks. The core idea is to use annealed Langevin dynamics to sample from conditional distributions without requiring additional training. I've read this paper carefully and have mixed feelings about it.

On the positive side, the theoretical grounding is one of the stronger aspects here. The connection to annealed Langevin dynamics and the convergence arguments are reasonably well-developed, and I appreciate that the authors are upfront about what their method is actually doing rather than just presenting another heuristic. The breadth of applications is genuinely impressive — spanning images, protein structure, and sequence generation in a unified framework is non-trivial.

That said, I have significant concerns. The empirical improvements over baselines, while present, feel somewhat incremental in several of the comparisons. Looking at Table 2, the gains on image inpainting tasks are real but modest, and I'm not fully convinced the computational overhead is justified for those settings. The authors should be more explicit about runtime comparisons — this is conspicuously absent and matters a lot for a "training-free" method that relies on iterative Langevin steps.

The protein applications are more compelling to me, and honestly this is where the paper's contribution feels most meaningful. The multi-motif scaffolding results are interesting. But I found the description of how the conditioning is formulated for protein sequences (around Section 3.2 if I recall correctly) somewhat rushed and hard to follow. I had to read it twice and I'm still not entirely sure I understand how the amino acid mutagenesis task is set up formally. More care here would help.

I'm also a bit skeptical of the convergence claims in practice. Yes, the theory says annealed Langevin dynamics converges, but the gap between theory and practice for diffusion models is well-known. The conditions required for convergence — how sensitive are the results to the annealing schedule? The paper doesn't give much intuition here and the ablations feel thin.

One minor but real complaint: Figure 3 is quite small and the caption doesn't do enough work to make it self-contained. I found myself flipping back and forth more than I should have.

I might be wrong, but I think the framing of this as a strict improvement over "heuristics" is slightly oversold — the baselines like RePaint and related methods are heuristics, sure, but they work well in practice and the theoretical purity argument only matters if the empirical gains are consistent and large.

Overall this is interesting work with genuine contributions, especially for protein generation tasks. The theoretical angle is a real differentiator. But the paper needs stronger empirical validation, better runtime analysis, and clearer exposition in places before I'd feel confident recommending acceptance. Borderline for me right now, leaning weak accept if the authors can address the computational cost question seriously.

This paper tackles active localization — having an agent learn both where it is AND how to move to localize faster — which is a genuinely interesting problem. The core idea of combining a differentiable particle-filter-like belief representation with a learned policy trained via RL is sensible, and I appreciate that the authors ground their approach in classical filtering methods rather than treating this as a pure black-box learning problem.

That said, I have some fairly significant concerns.

The experimental evaluation feels thin to me. The 2D maze results (Section 4.1) are somewhat convincing, but honestly those environments are so idealized that I'm not sure what we're actually learning about the method's strengths. The Doom experiments are more interesting, but the baselines are weak — comparing against a random policy and a "passive" localization agent doesn't really tell us how much of the gain comes from the active policy versus the neural perceptual model. I'd really want to see a comparison against something like SLAM-based active localization or at minimum a stronger hand-crafted exploration policy. After reading this twice I still wasn't sure the ablation studies were comprehensive enough to isolate what's actually doing the work here.

The generalization result to Unreal (Table 3 I think, though the table numbering gets a bit confusing) is actually the most compelling part of the paper and I wish the authors had spent more time on it. That's the result that made me sit up. But it's almost treated as an afterthought.

On the technical side — the belief update described around Eq. 3-4 is reasonable but I'm not entirely sure the multiplicative interaction they describe is doing something fundamentally different from a standard Bayes filter update implemented in a neural network. The authors claim this is novel but I might be missing something. Could be I'm being unfair here.

The writing is generally clear but gets sloppy in places. Section 3.2 in particular felt rushed, and I had to re-read the description of the CNN observation model several times. Figure 2 is helpful but the caption doesn't adequately explain what we're looking at.

My bigger concern is about scalability and practical relevance. The maps are given to the agent — in real deployment you rarely have clean, accurate maps. The authors acknowledge this briefly but don't engage with it seriously. In my experience, this is where these methods tend to fall apart.

Overall, I think this is decent work that addresses a real problem, but it reads more like a workshop paper than a full conference contribution. The core technical contribution needs sharper articulation, the baselines need strengthening, and the analysis of failure cases is basically absent. I'm leaning toward rejection but I'd be willing to reconsider if the authors can significantly strengthen the experimental section and clarify the relationship to prior filtering-based approaches.

This paper attempts a fairly broad empirical evaluation of adversarial robustness across multiple SSL methods, which is a reasonable goal. I've been following the SSL robustness literature for a few years now and there's genuinely useful work to be done here — papers like Jiang et al. and the work on contrastive robustness have touched on pieces of this, but a systematic comparison is still somewhat lacking. So the motivation is fine.

That said, I have real concerns about the depth of the analysis. After reading through this twice, I'm left with the feeling that the paper is mostly a collection of observations without a coherent explanatory framework. The finding that "SSL models generally exhibit superior robustness" (abstract, and repeated throughout) is stated almost as a given, but I don't think the paper does enough work to explain *why* this might be the case. Is it the training objective? The absence of label information during pretraining? Feature geometry? The authors gesture at architectural choices in section 4 or so, but the analysis feels shallow. I might be wrong, but I think reviewers at this venue will expect more mechanistic insight, not just benchmark numbers.

The experimental setup also raises questions. Seven SSL models is a reasonable selection, but the choice of a single supervised baseline feels like a design choice that artificially flatters SSL. Supervised models trained with augmentation-heavy pipelines or AT can be quite robust — comparing against a vanilla supervised baseline is a bit of a strawman in 2024.

The segmentation and detection results (I think around Table 3-4?) are actually the most interesting part of the paper to me, because the robustness advantage shrinks considerably there. But this is kind of glossed over with a vague statement about tasks being "more complex." That's not an explanation, that's a restatement of the observation. I wanted to see transfer attack experiments, or at least some analysis of feature transferability under adversarial perturbation.

The writing is serviceable but uneven. Some sections read clearly, others are quite dense and hard to parse on first read — particularly the part discussing extended training durations, where the claims are hedged so heavily ("may offer slight improvements") that I'm not sure what the takeaway is supposed to be.

One more thing: the paper would benefit enormously from including certified robustness numbers alongside empirical attack results. Empirical robustness against PGD is well-known to be unreliable as a sole metric, and I'm surprised this wasn't addressed more carefully given how mature the robustness evaluation literature is.

Overall I think this is an okay empirical study but it reads more like a technical report than a conference paper. The contributions need sharpening, the analysis needs depth, and some of the experimental choices need better justification. I'm leaning toward rejection in the current form, but I could be convinced otherwise if the authors can clarify the mechanistic story and address the baseline concerns.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically important problem — how level sampling affects zero-shot generalization in deep RL — and I appreciate that the authors try to provide a theoretical grounding for what has largely been empirical observation in the UED literature. The mutual information framing is a nice conceptual contribution, and I found the argument connecting lower MI to reduced overfitting reasonably convincing, at least intuitively.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The empirical evaluation feels narrow to me. Most results are on ProcGen, and within ProcGen it seems like only a subset of games are really doing the heavy lifting in the comparisons. Table 2 shows a lot of variance, and I'm genuinely unsure whether the claimed "statistically significant improvements" hold up across the board or just in the headline numbers. The authors say improvements are statistically significant but I'd want to see this more carefully justified — which test, what correction for multiple comparisons, etc. After reading this twice I still couldn't find a complete answer to this in the main text.

The over-generalization concept is interesting and I think underexplored in the literature, so I give the authors credit for naming it and attempting to measure it. But the way it's operationalized feels a bit hand-wavy. How sensitive are the conclusions to the choice of distribution of interest? In practice, you rarely know this distribution precisely, which somewhat undermines the practical appeal of SSED.

The VAE-based level generation in SSED is reasonable but not particularly novel on its own — using VAEs for environment generation has been done before (Risi & Togelius, various procedural content generation papers). The novelty here is really the motivation and framing, not the technical machinery, and I think the paper should be more upfront about this rather than presenting SSED as if it were a significant architectural innovation.

I'm also a bit skeptical of the MI estimation. Estimating mutual information between an agent's representation and a discrete set of levels is notoriously tricky. The authors use MINE or a similar approach if I recall correctly — but the reliability of these estimates, especially when the number of levels is large, is questionable. I might be wrong about the specific estimator used (the paper is a bit unclear on this around Section 4), but this is something that needs more careful treatment.

The writing is generally clear but Section 5 in particular felt rushed, like the authors ran out of space and crammed in the SSED description. Figure 3 is also hard to read at print size.

Overall, I think the paper has a genuine contribution — the MI perspective on overfitting vs over-generalization is a useful lens — but the empirical validation needs strengthening and some of the technical claims need more rigor. I'm leaning toward weak reject but could be convinced to change my assessment if the authors address the evaluation breadth and the MI estimation concerns directly.

This paper proposes QuantEase, a coordinate descent-based post-training quantization method for LLMs, with an outlier-aware variant. I've spent a fair amount of time with this one and have mixed feelings.

The core idea is reasonably well-motivated — framing layer-wise quantization as a discrete non-convex optimization and applying CD updates is sensible, and I appreciate that the method avoids matrix inversions (which have been a practical headache with GPTQ-style approaches). The update rules are clean. That said, I'm not sure the technical novelty here is as substantial as the authors imply. Coordinate descent on quantization objectives isn't exactly new territory, and the connection to existing work like GPTQ and SpQR could be made more explicit in terms of what's *fundamentally* different versus what's an engineering improvement.

The empirical results look strong — Table 2 and Table 3 show consistent gains over GPTQ across model families, and the sub-3-bit results in Section 5.3 are genuinely impressive if they hold up. The 15% relative perplexity improvement claim in the abstract is attention-grabbing, though I found it a bit hard to pin down exactly which comparison this refers to on first reading. After going back through the paper, I think it's the W2 results on OPT models? The authors should make this clearer upfront rather than burying the lede.

My bigger concern is convergence. The paper claims CD converges well in practice, but the theoretical analysis in Section 4 is pretty thin. I get that this is a hard discrete optimization problem and you can't expect strong guarantees, but at minimum I'd want to see ablations on number of CD iterations vs. quality, and some discussion of sensitivity to initialization. How often does it get stuck in bad local minima? In my experience with similar discrete optimization schemes, this matters a lot and the paper is almost silent on it.

The outlier selection strategy also feels underspecified to me. How are outliers identified? Is it just magnitude thresholding? The description around equation (8) could be clearer. I might be wrong but I think this is essentially similar to what SpQR does, and the claimed 2x improvement over SpQR deserves more careful analysis — are the outlier budgets being compared fairly?

Wall-clock timing comparisons are largely missing. The authors mention CD is efficient but Table 4 only gives partial timing info. For practitioners deciding between methods, this matters enormously.

Overall, I think this is a solid empirical contribution with some methodological interest, but the theoretical depth is lacking and several design choices need better justification. The writing is generally clear though occasionally dense around the optimization formulations. I'd lean toward weak accept — the results are real and useful — but the authors have some work to do on the revision.

This paper proposes using an RNN to model the temporal evolution of probability distributions for time series forecasting, discretizing the output space via softmax and treating the problem as classification. I've read through this twice and have mixed feelings.

The core idea is reasonable and I can see the appeal — framing density estimation as classification over discretized bins is a known trick (it reminds me of WaveNet and similar work), but applying it systematically to probabilistic time series forecasting with an explicit focus on the distributional dynamics is a decent contribution. The smoothness regularization (both explicit via a penalty term and implicit through the bin discretization) is a sensible addition.

That said, I have several concerns. The experimental section is where this paper really struggles for me. Five datasets total, three of which are synthetic — that's not enough to convince me this generalizes. The real-world experiments feel thin. I kept looking for ablations that would clearly isolate what's doing the work: is it the RNN architecture, the distributional output, the smoothness regularization, or some combination? Table 2 gives aggregate numbers but I couldn't trace back clearly which design choices matter most. The baselines are also somewhat dated — there's no comparison to recent transformer-based probabilistic forecasters or even something like DeepAR, which feels like a significant omission in 2024.

The Monte Carlo procedure for multi-step forecasting described in Section 4 (I think around page 6-7?) is described fairly briefly and I'm not convinced the authors have fully accounted for error accumulation over longer horizons. How does performance degrade as the forecast horizon increases? I didn't see a systematic analysis of this, which is actually the most interesting regime for probabilistic forecasting.

On the technical side — the softmax discretization requires choosing the number of bins, and I found the discussion of this hyperparameter sensitivity pretty unsatisfying. Figure 3 shows some sensitivity analysis but the range tested feels narrow. In my experience this kind of discretization can be quite brittle when the distribution has heavy tails or the support shifts over time.

The writing is generally clear but there are places where notation gets sloppy. Around equation 5 I had to re-read several times to understand what the authors meant by the "operator" formulation. A cleaner definition upfront would help.

I might be wrong, but I also think the claim in the abstract that this shows "advantage over compared baselines" is overstated given the narrow experimental scope. Some results in Table 3 are marginal improvements within what I'd guess is noise.

Overall, I think the idea has merit and the paper is on the right track, but it needs stronger empirical validation, more modern baselines, and a cleaner theoretical exposition of what's actually novel about the operator learning framing versus prior distribution regression work. Not ready for acceptance in current form.

This paper presents WILDS 2.0, an extension of the existing WILDS benchmark to include curated unlabeled data across 8 datasets. The motivation is clear and I think the community genuinely needs this kind of resource — the gap between what distribution shift papers claim and what they actually evaluate has been frustrating to watch grow over the past few years.

That said, I have mixed feelings about this submission. Let me explain.

The core contribution is essentially a data curation effort plus a systematic evaluation. I don't want to dismiss that — good benchmarks are incredibly valuable and unglamorous work. But I found myself wanting more from the analysis. The finding that "success on WILDS is limited" is stated almost matter-of-factly, but I think this deserves much deeper investigation. Why are these methods failing? Is it the nature of the shifts (which vary enormously across datasets), the scale of unlabeled data, or something more fundamental about the methods themselves? The paper gestures at some explanations but doesn't commit to strong claims. After reading this twice, I still don't have a clear intuition for what practitioners should actually do with these results.

The dataset curation decisions are described in Section 3 and I appreciate the detail, but I'm not entirely sure the unlabeled data is always "realistic" in the way the authors claim. For iWildCam specifically, the argument for why the unlabeled distribution is deployment-realistic feels a bit thin to me. I might be wrong — I'm not a wildlife monitoring expert — but this deserves more justification.

Table 2 and Table 3 are dense and hard to parse. I understand there's a lot of information to convey but I found myself losing track of which improvements were meaningful versus noise. Some discussion of effect sizes or statistical significance would help enormously here. In my experience, benchmark papers that don't address variance across runs end up being cited in misleading ways later.

The self-supervised methods section (Section 5.3 I think) was the most interesting to me, and honestly I wanted more of this. The result that SSL pretraining helps in some settings but not others is genuinely interesting and I felt the paper moved past it too quickly.

One more thing — the related work situates this well against prior domain adaptation benchmarks, but I think the connection to test-time adaptation literature is underexplored. Methods like TTT or TENT seem highly relevant and their absence from the evaluation feels like a gap.

Overall: this is solid, useful work. The benchmark itself will be a real contribution to the community. But the paper reads more like a technical report than a research paper with a clear thesis, and I think it needs either sharper empirical findings or more theoretical framing to fully justify conference publication. I'm leaning toward acceptance given the practical value, but the authors need to do more work explaining *why* current methods fail, not just *that* they fail.

This paper proposes using latent diffusion models to encode trajectory segments as "latent skills" for offline RL, then learning a Q-function in this compressed latent space. The core intuition — that temporally abstract representations might help with credit assignment and reward propagation — is reasonable, and the empirical results on D4RL look competitive.

That said, I have significant concerns about both the novelty framing and the experimental validation.

On novelty: the combination of latent diffusion with offline RL is interesting, but the paper oversells how novel this is. Temporal abstraction via learned skills for RL has a long history (options framework, HIRO, SPiRL, etc.), and encoding trajectories with diffusion models has appeared in several concurrent works. The authors don't do nearly enough to differentiate from SPiRL and related skill-learning approaches. I kept waiting for a crisp "here is what we do fundamentally differently" and never got a fully satisfying answer. The batch-constraining via latent space is presented as a key contribution but I found the argument somewhat circular — you avoid extrapolation by only querying latents that were trained on in-distribution trajectories, but this is basically just saying the encoder hasn't seen OOD data. That's a property of the architecture, not a principled constraint mechanism.

The empirical results in Table 1 and 2 look good overall, especially on the maze and sparse reward tasks, which is where I'd expect this method to shine given the temporal abstraction argument. But I'm troubled by a few things. First, the ablations (Section 5.3 I think) feel thin — it's not entirely clear to me whether the gains come from the diffusion model specifically vs. just using any reasonably expressive sequence encoder. Did the authors try a transformer-based encoder as a baseline? That comparison is conspicuously absent. Second, the variance across seeds isn't always reported consistently across tables, which makes direct comparisons hard to trust.

The explanation of the Q-learning objective in Section 3.2 was genuinely confusing to me. After reading it twice, I'm still not 100% sure how the temporal abstraction affects the Bellman backup — specifically, how do you handle variable-length skill segments when computing target values? Figure 2 helps a bit but the math in equations (4) and (5) needs more careful exposition.

I'll also say — the claim of "state-of-the-art" in the abstract is a bit aggressive given that comparisons to very recent methods (post-2023) seem incomplete.

In my experience, papers in this space live or die by whether the representation is actually doing useful work or whether the improvement is just from better hyperparameter tuning / model capacity. I'm not yet convinced the authors have ruled out the latter.

Overall I think there's a real idea here worth pursuing, and the empirical results are promising enough that I don't want to dismiss it. But the paper needs a stronger ablation story and a clearer mechanistic argument for why latent diffusion specifically is the right choice. Borderline — leaning toward weak reject but could be swayed.

This paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem — pruning at initialization — and attempts to provide theoretical grounding for why existing methods sometimes fail catastrophically (e.g., entire layers getting pruned). I appreciate the motivation, and the problem of layer collapse is real and underappreciated in the literature.

That said, I have significant concerns about this submission.

The theoretical contributions, while presented as "comprehensive," feel somewhat incremental to me. After reading sections 3 and 4 twice, I'm not entirely convinced the theoretical results here go substantially beyond what could be derived from fairly straightforward applications of existing concentration inequalities and gradient flow analysis. The authors claim novel principled approaches, but the connection between the theory in Section 3 and the actual algorithmic proposals feels tenuous. I kept asking: does Theorem 2 actually *motivate* the proposed fix, or is the fix somewhat obvious and the theorem retrofitted around it? I think it's closer to the latter, though I might be wrong.

The experimental section is where things get shakier. The baselines are not comprehensive — notably, comparisons to SNIP (Lee et al. 2018) are present, but GraSP and SynFlow are either missing or only superficially addressed. Given that SynFlow was specifically designed to address the layer-collapse problem the authors are focused on, this omission is pretty glaring. The authors need to directly compare against SynFlow across all their benchmarks, not just mention it in passing.

Table 2 shows improvements on CIFAR but the gains on ImageNet are modest and I'd want to see statistical significance reported. In my experience, variance across seeds for these pruning experiments can be large enough to swallow the differences being claimed.

I found Figure 3 genuinely useful — it visually demonstrates the layer collapse issue clearly, and this is probably the most compelling piece of evidence in the paper. More of this kind of analysis would strengthen the work.

The writing needs work. The introduction is too long and the related work section reads like a laundry list. Page 6 in particular is dense to the point of being hard to follow — the notation around equation (4) is introduced too quickly.

One more thing: the claim in the abstract that this is a "comprehensive theoretical analysis" is overselling it. It's a partial analysis of specific pruning criteria under specific assumptions. Calling it comprehensive invites scrutiny the paper can't fully withstand.

Overall, I think there's a solid paper somewhere in here. The problem is real, the layer-collapse framing is useful, and some of the theoretical intuitions are interesting. But in its current form the experimental comparisons are incomplete, the theory-to-algorithm link is weak, and the writing needs tightening. I lean toward rejection but would genuinely reconsider with a strong revision addressing the SynFlow comparison and cleaner theoretical narrative.

This paper tackles an interesting and timely problem — using ML to quantify stylistic similarity for copyright infringement detection in AI-generated art. The legal framing (plaintiff vs. defendant artwork sets) is a nice touch and distinguishes it somewhat from generic image similarity work.

That said, I have significant concerns about the technical depth and novelty here. Fine-tuning ResNet and using softmax probabilities as "similarity scores" is... pretty much just standard image classification. I've been reviewing ML papers for over a decade and I struggle to see what the methodological contribution actually is beyond applying off-the-shelf tools to a new domain. The abstract even admits it's "straightforward" and "simple" — which I appreciate for honesty, but raises the question of whether this clears the bar for a research venue as opposed to, say, a technical report or industry blog post.

The Mickey Mouse case study is the only empirical evaluation, which is a real weakness. I read the experimental section twice and kept waiting for additional test cases. Mickey Mouse is iconic and visually distinctive, which probably makes this an easier-than-average case. How does this approach hold up for artists with more subtle, less visually codifiable styles? Think someone like Monet versus, say, a contemporary digital illustrator. The choice of a single, maximally recognizable character as the sole case study feels like cherry-picking, even if unintentionally so.

I'm also troubled by the legal grounding. The paper invokes copyright law framing but the connection feels superficial. The "total concept and feel" test in Arnstein v. Porter, or the more recent discussions around Andersen v. Stability AI, involve nuances that a softmax probability simply cannot capture. I might be wrong, but I think the paper oversells the legal alignment without engaging seriously with what courts actually examine. A collaboration with legal scholars would strengthen this considerably.

On the interpretability side — the saliency maps are a nice addition but Figure 4 (if I recall correctly) shows fairly generic activation patterns that don't obviously correspond to legally meaningful stylistic features. What exactly is the model keying in on? Ears? Color palette? The paper doesn't really interrogate this.

The hyperparameter tuning section is thorough, I'll give it that. The robustness analysis with distractor classes is useful and shows some genuine thought about real-world deployment.

Bottom line: the problem framing is valuable, the writing is clear, and I appreciate that the authors are honest about the simplicity of their approach. But the single case study, shallow legal engagement, and lack of comparison to existing similarity metrics (SSIM, perceptual hashing, CLIP embeddings — why not compare?) make this feel premature for publication in its current form. I'd encourage a substantial revision with broader evaluation and more rigorous engagement with both the legal and technical literature.

This paper proposes a framework for improving saliency maps by generating "decoy" samples through perturbations that preserve intermediate representations, then aggregating the resulting saliency maps. The core idea is interesting and I think there's something genuinely useful here, but I have significant concerns about the execution and evaluation that I need to see addressed.

Let me start with what I found compelling. The observation that gradient saturation and inter-feature dependence are underaddressed in existing aggregation schemes is fair, and the theoretical framing around robustness to adversarial perturbations on saliency (not the classifier itself — an important distinction) is a reasonable contribution. The connection to SmoothGrad is obvious and the authors should be more upfront about this relationship earlier in the paper rather than burying the comparison.

Here's my main problem: the perturbation mechanism described in Section 3 (around equations 4-6 if I'm counting correctly) is not clearly specified in terms of how you actually *find* perturbations that preserve intermediate representations. The authors wave at this being solved via an optimization procedure but the practical implementation details are vague enough that I genuinely couldn't reproduce this from the paper alone. Is this a gradient-based search? What layer counts as "intermediate"? The choice seems to matter enormously and I found no sensitivity analysis. After reading this section twice I still wasn't confident I understood the procedure.

The experimental evaluation in Table 2 uses pointing game and insertion/deletion metrics — fine choices — but the baselines feel cherry-picked. Where is Integrated Gradients with the full Sundararajan et al. setup? Where are the SHAP-based comparisons on the image tasks? I'm not saying the method doesn't work, but the strongest recent competitors don't seem to be included, and that makes it hard to trust the claimed outperformance.

Figure 3 is supposed to be the qualitative showcase and honestly... it's underwhelming. The differences between the proposed method and SmoothGrad are subtle at best on most examples. The authors claim superiority but I'd struggle to defend that claim visually. Maybe I'm missing something.

The theoretical results in Section 4 are the strongest part of the paper. The proof that the aggregated map is robust to a specific class of adversarial saliency attacks is clean and I think correct (I checked Proposition 2 carefully). But the connection between this theoretical guarantee and the empirical results is never made explicit — do any of the benchmark tasks actually involve adversarial manipulation of saliency? I don't think they do, which makes the theory feel disconnected from the experiments.

I might be wrong about the reproducibility concern if the appendix has more detail than I'm giving it credit for — but if so, that material needs to come forward into the main paper.

Overall: interesting idea, theory is solid, empirical evaluation needs significant work. I'm leaning reject but would genuinely reconsider with stronger baselines and clearer implementation details.

This paper investigates whether pretrained models are inherently better active learners than their randomly initialized counterparts, arguing that the ability to select informative/disambiguating examples is an "emergent property" of pretraining. I've read through this twice and have mixed feelings.

The core empirical finding — that uncertainty sampling with pretrained models achieves comparable accuracy with up to 6x fewer labels — is interesting, though I'm somewhat skeptical of how robust this claim is across the board. The 6x figure appears to be a best-case scenario cherry-picked from specific datasets (if I'm reading Table 2 correctly), and the average improvement is considerably more modest. I wish the authors were more upfront about this in the abstract.

The framing around "task ambiguity" and "safe deployment" feels a bit oversold to me. The connection between active learning efficiency and safety is asserted rather than demonstrated. I found this framing confusing — the paper is really about standard active learning with pretrained features, dressed up in a safety narrative. I don't think that narrative adds much and it muddies the contribution.

That said, the analysis of *why* pretrained models select better examples is genuinely the most interesting part of the paper. The observation that uncertainty sampling preferentially selects minority group examples and spurious-feature-decorrelated examples (Section 4, I believe) is a nice mechanistic story. I'd like to see this quantified more rigorously though. The current analysis feels somewhat qualitative.

My bigger concern is novelty. The idea that pretrained representations lead to better active learning is... not new? There's a substantial body of work on active learning with pretrained features, including contrastive learning papers that make similar observations. The authors cite some of this but I don't think they adequately distinguish their contribution. What exactly is new here beyond a more systematic empirical study? I might be wrong, but I feel like the "emergent property" framing is doing a lot of rhetorical work to dress up what is essentially a thorough ablation study.

The experiments are reasonably comprehensive across image and text domains, which I appreciate. But the unpretrained baseline comparisons feel somewhat unfair in places — are we controlling for model capacity properly? A randomly initialized ViT is quite different from a randomly initialized ResNet-18 in terms of optimization dynamics, and I wasn't convinced the comparisons are apples-to-apples throughout.

Figure 3 is helpful but honestly a bit busy. I had to stare at it for a while.

Overall: the paper has real empirical content and the core finding is plausible and useful to the community. But the theoretical framing is weak, the novelty claims are overstated, and the safety motivation feels tacked on. I'd lean toward weak reject in current form, but I think with a more honest framing of contributions and stronger analysis of the mechanistic claims, this could be a solid paper.

This paper proposes replacing the stochastic encoder in VAEs with a deterministic autoencoder plus explicit regularization, followed by an ex-post density estimation step. The core observation — that sampling from a Gaussian encoder is equivalent to injecting noise into the decoder — is clean and I think genuinely useful as a conceptual reframing, even if it's not entirely surprising in retrospect.

Let me start with what I liked. The paper is generally well-written and the empirical study is reasonably thorough. The ex-post density estimation idea (fitting a flow or GMM in the latent space after training) is practical and I can see this being adopted independently of the rest of the framework. The molecule generation experiments are a nice touch — too many generative modeling papers stay exclusively in image land. Figure 3 is informative and I appreciated the visualization of latent spaces under different regularization schemes.

That said, I have real concerns. The theoretical justification for *why* this should work as well as VAEs is thin. The authors essentially say "regularization + ex-post estimation ≈ VAE," but this is hand-wavy. The ELBO has a specific statistical interpretation, and I'm not convinced that swapping in dropout or spectral normalization gives you anything analogous in a principled sense. Section 3 gestures at this but doesn't deliver. I found myself reading it twice and still feeling unsatisfied.

The comparison to existing "regularized autoencoder" literature is also incomplete. WAE/MMD (Tolstikhin et al.), RAE (Ghosh et al. 2019 — which feels like a very close predecessor), and AAE are mentioned but I don't think the experimental comparison is thorough enough to establish clear superiority. Specifically, Table 2 shows mixed results on FID scores, and the authors' framing of these as "comparable or better" is somewhat optimistic. On CelebA the improvements are marginal and within what I'd expect from hyperparameter tuning variance.

I'm also a bit skeptical about the ex-post density estimation step being as straightforward as presented. In practice, if your latent space has poor structure, fitting a normalizing flow on top won't save you — and the paper doesn't discuss failure modes here. What happens when the regularization is insufficient?

Minor things: the notation in Section 4 gets cluttered around equations 6-8, and I had to re-read several times. Page 6 has a paragraph that reads like it was written at 2am and never edited — the one starting "We note that in practice..."

Overall, I think this is a solid empirical contribution with a useful practical takeaway, but it's oversold theoretically. The paper would be stronger if it dropped some of the theoretical framing and positioned itself more honestly as an empirical study of regularization strategies for autoencoders with a practical generative modeling recipe. As it stands, borderline accept — leaning toward accept if the authors can sharpen the theoretical claims or be more honest about their limitations.

This paper proposes a game-theoretic framework for LLM decoding that models the process as a multistage Bayesian game to improve consistency and reliability. The core idea — using game theory to adjudicate between "Valid" and "Specious" outputs — is genuinely interesting, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to address something real and important. That said, I have significant concerns about the execution and presentation.

Let me start with the theoretical framing. The Bayesian decoding game formulation (Section 3, around equations 4-7 if I'm reading the numbering right) is presented with a level of formality that doesn't quite match the actual implementation. I found myself reading through the equilibrium analysis twice and still unclear on whether the convergence guarantees are tight or just asymptotic hand-waving. What are the actual assumptions required for the Nash equilibrium to be reached in practice? This matters a lot because real decoding runs a finite number of steps, and the gap between theory and practice here feels papered over.

The headline result — LLaMA 13B at 78.1 vs PaLM 540B at 76.6 — is prominently featured in the abstract and I think it's being oversold. These numbers are on TruthfulQA if I'm not mistaken, and in my experience that benchmark is notoriously sensitive to prompt formatting and evaluation methodology. The comparison is also somewhat apples-to-oranges given the different training data, RLHF procedures, etc. I'm not saying the result is wrong, just that the framing feels a bit breathless.

The ablation studies in Table 3 are appreciated but feel incomplete. What happens when you vary the number of game rounds? Is the method sensitive to the prior initialization? These feel like obvious things to test and their absence is conspicuous.

I'm also puzzled by the "Ambiguity Calibration" component. The motivation is clear enough, but I found the connection to calibration in the traditional sense (ECE, reliability diagrams, etc.) quite loose. The authors use the word "calibration" in a way that doesn't really align with how the broader community uses it. This either needs to be more carefully distinguished or the methodology needs to be tightened up.

On the positive side — and I do want to be fair here — the writing is generally clear, the problem motivation is solid, and the idea of integrating multiple LLM strategies through a game mechanism is creative. Figure 2 does a reasonable job illustrating the framework intuitively.

My honest assessment: this is a paper with a genuinely interesting core idea that needs more rigorous theoretical grounding and more careful empirical validation. The current version reads somewhat like the authors got excited by a promising result and submitted before fully stress-testing the framework. I'd encourage a major revision focusing on tightening the theory and expanding the ablations. Borderline reject leaning toward rejection in current form, but I could be persuaded otherwise.

This paper proposes a flow-based approach for learning optimal transport maps between two empirically observed distributions P and Q. The core idea is to parameterize the transport as a continuous flow and minimize a transport cost objective. The applications to MI estimation, energy-based models, and image translation are reasonable demonstrations.

Let me be direct about my concerns. The technical novelty here feels incremental to me. The connection between normalizing flows and dynamic OT (via Benamou-Brenier) is well-established, and papers like those from Lipman et al. on flow matching, Albergo & Vanden-Eijnden on stochastic interpolants, and Liu et al. on rectified flows have already covered significant ground here. I read through Section 3 twice and I'm struggling to identify what is genuinely new beyond combining existing components. The authors should be much more explicit about this — right now the related work section (around page 3-4) reads as somewhat dismissive of very closely related concurrent work.

The experimental results are the paper's stronger suit, but I have reservations. Table 1 showing mutual information estimation results — the improvements over baselines like MINE and InfoNCE are modest in some regimes, and I notice the authors don't report variance across multiple runs consistently. This is sloppy. For high-dimensional MI estimation (the main claimed contribution), the comparison set feels cherry-picked. What about more recent neural estimators?

The "infinitesimal density ratio estimation" framing in Section 4.2 is interesting and I think this might actually be the most novel contribution, but it's buried and underdeveloped. I'd honestly like to see this expanded significantly at the expense of some of the other applications.

Figure 3 is hard to parse — the caption doesn't explain what we're actually looking at clearly enough, and the color scheme makes it difficult to distinguish distributions in the 2D examples.

I'm not entirely sure the scalability claims hold up. The paper says "high-dimensional" prominently but the image experiments are on relatively standard benchmarks (64x64 images if I'm reading correctly). This isn't really stress-testing the method at the scale I'd expect given the title's promise.

One more thing that bothered me: Equation 7 introduces a regularization term but the sensitivity to this hyperparameter is never properly ablated. In my experience with flow-based methods, this kind of regularization can completely dominate performance and the lack of analysis here is a real gap.

The writing is generally clear but occasionally imprecise. "Accessible via finite samples" in the abstract sounds obvious — all empirical work operates this way.

Overall I think this is borderline. There are interesting ideas here and the applications are legitimate, but the novelty case needs to be made much more carefully, and the experiments need tightening. I'd lean toward weak reject at this stage, though I'm open to being convinced otherwise if the authors can sharpen the novelty argument.

This paper tackles an interesting problem in high-dimensional Bayesian optimization — the "collision" phenomenon where points with very different objective values get mapped to nearby locations in the learned latent space. The motivation is clear and I think the core observation is genuinely useful. Figure 2 does a decent job illustrating the collision problem visually, though I wish they had been more quantitative about how prevalent this is across different architectures and datasets before jumping to the proposed fix.

The regularizer itself (equation 4 or so) is fairly intuitive — penalizing pairs that are too close in latent space relative to their target distance. This is essentially a margin-based or metric learning approach, and I have to say I was a bit surprised the authors didn't engage more seriously with the metric learning literature. There's a ton of work on contrastive losses, triplet losses, etc. that does something quite similar. The paper references some BO-specific works but feels somewhat siloed. I might be wrong, but I think a more careful discussion of how CoFLO differs from, say, siamese network approaches would strengthen the paper considerably.

The theoretical section is... okay. Theorem 1 provides regret bounds but I found myself skeptical about some of the assumptions — particularly the Lipschitz continuity assumption on the learned mapping, which is essentially what the regularizer is trying to enforce. There's a bit of circularity here that I found troubling after reading the proof sketch twice. The bound also seems to scale poorly with dimension in ways the authors don't really discuss.

Empirically, the results on synthetic benchmarks look good, and I appreciated the cosmic experimental design application — that's a genuinely interesting use case that I don't often see in BO papers. However, Table 2 shows CoFLO underperforming on a couple of benchmarks and the authors basically wave this away in a single sentence. That's not acceptable. When your method loses, you need to understand why.

A few smaller issues: the ablation in Section 4.3 is helpful but I wanted to see sensitivity to the margin hyperparameter more thoroughly explored. Also, the runtime comparison is oddly buried and the overhead of computing pairwise distances at scale seems non-trivial — the paper is a bit too breezy about this.

Overall I think this is a solid contribution with a real idea at its core. But the related work discussion needs serious work, the theory feels a bit hand-wavy in places, and the empirical analysis of failure cases is insufficient. I'm leaning toward borderline accept — the application is interesting and the collision framing is a nice conceptual contribution — but I'd want to see revisions addressing the metric learning connections and the underperformance cases before I'd feel comfortable recommending acceptance.

This paper proposes a framework for one-class classification and anomaly detection based on learning a projection to a known target distribution (truncated Gaussian, uniform in hyperball, etc.) while maintaining reconstruction fidelity. The core idea is reasonable and I can see the intuition behind wanting the normal data to map to a compact, simple distribution while abnormal data would presumably fall outside it.

That said, I have significant concerns about the novelty and execution here. This is essentially a constrained autoencoder with a specific regularization on the latent space — the connection to existing work on normalizing flows, VAE-based anomaly detection, and hyperspherical VAEs feels underexplored. The authors claim their method is "novel" but I spent a good chunk of my reading time thinking "wait, haven't I seen something very close to this?" Specifically, the relationship to Deep SVDD (Ruff et al., 2018) is briefly acknowledged but I don't think the authors adequately distinguish their contribution. The hypersphere target distribution in particular feels like it's treading very similar ground.

The experimental evaluation is where I think this paper has the most serious problems. "A few benchmark datasets" is not really sufficient for a paper making broad claims about anomaly detection in 2024. Where are results on MVTec? On more recent challenging benchmarks? The results presented (I'm looking at Table 1 and Table 2) show mixed improvements over baselines, and in my experience when results are this mixed with selective dataset choice, it often means the method has specific regimes where it works well but isn't as general as claimed. The ablation in Section 4.3 is helpful but doesn't fully convince me the architectural choices are principled rather than tuned.

I also found the theoretical justification a bit hand-wavy. The "informativeness" criterion is described qualitatively but never formally defined. What exactly does it mean for the transformed data to "preserve important information"? This is doing a lot of work in the paper's motivation and deserves more rigor. Equation (3) and the surrounding discussion felt rushed to me.

On the positive side — the writing is generally clear, the idea is intuitive, and I did appreciate that the authors compare multiple target distributions rather than committing to one. That's actually a nice empirical contribution and the analysis in Figure 3 is interesting, though I wish it were more thorough.

Honestly, I think this paper needs more work before it's ready for a top venue. The core idea might be publishable somewhere with substantially stronger experiments and a cleaner theoretical story. Right now it reads like a workshop paper that got extended without fully addressing what makes it distinct from the crowded field of distribution-matching approaches to anomaly detection. I'm recommending rejection, but I'd encourage the authors to resubmit with a stronger experimental suite.

I'll be honest — I struggled quite a bit with this paper, and after reading it twice I'm still not entirely sure what the core contribution is supposed to be.

The abstract mentions "probable dataset searching" but I genuinely cannot tell from either the abstract or the introduction what this means in a precise technical sense. The phrase "uncertain dataset information" is used repeatedly without a formal definition. Are we talking about missing labels? Distribution shift? Subset selection? The paper seems to gesture at all three but never commits to a clear problem formulation. By page 3 I was more confused than when I started.

The connection to unsupervised learning and domain adaptation mentioned in the abstract essentially disappears in the body of the paper. That's a red flag — it suggests the framing was retrofitted rather than principled.

On the technical side, the proposed loss functions (equations 3-5, if I'm reading the numbering correctly) appear to be fairly straightforward modifications of standard cross-entropy variants. I'm not convinced there's sufficient novelty here. The justification for why these particular formulations help "search for probable datasets" is hand-wavy at best. The theoretical grounding is minimal to nonexistent.

The experiments on 9 datasets sound promising on paper, but Table 2 is nearly unreadable — the column headers don't clearly indicate what baselines are being compared, and the performance deltas in many cases look within noise margins. I'd want to see statistical significance testing here. What are the variance estimates? Were experiments run multiple times?

Writing quality is a serious problem throughout. Sentences like "dataset labels are postponed to be obtained" are grammatically awkward and conceptually unclear. This isn't just nitpicking style — unclear writing usually reflects unclear thinking, and I think that's the case here. The paper needs substantial revision before it's ready for a venue like this.

My broader concern is that "adjusting architecture hyperparameters" and "searching for probable datasets" feel like two separate problems that haven't been convincingly unified. Why do you need to search for a dataset to tune hyperparameters? The motivation never clicked for me.

I might be wrong about some of this — maybe there's a coherent idea buried here that the writing is obscuring — but in my experience a paper that can't clearly state its own problem in the abstract is usually not ready for publication. I'd recommend rejection and encourage the authors to significantly tighten the problem statement, improve the experimental rigor, and clarify the relationship between the two "types" of uncertainty they claim to address. The 9-dataset evaluation could be compelling if presented properly, but right now it reads like a collection of numbers without a story.

This paper proposes WaveToken, a wavelet-based tokenization scheme for time series foundation models. The core idea is to decompose input time series into wavelet coefficients, threshold/quantize them, and then train an autoregressive model to predict future coefficients. I've read this twice and think it's a reasonable paper with some genuine contributions, though I have several concerns.

The main idea is intuitive and I appreciate that the authors ground it in the long history of wavelet analysis for time series. The empirical evaluation is fairly comprehensive — 42 datasets is nothing to sneeze at, and the zero-shot experiments are important for the "foundation model" framing. The results in Table 2 and 3 look solid, and I found the case studies in Section 5 (trends, spikes, non-stationary series) genuinely compelling. These are the kinds of qualitative results that help me actually understand what a method is doing, and I wish more papers included them.

That said, I have real concerns about the ablations. The vocabulary size comparison (1024 tokens being highlighted as a feature) is interesting but I'm not convinced the authors have done enough work to disentangle whether the wins come from wavelets specifically versus just having a more compact/structured representation. What happens with a simple DCT tokenization? Or even just patch-based tokens with learned quantization? The paper doesn't compare against these obvious alternatives and that bothers me. I might be wrong but this feels like the central empirical question and it's kind of dodged.

The thresholding step (Section 3.2, around equation 4-5 if I recall correctly) deserves more scrutiny. The choice of threshold feels somewhat ad-hoc and the sensitivity analysis in the appendix is not fully convincing. In my experience with wavelet methods, this step can have an outsized effect on downstream performance and I'd want to see a more principled treatment or at minimum a cleaner ablation.

On the writing: the paper is generally clear but Section 3 gets dense quickly. The phrase "eloquent and compact language for time series" in the abstract made me roll my eyes a bit — let the results speak for themselves.

I'm also a little skeptical of the "foundation model" framing throughout. The model sizes discussed here are not particularly large and the zero-shot generalization, while decent, is not dramatically better than strong baselines in all settings. The gap in Table 4 is sometimes quite small.

Overall I think this is above the bar for acceptance — the wavelet tokenization idea is clean, the evaluation is broad, and the method is well-motivated. But I'd push the authors to add the DCT/alternative tokenization comparison and strengthen the thresholding analysis before I'd feel fully comfortable accepting. Lean accept from me, conditional on those revisions.

This paper proposes combining pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation for compressing BERT models, along with a new metric called "inverted computational complexity." I have significant concerns about this work that I think need to be addressed before it could be considered for acceptance.

Let me start with what I think is the most fundamental problem: the novelty here is very thin. Combining pruning, quantization, and KD for transformer compression has been done extensively — DistilBERT, TinyBERT, MobileBERT, Q-BERT, and many others have explored this exact territory. After reading this twice, I couldn't identify what P-BERT actually does differently at a technical level. The "pyramid" structure is mentioned but never rigorously defined in terms of how it specifically differs from existing progressive compression schemes. What's the actual architectural contribution?

The proposed "inverted computational complexity" metric (Section 3, around page 5-6 I believe) is presented as a key contribution but I found the motivation circular and the formalization weak. The authors claim it "more accurately captures hardware-specific performance characteristics" but never actually validate this claim against real hardware measurements. Where are the actual latency numbers on target devices — the cellphones and smartwatches they mention? Without profiling on real hardware, this metric is essentially untethered from the physical reality it purports to represent. I might be wrong, but this feels like a metric invented to make their results look better rather than one solving a genuine measurement gap.

The experimental section is where things get particularly concerning. The comparisons against baseline BERT models are fine as far as they go, but why isn't there a direct comparison against TinyBERT or MobileBERT under equivalent compression ratios? The GLUE results in Table 2 show "comparable accuracy" but comparable to what exactly, and at what compression level? The 60% reduction in their custom metric sounds impressive but means very little without grounding it in something standardized. In my experience reviewing compression papers, this kind of bespoke metric with no external validation is a red flag.

The writing also needs substantial work. The abstract is vague to the point of being misleading — phrases like "correlated techniques work simultaneously" don't actually explain the method. The evolutionary techniques mentioned in the title feel like an afterthought in the actual paper; I expected this to be more central.

I want to be fair: the general direction is reasonable and the engineering effort seems real. But the combination of thin novelty, unvalidated metrics, weak baselines, and absence of real hardware experiments makes this not ready for publication at this venue. I'd strongly encourage the authors to either (1) identify a more specific technical contribution within the compression pipeline, or (2) make the hardware-aware optimization story rigorous with actual device benchmarks. As it stands, this reads more like a systems project report than a research paper.

Recommendation: Reject.

This paper proposes a fairly straightforward idea: progressively increase sequence length during LLM pretraining, starting from 128 tokens and scaling up to 4096. The core intuition is that shorter sequences allow more tokens to be processed per unit time early in training, which the authors argue leads to faster convergence and better final performance.

I'll be honest — this isn't a particularly novel idea. Curriculum learning over sequence lengths has been explored in various forms going back years, and I'm somewhat surprised the authors don't engage more seriously with this prior work. There's literature on similar ideas from the BERT-era (e.g., training with shorter sequences first), and more recently work on context length extension during fine-tuning. The related work section feels thin and I came away feeling like the novelty claim is overstated. The paper essentially needs to be much more careful about positioning itself relative to what already exists.

On the experimental side — I think the results are actually somewhat interesting, but there are serious methodological concerns. The comparisons in Table 2 (and elsewhere) don't make it entirely clear that baselines are being trained with equivalent computational budgets. This is crucial. If your method processes more tokens in less wall-clock time by virtue of shorter sequences, you need to be extremely careful about what "fair comparison" means here. Is the comparison token-matched? FLOP-matched? Wall-clock matched? After reading this twice I still wasn't fully sure the authors controlled for this properly throughout all experiments.

The writing is okay but occasionally imprecise. Phrases like "potentially boosting their performance" in the abstract are weak hedges that undermine confidence in the results. Either it does or it doesn't — show me the numbers.

I'm also not entirely convinced by the claim that this "does not require any additional engineering efforts." You still need to implement the schedule, handle the transitions between length stages, and potentially deal with issues around positional encodings as lengths grow. That's not zero effort.

What I found somewhat compelling is the empirical observation that models do seem to converge faster under this regime, and Figure 3 gives a reasonably clear picture of this. But I'd want to see ablations on the schedule itself — does it matter how quickly you increase length? What if you jump straight to 1024 first? The current experiments don't really disentangle what's driving the gains.

My overall assessment is that this is a potentially useful engineering contribution that could be of interest to practitioners, but the paper as written overclaims novelty, underdelivers on rigorous experimental controls, and doesn't engage sufficiently with related work. I'd encourage a significant revision. Not ready for acceptance in current form.

This paper tackles face clustering via GCN-based methods, focusing on the well-known problem of noisy edges in kNN graphs. The core idea — transforming features into a "structure space" and then doing adaptive neighbor discovery — is reasonable and the results look strong on the standard benchmarks (MS-Celeb and IJB-B/C).

Let me start with what I think works. The structure space transformation (Section 3.2, if I recall correctly) is genuinely interesting. The intuition that neighborhood structure itself carries discriminative information is sound, and I've seen similar ideas in manifold learning literature going back quite a while. The adaptive neighbor discovery piece also addresses a real pain point — fixed k in kNN is a longstanding annoyance in clustering work, and the authors' solution feels principled rather than hacky.

That said, I have several concerns.

The ablation in Table 4 is appreciated but feels incomplete. I want to understand the individual contribution of the structure space transformation *versus* the adaptive neighbor discovery. Right now it's hard to disentangle them. How much of the gain comes from each component independently? The current ablation doesn't fully answer this, and after reading this section twice I'm still not confident I understand the interaction.

The computational cost is glossed over. Building these structure space representations for millions of faces — what does that actually cost at inference time? The paper mentions scalability in passing but I found the analysis pretty thin. In my experience, methods that look great on MS-Celeb can become impractical at larger scales, and I'd want to see at least wall-clock comparisons.

I'm also not entirely sure the comparison to baselines is fully fair. Some of the compared methods are from 2019-2020, and the field has moved. I might be wrong but I think there are more recent GCN-based clustering methods that aren't included — the authors should double-check their related work coverage through 2022.

The writing is generally clear but Section 3.3 (the adaptive thresholding) gets dense fast. Figure 3 helps but honestly the notation in Eq. 5-7 is a bit hard to follow on first read. A concrete worked example would help a lot here.

One thing that bothers me: the claim of "clean yet rich edges" is repeated throughout the paper almost as a slogan, but I'd like to see a direct analysis of edge quality — like precision/recall of edges in the constructed graph compared to baselines. The paper shows downstream clustering metrics but doesn't directly validate the core claim about graph quality. That feels like a gap.

Overall I lean toward acceptance — the results are solid and the idea is novel enough — but the authors need to address the computational analysis, strengthen the ablation, and either include more recent baselines or explicitly justify why they're not included. This feels like a paper that's close but needs one more revision pass.

This paper introduces COMMA, a benchmark for evaluating multimodal multi-agent systems with a focus on language-based communication in collaborative settings. The core motivation — that existing benchmarks don't adequately capture asymmetric information sharing between agents — is legitimate and I think underexplored. So I was genuinely interested reading this.

That said, I have some significant concerns.

The benchmark design is described at a high level in the abstract and intro, but I found the actual task construction somewhat underspecified, at least in the sections I read most carefully. The four "key categories of agentic capability" are mentioned repeatedly but I felt like the paper took too long to actually define them concretely. By page 4 I was still waiting for a crisp taxonomy. When it finally arrives, I'm not fully convinced the categories are orthogonal or that the paper makes a compelling case for why *these four* and not others.

The finding that GPT-4o fails to beat a random baseline in agent-agent settings is genuinely striking — if true. And I say "if true" not to be dismissive, but because I think this result deserves much more careful analysis than it receives. What exactly is the random baseline doing? How is it defined? I might be wrong but I felt like the random agent setup wasn't described with enough precision for me to fully trust the comparison. This is the headline result of the paper and it needs to be bulletproof.

The human-in-the-loop experiments are interesting but feel somewhat tacked on. The sample sizes here concern me — how many human participants? What was the protocol? I couldn't find these details clearly stated and this matters a lot for interpreting those results.

I do appreciate that the authors test both open-source and closed-source models. That's good practice. Table 2 (if I'm reading the right one) shows a reasonable spread of baselines.

One thing that bothered me throughout: the paper frames the asymmetric information setup as novel, but there's a decent body of work in emergent communication and referential games (Lazaridou et al., various Lewis signaling game extensions) that deals with exactly this kind of setup. The related work section feels thin here and doesn't adequately situate COMMA relative to that literature. That's not a fatal flaw but it weakens the novelty claims.

In my experience reviewing benchmarks, the hardest thing to get right is showing the benchmark actually measures what you claim it measures — i.e., validity. I don't think the paper does enough work here. Why should I believe performance on COMMA correlates with real-world collaborative multimodal agent ability?

Overall: interesting problem, some genuinely surprising empirical results, but the paper needs tighter experimental methodology, better justification of design choices, and a more thorough related work treatment. I'm leaning toward weak reject but could be convinced otherwise with revisions. The core idea is worth pursuing.

This paper proposes incorporating causal structure into differentially private synthetic data generation, with the main claim being that causally-informed generative models achieve better privacy-utility tradeoffs than standard approaches. The core idea is interesting and I think the motivation is sound — natural data does have causal structure, and ignoring this seems like leaving information on the table.

That said, I have significant concerns about both the theoretical and empirical contributions.

On the theory side: the guarantees proven in Section 3 rely on linear Gaussian assumptions that feel quite restrictive. The authors gesture at extending to "a broader class of models" but I found the conditions for this extension (around equations 4-5, if I'm reading correctly) to be underspecified. After reading this section twice I'm still not confident I understand exactly what assumptions are being made for the general case. The theoretical result feels somewhat expected once you accept the setup — essentially you're reducing sensitivity by exploiting conditional independence structure — and I'd want to see a more careful discussion of when these conditions actually hold in practice. In my experience with causal inference literature, learned causal graphs are often wrong in ways that systematically bias downstream inferences, and the paper largely sidesteps this issue.

The empirical evaluation is my bigger concern. Using VAEs on what appear to be relatively small tabular datasets (I think Table 2 shows this, though the dataset descriptions are sparse) doesn't strongly validate the approach. The comparison baselines feel dated — where are comparisons against DP-CTGAN, PATE-GAN, or more recent DP synthetic data methods? The utility metrics reported are reasonable but I'd want to see downstream task performance, not just statistical similarity measures. The privacy accounting also needs more scrutiny — is the causal graph itself assumed to be public knowledge? If not, its discovery contributes to privacy loss and this needs to be accounted for.

I'm also worried about a somewhat circular aspect of the setup: you use causal structure to reduce sensitivity, but in practice you don't know the true causal graph. The paper uses... I think a learned graph? The sensitivity of the graph learning procedure itself to individual data points seems unaccounted for, which could substantially weaken the privacy guarantees in practice.

Figure 3 is hard to read — the color scheme doesn't reproduce well and I had to squint to distinguish the conditions.

I don't think this is a bad paper. The core idea is genuinely interesting and I believe there's something real here. But right now it reads like a promising workshop paper that needs more rigorous theoretical development and a substantially stronger empirical section. I'd encourage the authors to engage more seriously with the DP synthetic data literature (Jordon et al., Yoon et al., etc.) and to be much more explicit about where their assumptions break down. As it stands, I can't recommend acceptance.

This paper tackles an interesting and genuinely hard problem — unsupervised compositional world modeling from raw video without any auxiliary supervision. I appreciate the ambition here, and the core idea of the Recurrent Block-Slot Unit (RBSU) combining slot-based decomposition with recurrent dynamics is reasonable. However, I have several concerns that give me pause.

The evaluation methodology is where I'm most skeptical. The authors lean heavily on the DCI framework, which was designed for static image disentanglement evaluation. Applying it to video world models feels like a stretch, and I'm not convinced it captures what we actually care about — can the model usefully simulate compositional futures? The qualitative results on the website look decent, but cherry-picking is always a concern with generative models. I'd really want to see more systematic evaluation of the compositional imagination capability, not just the disentanglement metrics.

The RBSU itself — I read the architectural description twice and still found the interaction between the block-level and slot-level representations somewhat murky. Figure 3 helps, but the relationship between equations 4 and 5 could be explained more carefully. What exactly is being "blocked" versus "slotted"? In my experience, these kinds of hierarchical decomposition architectures live or die on the implementation details, and I felt the paper glossed over some key choices.
